```mermaid
    classDef aBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#1B5E20

    class start startBox
    class q1,q2,q3,q4,q5 qBox
    class a1,a2,a3,a4,a5 aBox
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style / Frequently Asked]**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP in terms of transport, architecture, and typical use case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable) | UDP (lightweight) |
| **Architecture** | Publish / Subscribe via a central **Broker** | Request / Response (REST-like) |
| **Header size** | ~2 bytes fixed header | 4 bytes minimum |
| **Default port** | 1883 (plain) / 8883 (TLS) | 5683 |
| **Typical use** | Continuous telemetry to cloud (AWS IoT) | Resource-constrained sensor networks (802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN) |
| **Quality of Service** | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | Confirmable / Non-confirmable messages |

**One-line takeaway:** *MQTT is broker-based pub/sub over TCP for cloud; CoAP is broker-less REST over UDP for constrained devices.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is 6LoWPAN? Why is it required in IoT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

6LoWPAN (**IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks**) is an adaptation layer protocol defined by the IETF (RFC 4944, 6282) that allows **IPv6 packets to be carried over IEEE 802.15.4** low-power radio frames.

**Why required:**

- IPv6 mandates every link to carry packets of at least **1280 bytes**, but 802.15.4 frames are capped at **127 bytes** (with at most **~102 bytes** payload).
- 6LoWPAN performs **header compression** (40-byte IPv6 header compressed to ~4–7 bytes) and **fragmentation/reassembly** to bridge this mismatch.
- It enables **every constrained device to have a globally routable IPv6 address** — a fundamental requirement for IoT scale.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries one full 14-mark question. Internal choice is provided.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1(a) + 1(b)]**

**(a)** Explain the **Physical Design of IoT** with a neat block diagram. List the main physical components and their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **IoT Communication Protocol Stack** in detail, classifying protocols at the **Link, Network, Transport, and Application layers** with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Design of IoT (7 marks)**

The Physical Design of IoT specifies the *physical entities* and *hardware building blocks* of an IoT system. The major components are:

1. **Things / Sensors** — Convert physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, motion, ECG, $\text{SpO}_2$) into electrical signals. Examples: DHT22, MAX30102, AD8232 (ECG).
2. **Actuators** — Convert electrical signals into physical action. Examples: relay, DC motor, solenoid valve, LED.
3. **IoT Nodes / End Devices** — Microcontroller + radio (e.g., ESP32, CC2530, nRF52840, Raspberry Pi Pico).
4. **IoT Gateways** — Protocol translators (e.g., Raspberry Pi running Mosquitto) that bridge 802.15.4/BLE to Wi-Fi/Ethernet.
5. **Communication Network** — Wi-Fi, Ethernet, Cellular (4G/5G), LoRaWAN, Satellite.
6. **Cloud / Edge Servers** — Data storage, analytics, ML inference, dashboards.
7. **Application Layer** — Mobile/web apps for users, alert systems, control panels.

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.2).**

**(b) IoT Communication Protocol Stack (7 marks)**

The IoT protocol stack maps onto the **OSI/TCP-IP** layers, with optimizations for constrained devices:

- **Link Layer:** IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee, 6LoWPAN), Bluetooth/BLE, Wi-Fi (802.11), LoRaWAN, RFID/NFC.
- **Network / Internet Layer:** IPv6 (128-bit), 6LoWPAN (adaptation), RPL (RFC 6550 routing in LLNs).
- **Transport Layer:** TCP (reliable, heavy) and UDP (lightweight, preferred for IoT telemetry).
- **Application Layer:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, DDS, XMPP, WebSocket.

A clean table is reproduced (refer to SECTION 2.5 above) summarizing ports, headers, and architectures.

**Engineering utility:** A typical wearable ECG monitor uses **BLE → MQTT (via phone) → Cloud HTTPS API → Doctor's dashboard**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MQTT protocol** in detail, including its architecture, message types, topic naming, and **three QoS levels**. Compare MQTT with **HTTP** for IoT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **CoAP**? Explain its **architecture, message format, Observe feature, and block-wise transfer**. How is it different from MQTT? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) MQTT Protocol (7 marks)**

- **Full form:** Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (OASIS standard, originally IBM, 1999).
- **Architecture:** **Publish/Subscribe** pattern with three logical roles — **Publisher** (sensor), **Subscriber** (app), **Broker** (server, e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX). All communication is **broker-mediated**; publishers and subscribers never connect to each other directly. This simplifies NAT/firewall traversal.

- **Connection flow:** Client opens TCP connection to broker on **port 1883** (plain) or **8883** (TLS) → sends **CONNECT** packet (with ClientID, username, password, Will topic, Will message) → broker replies **CONNACK** → thereafter **PUBLISH / SUBSCRIBE / UNSUBSCRIBE / PINGREQ / DISCONNECT** packets.

- **Topic naming:** Hierarchical strings separated by `/`, e.g., `hospital/icu/bed3/temp`. Wildcards: `+` (single level) and `#` (multi level).

- **QoS Levels:**
  - **QoS 0** — At most once. Fire-and-forget. No ACK. Suitable for frequent, non-critical telemetry.
  - **QoS 1** — At least once. PUBLISH → PUBACK. Possible duplicates. Default.
  - **QoS 2** — Exactly once. Four-packet handshake: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP. Slowest but no duplicates. Used for billing, alarms.

- **MQTT vs HTTP for IoT:**

| Aspect | MQTT | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Request/Response |
| Transport | TCP | TCP |
| Header | 2 B fixed | Hundreds of bytes |
| Power cost | Low | High (polling overhead) |
| Push from server | Native (broker) | Requires WebSocket / long-polling |
| Suitable devices | Constrained | Rich (gateways, cloud) |

- **Last Will and Testament (LWT):** Broker automatically publishes a "death" message if a client disconnects ungracefully — used for **offline detection** of IoT nodes.

**(b) CoAP Protocol (7 marks)**

- **Full form:** Constrained Application Protocol (IETF RFC 7252).
- **Architecture:** **Request/Response** (REST-like) running on top of **UDP (port 5683)**, designed for 802.15.4 + 6LoWPAN networks. Defined methods: **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE** — semantically identical to HTTP.
- **Message format:** Compact binary — only **4-byte header** containing Version (2 b), Type (2 b: CON/NON/ACK/RST), Token Length (4 b), Code (8 b), Message ID (16 b).
- **Confirmable (CON) vs Non-Confirmable (NON):** CON uses ACK + retransmit for reliability (like TCP lite); NON is fire-and-forget (like UDP).
- **Observe Pattern (RFC 7641):** A client marks a GET with the Observe option → server **pushes** new resource state whenever it changes. This is CoAP's native equivalent of MQTT's pub/sub — without a broker.
- **Block-wise Transfer (RFC 7959):** Splits large payloads into small blocks (default 16–1024 B) suitable for 802.15.4 frames, with **Block1 (request) and Block2 (response)** options.
- **Security:** CoAPs over **DTLS** (Datagram TLS) since it runs on UDP.

**MQTT vs CoAP — Final Comparison:**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Pattern | Pub/Sub + Broker | Request/Response (Observe) |
| Header | 2 B | 4 B |
| Port | 1883 / 8883 | 5683 / 5684 (DTLS) |
| Web friendly | Needs bridge | Maps to HTTP easily |
| Ideal for | Cloud telemetry, reliable delivery | Constrained sensor networks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT and CoAP transports** — MQTT is **TCP**, CoAP is **UDP**. Marks deducted even if other content is correct.
> 2. **Writing HTTP/HTTPS for "Application layer" without qualifying** — In Module 1, the focus is on IoT-specific protocols; mentioning HTTP is fine, but it should *not* be the central answer.
> 3. **Skipping the 6LoWPAN reason** — Many students describe 6LoWPAN but forget to derive the **127-byte vs 1280-byte mismatch**. Always show the calculation.
> 4. **Not labeling the DODAG / Objective Function in RPL** — Examiners expect RFC 6550 and the term "Objective Function" in any RPL question.
> 5. **Confusing ZigBee with 802.15.4** — ZigBee is the *full stack* built *on top of* 802.15.4. The radio is 802.15.4, the mesh + application is ZigBee.
> 6. **Missing ports** — Always quote standard ports (MQTT 1883, CoAP 5683, AMQP 5672, HTTPS 443) when describing a protocol. Examiners check this.
> 7. **Forgetting LWT and Observe** — These are signature features of MQTT and CoAP respectively. Missing them = -1 to -2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this list the night before the exam. Each item is a 1-mark hook in the KTU valuation key.**

- **Physical Design of IoT** = physical entities (sensors, actuators, tags, nodes, gateways, network, cloud, apps).
- **IoT Protocols** are classified into **Link, Network, Transport, Application** layers.
- **802.15.4** = 127-byte frames, 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz; foundation of ZigBee and 6LoWPAN.
- **Bluetooth/BLE** = GATT-based services and characteristics; range ~50–100 m; ideal for wearables.
- **Wi-Fi** = high data rate, high power; suited for gateways and mains-powered IoT.
- **RFID** = passive tag + reader; used in supply chain. **NFC** = subset, range < 10 cm, payments.
- **LoRaWAN** = 2–10 km range, 0.3–50 kbps, CSS modulation, LPWAN, smart cities.
- **ZigBee** = mesh networking *on top of* 802.15.4; smart homes.
- **IPv6** = 128-bit addressing; **6.67 × 10²³ addresses per m²** of Earth.
- **6LoWPAN** = adapts IPv6 over 802.15.4 via **header compression + fragmentation**; bridges 102 ↔ 1280 byte gap.
- **RPL (RFC 6550)** = routing in LLNs using **DODAG** and **Objective Function (OF/ETX)**.
- **UDP (8 B header)** = preferred transport for IoT (CoAP, RPL, MQTT-SN).
- **TCP (20 B header)** = reliable, heavier; used by MQTT, HTTP, AMQP.
- **MQTT** = TCP, **Pub/Sub + Broker**, port 1883/8883, **3 QoS levels**, LWT.
- **CoAP** = UDP, **Request/Response**, port 5683, 4-byte header, **Observe** + block-wise transfer.
- **HTTP/HTTPS** = web standard; ports 80/443; heavy for constrained nodes.
- **DDS** = brokerless pub/sub with **23 QoS policies**; real-time industrial.
- **AMQP** = port 5672, TCP, exchange/queue/binding model; enterprise IoT.
- **XMPP** = XML-based, presence, decentralized; legacy IM origins.
- **WebSocket** = full-duplex single TCP; used in real-time IoT dashboards.
- **Decision rule:** Coin-cell + mesh → **6LoWPAN+CoAP**; Cloud telemetry → **MQTT**; Long range → **LoRaWAN**; Sub-ms latency → **DDS**; Browser integration → **HTTP/WebSocket**.
- **Mnemonics:**
  - 802.15.4 → "**S**mall **S**low **S**hort" (frame, rate, range).
  - UDP → "**U Don't Pay**" (cheap).
  - TCP → "**T**ightly **C**ontrolled **P**rotocol" (reliable, expensive).
  - MQTT/CoAP → "**M**QTT is **M**ediation (broker), **C**oAP is **C**all (REST)."
- **Standard ports to memorize:** MQTT 1883/8883, CoAP 5683/5684, HTTP 80, HTTPS 443, AMQP 5672.
- **Headers to memorize:** UDP = 8 B, TCP = 20 B, IPv6 = 40 B, 802.15.4 MPDU = 127 B, CoAP = 4 B, MQTT fixed = 2 B.
- **Range tiers:** NFC < 10 cm < BLE/ZigBee < 100 m < Wi-Fi < 1 km < LoRaWAN < 10 km.
- **Always show derivation** for the 6LoWPAN question: 127 − 25 = 102 bytes vs IPv6's 1280 bytes.
- **Always state RFC** for RPL (RFC 6550), CoAP (RFC 7252), and MQTT (OASIS).
- **Always mention** LWT (MQTT) and Observe (CoAP) — these are KTU-favorite differentiators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Design of IoT - IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical Design of IoT - IoT Communication Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Communication Models** are standardized, abstract logical design patterns that define the rules, roles, and message-exchange choreography between heterogeneous IoT entities (things, gateways, cloud services, and applications). They specify **who initiates communication**, **how data flows**, and **what state is maintained** during the interaction, without prescribing the underlying physical transport (Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE, 5G, etc.).

The **logical design of IoT** decouples *what* must be communicated from *how* the bits traverse the wire. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies these logical patterns into **four canonical communication models**:

1. **Request-Response Model** — a synchronous, client-initiated dialogue.
2. **Publish-Subscribe Model** — an asynchronous, broker-mediated topic fan-out.
3. **Push-Pull Model** — a queue-buffered, producer-to-consumer pipeline.
4. **Exclusive Pair Model** — a stateful, full-duplex, bidirectional channel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Restaurant Analogy"** — Think of an IoT system as a busy restaurant:

| Communication Model | Restaurant Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Request-Response** | A customer walks to the counter, orders, and waits for food. The waiter goes back to the customer. (Customer = Client, Waiter = Server) |
| **Publish-Subscribe** | A radio station broadcasts news. Listeners tune in to the channel they care about. The station never knows who is listening. (Station = Broker, Listener = Subscriber) |
| **Push-Pull** | A conveyor belt carrying dishes. Chefs place (push) cooked plates; servers remove (pull) them whenever free. (Chefs = Producers, Servers = Consumers, Belt = Queue) |
| **Exclusive Pair** | A private phone call. Both parties have an open, dedicated line and can talk simultaneously without interruption. (Caller + Receiver = Bi-Duplex Channel) |

This mental model is the fastest way to recall the four models during KTU exams.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Board Emphasis)

- **Latency (L):** End-to-end delay, typically expressed in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Throughput (T):** Messages per second (**msg/s**) or bits per second (**bps**).
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Time taken for a request to reach the server and a response to return.
- **Bandwidth (B):** Maximum data rate, expressed in **bits per second (bps)**, **kbps**, or **Mbps**.
- **Jitter:** Variation in packet delay, in **ms**.
- **QoS (Quality of Service):** 0 (at-most-once), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once) for MQTT.

> [!TIP]
> For a **medical IoT** context (the dual focus of PCBRT402), **latency < 100 ms** is often clinically mandated (e.g., remote surgery, ICU alarms), making the *choice* of communication model a *life-critical decision*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Four IoT Communication Models

#### 2.1.1 Request-Response Communication Model

- **Initiator:** The **client** always initiates the dialogue.
- **State:** The server is **stateless** between calls (HTTP/1.1) or maintains minimal session state.
- **Flow:** `Client → Request → Server → Response → Client`.
- **Underlying Protocol Examples:** **HTTP**, **HTTPS**, **CoAP**, **REST APIs**, **gRPC**.
- **Why use it?** Simple, well-understood, cacheable, ideal for **on-demand queries** (e.g., "fetch latest ECG reading").
- **Limitation:** High overhead per request, unsuitable for continuous streaming.

#### 2.1.2 Publish-Subscribe Communication Model

- **Roles:** **Publisher** (sender), **Subscriber** (receiver), **Broker** (intermediary).
- **State:** Publisher and subscriber are **temporally and spatially decoupled** — they need not exist at the same time.
- **Flow:** Publisher sends a message *tagged with a topic*; broker filters and fan-outs to all subscribers of that topic.
- **Underlying Protocol Examples:** **MQTT**, **AMQP**, **DDS**, **XMPP**, **Apache Kafka**.
- **Why use it?** Excellent for **one-to-many** and **many-to-many** dissemination, low bandwidth, ideal for telemetry (e.g., broadcasting a patient's SpO₂ to multiple monitoring dashboards).
- **Limitation:** Requires a robust broker; QoS handling adds complexity.

#### 2.1.3 Push-Pull Communication Model

- **Roles:** **Producer** (pushes), **Queue/Buffer** (stores), **Consumer** (pulls).
- **State:** Queue maintains state; consumers operate at their own pace.
- **Flow:** `Producer → Queue → Consumer`. Multiple consumers can pull from the same queue (work-distribution).
- **Underlying Protocol Examples:** **Apache Kafka**, **RabbitMQ**, **ZeroMQ (PUSH/PULL sockets)**, **JMS**.
- **Why use it?** Decouples producer/consumer speeds, supports **load balancing**, ideal for **batch processing** of medical imaging or genomic data.
- **Limitation:** Higher end-to-end latency if queue is long.

#### 2.1.4 Exclusive Pair Communication Model

- **Roles:** **Client** and **Server** form a persistent, dedicated, **full-duplex** connection.
- **State:** Both endpoints maintain **full session state** for the lifetime of the connection.
- **Flow:** Bidirectional; either party can send at any time without re-establishing.
- **Underlying Protocol Examples:** **WebSocket**, **Bluetooth (RFCOMM)**, **TCP socket**, **gRPC streaming**.
- **Why use it?** Lowest latency after handshake, real-time bidirectional, ideal for **telemedicine video consultation** or **remote robotic surgery haptic feedback**.
- **Limitation:** Resource-intensive (one connection per client); no inherent fan-out.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **analytical formulas** that examiners may expect when reasoning about communication model performance in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_p + T_{proc}$ | $T_p$ = propagation delay (s), $T_{proc}$ = server processing time (s) |
| 2 | **Total Request Time (Request-Response)** | $T_{total} = N \times (RTT + T_{tx})$ | $N$ = number of requests, $T_{tx}$ = transmission time per packet (s) |
| 3 | **Transmission Time** | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{S}{B}$ | $S$ = packet size (bits), $B$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| 4 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance (m), $v$ = signal velocity ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) |
| 5 | **Throughput (Effective)** | $\eta = \dfrac{P \times (1 - p)}{T_{total}}$ | $P$ = payload bits, $p$ = packet loss probability |
| 6 | **Queue Wait Time (M/M/1)** | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho$ = $\lambda / \mu$ utilization, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate |
| 7 | **MQTT Broker Fan-out Latency** | $L_{ps} = T_{pub} + T_{broker} + N_s \cdot T_{sub}$ | $N_s$ = number of subscribers, $T_{broker}$ = broker processing time |
| 8 | **Jitter Bound (Streaming)** | $J = \vert L_{max} - L_{min} \vert$ | $L$ per-packet latency (ms) |
| 9 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \times RTT$ | Maximum unacknowledged bits in flight |
| 10 | **Reliability (Packet Loss)** | $R = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | Probability of at least one success in $n$ retransmissions |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value bars above use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert L_{max} - L_{min} \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals:** Publish-Subscribe (MQTT) for vitals telemetry → multiple dashboards; Request-Response (REST) for EHR queries.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Push-Pull (Kafka) for batch processing of machine logs.
- **Telemedicine:** Exclusive Pair (WebSocket) for real-time video and haptic control.
- **Wearables:** Request-Response (CoAP over 6LoWPAN) for low-power on-demand queries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Latency in a Request-Response IoT Transaction

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A wearable ECG sensor sends a **Request-Response** HTTP GET to a cloud server to fetch the last 10 seconds of waveform. Given:
- Packet size $S = 2 \text{ KB} = 16{,}000$ bits
- Bandwidth $B = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6$ bps
- One-way distance $d = 50$ km
- Signal velocity $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Server processing time $T_{proc} = 5$ ms
- Negligible queuing delay

**Step 1 — Compute Transmission Time $T_{tx}$:**
The transmission time is the time required to push all the bits of a packet onto the physical link.

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{16{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
T_{tx} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* Each bit occupies $1/B$ seconds of the link; 16,000 bits therefore need $16{,}000 / 10^6$ seconds.

**Step 2 — Compute One-Way Propagation Delay $T_p$:**
Propagation delay is governed by physics — the time for the electromagnetic signal to traverse the medium.

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
T_p = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* Light in fiber travels at roughly $2/3$ the speed of light in vacuum.

**Step 3 — Compute the Round-Trip Time (RTT):**
The RTT is the time from sending the first bit of the request to receiving the first bit of the response, ignoring transmission time of the response packet itself.

$$
RTT = 2 \times T_p + T_{proc}
$$

$$
RTT = 2 \times 0.25 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} = 5.5 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* One propagation each way + server processing.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Transaction Time $T_{total}$:**
The full end-to-end time includes both the request and response transmission.

$$
T_{total} = T_{tx,\text{req}} + RTT + T_{tx,\text{resp}}
$$

Assuming a symmetric response of equal size:

$$
T_{total} = 16 \text{ ms} + 5.5 \text{ ms} + 16 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 37.5 \text{ ms}
$$

*Logic:* Request bits leave the client (16 ms), travel and process (5.5 ms), response bits return to the client (16 ms).

> [!TIP]
> For **continuous ECG streaming** at 250 Hz, 37.5 ms is acceptable. For **haptic robotic surgery** (target < 10 ms), the **Exclusive Pair (WebSocket)** model would be mandatory, because the persistent connection eliminates re-handshake overhead.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Publish-Subscribe Broker Fan-Out Scalability

**Problem Setup:** A vital-signs publisher pushes 1 message/second to an MQTT broker. The broker forwards to $N_s$ subscribers. Each forwarding action takes $T_{sub} = 0.5$ ms. Find the maximum sustainable subscriber count before broker latency exceeds **100 ms** (a KTU board-style constraint).

**Step 1 — Write the Fan-Out Latency Equation:**

$$
L_{ps} = T_{pub} + T_{broker} + N_s \cdot T_{sub}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Quantities:**
Assume $T_{pub} = 1$ ms (publication) and $T_{broker} = 2$ ms (broker accept + topic match).

$$
100 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} + N_s \cdot 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N_s$:**

$$
N_s \cdot 0.5 \text{ ms} = 100 - 3 = 97 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
N_s = \frac{97 \text{ ms}}{0.5 \text{ ms}} = 194 \text{ subscribers}
$$

*Logic:* A linear fan-out is the broker's bottleneck. In real KTU/cloud deployments, this is mitigated with **topic hierarchy** and **shared subscriptions** (MQTT 5.0).

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Board Hot-Question)

| Property | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe | Push-Pull | Exclusive Pair |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tight (client knows server) | Loose (decoupled) | Loose | Tight (dedicated channel) |
| **Communication Style** | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous / Full-Duplex |
| **State** | Stateless server | Broker-mediated state | Queue-mediated state | Full session state |
| **Direction** | Half-duplex (request → response) | One-to-many / many-to-many | One-to-one (or work-distributed) | Bi-directional full-duplex |
| **Latency Profile** | Medium | Low–Medium (broker hop) | Variable (queue depth) | Lowest (after handshake) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (server bottleneck) | High (broker fan-out) | High (multi-consumer) | Limited (per-client socket) |
| **Example Protocols** | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP | Kafka, ZeroMQ | WebSocket, TCP socket |
| **Medical Use-Case** | EHR query, lab report fetch | ICU vitals to multiple monitors | Batch MRI processing | Tele-surgery haptic loop |
| **Failure Handling** | Easy (HTTP retries) | QoS levels 0/1/2 | Acknowledged offsets | TCP retransmit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT Communication Model Decision Flow

> The following Mermaid diagram maps a *decision tree* an IoT architect follows when picking a model — directly relevant to KTU design-type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: New IoT Data Flow] --> q1{Need Real-Time Bidirectional?}
    q1 -- Yes --> pairA[Exclusive Pair Model]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Need One-to-Many Fan-Out?}
    q2 -- Yes --> subA[Publish-Subscribe Model]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Producer and Consumer Speeds Differ?}
    q3 -- Yes --> pullA[Push-Pull Model]
    q3 -- No --> q4{On-Demand Query of a Resource?}
    q4 -- Yes --> reqA[Request-Response Model]
    q4 -- No --> subA

    pairA --> exA[WebSocket, TCP Socket]
    subA --> exB[MQTT, AMQP, Kafka]
    pullA --> exC[ZeroMQ PUSH PULL, Kafka Consumer]
    reqA --> exD[HTTP, CoAP, REST]

    style startA fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000000
    style pairA fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    style subA fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    style pullA fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000
    style reqA fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Publish-Subscribe (MQTT) Vitals Broadcast

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor Publisher
    participant B as MQTT Broker
    participant N1 as Nurse Dashboard
    participant N2 as Doctor Mobile App
    participant EMR as EMR Cloud Logger

    S->>B: PUBLISH topic=icu/bed5/spo2 value=98
    B-->>N1: Forward to subscribed topic
    B-->>N2: Forward to subscribed topic
    B-->>EMR: Forward to subscribed topic
    Note over B: Topic-based fan-out; publisher decoupled
    N1-->>S: (Optional) PUBACK QoS=1
    EMR-->>B: PUBACK QoS=1
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Request-Response (REST over HTTP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[Client App Wearable] -->|HTTP GET vitals bed5| gateA[API Gateway]
    gateA -->|route| serverA[App Server]
    serverA -->|query| dbA[Time Series DB]
    dbA -->|rows| serverA
    serverA -->|JSON response| gateA
    gateA -->|200 OK| clientA

    style clientA fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000000
    style gateA fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#854D0E,color:#000000
    style serverA fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    style dbA fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Push-Pull (Kafka Work Distribution)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prod1[CT Scanner 1] --> topicA[Kafka Topic MRI Scans]
    prod2[CT Scanner 2] --> topicA
    topicA --> cons1[Reconstruction Node A]
    topicA --> cons2[Reconstruction Node B]
    topicA --> cons3[Archive Node]

    style prod1 fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000000
    style prod2 fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000000
    style topicA fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    style cons1 fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    style cons2 fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    style cons3 fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000
```

### 4.5 State Diagram — Exclusive Pair (WebSocket Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Connecting: Client initiates handshake
    Connecting --> Open: HTTP 101 Switching Protocols
    Open --> Open: Bidirectional full-duplex frames
    Open --> Closing: Either side sends Close frame
    Closing --> Closed: TCP FIN acknowledged
    Closed --> [*]

    note right of Open: Persistent state; lowest latency
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between the Request-Response and Publish-Subscribe IoT communication models. Mention one example protocol for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Client always initiates | Either party can act independently |
| **Coupling** | Tight (client knows server URL) | Loose (publisher does not know subscribers) |
| **Communication** | Synchronous, one-to-one | Asynchronous, one-to-many |
| **State** | Server stateless | Broker holds topic state |
| **Example Protocol** | HTTP / CoAP | MQTT / AMQP |

*(Award 1 mark for the table, 1 mark for one correct protocol each, 1 mark for distinction clarity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**What is the Exclusive Pair communication model? Give one biomedical IoT use case where it is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Exclusive Pair model** establishes a **persistent, full-duplex, stateful connection** between a client and a server. Once established, either side can send messages at any time without re-handshaking. The connection is *exclusive* (dedicated) and *paired* (state is shared).

- **Use Case:** **Real-time telemedicine consultation** (e.g., video + audio + haptic feedback for remote robotic surgery), where continuous bidirectional low-latency communication is mandatory.
- **Underlying Protocol Example:** **WebSocket** (over TCP) — used because it avoids the overhead of repeated HTTP handshakes during the session.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the use case, 1 mark for the protocol example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **four logical communication models** of IoT with neat block diagrams. For each model, mention one **example protocol** and a **biomedical use case**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Request-Response Model (1.5 marks):**
- The **client** sends a request to the **server**, which processes and returns a response.
- Stateless server, synchronous, one-to-one.
- **Protocol:** HTTP / CoAP
- **Biomedical Use-Case:** A doctor's mobile app querying the hospital's REST API to fetch a patient's latest HbA1c lab value.

**2. Publish-Subscribe Model (2 marks):**
- A **publisher** sends messages tagged with topics to a **broker**. **Subscribers** register interest in topics; the broker fan-outs.
- Decoupled in time, space, and synchronization.
- **Protocol:** MQTT
- **Biomedical Use-Case:** A bedside ICU monitor publishing SpO₂, heart rate, and blood pressure to multiple subscribers (nurse station, doctor's tablet, central EMR) simultaneously.

**3. Push-Pull Model (1.5 marks):**
- **Producers push** messages into a **queue**; **consumers pull** at their own rate.
- Decouples producer/consumer speeds, supports load balancing.
- **Protocol:** Apache Kafka / ZeroMQ
- **Biomedical Use-Case:** Multiple MRI scanners pushing raw DICOM images into a Kafka topic; a cluster of reconstruction nodes pulls and processes them in parallel.

**4. Exclusive Pair Model (2 marks):**
- Persistent, full-duplex, stateful connection between client and server.
- After a one-time handshake, either side can send any time with minimal overhead.
- **Protocol:** WebSocket
- **Biomedical Use-Case:** A tele-surgery system streaming real-time video, audio, and haptic feedback between the surgeon's console and a remote robotic arm.

*(Diagrams: 2 marks distributed across the four models — full marks for clearly labeled components and flow direction.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** A wearable heart-rate sensor uses the **Publish-Subscribe model** to broadcast beats to a broker. The publication takes 1.2 ms, broker processing 2.5 ms, and each subscriber forwarding 0.4 ms. There are 50 subscribers. **Calculate the total broker fan-out latency**, and state whether it meets a clinical **real-time threshold of 30 ms**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
L_{ps} = T_{pub} + T_{broker} + N_s \cdot T_{sub}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
L_{ps} = 1.2 \text{ ms} + 2.5 \text{ ms} + 50 \times 0.4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute term by term:**

$$
N_s \cdot T_{sub} = 50 \times 0.4 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
L_{ps} = 1.2 + 2.5 + 20 = 23.7 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to threshold:**

$$
23.7 \text{ ms} < 30 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The system **meets** the 30 ms clinical real-time threshold with a **safety margin of 6.3 ms**. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **HTTP (Request-Response)** and **MQTT (Publish-Subscribe)** as IoT application-layer protocols. Discuss **bandwidth efficiency**, **latency**, **reliability mechanisms**, and **typical use cases** in a smart hospital.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | HTTP (Request-Response) | MQTT (Publish-Subscribe) |
|---|---|---|
| **Header Overhead** | Heavy (~200–800 bytes per request) | Light (fixed 2-byte header) |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Poor for tiny payloads | Excellent for tiny payloads |
| **Latency** | Higher (full handshake per request) | Lower (keep-alive persistent) |
| **Reliability** | Application-level retries | Built-in QoS 0/1/2 |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (unsuitable for battery devices) | Low (ideal for wearables) |
| **State** | Stateless | Stateful broker |
| **Direction** | One-to-one, client-initiated | Many-to-many, decoupled |
| **Hospital Use Case** | Fetching EHR via REST API | Continuous vitals to multiple dashboards |

**Discussion (full-marks elaboration):**

- **Bandwidth Efficiency (2 marks):** HTTP's verbose headers dominate small IoT packets (e.g., a 10-byte temperature reading in a 500-byte HTTP packet gives only 2% efficiency). MQTT's 2-byte fixed header and topic-based filtering make it 10–100× more efficient for telemetry.
- **Latency (1 mark):** HTTP must re-establish TCP/TLS for every cold connection (~100–200 ms); MQTT uses a long-lived connection (RTT 5–20 ms).
- **Reliability (2 marks):** MQTT offers three explicit QoS levels — at-most-once (fire-and-forget), at-least-once (PUBACK), and exactly-once (4-way handshake). HTTP relies on the application to add retry logic.
- **Use Case Mapping (2 marks):** HTTP → on-demand EHR queries by clinicians; MQTT → continuous ICU monitoring of multiple patients.

**(b) [7 marks]** An IoT gateway aggregates data from **5 biomedical sensors** (each producing a 256-byte packet every 1 s) and uploads it to the cloud over a **256 kbps** link. Compute: (i) the **transmission time per packet**, (ii) the **time to transmit 1 second worth of aggregated data**, and (iii) the **link utilization** in a 1-second window.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 5$ sensors, $S = 256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits}$ per packet, $B = 256 \text{ kbps} = 256{,}000 \text{ bps}$, window $T = 1$ s.

**(i) Transmission time per packet:**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{2048}{256{,}000} = 0.008 \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Time to transmit 1 second of aggregated data:**
Total bits in 1 s window:

$$
S_{total} = N \times S = 5 \times 2048 = 10{,}240 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T_{agg} = \frac{S_{total}}{B} = \frac{10{,}240}{256{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Link utilization:**

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{agg}}{T} = \frac{40 \text{ ms}}{1000 \text{ ms}} = 0.04 = 4\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Only **4%** of the link capacity is used, leaving abundant headroom for additional sensors, retransmissions, and parallel traffic — typical of a well-designed medical telemetry gateway.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "stateless" vs "stateful":** Many students incorrectly state the **Publish-Subscribe** model is stateless. The *publisher-subscriber pair* is decoupled, but the **broker holds the topic state** — state has merely *moved* to the broker.
> 2. **Mixing up Push-Pull with Publish-Subscribe:** Push-Pull uses a **queue (work distribution)**; Publish-Subscribe uses a **topic (broadcast)**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the protocol example:** A 7-mark sub-part that omits the *example protocol* typically loses **1 mark**. Always pair each model with MQTT/AMQP/HTTP/WebSocket/Kafka.
> 4. **Unit errors in latency calculations:** Mixing ms and s in the same equation. Always normalize to a single unit **before** substituting.
> 5. **No diagram in a 7-mark question:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a diagram for design/architecture questions; missing it costs **at least 2 marks**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **four canonical IoT communication models** are: **Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push-Pull, Exclusive Pair**.
- ✅ **Request-Response** → HTTP, CoAP → *on-demand queries*.
- ✅ **Publish-Subscribe** → MQTT, AMQP → *one-to-many telemetry* (the dominant model in medical IoT).
- ✅ **Push-Pull** → Kafka, ZeroMQ → *decoupled batch processing*.
- ✅ **Exclusive Pair** → WebSocket → *real-time full-duplex (tele-surgery, telemedicine)*.
- ✅ Latency formula: $T_{total} = T_{tx} + RTT$ for Request-Response; $L_{ps} = T_{pub} + T_{broker} + N_s \cdot T_{sub}$ for Publish-Subscribe.
- ✅ Transmission time: $T_{tx} = S / B$ (always convert to consistent units).
- ✅ **MQTT QoS levels** — 0 (fire-and-forget), 1 (at-least-once), 2 (exactly-once) — must be remembered verbatim.
- ✅ **Jitter** = $\vert L_{max} - L_{min} \vert$ — critical for streaming vital signs.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE, always **draw a block diagram** in communication-model questions; it earns easy marks.
- ✅ The **logical design** of IoT is **transport-agnostic** — the same model can run over Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE, or 5G.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication APIs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Communication APIs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT Communication APIs (Application Programming Interfaces)** are a standardized set of protocols, data exchange formats, and architectural rules that define **how IoT devices, gateways, and cloud services communicate, exchange data, and invoke remote services** over the network. They form the **software contract layer** of the IoT protocol stack, sitting just above the transport layer and enabling interoperability between heterogeneous devices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, IoT Communication APIs refer specifically to the **web-based service communication paradigms** used in the IoT reference architecture — primarily:

1. **REST-based Communication APIs (RESTful Web Services)**
2. **SOAP-based Communication APIs (Web Services)**
3. **WebSocket-based Communication APIs**
4. **Publish/Subscribe APIs (MQTT, AMQP, CoAP)** *(indirectly via the messaging layer)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBRT402 - Module 1):**
> The official KTU syllabus lists *"IoT Communication APIs"* as a sub-topic of *"IoT Architecture".* The board examination typically tests three (3) APIs: **REST, SOAP, and WebSocket**, along with comparative analysis of their suitability for IoT.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are at a restaurant in a foreign country where you don't speak the language:

| Real-World Analogy | IoT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 🧑‍🍳 **The Waiter** | The **API** — takes your request, delivers it to the kitchen, and brings the food back |
| 📜 **The Menu** | The **API Contract** — defines what you *can* order (which endpoints exist) |
| 🍳 **The Kitchen** | The **Server / IoT Cloud** — does the actual work |
| 🍔 **The Food** | The **Data / Response** (JSON, XML payload) |
| 🗣️ **English (Standard Language)** | The **Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)** — common language both sides understand |

So an **IoT Communication API is the standardized "waiter"** that allows a temperature sensor (customer) to ask the cloud (kitchen) for storage and analytics, without either side needing to know the internal implementation of the other.

---

## 1.3 Why Communication APIs Matter in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a well-defined **Communication API**, an IoT system collapses into a "tower of Babel" where every device speaks a different dialect. APIs provide:
> - **Interoperability** across vendors (e.g., a Samsung sensor talking to AWS IoT)
> - **Abstraction** hiding hardware complexity from application developers
> - **Scalability** enabling millions of devices to be managed uniformly
> - **Security boundary** with standardized authentication (OAuth 2.0, API Keys, TLS)

**Core Architectural Position:**

$$\text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{[ Communication API ]} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer} \rightarrow \text{Physical Layer}$$

---

## 1.4 The Three Primary API Paradigms (KTU Focus)

### A. REST (Representational State Transfer)
A **resource-oriented architectural style** that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on URIs. It is **stateless**, **cacheable**, and typically uses **JSON** as the data interchange format. REST is the most widely adopted API in modern web-IoT systems.

### B. SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)
A **protocol-based, heavyweight XML messaging standard** that uses WSDL (Web Services Description Language) for contract definition. It is **highly formal**, supports **WS-Security**, and is preferred in enterprise and healthcare IoT (e.g., HL7/FHIR).

### C. WebSocket
A **full-duplex, persistent connection protocol** over a single TCP socket. Unlike REST (which is request-response and half-duplex), WebSocket enables **real-time bidirectional streaming** — critical for live biosignal monitoring in the biomedical IoT domain of this course.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bold Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> Key KTU-referable metrics you must remember:
> - **REST default port:** TCP **80** (HTTP) / **443** (HTTPS)
> - **SOAP default port:** TCP **80** (HTTP) / **443** (HTTPS) — same as HTTP since it's an envelope over HTTP
> - **WebSocket default port:** TCP **80** (WS) / **443** (WSS)
> - **HTTP standard methods (REST):** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`
> - **Typical REST data format:** **JSON** (also XML, YAML)
> - **Typical SOAP data format:** **XML** (envelope-based)
> - **REST constraint count (Fielding's dissertation):** **6** architectural constraints
> - **WSDL element root:** `<definitions>` (XML namespace standard)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 REST-based Communication API — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Origin and Formal Definition

REST was introduced by **Roy Fielding** in his **2000 doctoral dissertation** at UC Irvine as the architectural style of the **World Wide Web**. It is **not a protocol** but a set of **architectural constraints** that, when applied to a system, make it "RESTful".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Fielding, 2000):** *REST is an architectural style that emphasizes scalability, statelessness, and uniform interfaces, where resources are identified by URIs and manipulated via standard HTTP methods.*

### 2.1.2 The Six REST Architectural Constraints (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Constraint | Meaning | Why It Matters for IoT |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Client-Server** | Separation of UI (client) from data storage (server) | Sensor and dashboard can evolve independently |
| 2 | **Stateless** | Each request contains all info needed; server stores no session | Critical for low-memory IoT devices |
| 3 | **Cacheable** | Responses declare cacheability | Saves bandwidth on battery-powered nodes |
| 4 | **Uniform Interface** | Standardized resource URIs and HTTP methods | Any device can talk to any cloud |
| 5 | **Layered System** | Client cannot tell if connected to end-server or proxy | Enables gateways, load balancers, CDNs |
| 6 | **Code on Demand** *(optional)* | Server can send executable code (e.g., JavaScript) | Rare in IoT due to security concerns |

### 2.1.3 HTTP Verbs Used in RESTful IoT APIs

| HTTP Method | CRUD Operation | IoT Example | Idempotent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | **R**ead | Fetch latest temperature from sensor | ✅ Yes |
| `POST` | **C**reate | Register a new device on cloud | ❌ No |
| `PUT` | **U**pdate/Replace | Update device firmware version | ✅ Yes |
| `PATCH` | **P**artial Update | Change just the sampling rate | ❌ No |
| `DELETE` | **D**elete | Decommission a sensor | ✅ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Idempotency** is a KTU favorite concept: an operation is idempotent if performing it multiple times yields the same result. Essential for unreliable IoT networks where retries are common.

### 2.1.4 REST Request/Response Structure

A typical RESTful IoT API call:

```http
GET /api/v1/sensors/temp001/data HTTP/1.1
Host: iot-cloud.example.com
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Accept: application/json
```

The JSON response payload:

```json
{
  "sensor_id": "temp001",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-26T10:30:00Z",
  "value": 36.8,
  "unit": "celsius",
  "battery_pct": 87
}
```

---

## 2.2 SOAP-based Communication API — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol)** is a **protocol-based, XML-driven messaging specification** for exchanging structured information in a decentralized, distributed environment. It relies on **WSDL** (Web Services Description Language) for service description and **XML Schema** for data typing.

### 2.2.2 SOAP Message Architecture (Anatomy)

A SOAP message is a complete XML document with the following structure:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<soap:Envelope 
    xmlns:soap="http://www.w3.org/2003/05/soap-envelope"
    xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance">
  <soap:Header>
    <!-- Optional: Authentication, transaction IDs, WS-Security tokens -->
    <wsse:Security>
      <wsse:UsernameToken>
        <wsse:Username>iot_device_42</wsse:Username>
        <wsse:Password>p@ssw0rd!</wsse:Password>
      </wsse:UsernameToken>
    </wsse:Security>
  </soap:Header>
  <soap:Body>
    <!-- Mandatory: The actual payload -->
    <GetVitalSigns xmlns="http://biomedical.iot/hl7">
      <PatientID>P-1024</PatientID>
      <Timestamp>2026-01-26T10:30:00Z</Timestamp>
    </GetVitalSigns>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Mandatory Parts of a SOAP Message:**
> 1. **SOAP Envelope** — root element, namespaces
> 2. **SOAP Header** *(optional)* — meta info, security, routing
> 3. **SOAP Body** *(mandatory)* — actual request/response payload

### 2.2.3 WSDL — The Service Contract

**WSDL (Web Services Description Language)** is an XML document that describes:
- **What** the service does (operations)
- **How** to invoke it (message format)
- **Where** to find it (endpoint URL)

WSDL Structure (abstract → concrete):

| Element | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `<types>` | XML Schema data types |
| `<message>` | Input/output parameters |
| `<portType>` | Abstract operations (like a Java interface) |
| `<binding>` | Protocol mapping (e.g., SOAP over HTTP) |
| `<port>` / `<service>` | Concrete network address |

---

## 2.3 WebSocket-based Communication API — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WebSocket** is a computer communications protocol, standardized by the **IETF as RFC 6455 (2011)**, providing **full-duplex communication channels over a single TCP connection**. It enables real-time data push from server to client without the client repeatedly polling.

### 2.3.2 Why WebSocket Was Invented (The HTTP Polling Problem)

Traditional REST over HTTP is **half-duplex** and **request-initiated**. For real-time IoT (e.g., ECG waveform streaming), the client would have to poll every 100ms — wasting bandwidth and battery.

**The "Handshake" Analogy:**

| Step | HTTP (REST) | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|
| Greeting | "Hi, got any news?" | "Hi, let's keep this line open" |
| Server's reply | "Here's the data" | (can push anytime) |
| Closing | Hangs up | Stays open until either side hangs up |
| Cost | New connection each time | Single persistent TCP socket |

### 2.3.3 The WebSocket Handshake (RFC 6455)

The client initiates an **HTTP Upgrade request**:

```http
GET /ws/sensors/ecg HTTP/1.1
Host: iot-cloud.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
```

The server responds with `101 Switching Protocols`, and the TCP socket is **upgraded** from HTTP to WebSocket protocol. From then on, both sides can send **text** or **binary** frames.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is the **single most testable artifact** in this module. KTU frequently asks for a comparative study (7-mark question).

| Parameter | REST | SOAP | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architectural Style** | Resource-oriented (REST) | Protocol-based | Message-oriented |
| **Data Format** | JSON (typically), XML, YAML | XML (mandatory) | Text or Binary frames |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP (any) | TCP (single socket) |
| **Communication Mode** | Request-Response (Half-duplex) | Request-Response (Half-duplex) | Full-Duplex, Bidirectional |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Can be stateful | Stateful (persistent connection) |
| **Contract Definition** | OpenAPI (Swagger), RAML | WSDL (mandatory, formal) | No formal contract |
| **Standards Body** | None (de facto) | W3C | IETF (RFC 6455) |
| **Overhead / Payload Size** | Low (JSON is compact) | High (XML verbose + envelope) | Lowest (after handshake) |
| **Real-time Capability** | Poor (polling required) | Poor | Excellent (native) |
| **Security** | HTTPS + OAuth 2.0 + API Keys | WS-Security, WS-Trust, SSL/TLS | WSS (TLS over WebSocket) |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Medium | Low | High |
| **IoT Suitability** | High (cloud integration) | Medium (enterprise, medical) | Very High (real-time streams) |
| **Best Use Case in Biomedical IoT** | Fetching patient records on demand | HL7/FHIR medical records exchange | Live ECG/SpO2 waveform streaming |
| **Error Handling** | HTTP status codes (200, 404, 500) | SOAP Fault element | Custom application-level |
| **Discovery** | API Directory / OpenAPI | UDDI (deprecated) | N/A |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each API shines in the biomedical IoT world (relevant to PCBRT402):**
> - **REST** → Querying Electronic Health Records (EHR), device management dashboards, non-critical telemetry (e.g., room temperature)
> - **SOAP** → Hospital Information Systems (HIS), HL7-based legacy integration, secure insurance claims, regulated medical workflows
> - **WebSocket** → Real-time ECG/EEG waveform to a cardiologist's dashboard, ICU alarm push notifications, remote surgery haptic feedback
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Communication APIs are not heavily formula-based, but KTU expects you to **derive, justify, and quantify** design choices. Below are the analytical frameworks and worked numerical/architectural problems that examiners value.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Framework 1 — REST Constraint Compliance Score

To evaluate whether an IoT API is "truly RESTful", we can assign a compliance score.

### 3.1.1 Definition

$$\text{REST}_{\text{score}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot c_i}{W_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is compliance (1 = satisfied, 0 = violated) for constraint $i$
- $w_i$ is the importance weight (engineering judgement)
- $W_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i$

### 3.1.2 Worked Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An IoT startup built a sensor data API. Evaluate its REST compliance:
> - Uses HTTP ✅
> - Server stores session in memory (violates stateless) ❌
> - Resources identified by URI ✅
> - Uses JSON responses ✅
> - No caching headers ❌
> - Layered with CDN gateway ✅

Assigning standard weights $w_1 = 2, w_2 = 3, w_3 = 2, w_4 = 3, w_5 = 2, w_6 = 1$:

| Constraint | $c_i$ | $w_i$ | $w_i \cdot c_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Client-Server | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Stateless | 0 | 3 | 0 |
| Cacheable | 0 | 2 | 0 |
| Uniform Interface | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| Layered System | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| Code on Demand | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Sum** | — | **13** | **7** |

$$\text{REST}_{\text{score}} = \frac{7}{13} \times 100\% \approx 53.85\%$$

**Conclusion:** The API is **partially RESTful** but fails the two highest-weighted constraints (stateless, cacheable), making it unsuitable for battery-powered IoT nodes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Framework 2 — Polling vs WebSocket Bandwidth Analysis

This is a **favorite KTU numerical problem** for the 14-mark question.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A wearable ECG device sends data to a hospital dashboard. Each data packet is **256 bytes**. The cardiologist requires updates every **$T = 0.1$ seconds** (10 Hz refresh). Compare the bandwidth used by:
> - **REST polling** over HTTP/1.1
> - **WebSocket** streaming

Assume:
- HTTP request header overhead = **400 bytes** per poll
- HTTP response header overhead = **200 bytes** per response
- WebSocket frame header = **2 bytes** (smallest possible, no masking)
- Time period = **1 hour** = **3600 seconds**

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute polling frequency:**

$$f_{\text{poll}} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 10 \text{ polls/second}$$

**Step 2 — REST total payload per poll:**

$$P_{\text{REST/poll}} = H_{\text{req}} + P_{\text{data}} + H_{\text{res}}$$

$$P_{\text{REST/poll}} = 400 + 256 + 200 = 856 \text{ bytes/poll}$$

**Step 3 — REST bandwidth per second:**

$$B_{\text{REST}} = f_{\text{poll}} \times P_{\text{REST/poll}}$$

$$B_{\text{REST}} = 10 \times 856 = 8560 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 8.36 \text{ KB/s}$$

**Step 4 — REST bandwidth per hour:**

$$B_{\text{REST, 1h}} = 8560 \times 3600 = 30{,}816{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 29.39 \text{ MiB}$$

**Step 5 — WebSocket payload per frame:**

$$P_{\text{WS/frame}} = H_{\text{WS}} + P_{\text{data}} = 2 + 256 = 258 \text{ bytes/frame}$$

**Step 6 — WebSocket bandwidth per second:**

$$B_{\text{WS}} = 10 \times 258 = 2580 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 2.52 \text{ KB/s}$$

**Step 7 — WebSocket bandwidth per hour:**

$$B_{\text{WS, 1h}} = 2580 \times 3600 = 9{,}288{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 8.86 \text{ MiB}$$

**Step 8 — Compute bandwidth savings ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{B_{\text{REST}} - B_{\text{WS}}}{B_{\text{REST}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{8560 - 2580}{8560} \times 100\% \approx 69.86\%$$

### 3.2.3 Verdict

$$\boxed{B_{\text{WS}} \approx 0.30 \times B_{\text{REST}} \quad \text{(WebSocket uses only } \sim 30\% \text{ of REST bandwidth)}}$$

This is why WebSocket is preferred for real-time biomedical IoT streams.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Framework 3 — SOAP Envelope Parsing Cost

> **Problem:** A SOAP message has a total size of **$S_{\text{SOAP}} = 4$ KB**. The actual useful payload (the `<soap:Body>` content) is only **$P_{\text{useful}} = 600$ bytes**. Calculate the **SOAP Overhead Ratio** $O_R$.

**Step 1 — Compute overhead bytes:**

$$O_{\text{SOAP}} = S_{\text{SOAP}} - P_{\text{useful}}$$

$$O_{\text{SOAP}} = 4096 - 600 = 3496 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute overhead ratio:**

$$O_R = \frac{O_{\text{SOAP}}}{S_{\text{SOAP}}} = \frac{3496}{4096} \approx 0.8535 \text{ or } 85.35\%$$

$$\boxed{O_R \approx 85.35\% \text{ of every SOAP byte is metadata/envelope overhead}}$$

**Conclusion:** SOAP is bandwidth-inefficient for resource-constrained IoT — only **14.65% signal-to-noise ratio**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Framework 4 — Choosing the Right API (Decision Matrix)

A KTU-favorite 7-mark sub-question asks: *"For a given IoT scenario, justify which API is best."*

### 3.4.1 Decision Algorithm

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Is real-time, bidirectional streaming   │
│ required (e.g., waveform, alerts)?       │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
               │ YES → WebSocket
               │ NO
┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│ Is the application in a regulated,       │
│ enterprise, or medical-records domain?   │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
               │ YES → SOAP
               │ NO
               ▼
         REST (default)
```

### 3.4.2 Justification Table for KTU Answers

| Scenario | Best API | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Live ICU vitals to doctor's tablet | **WebSocket** | Real-time push, low latency <100ms |
| Daily summary of 10,000 patients to EHR | **REST** | Batch fetch, stateless, JSON compact |
| Insurance company pulling claims from hospital | **SOAP** | WS-Security, formal contract (WSDL), audit trail |
| Patient wearable pushing step count to cloud | **REST (POST)** | Occasional, simple, JSON-friendly |
| Smart city traffic signal coordination | **MQTT + WebSocket** | Pub/Sub + dashboard streaming |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **professional Mermaid block diagrams** illustrating the architecture of REST, SOAP, and WebSocket communication APIs in an IoT context. All node IDs follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — REST Request-Response Flow (IoT Context)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IOT SENSOR
    participant Gateway as GATEWAY
    participant API as REST API SERVER
    participant Cloud as CLOUD DATABASE
    participant App as MOBILE APP

    Note over Sensor,App: REST ARCHITECTURE - STATELESS CLIENT SERVER
    Sensor->>Gateway: 1. Raw Telemetry Data
    Gateway->>API: 2. HTTPS GET /api/v1/sensors/temp001/data
    API->>Cloud: 3. SQL Query SELECT value FROM sensor_data
    Cloud-->>API: 4. Return row 36.8 C
    API-->>Gateway: 5. HTTP 200 OK JSON Payload
    Gateway-->>Sensor: 6. Acknowledge
    App->>API: 7. HTTPS GET /api/v1/patients/P1024
    API-->>App: 8. HTTP 200 OK JSON Patient Record
    Note over App,API: EACH REQUEST INDEPENDENT NO SESSION STORED
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — SOAP Web Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide["CLIENT SIDE"]
        AppA["BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION"]
        StubA["SOAP STUB GENERATED FROM WSDL"]
    end

    subgraph Transport["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        HTTPA["HTTP OR SMTP PROTOCOL"]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["SERVER SIDE"]
        SkeletonA["SOAP SKELETON HANDLER"]
        ServiceA["SERVICE IMPLEMENTATION"]
        DBB["MEDICAL DATABASE"]
        WSDLA["WSDL CONTRACT FILE"]
    end

    AppA -- 1. Invoke Method --> StubA
    StubA -- 2. Serialize to XML Envelope --> HTTPA
    HTTPA -- 3. Send SOAP Message --> SkeletonA
    SkeletonA -- 4. Deserialize XML --> ServiceA
    ServiceA -- 5. Query Records --> DBB
    DBB -- 6. Return Data --> ServiceA
    ServiceA -- 7. Build SOAP Response --> HTTPA
    HTTPA -- 8. Return to Client --> AppA
    WSDLA -.->|Defines Contract| StubA
    WSDLA -.->|Defines Contract| SkeletonA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — WebSocket Full-Duplex Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HTTPRequest: Client Sends GET with Upgrade Header
    HTTPRequest --> Handshake: Server Responds 101 Switching Protocols
    Handshake --> OpenState: TCP Socket Upgraded to WebSocket
    OpenState --> OpenState: Bidirectional Frames Data ECG Waveform
    OpenState --> PingState: Heartbeat Ping Sent
    PingState --> OpenState: Pong Received Connection Alive
    OpenState --> CloseState: Either Side Sends Close Frame
    CloseState --> [*]: TCP Connection Released
```

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — REST vs SOAP vs WebSocket Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end
```
