# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU ESE):**
> - **Part A:** 2 marks × 5 questions (10 marks) — definitions and short concepts
> - **Part B:** Module-based choice — answer **ONE full 14-mark question** per module
> - Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks
> - Total ESE: **70 marks** (4 modules × 14 + 10 from Part A + others)

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
> **Define IoT Communication API. List any three types of IoT communication APIs.**

**Model Answer:**

An **IoT Communication API** is a set of standardized protocols, data formats, and architectural rules that enable IoT devices, gateways, and cloud services to exchange data and invoke services over a network. It abstracts the underlying hardware and transport, providing a uniform interface for heterogeneous IoT components.

**Three types of IoT Communication APIs (KTU Syllabus):**
1. **REST-based Communication API** — uses HTTP methods on URI-identified resources, typically JSON
2. **SOAP-based Communication API** — XML-based protocol with WSDL contract, used in enterprise
3. **WebSocket-based Communication API** — full-duplex persistent TCP connection for real-time data

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
> **What is a RESTful web service? Mention any four HTTP methods used in REST.**

**Model Answer:**

A **RESTful web service** is a web service that conforms to the REST architectural constraints (Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System) defined by Roy Fielding. It treats data as **resources** identified by URIs and manipulated using standard HTTP methods.

**Four HTTP methods:**
| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource |
| `POST` | Create a new resource |
| `PUT` | Update/replace an existing resource |
| `DELETE` | Remove a resource |

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Examiner Instruction:** *Answer ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks divided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of **REST-based Communication API** in IoT with a neat diagram. List the **six REST constraints** defined by Roy Fielding.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, describe the **SOAP message structure**. Explain the role of **WSDL** in SOAP-based web services.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

### (a) REST-based Communication API

**Definition:** REST (Representational State Transfer) is a resource-oriented architectural style for designing networked applications. It uses HTTP as the underlying transport and treats every entity (sensor, patient, device) as a **resource** addressable via a **URI**.

**Architecture Explanation:**

The REST architecture for IoT has the following layered structure:

1. **IoT Device / Sensor Layer** — Generates raw data (e.g., temperature, ECG)
2. **Gateway / Edge Layer** — Aggregates and forwards data to the cloud
3. **REST API Server** — Implements endpoints like `GET /sensors/{id}/data`
4. **Cloud Database** — Stores and processes the data
5. **Client Application** — Mobile app / dashboard that consumes the data

**Six REST Constraints (Roy Fielding, 2000):**
1. **Client-Server** — UI separated from data storage
2. **Stateless** — No client session stored on server
3. **Cacheable** — Responses must indicate cacheability
4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized URIs and HTTP methods
5. **Layered System** — Hierarchical proxies/gateways allowed
6. **Code on Demand** *(optional)* — Server can send executable scripts

**RESTful IoT Example Request:**
```http
POST /api/v1/devices HTTP/1.1
Host: iot.example.com
Content-Type: application/json

{ "device_id": "BP_Monitor_07", "type": "blood_pressure" }
```

**Response:**
```json
{ "status": "registered", "device_id": "BP_Monitor_07" }
```

---

### (b) SOAP Message Structure & WSDL Role

**SOAP Message Structure:**

A SOAP message is an XML document consisting of:

1. **SOAP Envelope** (root, mandatory) — defines XML namespaces
2. **SOAP Header** (optional) — contains metadata (auth, routing, transactions)
3. **SOAP Body** (mandatory) — contains the actual request/response payload
4. **SOAP Fault** (optional, inside body) — carries error/status information

**Sample SOAP Message:**
```xml
<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://www.w3.org/2003/05/soap-envelope">
  <soap:Header>
    <auth:Token>abc123</auth:Token>
  </soap:Header>
  <soap:Body>
    <GetPatient xmlns="http://biomed.iot">
      <PatientID>P1024</PatientID>
    </GetPatient>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>
```

**Role of WSDL (Web Services Description Language):**

WSDL is an XML-based contract document that describes a SOAP web service. It tells the client:
- **What** operations are available
- **What** input/output messages look like (XML Schema)
- **Which** protocol to use (SOAP over HTTP)
- **Where** the service is located (endpoint URL)

**WSDL Structure (key elements):**
- `<types>` — Data type definitions
- `<message>` — Parameters
- `<portType>` — Abstract operations
- `<binding>` — Protocol mapping
- `<service>` / `<port>` — Network address

**Why WSDL Matters in IoT:** Allows **machine-readable service contracts** — the client stub is auto-generated from WSDL, ensuring type safety and reducing integration errors in medical IoT deployments.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **WebSocket Communication API** with reference to **RFC 6455**. Explain the WebSocket handshake with a suitable example.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **REST, SOAP, and WebSocket** APIs across **at least eight parameters** in a tabular form, highlighting their suitability for IoT.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

### (a) WebSocket Communication API (RFC 6455)

**Definition:** WebSocket is a **full-duplex, bidirectional communication protocol** standardized by the IETF as **RFC 6455 (December 2011)**. It provides a persistent TCP connection between client and server, allowing both sides to send data at any time without the overhead of repeated HTTP requests.

**Why WebSocket was needed (The Polling Problem):**
Traditional HTTP-based REST requires the client to repeatedly send requests (polling) to check for new data. For real-time biomedical IoT (e.g., ECG waveform), polling every 100ms is bandwidth-wasteful and introduces latency.

**WebSocket Handshake (RFC 6455):**

**Step 1 — Client sends an HTTP Upgrade request:**
```http
GET /ws/sensors/ecg HTTP/1.1
Host: iot-cloud.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
```

**Step 2 — Server responds with 101 Switching Protocols:**
```http
HTTP/1.1 101 Switching Protocols
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Accept: s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo=
```

**Step 3 — Connection is now upgraded** to a WebSocket. Both client and server can send **text** or **binary** frames bidirectionally.

**Key WebSocket Features:**
- Single TCP connection (port 80/443)
- Frames have only **2–14 bytes** of overhead (vs. 600+ bytes in HTTP)
- Supports sub-protocols (e.g., `wamp`, `mqtt`)
- Built-in **ping/pong** heartbeat for connection liveness
- Secure variant: **WSS** (WebSocket over TLS)

**IoT Use Case:** Real-time ICU vital sign streaming — a bedside monitor pushes every 100ms via WebSocket to a doctor's tablet with sub-second latency.

---

### (b) Comparison Table: REST vs SOAP vs WebSocket

| Parameter | REST | SOAP | WebSocket |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Style** | Architectural style | Protocol | Protocol |
| **Data Format** | JSON (default), XML, YAML | XML (mandatory) | Text / Binary frames |
| **Transport** | HTTP/HTTPS | HTTP, SMTP, TCP | TCP (single socket) |
| **Communication** | Request-Response | Request-Response | Full-Duplex, Bidirectional |
| **Statefulness** | Stateless | Stateful or Stateless | Stateful (persistent) |
| **Overhead** | Low | High | Lowest (after handshake) |
| **Real-time Capability** | Poor (polling needed) | Poor | Excellent (native) |
| **Contract** | OpenAPI / Swagger | WSDL (formal) | None (sub-protocols) |
| **Standards Body** | None (de facto) | W3C | IETF (RFC 6455) |
| **Security** | OAuth 2.0, HTTPS | WS-Security, WS-Trust | WSS (TLS) |
| **IoT Suitability** | High (general) | Medium (regulated) | Very High (real-time) |

**Suitability Summary:**
- **REST** → Default for cloud integration, dashboards, non-time-critical telemetry
- **SOAP** → Regulated medical records (HL7), enterprise HIS, where WS-Security is required
- **WebSocket** → Real-time biosignal streams (ECG, EEG), ICU alarms, remote surgery control

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing REST with HTTP:** REST is an *architectural style*, not a protocol. HTTP is just one transport. Students who say "REST is a protocol" lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting WSDL in SOAP answers:** If a SOAP question doesn't mention WSDL, the answer is incomplete. WSDL is the *contract* that defines the SOAP service.
> 3. **Missing the 101 Switching Protocols status code:** The WebSocket handshake's success code is **`101 Switching Protocols`**, NOT `200 OK`. Examiners mark this specifically.
> 4. **Wrong default ports:** REST/SOAP/WebSocket all default to **80/443** when over HTTP/HTTPS. Students who say "SOAP uses port 8080" lose credit.
> 5. **Saying "WebSocket is a replacement for REST":** WebSocket *complements* REST; they serve different use cases. Examiners expect nuanced understanding.
> 6. **Confusing JSON with SOAP:** SOAP is **XML-only**. Students who write a SOAP message in JSON format will lose all marks for the message structure.
> 7. **Not labeling state in the comparative table:** Always include "Stateful/Stateless" in the comparison — it's a high-weight parameter.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Exam-Day Quick Recall Points — IoT Communication APIs**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- ✅ **IoT Communication API** = Standardized protocol layer enabling IoT device ↔ cloud communication
- ✅ **REST** = Resource-oriented architectural style, JSON over HTTP, stateless
- ✅ **SOAP** = XML-based formal protocol with WSDL contract, supports WS-Security
- ✅ **WebSocket** = Full-duplex persistent TCP connection (RFC 6455), real-time streaming

### 📌 REST — Must Remember
- ✅ **6 Constraints**: Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code on Demand
- ✅ **HTTP Methods**: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (update), DELETE (remove)
- ✅ **Idempotent methods**: GET, PUT, DELETE (multiple calls = same result)
- ✅ **Data format**: JSON (most common), XML, YAML
- ✅ **Stateless** = no session stored on server

### 📌 SOAP — Must Remember
- ✅ **Mandatory parts**: `<soap:Envelope>`, `<soap:Body>`; **Optional**: `<soap:Header>`, `<soap:Fault>`
- ✅ **Data format**: XML only
- ✅ **WSDL** = Web Services Description Language (the service contract)
- ✅ **Strength**: WS-Security, formal contracts, enterprise-grade
- ✅ **Weakness**: High overhead (~85% metadata ratio)

### 📌 WebSocket — Must Remember
- ✅ **Standard**: IETF **RFC 6455** (2011)
- ✅ **Handshake success code**: **`101 Switching Protocols`**
- ✅ **Headers**: `Upgrade: websocket`, `Connection: Upgrade`, `Sec-WebSocket-Key`
- ✅ **Port**: 80 (WS) / 443 (WSS)
- ✅ **Frame overhead**: 2–14 bytes (vs 600+ in HTTP)
- ✅ **Use case**: Real-time ECG/EEG streaming, ICU alarms

### 📌 Comparative — Must Remember
- ✅ REST = default for cloud
- ✅ SOAP = regulated/enterprise (medical, banking)
- ✅ WebSocket = real-time streaming
- ✅ All three use **port 80/443** by default
- ✅ REST is **stateless**; SOAP can be stateful; WebSocket is **stateful**

### 📌 KTU-Mandated Keywords (use these in answers for marks)
- "Resource-oriented", "Stateless", "URI", "HTTP methods", "JSON", "WSDL", "XML envelope", "WS-Security", "RFC 6455", "Full-duplex", "101 Switching Protocols", "Persistence connection", "Real-time streaming"

### 📌 Common Numerical Problems
- ✅ Polling vs WebSocket bandwidth comparison (expect 60–70% savings with WebSocket)
- ✅ SOAP overhead ratio (typically 80–90%)
- ✅ REST compliance scoring (weight × compliance)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Enabled Technologies - Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Enabled Technologies - Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous devices called **sensor nodes** (or motes) that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions, such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or biomedical signals, and pass their data through the network to a central sink (Base Station) via wireless communication links.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framing, WSNs form the **perception layer (sensing tier)** of the IoT stack, providing the foundational data acquisition substrate for the entire Internet of Things ecosystem. Each node typically integrates four subsystems: **sensing, processing, communication, and power**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCBRT402 / Module 1):** A WSN is treated as a *distributed embedded system* where the network itself is the computer. Individual node intelligence is sacrificed in favour of **collective intelligence, energy efficiency, and fault tolerance**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital ICU where dozens of small adhesive patches are stuck on patients' chests. Each patch measures heart rate, SpO₂, and temperature. Instead of each patch connecting directly to a central monitor via a thick wire (which would create a "spaghetti mess"), they whisper to each other wirelessly. One patch talks to its nearest neighbour patch, which forwards the message to the next, until the data eventually reaches a central nursing station display.

That chain of whispered messages is exactly a **Wireless Sensor Network**.

- The patches = **Sensor Nodes / Motes**
- The whisper-to-nearest-neighbour rule = **Multi-hop Routing**
- The central nursing station = **Sink / Base Station (BS)**
- The patient's body = the **monitored environment**
- The battery inside each patch = the most precious resource (**Energy**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** A WSN is **energy-constrained, bandwidth-limited, and data-centric** — not address-centric like the Internet.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (used throughout the WSN literature)

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Node transmission range | **10 m – 100 m** | Determines hop count |
| Node count per network | **100 – 10,000+** | Scalability benchmark |
| Battery capacity | **1 – 5 Ah @ 1.2 – 3.6 V** | Energy budget |
| Node lifetime target | **Months to years** | Network longevity |
| Data rate per node | **1 – 250 kbps** | Bandwidth constraint |
| Duty cycle | **< 1% (typical)** | Energy saving mode |

> [!NOTE]
> For biomedical IoT (a core concern of PCBRT402), the standard wireless standard is **IEEE 802.15.6 (Wireless Body Area Network, WBAN)**, supporting data rates from **75.9 kbps to 15.6 Mbps** with ultra-low power consumption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Wireless Sensor Node

Every WSN node (mote) has four fundamental building blocks. Each block is a major area of research, but the *energy budget* of the entire node is dominated by the **radio (communication) subsystem**, not the sensor or processor.

### 2.1.1 The Four Subsystems

1. **Sensing Subsystem**
   - Comprises one or more **physical transducers** (e.g., LM35 for temperature, ADXL345 for acceleration, MAX30100 for SpO₂).
   - Includes an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** and signal conditioning circuits.
   - Consumes the *least* energy in a well-designed node.

2. **Processing Subsystem**
   - Built around a microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0+) or a low-power SoC.
   - Runs the **MAC protocol, routing logic, data aggregation, and duty-cycling** firmware.
   - Has small on-chip memory (typically **10 – 512 KB RAM**).

3. **Communication Subsystem (Radio)**
   - Short-range transceiver (e.g., CC2420 for ZigBee, nRF24L01 for BLE).
   - **This is the dominant energy consumer** — typically **70 – 80% of total node energy**.

4. **Power Subsystem**
   - Battery (alkaline, Li-ion, or coin cell) often augmented with **energy harvesting** (solar, piezoelectric, thermal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Energy Paradox of WSN:** Reducing transmission distance (more hops) reduces per-hop transmit energy, but increases the number of hops — and *receive* energy is non-zero. An optimal hop distance therefore exists.

## 2.2 Network Topologies

WSNs are deployed in three canonical topologies. Each has very different energy and reliability profiles.

| Topology | Hop Structure | Energy Profile | Reliability | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | Single-hop (all → BS) | High at far nodes | Low (BS is SPOF) | Home automation, WBAN |
| **Mesh** | Multi-hop, peer-to-peer | Balanced | Very high | Industrial monitoring |
| **Cluster / Tree (Hybrid)** | Intra-cluster + inter-cluster | Most balanced | High | Large-scale IoT, LEACH |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are the **most repeatedly tested formulas** in KTU WSN problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Transmit energy for $k$ bits over distance $d$ |
| F2 | $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Receive energy for $k$ bits |
| F3 | $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}$ | Critical distance where free-space equals multipath model |
| F4 | $E_{total} = N \cdot \left( E_{sense} + E_{proc} + E_{comm} \right)$ | Total network energy for $N$ nodes per round |
| F5 | $\tau = \dfrac{T_{sleep}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Duty cycle ratio |
| F6 | $L_{network} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}}$ | Network lifetime in rounds (LEACH-like) |
| F7 | $P_{coverage} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{\pi r^{2}}{A}\right)^{N}$ | Probabilistic area coverage ($k$-out-of-$n$ model) |
| F8 | $P_{connectivity} \approx e^{-N \cdot \pi r^{2} / A}$ | Probability of network being connected |
| F9 | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) |
| F10 | $SNR_{dB} = P_{tx} - PL - N_{floor}$ | Link budget equation |

**Constants typically used in KTU numericals:**

- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free-space)
- $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath)
- $d_{crossover} \approx 87.7$ m

> [!WARNING]
> **Use $\vert$ not $\mid$ for absolute value in tables.** Always express absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking KTU PDF / Markdown parsers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare:** Continuous wearable ECG/SpO₂ monitoring with WBAN, replacing wired ICU setups.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture and micro-climate sensing across hundreds of hectares.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridge vibration sensors with 10-year battery life.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance via vibration and temperature sensors on motors.
- **Disaster Management:** Forest fire detection, flood early-warning systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman, 2002)

This is the **single most important derivation** in WSN theory and is a guaranteed KTU long-answer question (14 marks).

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

We model the energy dissipated by the radio of a sensor node to transmit or receive a $k$-bit packet over a distance $d$.

**Assumptions:**

1. The radio dissipates $E_{elec}$ (Joules/bit) to run the **transmitter or receiver circuitry**.
2. The transmit amplifier dissipates $\varepsilon_{amp}$ (Joules/bit/m² or m⁴) to achieve an acceptable **bit error rate (BER)**.
3. The channel model is symmetric — energy to send from A → B equals B → A for the same $k, d$.

### 3.1.2 Transmit Energy Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TX}(k, d) &= E_{TX\text{-}elec}(k) + E_{TX\text{-}amp}(k, d) \\
&= E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp}(k, d)
\end{aligned}
$$

The amplifier energy depends on the **path-loss exponent $n$**:

$$
E_{amp}(k, d) = \begin{cases} \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}, & d < d_{crossover} \quad \text{(free space)} \\ \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}, & d \geq d_{crossover} \quad \text{(multipath)} \end{cases}
$$

Therefore, the unified transmit equation is:

$$
\boxed{E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Receive Energy Derivation

The receiver only has to run the radio electronics — **no amplifier energy is needed** (it is amplifying an incoming signal, not generating one):

$$
\boxed{E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k}
$$

### 3.1.4 Crossover Distance Derivation

The crossover distance is found by equating the two amplifier models:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} &= \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} \\
\varepsilon_{fs} &= \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{2} \\
d^{2} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}} \\
d_{crossover} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For $d < d_{crossover}$, $d^2$ (free-space) model applies. For $d \geq d_{crossover}$, $d^4$ (multipath) model applies. This is why direct long-range transmission is so energy-expensive — quadrupling the distance **16×s** the energy.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Transmit $k = 4000$ bits over $d = 90$ m. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴.

**Step 1 — Check crossover:**

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

Since $d = 90$ m $> 87.7$ m, we use the **multipath model**.

**Step 2 — Compute $E_{TX}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TX} &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4} \\
&= (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(90)^{4} \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + (5.2 \times 10^{-15})(6.561 \times 10^{7}) \\
&= 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 3.41 \times 10^{-7} \\
&\approx 2.003 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_{RX}$:**

$$
E_{RX} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
$$

**Observation:** For long distances, transmit energy is dominated by the amplifier term; for short distances, $E_{elec}$ dominates.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Multi-Hop Distance (Why "More Hops = Less Energy")

For a linear chain of $h$ equal hops covering total distance $D$, the per-hop distance is $d = D / h$. Total transmit energy becomes:

$$
E_{total} = h \cdot \left[ E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{h}\right)^{n} \right]
$$

Minimising with respect to $h$ (differentiate, set to zero):

$$
\dfrac{dE_{total}}{dh} = E_{elec} \cdot k - \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{h}\right)^{n} \cdot (n - 1) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{h}\right) = 0
$$

Solving for the **optimum number of hops $h^{*}$** (using $n = 2$ free-space):

$$
\boxed{h^{*} = \dfrac{D}{d_{crossover}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{mp}}{\varepsilon_{fs}}}}
$$

This proves analytically that there exists an **optimal hop distance**, validating the multi-hop design philosophy of WSNs.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network Lifetime in LEACH (Clustered Architecture)

In **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)**, each node becomes a Cluster Head (CH) with probability $p$. In steady state, the number of clusters per round is:

$$
E[CH] = N \cdot p
$$

Energy per cluster head per round (intra-cluster aggregation + transmission to BS):

$$
E_{CH} = \dfrac{N}{N p} \left( E_{TX}(k, d_{toBS}) \right) = \dfrac{1}{p} E_{TX}(k, d_{toBS})
$$

The **first node death (FND)** round $r_{FND}$ defines the network lifetime:

$$
\boxed{r_{FND} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{CH}} = \dfrac{E_{initial} \cdot p}{E_{TX}(k, d_{toBS})}}
$$

This equation appears verbatim in KTU 14-mark problems on cluster-based WSNs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Node Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSE[Sensing Subsystem\nSensors and ADC]
    PROC[Processing Subsystem\nMicrocontroller and Memory]
    COMM[Communication Subsystem\nRadio Transceiver]
    POWER[Power Subsystem\nBattery and Harvester]
    APP[Application Layer\nUser Software]

    SENSE --> PROC
    PROC --> COMM
    POWER --> SENSE
    POWER --> PROC
    POWER --> COMM
    APP --> PROC
    COMM -->|Wireless Link| NET((WSN))
```

## 4.2 Typical Multi-Hop WSN Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD[Monitored Region]
        N1((Node 1))
        N2((Node 2))
        N3((Node 3))
        N4((Node 4))
        N5((Node 5))
        N6((Node 6))
        N7((Node 7))
        N8((Node 8))
    end

    BS[Base Station / Sink Gateway]

    N1 --> N3
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> N6
    N4 --> N6
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N8
    N7 --> N8
    N8 -->|Long Range| BS

    BS --> CLOUD[(Cloud / IoT Server)]
```

## 4.3 Clustering Architecture (LEACH-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CL1[Cluster 1]
        A1((Sensor A))
        A2((Sensor B))
        A3((Sensor C))
        CH1[Cluster Head 1]
    end

    subgraph CL2[Cluster 2]
        B1((Sensor D))
        B2((Sensor E))
        B3((Sensor F))
        CH2[Cluster Head 2]
    end

    subgraph CL3[Cluster 3]
        C1((Sensor G))
        C2((Sensor H))
        C3((Sensor I))
        CH3[Cluster Head 3]
    end

    A1 --> CH1
    A2 --> CH1
    A3 --> CH1
    B1 --> CH2
    B2 --> CH2
    B3 --> CH2
    C1 --> CH3
    C2 --> CH3
    C3 --> CH3

    CH1 --> BS((Base Station))
    CH2 --> BS
    CH3 --> BS

    BS --> CLOUD2[(Internet / Cloud)]
```

## 4.4 Energy Consumption Breakdown (Pie-Style Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOTAL[Total Node Energy Budget 100 percent] --> RADIO[Radio Communication 70 to 80 percent]
    TOTAL --> PROC2[Processing and MCU 15 to 20 percent]
    TOTAL --> SENS[Sensing and ADC 5 to 10 percent]
    TOTAL --> IDLE[Idle and Sleep Leakage 2 to 5 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram reading key:** The 70–80% radio share is the *single most important reason* why every WSN research paper (LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED, SEP) targets communication energy reduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Wireless Sensor Network. List any four applications of WSN in the biomedical field.**

**Model Answer:**

A Wireless Sensor Network is a collection of spatially distributed, autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, process, and communicate environmental or physiological data over wireless links to a central sink.

**Four biomedical applications:**

1. **Wearable ECG / heart-rate monitoring** patches.
2. **Continuous glucose monitoring** for diabetic patients.
3. **Wireless Capsule Endoscopy** for GI tract imaging.
4. **Remote patient monitoring** for elderly / post-operative care.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for any four applications)*

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between single-hop and multi-hop communication in WSNs with respect to energy efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Hop | Multi-Hop |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission distance | Long (direct to BS) | Short (per hop) |
| Transmit energy | High (d⁴ for d > d_crossover) | Low per hop |
| Receive energy | Zero per intermediate node | Accumulates at each hop |
| Optimality | Sub-optimal for sparse BS | Optimal when hop distance is tuned |
| Delay | Low | Higher (store-and-forward) |

For $d > d_{crossover}$, the $d^4$ model makes single-hop communication **quadratically more expensive**, so multi-hop is preferred. **This is the cornerstone of all WSN routing protocols.**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part B question carries an **internal choice** between two full-question alternatives. Marks are split 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a wireless sensor node. Explain the role of each subsystem. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the first-order radio energy model used in WSNs. Compute the energy to transmit 2000 bits over 100 m, given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram:**

```
+--------------------+
|  Power Subsystem   |
|  (Battery/Harvester)|
+--------------------+
          |
          v
+------------------+      +-------------------+
| Sensing          | <--> | Processing       |
| (Sensors + ADC)  |      | (MCU + Memory)   |
+------------------+      +-------------------+
                                   |
                                   v
                        +-------------------+
                        |  Communication    |
                        |  (Radio Tx/Rx)    |
                        +-------------------+
```

**Roles:**

- **Sensing:** Captures physical parameters (temperature, SpO₂, motion) using transducers; converts analog to digital via ADC. (1.5 marks)
- **Processing:** Runs the embedded firmware, MAC protocol, routing, data aggregation, and duty-cycle management. (1.5 marks)
- **Communication:** Handles wireless transmission and reception; consumes the most energy. (2 marks)
- **Power:** Provides regulated supply; may include energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric, RF). (2 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The first-order radio model expresses transmit and receive energy as:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

$$
E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Step 1 — Crossover distance:**

$$
d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0013 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Since 100 m > 87.7 m, use multipath model ($n = 4$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{TX} &= (50 \times 10^{-9})(2000) + (0.0013 \times 10^{-12})(2000)(100)^{4} \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + (2.6 \times 10^{-15})(1.0 \times 10^{8}) \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.6 \times 10^{-7} \\
&\approx 1.0026 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $E_{TX} \approx 1.0026 \times 10^{-4}$ J = **100.26 μJ** (3 marks for derivation + 2 marks for computation + 2 marks for units and final answer)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare star, mesh, and cluster topologies of WSN with respect to energy efficiency, reliability, and scalability. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the LEACH protocol. Derive the expected number of cluster heads per round and the first-node-death (FND) network lifetime. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Star | Mesh | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy efficiency | Poor (far nodes die first) | Balanced | Best (CHs aggregate) |
| Reliability | Low (BS SPOF) | Very high | High |
| Scalability | Limited | Good | Excellent |
| Latency | Lowest | High | Moderate |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex | Medium |

**Discussion:** In a star topology, the node farthest from the BS exhausts its battery first (the **energy hole problem**). Mesh resolves this via multi-hop. Cluster is the most energy-efficient because it adds **data aggregation** at CHs, reducing traffic to the BS by a factor equal to the cluster size. (3 marks for table + 4 marks for explanation)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** is a **clustering + rotation** protocol.

**Step 1 — Cluster Head Selection (Probabilistic):**

Each node generates a random number in $[0, 1]$ and becomes a CH if it is below a threshold $T(n)$:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{p} \right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $p$ = desired CH fraction, $r$ = current round, $G$ = set of nodes not yet CH in this epoch. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Expected Number of CHs:**

$$
E[CH] = N \cdot p
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — FND Network Lifetime:**

Energy consumed by a CH per round (assuming CH aggregates and transmits to BS):

$$
E_{CH} = \dfrac{N}{N p} \left[ E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_{toBS}^{4} \right]
$$

Network lifetime in rounds until the first node dies:

$$
\boxed{r_{FND} = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{CH}}}
$$

(3 marks for derivation + 1 mark for final equation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to switch from $d^2$ to $d^4$ model** when $d > d_{crossover}$ — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $E_{elec}$ with $\varepsilon_{amp}$** — they have different units (nJ/bit vs pJ/bit/m²).
> 3. **Missing the "data aggregation" benefit of LEACH** — write it explicitly; it is worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the threshold $T(n)$** in LEACH — students often skip it and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up FND (First Node Death) with HND (Half Nodes Dead)** — KTU prefers FND as the lifetime metric.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- WSN is the **perception layer of IoT** — it produces the data the rest of the stack consumes.
- A sensor node = **Sensing + Processing + Communication + Power** subsystems.
- **Communication (radio) consumes 70–80% of node energy** — design implication: minimize radio-on time and transmission distance.
- **First-order radio model:**

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}, \quad E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

- **Crossover distance:** $d_{crossover} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m (with standard values).
- For $d < d_{crossover}$: $n = 2$ (free space); for $d \geq d_{crossover}$: $n = 4$ (multipath).
- **Network lifetime** is most commonly defined as the **round of first node death (FND)**: $r_{FND} = E_{initial} / E_{round}$.
- **Star** topology is simplest but has the **energy hole** problem; **Mesh** is most reliable; **Cluster (LEACH)** is most energy-efficient.
- **LEACH** uses **probabilistic CH rotation** with threshold $T(n) = p / (1 - p \cdot (r \bmod 1/p))$.
- **Biomedical WSNs** typically use **IEEE 802.15.6 (WBAN)** standard, with **IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee** as a common general-purpose alternative.
- **Data aggregation** at cluster heads is what makes clustering protocols (LEACH, PEGASIS, HEED, SEP) outperform flat routing.
- **Duty cycling** ($\tau = T_{sleep} / (T_{active} + T_{sleep})$) is the simplest, most effective energy-saving knob.
- The **probabilistic coverage model** is $P_{cov} = 1 - (1 - \pi r^2 / A)^N$ — assume independence of node failures.
- **Key insight to write in every WSN answer:** *"In a WSN, the network is the computer, and energy is its clock."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Computing** is an on-demand, network-based delivery model of computing resources — including servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics, and intelligence — over the Internet (the "cloud") with pay-per-use economics. It enables ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (NIST SP 800-145 aligned):**
> Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model**
> Think of Cloud Computing exactly like the **electricity supply in your home**. You do not build your own power plant; you simply plug into the grid and pay only for the units (kWh) you consume. Similarly, in cloud computing, you do not purchase, install, and maintain physical servers; you "plug into" a provider like AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud and pay only for the compute, storage, and bandwidth you actually use. The grid scales up during summer (peak load) and scales down in winter — just as the cloud auto-scales your resources based on traffic.

### 1.3 Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing (The 5 Pillars)

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** defines **five essential characteristics** that any true cloud service must exhibit:

| # | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **On-Demand Self-Service** | Provision resources automatically without human interaction with the provider |
| 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Access via standard networks using thin/thick clients (mobile, laptop, IoT) |
| 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Multi-tenant model — physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned per demand |
| 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities can be scaled out/in quickly, appearing as virtually unlimited |
| 5 | **Measured Service** | Metering of storage, processing, bandwidth — transparent to both provider and consumer |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic — "O-B-R-R-M"** → **O**n-demand, **B**road access, **R**esource pooling, **R**apid elasticity, **M**easured service.

### 1.4 Cloud Computing in the IoT Context

In **IoT architectures**, the cloud acts as the central data ingestion, processing, analytics, and storage layer. Billions of sensor-generated data points are transmitted via gateway networks to the cloud for:
- **Big-data analytics**
- **Machine learning / AI inference**
- **Long-term archival storage**
- **Cross-device orchestration and dashboards**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants):**
> - Typical cloud data center **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)**: **1.1 – 1.5**
> - Standard SLA uptime: **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.999% ("five nines")**
> - Latency from IoT device → Cloud → Response: typically **50 – 200 ms** (regional)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Service Models — The Cloud Stack (Layered Architecture)

Cloud services are delivered in **three primary layers**, often visualized as a "stack":

### (A) Infrastructure as a Service — **IaaS**
- Provides raw compute, storage, and networking primitives.
- The **user manages** the OS, middleware, runtime, data, and applications.
- **Analogy**: Renting an empty plot of land and building your own house.
- Examples: **Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine, Microsoft Azure VMs**.

### (B) Platform as a Service — **PaaS**
- Provides a managed runtime + OS + middleware; the user only deploys code/data.
- **Analogy**: Renting a fully furnished apartment — you only bring your belongings.
- Examples: **Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku**.

### (C) Software as a Service — **SaaS**
- Delivers complete, ready-to-use applications over the web.
- **Analogy**: Staying in a hotel — every service is pre-packaged.
- Examples: **Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Emerging Variants (Frequently Asked in KTU 2024):**
> - **FaaS (Function as a Service) / Serverless**: e.g., AWS Lambda — executes code in response to events without managing servers.
> - **MBaaS / IoT Cloud**: e.g., AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, Azure IoT Hub — specialized for IoT telemetry.

### 2.2 Deployment Models

| Model | Ownership & Access | Typical Use Case |
|-------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Owned by 3rd-party provider; open to public | Startups, web hosting |
| **Private Cloud** | Dedicated to a single organization | Hospitals, defense, banks |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of public + private, with orchestration | Burst-to-cloud workloads |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., govt. agencies) | Smart-city deployments |
| **Multi-Cloud** | Using multiple public providers simultaneously | Avoiding vendor lock-in |

### 2.3 Cloud Architecture for IoT — Layered View

A typical **cloud-side IoT architecture** has four layers:

1. **Ingestion Layer** — Message brokers (MQTT, Kafka, AMQP)
2. **Processing Layer** — Stream processors (Apache Spark, Flink)
3. **Storage Layer** — Data lakes (S3, HDFS), time-series DBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
4. **Application / Analytics Layer** — Dashboards, ML models, alerting engines

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for Numerical / Analytical Questions**

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units |
|---------|------------------------|-------|
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{capex} + C_{opex} + C_{maint} + C_{downtime}$ | Currency |
| **Pay-per-Use Billing** | $Cost = (CPU\_hours \times P_{cpu}) + (Storage\_GB \times P_{st}) + (Egress\_GB \times P_{bw})$ | Currency / month |
| **Availability (from SLA)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| **Downtime per Year** | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes/year |
| **Elasticity Efficiency** | $\eta_{elas} = \dfrac{U_{peak} - U_{idle}}{U_{peak}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| **Storage Scalability** | $S_{req} = N_{devices} \times R_{sample} \times T_{retention} \times C_{compression}$ | GB / TB |
| **Bandwidth Requirement** | $BW = N_{devices} \times \dfrac{S_{payload}}{T_{interval}} \times 8$ | bits/sec |
| **Latency Budget (IoT)** | $L_{total} = L_{net} + L_{queue} + L_{process} + L_{return}$ | ms |
| **PUE (Data Center)** | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | ≥ 1.0 |

> **Mnemonic for Latency Equation:** "**N**et + **Q**ueue + **P**rocess + **R**eturn = **N-Q-P-R** (think 'NQPR — Never Quit, Push Results')".

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare (Biomedical IoT)**: ECG/EHR data streamed to HIPAA-compliant cloud for ML-based cardiac anomaly detection.
- **Smart Manufacturing**: Predictive maintenance using cloud-streamed vibration/temperature data.
- **Smart Agriculture**: Satellite + sensor telemetry aggregated in cloud for crop-yield prediction.
- **Disaster Response**: Cloud-based coordination platforms receiving data from distributed IoT sensor networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Downtime from SLA Availability

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 3-Mark Analytic):** A cloud provider advertises an SLA of **99.95%** monthly availability. Compute the **maximum allowable annual downtime in minutes**.

**Given:**
- Monthly SLA availability: $A_{month} = 99.95\% = 0.9995$
- Minutes in a year: $365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Begin by computing the monthly downtime fraction.

$$\text{Allowed Down Fraction} = 1 - A_{month}$$

Substitute the value.

$$\text{Allowed Down Fraction} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

Compute the minutes in a month.

$$\text{Minutes per Month} = 30.44 \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}833.6 \text{ min}$$

Compute the monthly downtime.

$$D_{month} = 0.0005 \times 43{,}833.6 = 21.92 \text{ min/month}$$

Compute the annual downtime.

$$D_{year} = 21.92 \times 12 = 263.04 \text{ min/year}$$

**Equivalent in hours:**

$$D_{year}^{hr} = \dfrac{263.04}{60} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference Table — SLA → Annual Downtime**

| SLA Tier | "Nines" | Annual Downtime |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| 99.0% | Two nines | 3 days 15 h 36 min |
| 99.9% | Three nines | 8 h 45 min 36 s |
| 99.95% | Three-and-half nines | 4 h 22 min 48 s |
| 99.99% | Four nines | 52 min 33 s |
| 99.999% | Five nines | 5 min 15 s |

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cloud Storage Requirement for an IoT Fleet

**Problem Statement:** A hospital deploys **5,000 wearable ECG sensors**. Each sensor transmits a **2 KB** payload every **10 seconds**, running **24/7**. Data must be retained for **5 years** with a compression ratio of **0.4** (i.e., compressed size is 40% of original). Compute the **total cloud storage** required.

**Step 1 — Compute raw data generated per sensor per day.**

$$D_{sensor/day} = \dfrac{86{,}400 \text{ s}}{10 \text{ s}} \times 2 \text{ KB} = 8{,}640 \times 2 = 17{,}280 \text{ KB/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MB per sensor per day.**

$$D_{sensor/day}^{MB} = \dfrac{17{,}280}{1024} \approx 16.875 \text{ MB/day}$$

**Step 3 — Scale to 5,000 sensors.**

$$D_{fleet/day} = 16.875 \times 5{,}000 = 84{,}375 \text{ MB/day} \approx 82.4 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Step 4 — Scale to 5 years (1,826 days).**

$$D_{5yr}^{raw} = 82.4 \times 1{,}826 \approx 150{,}442 \text{ GB} \approx 146.9 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 5 — Apply compression factor (0.4).**

$$D_{5yr}^{compressed} = 146.9 \times 0.4 \approx 58.76 \text{ TB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Storage ≈ 60 TB.** This justifies why IoT pipelines always use **columnar compression (Parquet/ORC)** and **tiered storage** (hot/warm/cold).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth Sizing for IoT Cloud Uplink

Using the formula $BW = N_{devices} \times \dfrac{S_{payload}}{T_{interval}} \times 8$:

- $N = 5{,}000$ devices
- $S = 2 \text{ KB} = 2{,}048 \text{ bytes}$
- $T_{interval} = 10 \text{ s}$

Compute the per-device rate.

$$R_{device} = \dfrac{2{,}048 \times 8}{10} = 1{,}638.4 \text{ bps} \approx 1.64 \text{ kbps}$$

Compute the aggregate fleet rate.

$$BW_{total} = 5{,}000 \times 1{,}638.4 = 8{,}192{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 8.19 \text{ Mbps}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Even with 5,000 sensors, the bandwidth is only ~8 Mbps — easily handled by a single VPN tunnel. **Storage, not bandwidth, is the dominant cost** in cloud-IoT design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service vs Deployment Model Mapping

| Deployment \ Service | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | FaaS |
|---------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Public Cloud | AWS EC2, GCE | App Engine | Gmail, M365 | Lambda |
| Private Cloud | OpenStack | Cloud Foundry | Nextcloud | Knative |
| Hybrid Cloud | Azure Stack | Anthos | O365 Hybrid | Lambda@Edge |
| Community Cloud | Eucalyptus | GovPaas | G-Suite Gov | LocalStack |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
> **Define Cloud Computing. List any THREE essential characteristics as per NIST.**

**Model Answer:**

Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction (NIST definition).

**Three Essential Characteristics (out of 5):**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources are provisioned automatically without human intervention.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Capabilities are available over the network through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, IoT).
3. **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (e.g., storage, processing, bandwidth).

*(Any 3 of: On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service — full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **What user manages** | OS, middleware, runtime, apps, data | Apps and data only | Nothing (just usage) |
| **What provider manages** | Hardware, network, virtualization, storage | OS, middleware, runtime, hardware | Entire stack |
| **Analogy** | Empty plot of land | Furnished apartment | Hotel room |
| **Example** | Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Microsoft 365 |
| **Typical User** | System admins, DevOps | Application developers | End users |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **NIST reference model of Cloud Computing** with a neat diagram. List and briefly describe the **five essential characteristics, three service models, and four deployment models**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A wearable ECG monitoring system is deployed in a smart hospital. The cloud platform receives telemetry from **1,000 wearable ECG sensors**, each producing a **4 KB payload every 5 seconds**, operating **24/7**. Compute the **aggregate bandwidth** required for cloud uplink and the **total raw storage** generated per year. Assume 365 operating days and a compression ratio of **0.35**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model** comprises three sub-models:

1. **Cloud Computing Definition (5 Essential Characteristics)**
   - On-Demand Self-Service
   - Broad Network Access
   - Resource Pooling (multi-tenant)
   - Rapid Elasticity
   - Measured Service

2. **Service Models (3)**
   - **IaaS** — raw infrastructure (compute, storage, network). User controls OS up to applications. *Example: AWS EC2.*
   - **PaaS** — managed platform with runtime, OS, middleware. User deploys apps/data. *Example: Google App Engine.*
   - **SaaS** — fully managed application. *Example: Gmail, Microsoft 365.*

3. **Deployment Models (4)**
   - **Public Cloud** — open to general public; owned by provider (AWS, Azure).
   - **Private Cloud** — exclusive to one organization; on-premise or hosted.
   - **Hybrid Cloud** — composition of two or more clouds (public+private) bound by standard tech.
   - **Community Cloud** — shared by organizations with common mission/security needs.

*(Refer to the diagrams in Section 4.1 for visualization — 1 mark reserved for diagram.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Per-sensor data rate.**

$$R_{sensor} = \dfrac{4 \text{ KB}}{5 \text{ s}} = \dfrac{4 \times 8 \times 1024 \text{ bits}}{5} = 6{,}553.6 \text{ bps} \approx 6.55 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate fleet bandwidth.**

$$BW = 1{,}000 \times 6{,}553.6 = 6{,}553{,}600 \text{ bps} \approx 6.55 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Per-sensor data per day.**

$$D_{sensor/day} = \dfrac{86{,}400}{5} \times 4 \text{ KB} = 17{,}280 \times 4 = 69{,}120 \text{ KB/day} = 67.5 \text{ MB/day}$$

**Step 4 — Fleet data per day.**

$$D_{fleet/day} = 67.5 \times 1{,}000 = 67{,}500 \text{ MB/day} = 65.92 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Step 5 — Annual raw storage.**

$$D_{year}^{raw} = 65.92 \times 365 \approx 24{,}060 \text{ GB} \approx 23.5 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 6 — Apply compression (0.35).**

$$D_{year}^{compressed} = 23.5 \times 0.35 \approx 8.23 \text{ TB}$$

> **Final Answers:** $BW \approx 6.55 \text{ Mbps}$, $Storage_{raw} \approx 23.5 \text{ TB/year}$, $Storage_{compressed} \approx 8.23 \text{ TB/year}$.

---

> **Q3(B). [Alternative Choice – 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **role of Cloud Computing in IoT-based biomedical applications**. Provide a detailed **layered architecture** showing the flow of data from a wearable biosensor to the cloud and finally to the clinician's dashboard. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A remote patient-monitoring cloud service guarantees an **SLA of 99.9%**. Compute the **maximum allowable annual downtime** in minutes, hours, and days. If a maintenance upgrade takes **3 hours** in a quarter, verify whether the provider violates the SLA. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Role of Cloud in Biomedical IoT (4 marks):**
1. **Centralized Data Aggregation** — Cloud acts as the unified sink for high-velocity biosensor streams (ECG, SpO2, glucose).
2. **Scalable Storage** — Handles petabyte-scale longitudinal patient records (EHR/EMR).
3. **Advanced Analytics** — Hosts ML/DL models for arrhythmia detection, sepsis prediction, and personalized treatment.
4. **Interoperability & Accessibility** — RESTful APIs and HL7/FHIR standards enable seamless clinician access from any device.
5. **Disaster Recovery & Compliance** — Built-in redundancy meets HIPAA/GDPR data-protection mandates.

**Layered Architecture (3 marks):**

1. **Perception Layer** — Wearable sensors (ECG patch, smart band, glucose monitor).
2. **Network/Gateway Layer** — Bluetooth → WiFi/5G → MQTT broker.
3. **Cloud Ingestion Layer** — Secure message broker (HiveMQ, EMQ X).
4. **Processing Layer** — Apache Spark / Flink for real-time stream analytics.
5. **Storage Layer** — Time-series DB (InfluxDB) + Data Lake (S3).
6. **Application Layer** — ML inference, alerting, clinician dashboards.
7. **Presentation Layer** — Web/mobile UI for doctors, patients, and caregivers.

*(Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.2 — 1 mark reserved for diagram.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Annual downtime fraction.**

$$\text{Down Fraction} = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001$$

**Step 2 — Total minutes in a year.**

$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum allowable annual downtime.**

$$D_{max} = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6 \text{ min/year}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours and days.**

$$D_{max}^{hr} = \dfrac{525.6}{60} = 8.76 \text{ hours/year}$$

$$D_{max}^{days} = \dfrac{8.76}{24} \approx 0.365 \text{ days/year}$$

**Step 5 — Quarterly SLA budget.**

$$D_{quarter}^{budget} = \dfrac{8.76}{4} = 2.19 \text{ hours/quarter}$$

**Step 6 — Compliance check.**

Maintenance window: $3 \text{ hours/quarter}$ > Budget: $2.19 \text{ hours/quarter}$.

Since $3 > 2.19$, the provider **VIOLATES** the SLA in that quarter, and by extension, likely the annual SLA as well.

> **Final Verdict:** **SLA is violated.** The provider must compensate the customer per the SLA credit terms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "IaaS" and "PaaS"** in the responsibility chart — students often swap OS and middleware rows, losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** When converting KB ↔ MB ↔ GB in storage problems, always use $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB}$ (binary) **OR** $1000$ (decimal/SI). The KTU convention for cloud storage billing is **decimal (1 MB = 1000 KB)**, but for raw RAM calculations, **binary** is used. State your assumption clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting the "×8" for bits conversion** in bandwidth problems — a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **SLA Verification** must explicitly compare maintenance time with the *quarterly* budget, not annual. Many students divide by 12 months incorrectly.
> 5. **Do not write "Cloud = Internet"** in definitional questions. The Internet is the transport; the cloud is the *abstraction of services over that transport*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cloud Computing**

- ✅ **NIST 5 Characteristics:** On-Demand, Broad Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service (Mnemonic: **O-B-R-R-M**).
- ✅ **3 Service Models:** IaaS (user manages OS↑), PaaS (user manages app+data), SaaS (user just consumes).
- ✅ **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community (+ Multi-Cloud).
- ✅ **FaaS/Serverless** is the 4th service model — runs code in response to events (e.g., AWS Lambda).
- ✅ **Cloud-IoT Architecture** has at least 4 layers: Ingestion → Processing → Storage → Application.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **99.9% SLA = 8.76 hours/year** of allowable downtime (memorize this number).
- ✅ **PUE** of a green data center: **~1.1**; legacy: **2.0+**.
- ✅ **Storage dominates cost** in IoT-Cloud pipelines — always apply compression and tiered storage.
- ✅ **Bandwidth = (Devices × Payload × 8) / Interval** — the 8 is for bits conversion.
- ✅ **Fog/Edge computing** is the *complement* of cloud computing — used when **latency < 10 ms** is required (e.g., ICU alarms, robotic surgery).
- ✅ **HIPAA (US) / DISHA (India)** compliance is mandatory for biomedical data in the cloud.
- ✅ **Serverless ≠ No servers** — servers exist; the user just doesn't manage them.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the de-facto IoT-cloud protocol (port 1883 plaintext, 8883 TLS).
- ✅ **Hybrid Cloud** is the most common enterprise deployment — it balances cost (public) with security (private).

> **End of Module 1 — Cloud Computing Notes** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the context of the **PCBRT402 – IoT & Biomedical Applications** course (2024 Scheme), a **Communication Protocol** in IoT is formally defined as a standardized set of rules, message formats, timing conventions, and physical/electrical specifications that govern the exchange of data between two or more heterogeneous devices, sensors, actuators, edge gateways, and cloud servers across a constrained network infrastructure.

Communication protocols in IoT are typically organized as a **multi-layered protocol stack** analogous to the OSI/TCP-IP model, but heavily optimized for the unique constraints of IoT — namely **low power, low bandwidth, lossy networks (LLNs), heterogeneous devices, intermittent connectivity, and massive scalability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master the classification of IoT communication protocols into **Infrastructure Protocols (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT)** and **Service/Application Protocols (CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, HTTP, DDS, XMPP, WebSockets)**. Biomedical applications (Module 1 extension) demand special attention to **BLE, IEEE 11073, and Continua Health Alliance frameworks**.

---

## 1.2 Why IoT Communication Protocols Differ from Traditional Networking

| Dimension | Traditional Internet (TCP/IP) | IoT Communication |
|---|---|---|
| **Device Power** | Mains-powered, abundant energy | Battery-operated, **µW–mW budget** |
| **Packet Size** | 1500 bytes (Ethernet MTU) | **≤ 127 bytes (IEEE 802.15.4)** |
| **Network Density** | Hundreds of devices | **Thousands/millions per sq. km** |
| **Loss Tolerance** | Reliable, retransmit-heavy | Lossy, application-tolerant |
| **Addressing** | IPv4/IPv6 everywhere | Often **6LoWPAN compressed IPv6** |
| **Data Model** | Human-oriented (HTML/JSON large) | **CBOR, SenML** compact binary |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Multilingual Hospital**
>
> Imagine a **large multi-specialty hospital** where doctors, nurses, pharmacists, lab technicians, and administrators must exchange critical information every second.
>
> - **Wi-Fi (TCP/IP)** is like the **hospital's main PA system** — loud, fast, ideal for transferring big files like MRI images.
> - **BLE** is the **bedside intercom** between a nurse's wristband and the IV pump — short range, ultra-low power, near-instant.
> - **ZigBee** is the **nurse-call mesh network** — every room passes a message to the next, no single point of failure.
> - **LoRa / NB-IoT** is the **ambulance-to-hospital telemetry** — kilometers away, tiny data packets (heart rate, GPS), but reaches the ER reliably.
> - **MQTT** is the **centralized nurses' station dashboard** — every monitor "publishes" vitals to a single "broker" that all doctors "subscribe" to.
>
> Each "language" is a **protocol**, chosen because the message type, distance, urgency, and device battery all demand a different compromise.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory constants for IoT Protocol numerics:**
>
> - Speed of light, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
> - Boltzmann constant, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - Thermal noise floor, $N_0 = -174$ **dBm/Hz**
> - Standard ISM bands: **2.4 GHz** (worldwide), **868 MHz** (Europe), **915 MHz** (North America), **433 MHz** (Asia/Industrial)
> - IEEE 802.15.4 O-QPSK chip rate: **2 Mchips/s** (2.4 GHz)
> - Typical Tx power for IoT nodes: **0 dBm to +20 dBm**

---

## 1.5 The KTU 2024 Classification Pyramid (Preview)

$$
\boxed{
\text{IoT Protocols} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Perception / Link Layer} & \rightarrow \text{Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT, RFID} \\[2pt]
\text{Network Layer} & \rightarrow \text{6LoWPAN, RPL, IPv6} \\[2pt]
\text{Transport Layer} & \rightarrow \text{UDP, CoAP, DTLS, TCP} \\[2pt]
\text{Service / Application Layer} & \rightarrow \text{MQTT, AMQP, HTTP, DDS, XMPP, WebSocket} \\[2pt]
\text{Bio-Medical Specific} & \rightarrow \text{IEEE 11073 (X73), BLE GATT, Continua H.810}
\end{cases}
}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer IoT Protocol Stack (KTU 2024 Standard)

### 2.1.1 Layer 1 — Perception / Physical / Link Layer Protocols

These define the **radio (or wired) electrical interface**, modulation, channel access, and bit-level framing. They answer: *"How is a bit actually pushed onto the medium?"*

#### **A. Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)**
- **Standard bands:** 2.4 GHz & 5 GHz (6 GHz for Wi-Fi 6E)
- **Max data rate:** 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6, 160 MHz, 1024-QAM)
- **Modulations:** DSSS → OFDM → OFDMA (Wi-Fi 6)
- **CSMA/CA** with DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)
- **Pros:** High throughput, ubiquitous IP, mature
- **Cons:** **Power-hungry (~1 W peak)** — not ideal for coin-cell sensors
- **Biomedical use:** Hospital APs, streaming high-res ultrasound/EEG over LAN

#### **B. Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.x / IEEE 802.15.1)**
- **Band:** 2.4 GHz ISM, **40 channels** (3 advertising + 37 data)
- **Modulation:** GFSK, 1 Msps (BLE 4) → 2 Msps (BLE 5)
- **Range:** ~50–200 m line-of-sight (Coded PHY extends to ~1 km)
- **Topology:** Star (piconet, 1 master + 7 active slaves logical, unlimited in adv)
- **Key feature:** **Connection intervals 7.5 ms – 4 s** → perfectly tunable duty cycle for wearables
- **Biomedical use:** Heart-rate straps, pulse oximeters, continuous glucose monitors (CGM), hearing aids (ASHA protocol)

#### **C. ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4 + ZigBee PRO / 3.0)**
- **PHY/MAC:** IEEE 802.15.4 (O-QPSK @ 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps)
- **Network layer:** ZigBee PRO supports **mesh (up to 65 530 nodes)**
- **Addressing:** 16-bit short or 64-bit IEEE EUI-64
- **Security:** AES-128-CCM\*
- **Pros:** Self-healing mesh, very low sleep current (~1 µA)
- **Cons:** Limited bandwidth, requires coordinator
- **Biomedical use:** Asset tracking, ward-level environmental sensing

#### **D. LoRa / LoRaWAN (Semtech + LoRa Alliance)**
- **PHY:** Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) — patented by Semtech
- **Bands:** 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia)
- **Bit rate:** 0.25 kbps – 50 kbps (adaptive based on SF and BW)
- **Range:** 2–15 km (rural), 1–5 km (urban)
- **LoRaWAN classes:**
  - **Class A** — uplink-only triggered, lowest power
  - **Class B** — adds scheduled downlink slots
  - **Class C** — always-on downlink (mains powered)
- **Biomedical use:** Remote patient monitoring (RPM), elderly fall detection, rural telehealth

#### **E. NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT) & LTE-M**
- **Standard:** 3GPP Release 13 (Cat-NB1) and Release 14 (Cat-NB2)
- **Bandwidth:** 180 kHz (NB-IoT), 1.4 MHz (LTE-M)
- **Deploy mode:** In-band / guard-band / standalone
- **Data rate:** ~26 kbps DL / ~62 kbps UL (NB-IoT)
- **Pros:** Licensed spectrum, deep indoor penetration (+20 dB vs LTE), battery life 10+ years
- **Cons:** Operator subscription, latency ~1.6–10 s
- **Biomedical use:** Implanted cardiac monitors, smart pill dispensers

---

### 2.1.2 Layer 2 — Network Layer (Adaptation Protocols)

#### **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, RFC 6282)**
- Compresses IPv6 headers (40 bytes) into tiny packets
- Fragmentation & reassembly for 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frames
- **Header compression (IPHC)** reduces to as low as **2 bytes**
- Crucial for bringing **full IP to 8/16-bit MCUs**

#### **RPL — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RFC 6550)**
- **Distance Vector** protocol optimized for LLNs
- Builds a **DODAG** (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)
- Uses **Objective Functions (OF0, MRHOF)** to pick parent based on ETX, latency, energy
- Stores / non-storing modes for constrained memory nodes

---

### 2.1.3 Layer 3 — Transport / Session

| Protocol | Transport | Header Size | Confirmable | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UDP** | Connectionless | 8 B | No | Real-time vitals stream |
| **TCP** | Connection-oriented | 20 B | Yes | Firmware update (large) |
| **DTLS** | UDP + encryption | 13 B+ | Yes (coaps) | Secure CoAP |
| **TLS** | TCP + encryption | 25 B+ | Yes (https) | Secure HTTP |

---

### 2.1.4 Layer 4 — Service / Application Protocols (The "Big Four")

#### **A. MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — OASIS Standard
- **Architecture:** Publish/Subscribe over **TCP (port 1883, 8883 TLS)**
- **Components:** **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX), **Publisher**, **Subscriber**
- **Topics:** Hierarchical, slash-separated (e.g., `hospital/ward3/bed12/hr`)
- **QOS Levels:**
  - **QoS 0** — At most once (fire & forget)
  - **QoS 1** — At least once (PUBACK handshake)
  - **QoS 2** — Exactly once (4-way handshake)
- **Packet overhead:** Just **2 bytes minimum** (vs HTTP's hundreds)
- **Keep-alive:** 60 s default, doubles as connection liveness
- **Last Will & Testament (LWT):** Notifies other clients on abnormal disconnect — **critical for medical alarm monitoring**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 must-know:** MQTT is the **de-facto standard for IoT telemedicine** because of its tiny footprint, pub/sub decoupling, and quality-of-service guarantees.

#### **B. CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) — RFC 7252**
- **Architecture:** Request/Response (REST-like), runs over **UDP (port 5683)**
- **Header:** 4 bytes compact binary
- **Methods:** GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
- **Confirmable (CON) / Non-Confirmable (NON) / Acknowledgement (ACK) / Reset (RST)**
- **Observability:** CoAP `Observe` option (RFC 7641) pushes sensor updates without polling
- **Security:** DTLS (CoAPs on 5684)
- **Use case:** Constrained devices that cannot afford TCP overhead

#### **C. AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) — ISO/IEC 19464**
- **Architecture:** Pub/Sub **plus** queuing, **broker-centric** with exchanges & queues
- **Use case:** Enterprise hospital integration (HL7 over AMQP), banking-grade reliability
- **Port:** 5672 (5671 TLS)

#### **D. HTTP / REST / WebSocket**
- **HTTP/HTTPS:** Familiar but heavy (kilobyte headers)
- **WebSocket:** Full-duplex over a single TCP connection — used for live telemetry dashboards
- **Status:** Still common in gateway-to-cloud edges, but rarely used device-to-device

---

### 2.1.5 Bio-Medical-Specific Protocols (High-Weightage for PCBRT402)

#### **IEEE 11073 (X73 / ISO 11073)**
The **gold standard for personal health device communication**. Stack:

$$
\text{IEEE 11073} = \begin{cases}
\text{11073-20601} & \text{Optimized Exchange Protocol (transport-independent)} \\
\text{11073-104xx} & \text{Device specializations (10404 pulse ox, 10407 BP, 10408 thermometer, 10415 weighing, 10417 glucose, 10441 ECG, 10471 activity)} \\
\text{11073-30200} & \text{Transport: Bluetooth / USB / ZigBee} \\
\end{cases}
$$

- **Domain Information Model (DIM)** — object-oriented (MDS, Metric, Numeric objects)
- **Medical Device Encoding Rules (MDER)** — binary, ultra-compact
- **Plug-and-Play** association model — crucial for consumer health

#### **Continua Health Alliance (H.810–H.880)**
- Certifies interoperability between personal health devices, gateways, and EHRs
- Combines **IEEE 11073 + BLE / USB / ZigBee transports**
- Adopted by **Apple Health, Samsung Health, Google Fit** for accessory data

#### **HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)**
- Modern REST/JSON successor to HL7 v2/v3
- **Resources** like `Patient`, `Observation`, `Device` exchanged over HTTPS
- The "**lingua franca**" of modern hospital → cloud

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every numerical problem in KTU 2024 ESE will reduce to one of these equations.**

### 2.2.1 Wireless Link Budget & Range Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} - L_{misc}$ | **Received power (Friis link budget)** | dBm |
| 2 | $L_{path} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**, d in km, f in MHz | dB |
| 3 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Friis in free space (linear form) | W |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of carrier | m |
| 5 | $SNR = P_r - N_0 - 10\log_{10}(BW) - N_F$ | Receiver SNR | dB |

### 2.2.2 Shannon Channel Capacity

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

where $S/N$ is the linear signal-to-noise ratio. In dB form:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}\right)
$$

### 2.2.3 LoRa Time-on-Air (ToA) and Bit-Rate

$$
R_b = SF \cdot \dfrac{4/CR}{\left(\frac{2^{SF}}{BW}\right)} \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

$$
ToA = T_{symbol} \cdot N_{symbols} = \left(\frac{2^{SF}}{BW}\right) \cdot \left(8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil\frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF-2)}\right\rceil \cdot (CR+4),\, 0\right)\right)
$$

Spreading Factor **SF ∈ {7,8,9,10,11,12}**, Bandwidth **BW ∈ {125, 250, 500 kHz}**, Coding Rate **CR ∈ {1,2,3,4}** (representing 4/5, 4/6, 4/7, 4/8).

### 2.2.4 Battery Life Estimation (Peukert's Law adapted)

$$
L_{years} = \frac{Q_{mAh} \cdot V_{nom}}{I_{sleep} \cdot V_{nom} \cdot 8760 + I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} \cdot N_{events}/hr \cdot 8760}
$$

(Approximate; full forms in textbooks.)

### 2.2.5 CoAP/MQTT Header Compactness Ratio

$$
\eta = \frac{H_{effective}}{H_{overhead}} \quad ; \quad \text{CoAP} \approx \frac{4}{N+4}, \quad \text{MQTT} \approx \frac{2}{2+N}
$$

For an N-byte payload, CoAP is more efficient for N < ~100 B; MQTT scales better with QoS.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Network Designers** use Wi-Fi 6E mesh + BLE gateways; this combination is the only one that simultaneously supports high-bandwidth imaging and ultra-low-power wearables.
- **Ambulance / RPM manufacturers** prefer **LoRa + MQTT** because the cellular fallback to NB-IoT is "always available" but LoRa costs zero per packet.
- **Implantable medical devices** (pacemakers, ICDs) demand **inductive BLE or Medical Implant Communication Service (MICS) band 402–405 MHz** for safety, security, and minimal SAR.
- **Pharma cold chain** uses **NB-IoT + MQTT QoS 1** so that a single lost temperature reading cannot falsely certify spoilage.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Note:** Numerical problems on this topic almost always fall into one of three categories. Below are fully worked-out, exam-ready derivations for each.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wireless Range using Friis Transmission Equation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A wearable ECG patch transmits at **+8 dBm** with a monopole antenna of gain **+2.1 dBi**. The receiver (smartphone) has gain **+0 dBi** and a minimum sensitivity of **−92 dBm**. Carrier frequency is **2.45 GHz** (BLE). Misc losses (body, fading margin) total **18 dB**. Calculate the **maximum line-of-sight range**.

### Step 1 — Identify unknowns
We need $d_{max}$ such that $P_r \geq P_{sensitivity}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Friis link budget
$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path}(d) - L_{misc}
$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the maximum allowable path loss
$$
L_{path,max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - P_{sensitivity} - L_{misc}
$$

Substitute values:
$$
L_{path,max} = (+8) + (+2.1) + (0) - (-92) - 18
$$

$$
L_{path,max} = 8 + 2.1 + 92 - 18 = 84.1 \text{ dB}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert the FSPL formula to solve for $d$
The free-space path loss formula (d in km, f in MHz) is:
$$
L_{path} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

Isolate $20\log_{10}(d)$:
$$
20\log_{10}(d) = L_{path,max} - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44
$$

### Step 5 — Plug in $f = 2450$ MHz
$$
20\log_{10}(2450) = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(2450) = 20 \cdot (3.3892) = 67.784
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 84.1 - 67.784 - 32.44 = -16.124
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = -0.8062
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for d
$$
d_{max} = 10^{-0.8062} = 0.1562 \text{ km} = 156.2 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 7 — Sanity check
A BLE 5 patch with +8 dBm and −92 dBm sensitivity should reach ~150 m in free space. **156 m is realistic.** Indoors, this drops to ~30 m (multipath + body absorption).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU 2024:** A common mistake is forgetting to add the 32.44 dB constant or to convert MHz. Always **double-check the units of $f$ and $d$** before substituting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shannon Capacity for a Biomedical Uplink

**Problem:** A wireless capsule endoscope (WCE) transmits at **−10 dBm** through the body to an external receiver with **−100 dBm** sensitivity over a **10 MHz** channel. The receiver has a noise figure of **6 dB**. Find the **maximum achievable data rate**.

### Step 1 — Received power
Given by problem: $P_r = -100$ dBm (sensitivity floor).

### Step 2 — Noise floor in dBm
$$
N = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz} + 10\log_{10}(BW) + N_F
$$

$$
10\log_{10}(10 \times 10^6) = 10 \cdot 7 = 70 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
N = -174 + 70 + 6 = -98 \text{ dBm}
$$

### Step 3 — Linear SNR
SNR in dB:
$$
SNR_{dB} = P_r - N = -100 - (-98) = -2 \text{ dB}
$$

Convert to linear:
$$
SNR = 10^{-2/10} = 0.6310
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Shannon's capacity formula
$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)
$$

$$
C = 10 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 0.6310)
$$

$$
C = 10^7 \cdot \log_2(1.6310)
$$

$$
\log_2(1.6310) = \frac{\ln(1.6310)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{0.4895}{0.6931} = 0.7063
$$

### Step 5 — Final value
$$
\boxed{C \approx 7.06 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation
A WCE imaging at 7 Mbps can stream 1280×720 @ 25 fps after modern intra-frame compression, fitting realistic capsule-endoscope video. NB-IoT cannot do this — hence the use of **MICS-band custom PHYs** for ingestibles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LoRaWAN Time-on-Air & Energy Budget

**Problem:** A rural patient-wearable uses **LoRa SF = 10, BW = 125 kHz, CR = 1 (4/5)**, payload **PL = 20 bytes**, header enabled (H = 0), CRC enabled. Find **(a)** bit rate, **(b)** time-on-air, **(c)** energy per packet at **+14 dBm Tx** with **110 mA TX current, 3.0 V supply**.

### Step 1 — Bit rate
$$
R_b = SF \cdot \frac{4/CR}{\left(\frac{2^{SF}}{BW}\right)}
$$

$$
\frac{2^{10}}{125{,}000} = \frac{1024}{125{,}000} = 0.008192 \text{ s}
$$

$$
R_b = 10 \cdot \frac{4/5}{0.008192} = 10 \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.008192} = 10 \cdot 97.656 = 976.56 \text{ bps}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of payload symbols
The LoRa packet has a **preamble (8 symbols) + header (if H=1) + payload**. The number of payload symbols:

$$
N_{payload} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8PL - 4SF + 28 + 16CRC - 20H}{4(SF-2)} \right\rceil \cdot CR, \, 0\right)
$$

With H=0, CRC=1 (so +16), PL=20, SF=10, CR=4:
$$
\text{Numerator} = 8(20) - 4(10) + 28 + 16 - 0 = 160 - 40 + 28 + 16 = 164
$$

$$
4(SF-2) = 4(8) = 32
$$

$$
\frac{164}{32} = 5.125 \rightarrow \lceil 5.125 \rceil = 6
$$

$$
6 \cdot CR = 6 \cdot 4 = 24
$$

$$
N_{payload} = 8 + \max(24, 0) = 32 \text{ symbols}
$$

### Step 3 — Preamble + sync overhead
Preamble = 8 symbols (4 LoRa preamble + 4 sync), plus 4.25 symbols for downchirps:
$$
N_{total} \approx 8 + 4.25 + 32 = 44.25 \text{ symbols}
$$

### Step 4 — Time on air
$$
T_{sym} = \frac{2^{SF}}{BW} = \frac{1024}{125{,}000} = 8.192 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{ToA = 44.25 \cdot 8.192 \text{ ms} \approx 362.5 \text{ ms}}
$$

### Step 5 — Energy per packet
$$
E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 3.0 \cdot 0.110 \cdot 0.3625
$$

$$
E = 0.1196 \text{ J} \approx 119.6 \text{ mJ}
$$

### Step 6 — Battery-life implication
A 2400 mAh @ 3.0 V Li-coin cell = 25 920 J.
$$
N_{packets} = \frac{25{,}920}{0.1196} \approx 2.17 \times 10^5 \text{ packets}
$$
At 1 packet per hour, that's **~24.7 years** theoretical (real life ~5–10 years after self-discharge, sleep, regulator losses).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Selection Heuristics (Exam Favorite)

| Constraint | Best Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Range > 5 km, few bytes/min | **LoRaWAN** | CSS modulation, +20 dB link budget |
| Power budget < 50 µA avg | **BLE** | Connection intervals, event-driven |
| Hospital LAN, video | **Wi-Fi 6** | OFDMA, multi-Gbps |
| Implant, body-internal | **MICS / BLE** | Sub-µW, body-safe SAR |
| Wall-penetrating rural | **NB-IoT** | Licensed, 164 dB MCL |
| Mesh, lighting/asset | **ZigBee / Thread** | Self-healing mesh |
| Cloud pub/sub telemetry | **MQTT** | Tiny pub/sub, QoS |
| Constrained device→device | **CoAP** | UDP-based REST |
| EHR-grade reliability | **AMQP / HL7 FHIR** | Queues, transactions |

```mermaid
    participant GW as LoRa Gateway
    participant NS as Network Server
    participant APP as Hospital Application

    GW->>NS: Forward packet
    NS->>APP: Decode + Decrypt
    Note over ED: Wait RX1 delay
    ED->>GW: Uplink Window 2 (RX2)
    GW->>NS: Forward
    NS->>APP: Decode
    NS-->>GW: Downlink (ACK) optional
    GW-->>ED: Downlink in RX1
    GW-->>ED: Downlink in RX2
    Note over ED: Sleep until next event
```
