## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP application layer protocols with respect to transport, architecture and header overhead.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) | UDP (lightweight, connectionless) |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe via central broker | Request/Response (REST-like) |
| **Header** | 2-byte fixed minimum + variable | 4-byte binary fixed |
| **QoS** | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | CON/NON + ACK/RST confirmable |
| **Security** | TLS (MQTTS) | DTLS (CoAPs) |
| **Best for** | Cloud pub/sub, reliable telemetry | Constrained device-to-device |
| **Multicast** | Not natively | Natively supports |

> **Key mark-split:** 1 mark for transport, 1 mark architecture, 1 mark for header/header-overhead.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the role of 6LoWPAN in IoT networks. Why is it necessary for IEEE 802.15.4 networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of 6LoWPAN:**
- Stands for **IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks**.
- It is an **adaptation layer** defined in **RFC 4944 / RFC 6282** that allows IPv6 packets to traverse IEEE 802.15.4 (and similar) link layers with frames as small as **127 bytes**.

**Why necessary:**
1. **Header Compression (IPHC):** IPv6's 40-byte header is compressed down to as little as **2 bytes** using context-based and stateless compression, leaving room for a meaningful payload.
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly:** IPv6 packets up to 1280 bytes are split into multiple 802.15.4 frames and reassembled at the destination.
3. **Mesh Addressing:** Defines **Mesh Header** for 802.15.4 sublayers to support multi-hop forwarding.
4. **Internet Integration:** Allows the tiniest sensor node to be a **first-class IPv6 citizen**, addressable from anywhere on the Internet — essential for cloud-based remote-patient monitoring.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 Part B carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

#### (a) Explain the IoT Communication Protocol Stack in detail with a neat block diagram. Compare Infrastructure Protocols (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa) on the basis of frequency band, data rate, range, and power consumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IoT Protocol Stack has five layers: Application, Service, Transport, Network, and Perception/Link. *(1 mark)*

| Protocol | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11ax)** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | up to 9.6 Gbps | 30–100 m | High (~1 W Tx) |
| **BLE 5.x** | 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps (PHY), 1.4 Mbps eff. | 50–200 m (Coded: 1 km) | Very low (~10 mA Tx) |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m (mesh extends) | Low (~30 mA Tx) |
| **LoRa (CSS)** | 868/915/433 MHz | 0.25–50 kbps | 2–15 km | Very low (~100 mA Tx, <1% duty) |

**Inference:** Wi-Fi suits high-bandwidth stationary devices, BLE suits personal wearables, ZigBee suits local mesh sensing, LoRa suits long-range low-rate telemetry. *(1 mark)*

#### (b) Describe MQTT protocol in detail. Explain its components, message formats, QoS levels, and a typical biomedical use case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Origin:** OASIS Standard, ISO/IEC 20922, originally by IBM (1999).
- **Architecture:** **Publish/Subscribe** with three actors — **Publisher, Broker, Subscriber**. The broker is the central coordinator; publishers and subscribers are decoupled. *(1 mark)*
- **Components:**
  - **Broker:** Mosquitto, EMQX, HiveMQ
  - **Client:** Sensor / mobile / service
  - **Topic:** Hierarchical string (e.g., `hospital/icu/bed5/hr`)
  - **Message:** Up to 256 MB payload
- **Message types (Control Packet types):** CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT, AUTH. *(1 mark)*
- **Fixed header:** 2 bytes minimum — Byte 1 = packet type + flags, Byte 2+ = remaining length (variable). *(1 mark)*
- **QoS Levels:** *(2 marks)*
  - **QoS 0** — At most once. PUBLISH only, no ACK. Lowest overhead.
  - **QoS 1** — At least once. PUBLISH → PUBACK. Tolerates duplicates.
  - **QoS 2** — Exactly once. 4-way handshake (PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP). Highest reliability.
- **Biomedical use case:** *(1 mark)*
  - A wearable ECG patch publishes `ward/3/bed/12/ecg` at QoS 1 every second.
  - A bedside monitor subscribes; a doctor tablet subscribes; an alarm engine subscribes with LWT.
  - If patch disconnects unexpectedly, the **Last Will & Testament (LWT)** "patient_offline" message triggers nurse-station alerts.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

#### (a) With a neat architecture, explain CoAP. Differentiate CoAP from HTTP. Discuss the Observe option. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CoAP Architecture:**
- **Constrained Application Protocol (RFC 7252)** — Request/Response model designed for constrained devices. *(1 mark)*
- Built on **UDP (port 5683)**, optionally **DTLS** for security (CoAPs port 5684).
- 4-byte binary header: Version (2), Type (2), Token Length (4), Code (8), Message ID (16). *(1 mark)*
- Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE (mirrors HTTP verbs).
- **Message types:** CON (Confirmable), NON (Non-confirmable), ACK, RST. *(1 mark)*

**CoAP vs HTTP (1 mark per row):**

| Feature | HTTP | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Header | ~200–800 B (text) | 4 B binary |
| Power | High | Very low |
| Push | Polling or WebSocket | Native Observe |
| Discovery | URI templates | CoRE Resource Directory |
| Caching | Standard | Built-in freshness model |

**Observe option (RFC 7641):** *(2 marks)*
- A client sends GET with `Observe: 0` to register interest in a resource.
- Server responds with current value, then **pushes** new values whenever the state changes — without client polling.
- The server includes an **Observe Option Sequence Number** in each notification; client cancels with `Observe: 1` (deregister).
- **Biomedical example:** A pulse-oximeter resource `coap://192.168.1.42/spo2` sends updates the moment SpO₂ drops below 90% — no need to keep polling.

#### (b) Explain IEEE 11073 standard and Continua Health Alliance in the context of biomedical IoT. List at least four device specialization sub-standards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **IEEE 11073 (X73):** International standard for **personal health device communication**, optimized for exchanging data from wearable / point-of-care devices. *(1 mark)*
- **Architecture:** Three core components — **Domain Information Model (DIM)**, **Service Model**, **Communication Model**. *(1 mark)*
  - **DIM:** Object-oriented. `MDS` (Medical Device System) is the root; children include `Metric`, `Numeric`, `Enumeration`, `RT-SA` (Real-Time Sample Array for waveforms like ECG).
  - **MDER (Medical Device Encoding Rules):** Binary ASN.1 variant that compresses data ~80% vs XML.
- **Transport Independence:** 11073-20601 is transport-agnostic. 11073-30200 defines the BLE / USB / ZigBee transports. *(1 mark)*
- **Plug-and-Play Association:** Devices advertise themselves; the manager (phone, hub) negotiates configuration; data flows with minimal user intervention. *(1 mark)*

**Device Specialization Sub-Standards (any 4+):** *(≥ 1 mark)*
- 11073-10404 — Pulse Oximeter
- 11073-10407 — Blood Pressure Cuff
- 11073-10408 — Thermometer
- 11073-10415 — Weighing Scale
- 11073-10417 — Glucose Meter
- 11073-10441 — Cardiovascular (ECG, Holter)
- 11073-10471 — Activity Monitor
- 11073-10442 — Strength Fitness Equipment

**Continua Health Alliance (H.810–H.880):** *(1 mark)*
- Industry consortium (now part of **Personal Connected Health Alliance**).
- Certifies end-to-end interoperability of personal connected health devices and services.
- Defines "Design Guidelines" that combine **IEEE 11073** with **Bluetooth LE, USB, ZigBee, NFC** as transports, and **HL7 FHIR** for EHR exchange.
- Reference architecture: **PAN-Device → PAN-Manager (phone/hub) → Health Record Network → EHR**.
- Adopted in Apple Health, Samsung Health, and many consumer wearables to "just work" with smartphones.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Communication Protocol questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Confusing MQTT pub/sub with HTTP req/resp** — examiners will give **0** if you claim MQTT is "request-response".
> 2. **Forgetting QoS distinctions** — never write "MQTT is reliable". Specify **which QoS** you mean.
> 3. **Misnaming layers** — saying "ZigBee is an application protocol" is wrong; it is a **link + network** protocol.
> 4. **No diagrams** — a 14-mark question without a labeled block diagram typically loses **2–3 marks** under KTU valuation.
> 5. **Wrong header sizes** — 2 bytes for MQTT, 4 bytes for CoAP. Mixing them is a free mark-loss.
> 6. **No real biomedical tie-in** — Module 1 of PCBRT402 demands biomedical context. A generic IoT answer without an ECG/SpO²/CGM example **loses 1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Skipping numeric in 14-mark questions** — whenever the question allows a "design" sub-part, computing the **link budget, ToA, or battery life** adds a guaranteed 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (save this for the day before ESE):**

- ✅ IoT protocols must support **low power, low bandwidth, lossy networks, massive scale**.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi** = high throughput, mains-powered, used in hospital LAN.
- ✅ **BLE 5.x** = 2 Mbps PHY, 2.4 GHz, 40 channels (3 adv + 37 data), the **de-facto choice for wearables**.
- ✅ **ZigBee** = IEEE 802.15.4 + mesh, 250 kbps, used in meshed building/ward automation.
- ✅ **LoRa** = CSS modulation, sub-GHz, 0.25–50 kbps, up to 15 km, **de-facto choice for RPM**.
- ✅ **NB-IoT** = licensed 3GPP, deep indoor penetration, 10-year battery, operator subscription.
- ✅ **6LoWPAN** compresses IPv6 from **40 B to ~2 B** and is mandatory for IEEE 802.15.4.
- ✅ **RPL** builds a DODAG for LLNs using OF0/MRHOF objective functions.
- ✅ **MQTT** = TCP + pub/sub + broker + topics + **3 QoS levels + LWT** + 2-byte header.
- ✅ **CoAP** = UDP + req/resp + 4-byte header + **Observe option** for push semantics.
- ✅ **AMQP** = queuing + exchanges, enterprise-grade; **HL7 FHIR** = REST/JSON for EHRs.
- ✅ **IEEE 11073** = the "**lingua franca**" of personal health devices (20601 + 104xx).
- ✅ **Continua H.810** = interoperability framework tying 11073 + BLE + FHIR.
- ✅ **Friis:** $L_{path}=20\log_{10}(d)+20\log_{10}(f)+32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- ✅ **Shannon:** $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ — fastest way to score 2 marks in numerics.
- ✅ **LoRa ToA** depends on SF, BW, CR, PL — doubling SF roughly **doubles** ToA and **halves** bit rate.
- ✅ Always give a **biomedical context** (ECG, SpO², CGM, infusion pump) — KTU rewards application.
- ✅ **Last Will & Testament (LWT)** in MQTT is critical for medical alarm systems.
- ✅ **CoAP Observe** eliminates polling — perfect for low-power patient monitors.
- ✅ Remember the **2/4/8-byte header rule**: **MQTT 2, CoAP 4, IPv6 40, HTTP 200–800**.
- ✅ Master the **MQTT QoS 2 four-way handshake**: PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP.

---

**END OF MODULE 1 — COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS NOTES**
*Total estimated KTU marks coverage: Part A × 2 (6 M) + Part B × 1 (14 M) = 20 marks of every ESE.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Systems, APIs, Sensor Interfaces - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded Systems, APIs, Sensor Interfaces - ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Embedded Systems (In the IoT–Biomedical Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An **Embedded System** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based computing system that is *purpose-built*, *resource-constrained*, and designed to perform a *dedicated function* within a larger mechanical or electronic system, often operating under **real-time constraints** with deterministic response behaviour.

In the IoT-biomedical domain, an embedded system is the **on-body or bedside "brain"** that:
1. Acquires the biosignal (here, ECG),
2. Digitises and processes it locally (edge computing),
3. Decides locally (e.g., arrhythmia detection), and
4. Pushes data to the cloud using an API.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Stethoscope Upgrade:**
Imagine a traditional stethoscope. It is purely *passive* — it just conducts sound. Now imagine a smart stethoscope that *listens* to your heart, *understands* the rhythm, *detects* an irregular beat, *alerts* your doctor, and *uploads* the recording to a hospital cloud — all in 1 second. That tiny circuit inside the smart stethoscope is the **embedded system**.

| Layer | Typical Component | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | MCU (ARM Cortex, ESP32) | AD8232 ECG module + STM32 |
| **Software** | RTOS / Bare-metal firmware | FreeRTOS scheduler |
| **Connectivity** | Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa | BLE patch to smartphone |
| **Application Logic** | Signal processing, decision | R-peak detection algorithm |

---

### 1.2 API (Application Programming Interface)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> An **API** is a *contract*-based communication interface that allows two software layers (or two physical devices) to exchange structured data without needing to know each other's internal implementation.

**In IoT-Biomedical terms**, an API is the *language* the embedded device speaks with the cloud/server.

**Real-world Analogy — The Waiter in a Restaurant:**
- You (the embedded device) want food (data) from the kitchen (cloud server).
- The **waiter (API)** carries your order in a *standardised menu card* (JSON/HTTP request) and returns the dish (JSON response).
- You don't enter the kitchen; you don't know the recipe — the waiter handles it.

The most common API style in IoT is **REST over HTTP/HTTPS**, but for low-power devices, **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is preferred.

---

### 1.3 Sensor Interface

> [!NOTE]
> A **Sensor Interface** is the *electronic and protocol-level pathway* that physically and logically connects a transducer (e.g., ECG electrode) to the processing unit (MCU).

The interface converts the **raw physical phenomenon** (mV of cardiac electrical activity) into a **digitally processable form** (a 12-bit or 16-bit integer).

**Analogy — The Translator at a Foreign Embassy:**
A diplomat (ECG electrode) speaks a soft, noisy, low-voltage electrical "language." The MCU only understands a clean, digital, well-defined "language." The **sensor interface (front-end analogue + ADC + digital protocol)** is the *certified translator* that turns the diplomat's whisper into a crisp digital document.

**Standard biomedical sensor interfaces:**

| Interface | Bus Type | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **ADC (Analogue Front-End)** | Single-ended / Differential | ECG, EMG, EEG electrodes |
| **I²C** | 2-wire serial | Digital temp, SpO₂, accelerometer |
| **SPI** | 4-wire serial | High-speed ADC, display, SD card |
| **UART** | Asynchronous serial | Bluetooth, GPS modules |
| **BLE / Wi-Fi** | Wireless | Cloud sync to phone/cloud |

---

### 1.4 ECG (Electrocardiogram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** is a *transcutaneous, non-invasive graphical recording* of the **electrical activity of the heart** over a period of time, captured via surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest.

**Analogy — The Heart's Public Address System:**
- Every time the heart beats, a wave of *electrical depolarisation* sweeps across the cardiac muscle.
- The body acts as a giant volume conductor; surface electrodes "listen in" to this wave.
- The ECG is essentially the **loud-speaker output** of the heart's internal pacemaker (SA node) — *visualised on paper or screen*.

**Standard ECG Signal Characteristics (Bold = KTU high-yield values):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Must-Know |
|---|---|---|
| Amplitude | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** | Required for ADC gain design |
| Frequency Band | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Determines filter cut-offs |
| QRS Duration | **80 – 120 ms** | Diagnostic window |
| Heart Rate Range | **40 – 200 bpm** | Determines buffer size |
| Sampling Frequency | **250 – 500 Hz** (clinical: 1000 Hz) | Nyquist compliance |
| ADC Resolution | **12-bit (minimum)** | ±5 mV → 2.44 µV/LSB |
| Electrode Type | **Ag/AgCl (silver/silver-chloride)** | Polarisation stability |

The classical **Einthoven's Triangle** (Right Arm – Left Arm – Left Leg) forms the basis of Leads I, II, and III, and serves as the foundation for the modern 12-lead ECG.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Embedded ECG System — Layered Operation

The system can be broken into **five functional layers**, each with a clearly defined role.

**Layer 1 — Acquisition (Sensor) Layer**
- Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl) detect skin potential differences (μV–mV range).
- Three-electrode differential configuration is the KTU-benchmark.

**Layer 2 — Analogue Front-End (AFE)**
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with high **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) ≥ 90 dB**.
- Gain stage: amplifies ~1 mV ECG to ~1–3 V (suitable for ADC).
- **High-pass filter (HPF)**: removes DC electrode offset (< 0.5 Hz cut-off).
- **Low-pass filter (LPF)**: anti-alias filter (< 150 Hz cut-off).

**Layer 3 — Digitisation Layer**
- **Analogue-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** samples the conditioned signal.
- Sampling rate must satisfy **Nyquist–Shannon theorem**.

**Layer 4 — Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**
- Pan–Tompkins algorithm (QRS detection), baseline wander removal, R-peak detection.
- Heart-rate computation from R-R interval.

**Layer 5 — Connectivity & API Layer**
- MCU packages ECG frames into JSON.
- Sends via HTTPS REST `POST /api/v1/ecg` or via MQTT topic `patient/123/ecg`.

---

### 2.2 Why Each Step Is Necessary (The 'Why' Behind the 'How')

| Step | Why It Is Needed |
|---|---|
| **INA (Instrumentation Amp)** | Body picks up 50/60 Hz mains noise as a *common-mode* signal — INA rejects it via high CMRR. |
| **High-Pass Filter** | Skin–electrode interface produces a slow DC drift (sweat, motion) — HPF removes it without affecting QRS. |
| **Low-Pass Filter** | ECG is band-limited; LPF prevents **aliasing** during ADC sampling. |
| **ADC with 12-bit+ resolution** | Smallest ECG feature (P-wave) is ~0.1 mV; 12-bit resolution is the *minimum* to preserve clinical fidelity. |
| **Microcontroller with DSP** | Real-time QRS detection must complete within the **R-R interval** (≈ 1 sec at 60 bpm). |
| **API Push to Cloud** | Allows remote cardiologist monitoring, ML-based arrhythmia classification, EMR integration. |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula list**. Memorising these is non-negotiable for KTU 2024 scheme ESE.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | $f_s = 500$ Hz for ECG |
| 2 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC resolution per LSB | $\Delta V = 10\,\text{mV}/4096 = 2.44\,\mu V$ |
| 3 | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection | $\geq 90$ dB required |
| 4 | $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}\,(\text{s})}$ | Heart rate from R-R interval | $T_{RR} = 1$ s → HR = 60 bpm |
| 5 | $A_{v} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | INA gain (3-amp topology) | $A_v = 1000$ for ECG |
| 6 | $f_c^{HPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | High-pass cut-off | $0.05$ Hz |
| 7 | $f_c^{LPF} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Low-pass cut-off | $150$ Hz |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f}$ | Quality factor of band-pass | $Q \approx 5$–$10$ for QRS filter |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | Ideal ADC SNR | $n = 12$ → 74 dB |
| 10 | $\text{Time per sample} = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Sample interval | $2$ ms at 500 Hz |

> **Important:** Note that all vertical bar expressions (e.g., absolute value) have been written as `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to keep the table syntax intact.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Wearables** | Apple Watch, Fitbit — single-lead ECG using dry electrodes |
| **Clinical Monitoring** | ICU multi-parameter monitors, Holter (24-h ambulatory) |
| **Telemedicine** | IoT-ECG patch transmits to cloud via 4G/5G → cardiologist phone |
| **Implantables** | Pacemakers use embedded ECG to detect arrhythmias |
| **Sports Medicine** | Athlete ECG during training — real-time HRV analysis |
| **AI/ML Cardiology** | Cloud-side CNN/LSTM arrhythmia classifiers trained on IoT-ECG streams |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Frequency for ECG (Nyquist)

The ECG signal's highest significant frequency component is approximately **$f_{max} = 150$ Hz** (high-frequency QRS notches and pacemaker spikes). To avoid aliasing, the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** requires:

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \times 150\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 300\,\text{Hz}
$$

However, in clinical practice, a **safety factor of 1.5× to 2×** is applied, so:

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2.5 \times f_{max} \;=\; 2.5 \times 150 \;=\; 375\,\text{Hz}
$$

Therefore, the **industry-standard** ECG sampling rate is chosen as:

$$
f_s \;=\; 500\,\text{Hz} \quad (\text{or } 1000\,\text{Hz} \text{ for clinical-grade systems})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the safety margin?** A finite-slope low-pass filter never provides an infinitely sharp cut-off. A higher $f_s$ creates a wider *transition band* and protects the QRS fidelity from aliasing distortion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Resolution Required to Capture P-Wave

The smallest clinically meaningful ECG wave (P-wave) has amplitude:

$$
V_{min} \;\approx\; 0.1\,\text{mV} \;=\; 100\,\mu\text{V}
$$

The full-scale ECG range is:

$$
V_{FS} \;\approx\; \pm 5\,\text{mV} \;=\; 10\,\text{mV peak-to-peak}
$$

For a faithful digitisation, the **quantisation step (LSB)** must be smaller than the smallest feature:

$$
\Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \;\leq\; \frac{V_{min}}{2}
$$

**Step-by-step algebraic solution for $n$:**

$$
\frac{10\,\text{mV}}{2^n} \;\leq\; \frac{0.1\,\text{mV}}{2}
$$

$$
2^n \;\geq\; \frac{10\,\text{mV} \times 2}{0.1\,\text{mV}} \;=\; 200
$$

$$
n \;\geq\; \log_2(200) \;=\; 7.64
$$

Rounding up to the next integer:

$$
n \;\geq\; 8\,\text{bits}
$$

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Although *8 bits* mathematically suffices, **12 bits is the KTU/clinical-standard** choice because it adds *headroom* for noise, baseline wander, and provides $\Delta V = 2.44\,\mu\text{V}$, which preserves QRS morphology in detail.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heart Rate from R-R Interval

In any ECG recording, the **R-peak** is the tallest, sharpest spike. The time between two successive R-peaks is the **R-R interval**, denoted $T_{RR}$ in seconds.

By definition:

$$
\text{Heart Rate (bpm)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of beats in 1 minute}}{1}
$$

If one beat takes $T_{RR}$ seconds, then the number of beats in 60 seconds is:

$$
\text{HR} \;=\; \frac{60}{T_{RR}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given an embedded ECG patch reports $T_{RR} = 0.8$ seconds, calculate the heart rate.

$$
\text{HR} \;=\; \frac{60}{0.8} \;=\; 75\,\text{bpm}
$$

**Inverted form (for arrhythmia detection):**

$$
T_{RR} \;=\; \frac{60}{\text{HR}}
$$

For HR = 60 bpm (normal resting):

$$
T_{RR} \;=\; \frac{60}{60} \;=\; 1.0\,\text{second}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — High-Pass Filter Cut-off (Baseline Wander Removal)

The high-pass filter (RC type) has cut-off frequency:

$$
f_c^{HPF} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C}
$$

For an ECG, the cut-off should be **0.05 Hz** (below this, only baseline drift lives). Solving for $C$ when $R = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$:

$$
0.05 \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi (1 \times 10^6)\, C}
$$

$$
C \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
C \;=\; \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
C \;\approx\; 3.18\,\mu\text{F}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — SNR of an Ideal N-bit ADC

For an ideal ADC with $n$ bits, the theoretical **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \cdot n \;+\; 1.76
$$

For a **12-bit** ADC:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 \;+\; 1.76 \;=\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;=\; 74\,\text{dB}
$$

For a **16-bit** ADC (used in premium ECG systems):

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \times 16 \;+\; 1.76 \;=\; 96.32 + 1.76 \;=\; 98.08\,\text{dB}
$$

> **KTU Insight:** Every additional bit of ADC resolution increases SNR by **6.02 dB**, but doubles memory/storage requirements. Therefore, *16-bit* is the sweet-spot for high-fidelity ECG.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Interface for ECG

| ECG Use-Case | Recommended Sensor Interface | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient Monitor (Wired)** | Analogue → ADC (SPI) | Low-latency, deterministic |
| **Wearable Patch** | Analogue → Internal ADC (I²S/PDM) | Compact, low-power |
| **Remote IoT Patch** | ADC → MCU → BLE/Wi-Fi → MQTT | Cloud sync, long battery life |
| **Multi-lead Clinical ECG** | Multiplexed ADC + UART/BLE | 12-lead needs fast streaming |
| **Implantable Loop Recorder** | Internal ADC + low-power radio | Ultra-low power critical |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical — Full Signal Chain Verification

> **Problem:** A 12-bit ADC with range 0–3.3 V samples an ECG signal at 500 Hz. The amplified ECG peak is 2.5 V. Find (a) LSB value, (b) Digital code for the peak, (c) Quantisation noise RMS.

**(a) LSB value:**

$$
\Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{12}} \;=\; \frac{3.3}{4096} \;=\; 0.000806\,\text{V} \;\approx\; 806\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**(b) Digital code for 2.5 V peak:**

$$
D \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{2.5}{0.000806} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 3101.7 \right\rfloor \;=\; 3101
$$

$$
D_{\text{hex}} \;=\; 0x0C1D
$$

**(c) Quantisation noise RMS (for uniform quantiser):**

$$
\sigma_q \;=\; \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}} \;=\; \frac{0.000806}{\sqrt{12}} \;=\; \frac{0.000806}{3.464}
$$

$$
\sigma_q \;\approx\; 2.33 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \;=\; 233\,\mu\text{V}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT ECG System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete signal and data flow** of an IoT-based ECG system, from skin to cloud dashboard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PATIENT SKIN SURFACE] --> B[Ag/AgCl ELECTRODES x3]
    B --> C[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER INA128]
    C --> D[HIGH PASS FILTER 0.05 Hz]
    D --> E[LOW PASS FILTER 150 Hz]
    E --> F[NOTCH FILTER 50 Hz MAINS REJECTION]
    F --> G[12-BIT ADC]
    G --> H[STM32 MICROCONTROLLER]
    H --> I[QRS DETECTION ALGORITHM]
    I --> J[HEART RATE COMPUTATION]
    J --> K[JSON PACKAGING MODULE]
    K --> L{WIRELESS LINK SELECTION}
    L --> M[BLE MODULE]
    L --> N[WI-FI MODULE ESP8266]
    L --> O[MQTT BROKER]
    O --> P[CLOUD SERVER REST API]
    P --> Q[DASHBOARD FOR CARDIOLOGIST]
    P --> R[ML MODEL ARRHYTHMIA CLASSIFIER]
    R --> S[ALERT NOTIFICATION PUSH OR SMS]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top half (A–H):** Analogue acquisition chain (electrodes → INA → filters → ADC → MCU).
- **Middle (I–J):** Digital signal processing — QRS detection + heart-rate math.
- **Bottom (K–S):** Connectivity + cloud + AI pipeline.

---

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — REST API Call from ECG Device to Cloud

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as ECG Device
    participant API as REST API Server
    participant DB as Cloud Database
    participant Doc as Cardiologist App

    Dev->>API: POST /api/v1/ecg/upload (JSON body)
    API->>API: Validate JWT token
    API->>DB: INSERT INTO ecg_records
    DB-->>API: record_id = 4821
    API-->>Dev: HTTP 201 Created
    Dev->>API: GET /api/v1/patient/123/ecg/latest
    API->>DB: SELECT * FROM ecg_records
    DB-->>API: ECG JSON payload
    API-->>Dev: HTTP 200 OK with ECG data
    Doc->>API: GET /api/v1/ecg/4821
    API->>DB: Fetch record
    DB-->>API: ECG + metadata
    API-->>Doc: HTTP 200 with full ECG + ML result
    Doc->>API: POST /api/v1/alert
    API-->>Doc: HTTP 200 alert acknowledged
```

**Key API Design Points (KTU Exam Hot-Spots):**
- **HTTP verbs:** `POST` to upload, `GET` to retrieve.
- **Auth:** JWT (JSON Web Token) for security.
- **Data format:** JSON `{timestamp, heart_rate, lead_II_array[], patient_id}`.

---

### 4.3 Flowchart — Embedded Firmware Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power ON]) --> Init[Init GPIO, ADC, BLE, Timer]
    Init --> Loop{Is ADC sample ready?}
    Loop -- No --> Loop
    Loop -- Yes --> Buffer[Append to circular buffer]
    Buffer --> QRS{R-peak detected?}
    QRS -- No --> Loop
    QRS -- Yes --> RR[Compute R-R interval]
    RR --> HR[Compute HR = 60/RR]
    HR --> Thresh{HR abnormal?}
    Thresh -- Yes --> Alert[Trigger local LED buzzer alert]
    Thresh -- No --> Loop
    HR --> Pack[Pack JSON packet]
    Pack --> TX[Broadcast via BLE or MQTT]
    TX --> Loop
    Alert --> Loop
```

---

### 4.4 Layered Architecture — API Stack in IoT-ECG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[APPLICATION LAYER: Cardiologist Dashboard] --> L2[API LAYER: REST endpoints over HTTPS]
    L2 --> L3[AUTHENTICATION LAYER: JWT or OAuth2]
    L3 --> L4[BUSINESS LOGIC: ECG validation, ML inference]
    L4 --> L5[DATA LAYER: PostgreSQL time-series DB]
    L5 --> L6[DEVICE LAYER: MCU firmware + MQTT publisher]
    L6 --> L7[PHYSICAL LAYER: BLE Wi-Fi 4G radio]
```

> **Reading the layers:** From top to bottom — user-facing app on top, radio waves on the bottom. The **API layer is the contract** that the embedded device and the cloud agree upon, decoupling the hardware from the application.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define an embedded system. List any two biomedical applications of embedded systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, dedicated-function computing system that operates under real-time constraints and is integrated into a larger device.
- **Two biomedical applications (1.5 marks):**
  1. ECG patient monitor
  2. Insulin pump / Pacemaker / Digital stethoscope / Pulse oximeter (any two)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an API? How is it used in IoT-based biomedical devices?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** API (Application Programming Interface) is a structured communication interface that allows two software systems to exchange data in a defined format (commonly JSON over HTTP).
- **Use in IoT-biomedical (2 marks):**
  1. The embedded ECG device sends data to a cloud server using a REST API (`POST /api/v1/ecg`).
  2. The server exposes endpoints for the doctor app to retrieve data, enabling remote patient monitoring and ML-based diagnostics.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of an IoT-based ECG monitoring system** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum sampling frequency** required for faithful ECG digitisation. State and justify the **industry-standard sampling rate** used in clinical ECG systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

A typical IoT-ECG system has the following blocks:

| Block | Function | KTU Key Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** | Detect skin potential difference (mV range) | 3-electrode differential config |
| **Instrumentation Amplifier** | Amplify differential ECG, reject common-mode noise | CMRR ≥ 90 dB |
| **HPF (0.05 Hz)** | Remove DC baseline drift | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ |
| **LPF (150 Hz)** | Anti-alias filter | Prevents Nyquist violation |
| **Notch Filter (50 Hz)** | Reject mains interference | India uses 50 Hz |
| **ADC (12–16 bit)** | Convert analogue ECG to digital samples | $f_s = 500$ Hz |
| **MCU (STM32/ESP32)** | Run QRS detection + HR algorithm | Real-time DSP |
| **Connectivity (BLE/Wi-Fi)** | Wireless data transmission | Low-power |
| **Cloud (REST API + DB)** | Store, retrieve, ML classify | JSON over HTTPS |
| **Mobile/Web Dashboard** | Visualisation for doctor | Real-time display |

The signal flows linearly: **Electrodes → AFE → ADC → MCU → Wireless → Cloud → Dashboard**.

---

**(b) Sampling Frequency Derivation (7 marks):**

The ECG signal has significant frequency components up to $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz.

By the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, the sampling frequency must be:

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

Substituting:

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \times 150 \;=\; 300\,\text{Hz}
$$

A **safety margin of 2× to 3×** is added to allow for the non-ideal slope of the anti-aliasing LPF:

$$
f_s \;=\; 2.5 \times 150 \;=\; 375\,\text{Hz}
$$

Rounded up to a **standard value**:

$$
\boxed{f_s \;=\; 500\,\text{Hz (clinical: 1000 Hz)}}
$$

**Justification:** A 500 Hz sampling rate provides:
- Comfortable Nyquist margin (3.33× over $f_{max}$).
- Storage efficiency (≈ 1 kB/sec per channel at 12-bit).
- Sufficient temporal resolution to capture the QRS width (~ 100 ms → 50 samples).
- Compatibility with the Pan–Tompkins QRS detection algorithm.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What is an **API**? Explain **REST API** with a suitable example of an IoT-ECG data upload. List four common HTTP methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG signal is digitised by a **12-bit ADC with range 0–3.3 V**. Find:
  1. The LSB resolution in µV.
  2. The digital code for a 2.0 V input.
  3. The ideal SNR in dB.
  4. The quantisation noise RMS voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) API & REST API (7 marks):**

- **API Definition (1.5 marks):** An API is a contract-based communication layer that allows two software systems to exchange data in a structured format without exposing internal implementation.
- **REST API (3.5 marks):**
  - REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style that uses **HTTP** as the transport and **JSON** (or XML) as the data format.
  - **Example – IoT ECG Upload:**

```http
POST /api/v1/ecg/upload HTTP/1.1
Host: cloud.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <JWT_TOKEN>

{
  "patient_id": "P123",
  "timestamp": "2024-06-15T10:30:00Z",
  "heart_rate": 78,
  "lead_II_samples": [0.12, 0.15, 1.85, 0.10, ...]
}
```

- **Four HTTP Methods (2 marks):**
  1. **GET** – retrieve data.
  2. **POST** – create/upload new data.
  3. **PUT** – update existing data.
  4. **DELETE** – remove data.

---

**(b) ADC Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V, $n = 12$ bits.

**(i) LSB Resolution (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \;=\; \frac{3.3}{4096} \;=\; 8.06 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
\Delta V \;=\; 806\,\mu\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Digital Code for 2.0 V (1.5 marks):**

$$
D \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{2.0}{8.06 \times 10^{-4}} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 2480.39 \right\rfloor \;=\; 2480
$$

In hex: $D = 0x09B0$

**(iii) Ideal SNR in dB (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 \;+\; 1.76 \;=\; 74\,\text{dB}
$$

**(iv) Quantisation Noise RMS (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_q \;=\; \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}} \;=\; \frac{806 \times 10^{-6}}{3.464} \;=\; 232.7\,\mu\text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_q \;\approx\; 233\,\mu\text{V}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor in Nyquist** — students often write $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ and stop. Examiners expect justification of the **2.5× to 3× practical margin** and the *industry-standard 500 Hz* value.
> 2. **Confusing $n$ with $2^n$** in ADC resolution — write the LSB formula explicitly; don't just state the number.
> 3. **Mixing up HPF and LPF roles** — HPF removes baseline wander (low freq), LPF prevents aliasing (high freq). Reversing these loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting units** in derivations — $f_s$ in Hz, $\Delta V$ in µV/mV, $T_{RR}$ in seconds. *Always include units*.
> 5. **API questions** — many students describe API generically but fail to show a **sample JSON payload** or **HTTP method list**. Both are required for full marks.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — a block diagram without signal flow arrows is considered *incomplete* by KTU examiners.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Embedded System** = dedicated, real-time, resource-constrained MCU system → used in ECG monitors, pacemakers, insulin pumps.
- **API** = standardised communication contract; **REST + JSON** is the IoT default; **MQTT** is preferred for low-power devices.
- **Sensor Interface** = electrode → AFE → filter → ADC → MCU pipeline; key chips are **INA128** and **ADS1292**.
- **ECG Signal Band = 0.05 – 150 Hz**; **Amplitude = 0.5 – 5 mV**; **HR = 60 / R-R interval (s)**.
- **Sampling Rate** = 500 Hz (minimum clinical); **ADC resolution** = 12-bit (entry), 16-bit (premium).
- **Nyquist Theorem** = $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; always add a **practical safety factor of 2.5× – 3×**.
- **HPF (0.05 Hz)** removes baseline drift; **LPF (150 Hz)** prevents aliasing; **Notch (50 Hz)** removes mains noise.
- **Einthoven's Triangle** = RA–LA–LL gives Leads I, II, III — foundation of 12-lead ECG.
- **CMRR of INA ≥ 90 dB** is mandatory for biomedical front-ends.
- **Quantisation Noise RMS** = $\Delta V / \sqrt{12}$; **SNR** = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- **JSON packet for ECG must include**: `patient_id`, `timestamp`, `heart_rate`, `lead_samples[]`.
- **Draw block diagrams with arrows**; **always state units**; **show all derivation steps** for full marks in KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Oximeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Oximeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Pulse Oximeter** is a non-invasive, photoelectric biomedical sensor device used to continuously monitor two critical physiological parameters of a patient:

1. **Arterial Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂)** – the percentage of hemoglobin in arterial blood that is saturated with oxygen.
2. **Pulse Rate (PR)** – the number of heartbeats per minute derived from blood-volume pulsations.

The device operates on the principle of **spectrophotometry** combined with **photoplethysmography (PPG)**. It illuminates the skin (typically fingertip, earlobe, or toe) with two specific wavelengths of light and analyzes the differential absorption caused by **Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)** and **Deoxyhemoglobin (HHb)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Pulse Oximeter is a core biomedical transducer topic under "Sensors for Biomedical Applications." Students must master the optical principle, Beer–Lambert Law, two-wavelength analysis, and the calculation of the **Ratio of Ratios (R)** to map it to SpO₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric (KTU expected value):** Normal SpO₂ range for a healthy adult is **95%–100%**. Values below **90%** are clinically termed **hypoxemia** and require immediate medical intervention.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English)

Imagine shining a torch through two different glasses of juice:

- One glass contains **red-colored** liquid (oxygen-poor blood).
- The other contains **bright cherry-red** liquid (oxygen-rich blood).

Now imagine you cannot see the liquid directly — you can only see how much light passes **through** the glass. If the light comes out **bright red**, the blood is rich in oxygen. If the light comes out **dark**, the blood is poor in oxygen.

A **pulse oximeter does exactly this** but uses two invisible colors of light:
- **Red light (~660 nm)** → absorbed strongly by *deoxygenated* blood.
- **Infrared light (~940 nm)** → absorbed strongly by *oxygenated* blood.

The clever trick: blood volume in your fingertip **pulses with every heartbeat** (systole vs. diastole). By measuring the **changing** part of the light signal (AC) versus the **constant** part (DC), the device isolates arterial blood from skin, bone, and venous blood. This pulsatile change is the **photoplethysmographic (PPG) waveform**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the pulse oximeter like a toll booth counter. The "AC" component is the cars moving past (pulsing arteries), while the "DC" component is the parked cars in the lot (steady tissues). The oximeter only counts the moving cars — that is arterial saturation.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Design Wavelengths

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Red LED wavelength | $\lambda_R$ | **660 nm** |
| Infrared LED wavelength | $\lambda_{IR}$ | **940 nm** |
| Normal SpO₂ range | SpO₂ | **95% – 100%** |
| Hypoxemia threshold | SpO₂ | **< 90%** |
| Pulse rate range | PR | **60 – 100 bpm** |
| Sampling rate (typical) | $f_s$ | **100 – 500 Hz** |
| Update period on display | $T_{upd}$ | **~ 1 second** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of **660 nm** and **940 nm** is not arbitrary — these are the **isosbestic crossover-free wavelengths** where the absorption coefficients of HbO₂ and HHb differ maximally, producing the best signal-to-noise ratio for SpO₂ estimation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The pulse oximeter functions through a chain of well-defined physical and signal-processing stages:

### **Step 1: Dual-Wavelength Illumination**
- A probe clipped on the fingertip contains **two light-emitting diodes (LEDs)** — one Red ($\lambda_R = 660\,\text{nm}$) and one Infrared ($\lambda_{IR} = 940\,\text{nm}$).
- A **photodetector (PD)** on the opposite side captures the transmitted light.

### **Step 2: Differential Absorption by Hemoglobin Species**
- **Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)** absorbs **more Infrared** and **less Red**.
- **Deoxyhemoglobin (HHb)** absorbs **more Red** and **less Infrared**.
- The transmitted light intensity at each wavelength is governed by the **Beer–Lambert Law**.

### **Step 3: Photoplethysmographic (PPG) Signal Generation**
- During **systole** (heart contraction), arterial blood volume increases → more absorption → lower transmitted intensity.
- During **diastole** (heart relaxation), arterial blood volume decreases → less absorption → higher transmitted intensity.
- This produces a time-varying **PPG waveform** with a clear pulsatile component.

### **Step 4: Separation of AC and DC Components**
The PPG signal at each wavelength is decomposed as:
- **DC component** → constant absorption due to skin, bone, tissue, venous blood, and non-pulsatile arterial blood.
- **AC component** → pulsatile absorption due solely to the changing arterial blood volume.

### **Step 5: Computation of the "Ratio of Ratios" (R)**
The dimensionless ratio $R$ cancels out many variables (LED intensity drift, tissue thickness, skin pigmentation) and yields a clean saturation-dependent metric:

$$R = \frac{\left(\dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}}\right)}$$

### **Step 6: Empirical Calibration to SpO₂**
$R$ is mapped to SpO₂ via an empirically derived calibration curve stored in firmware. A widely used linear approximation is:

$$\text{SpO}_2 \;[\%] = 110 - 25 \cdot R$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why and How" matters (KTU examiner perspective):** The examiner expects you to explain *why two wavelengths are used* (to decouple two unknowns: HbO₂ and HHb concentrations) and *why the ratio R is dimensionless* (to make the measurement independent of absolute light intensity and skin color).

---

## 2.2 The Beer–Lambert Law — Foundation of Pulse Oximetry

When monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ passes through an absorbing medium of concentration $c$ and path length $l$, the transmitted intensity $I$ follows:

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \, c \, l}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = concentration of the absorbing species (mol/L)
- $l$ = optical path length (cm)

The **absorbance** $A$ is defined as:

$$A = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Beer–Lambert Law is the bedrock of all **spectrophotometric biomedical sensors** — not just pulse oximeters, but also bilirubin meters, hemoglobin analyzers, and non-invasive glucose monitors. Mastering it unlocks ~70% of optical biosensor questions in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bars use `\vert` (not `\|` or `|`), and the symbol `\leq` is used instead of `<=` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variable Meaning | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer–Lambert Law (intensity) | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \, c \, l}$ | $I_0$ incident, $I$ transmitted intensity | W/m² | Valid for dilute, non-scattering media |
| 2 | Absorbance | $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I) = \varepsilon \, c \, l$ | Logarithmic absorption measure | dimensionless | Additive for multiple absorbers |
| 3 | Molar Extinction Coefficient | $\varepsilon_\lambda = A / (c \cdot l)$ | Wavelength-dependent absorption strength | L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | Unique to each chromophore |
| 4 | Ratio of Ratios | $R = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ | Saturation-dependent dimensionless metric | dimensionless | Independent of LED intensity |
| 5 | SpO₂ Linear Approximation | $\text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$ | Empirical calibration curve | % | Valid for $0.4 \leq R \leq 1.0$ |
| 6 | Heart Rate from PPG | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{60}{\Delta t_{peak-peak}}$ | Beats per minute from peak intervals | bpm | $\Delta t$ in seconds |
| 7 | PPG DC Component | $DC_\lambda = I_{diastole}$ | Steady transmitted intensity at $\lambda$ | W/m² | Set by baseline tissue absorption |
| 8 | PPG AC Component | $AC_\lambda = I_{diastole} - I_{systole}$ | Pulsatile transmitted intensity at $\lambda$ | W/m² | Set by arterial blood volume change |
| 9 | Normal SpO₂ Range | $95\% \leq \text{SpO}_2 \leq 100\%$ | Healthy adult arterial saturation | % | $< 90\%$ = hypoxemia |
| 10 | Isosbestic Wavelength | $\varepsilon_{HbO_2}(\lambda^*) = \varepsilon_{HHb}(\lambda^*)$ | Wavelength of equal absorption | nm | ~ 805 nm for hemoglobin |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Field | Role of Pulse Oximeter |
|---|---|
| **Critical Care (ICU/OT)** | Continuous SpO₂ monitoring during anesthesia, ventilation, and surgery |
| **COVID-19 / Pneumonia Triage** | Early detection of silent hypoxemia |
| **Neonatology** | Monitoring preterm infants for oxygen toxicity |
| **Sleep Medicine** | Detecting nocturnal desaturation in sleep apnea |
| **Aviation / High-Altitude Medicine** | Pilot and mountaineer oxygen monitoring |
| **Wearables (IoT Integration)** | Smartwatches and fitness bands (Apple Watch, Fitbit) for 24×7 SpO₂ tracking — directly aligned with the **IoT & Biomedical Applications** theme of PCBRT402 |
| **Sports Physiology** | Real-time exertion and recovery analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Beer–Lambert Law

### **Starting Point: Differential Absorption**

Consider a thin slab of absorbing medium of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ at distance $x$ from the light source. The fractional decrease in light intensity as it traverses this slab is proportional to the incident intensity, the concentration $c$, and the thickness $\mathrm{d}x$:

$$-\frac{\mathrm{d}I}{I} = k \, c \, \mathrm{d}x$$

Where $k$ is a proportionality constant related to the absorption cross-section.

### **Step 1 — Integrate Over the Total Path Length $l$**

Integrate both sides from $x = 0$ (where $I = I_0$) to $x = l$ (where $I = I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} -\frac{\mathrm{d}I'}{I'} = \int_{0}^{l} k \, c \, \mathrm{d}x$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k \, c \, l$$

### **Step 2 — Convert from Natural Log to Base-10 Logarithm**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$, and defining the **molar extinction coefficient** $\varepsilon = k / 2.303$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

### **Step 3 — Define Absorbance**

$$\boxed{A \equiv \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \, c \, l}$$

### **Step 4 — Recover the Transmission Form**

Raising 10 to both sides yields the canonical Beer–Lambert transmission equation:

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon \, c \, l}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $A$ is linear in concentration $c$ and path length $l$, which is the cornerstone property that allows pulse oximeters to *quantitatively* infer hemoglobin saturation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the "Ratio of Ratios" (R) and SpO₂ Mapping

### **Step 1: Two-Wavelength Absorbance Equations**

Using Beer–Lambert at the two wavelengths (Red and IR), and assuming the arterial blood layer has pulsatile thickness $l_{AC}$ superimposed on a baseline tissue thickness $l_{DC}$:

$$A_{660} = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \, c_{HbO_2} \, l + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} \, c_{HHb} \, l$$

$$A_{940} = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \, c_{HbO_2} \, l + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} \, c_{HHb} \, l$$

### **Step 2: Subtract Diastolic from Systolic Absorbance (Get AC Component)**

The pulsatile change in absorbance $\Delta A_\lambda$ corresponds to the AC component and contains **only arterial blood information** (since only arterial blood volume changes between systole and diastole):

$$\Delta A_{660} = \left(\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} \, c_{HHb}\right) \cdot \Delta l$$

$$\Delta A_{940} = \left(\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} \, c_{HHb}\right) \cdot \Delta l$$

### **Step 3: Take the Ratio of AC Absorbances**

The unknown path-length change $\Delta l$ cancels:

$$\frac{\Delta A_{660}}{\Delta A_{940}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} \, c_{HHb}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \, c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} \, c_{HHb}}$$

### **Step 4: Introduce the Ratio of Ratios (R)**

Divide numerator and denominator by $c_{HHb}$, and let $S = c_{HbO_2}/c_{HHb}$:

$$\frac{\Delta A_{660}}{\Delta A_{940}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940}}$$

Define the **Ratio of Ratios** $R$ as this ratio (and recalling $\Delta A \propto AC$ and the DC component represents the constant baseline):

$$\boxed{R = \frac{(AC_{660}/DC_{660})}{(AC_{940}/DC_{940})}}$$

### **Step 5: Solve for $S$ (Hemoglobin Saturation Ratio)**

Invert the ratio and isolate $S$:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} = R \cdot \left(\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} \cdot S + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940}\right)$$

$$S \cdot \left(\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} - R \cdot \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940}\right) = R \cdot \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} - \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{R \cdot \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} - \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} - R \cdot \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940}}}$$

### **Step 6: Convert $S$ to SpO₂ Percentage**

The fractional oxygen saturation is:

$$\text{SaO}_2 = \frac{c_{HbO_2}}{c_{HbO_2} + c_{HHb}} = \frac{S}{S + 1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SpO}_2 \;[\%] = \frac{S}{S + 1} \times 100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Insight:** Because exact $\varepsilon$ values vary with temperature, pH, and individual physiology, manufacturers (Masimo, Nellcor, etc.) use **empirical linear calibration** of the form $\text{SpO}_2 = a - b \cdot R$, fitted from large volunteer studies at controlled desaturation levels (typically $a \approx 110$, $b \approx 25$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A pulse oximeter records the following PPG signal statistics:

| Wavelength | AC (mV) | DC (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 660 nm (Red) | 12 | 600 |
| 940 nm (IR) | 18 | 900 |

Calculate the **Ratio of Ratios (R)** and estimate **SpO₂** using the empirical formula $\text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute normalized AC at Red wavelength:**

$$\frac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} = \frac{12}{600} = 0.0200$$

**Step 2 — Compute normalized AC at IR wavelength:**

$$\frac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}} = \frac{18}{900} = 0.0200$$

**Step 3 — Compute Ratio of Ratios R:**

$$R = \frac{0.0200}{0.0200} = 1.000$$

**Step 4 — Apply SpO₂ Empirical Formula:**

$$\text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot (1.000) = 110 - 25 = 85\%$$

### **Interpretation:**

- $R = 1.0$ indicates **equal fractional pulsatile absorption at both wavelengths**, suggesting **low oxygen saturation**.
- SpO₂ = **85%** falls **below 90%** → **Hypoxemia** → clinical alarm triggered.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In practice, $R = 1$ corresponds to roughly 85% SpO₂. For a healthy patient, $R \approx 0.5$–$0.6$, giving SpO₂ ≈ 95%–98%. **Lower R ⇒ Higher SpO₂.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources & Design Solutions

| Error Source | Effect on SpO₂ | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Motion artifact | False desaturation alarms | Accelerometer-based active noise reduction (Masimo SET) |
| Low peripheral perfusion (cold fingers) | Weak PPG signal | Signal quality index (SQI) + reflective probe |
| Skin pigmentation (melanin) | Slight under-reading at low SpO₂ | Multi-wavelength (8-wavelength) sensors |
| Ambient light interference | AC contamination | Synchronous detection at LED modulation frequency |
| Nail polish (especially dark colors) | Reduced red transmittance | Removal of nail polish or use of toe/ear probe |
| Venous pulsation | Over-estimation of SpO₂ | Differential path-length algorithms |
| Dysfunctional hemoglobin (COHb, MetHb) | Inaccurate SpO₂ | Co-oximetry (invasive reference) for calibration |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pulse Oximeter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller MCU] --> B[Red LED Driver 660 nm]
    A --> C[Infrared LED Driver 940 nm]
    B --> D[Fingertip Probe Optics]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Photodetector PIN Diode]
    E --> F[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    F --> G[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 5 Hz]
    G --> H[ADC 12 bit at 500 Hz]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    I --> J[AC DC Separation]
    J --> K[Ratio of Ratios R Computation]
    K --> L[SpO2 Lookup Table Calibration]
    L --> M[LCD OLED Display]
    I --> N[Peak Detection HR Algorithm]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Bluetooth BLE IoT Module]
    O --> P[Cloud Dashboard or Mobile App]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Light from the dual LEDs passes through the finger, is collected by the photodetector, amplified, filtered, digitized, and processed by the DSP. The DSP computes both **SpO₂** (via the Ratio of Ratios) and **Heart Rate** (via peak detection), displaying them on screen and optionally streaming them to the cloud for IoT-based remote monitoring.

---

## 4.2 Signal Processing Flow — PPG Extraction & R Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawPPG[Raw PPG Signal Mixed Wavelengths] --> mux[Time Division Multiplexer LED Selector]
    mux --> redCh[Red Channel 660 nm]
    mux --> irCh[Infrared Channel 940 nm]
    redCh --> redFilt[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    irCh --> irFilt[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    redFilt --> redAC[Extract AC Component Systole to Diastole]
    redFilt --> redDC[Extract DC Component Mean Value]
    irFilt --> irAC[Extract AC Component Systole to Diastole]
    irFilt --> irDC[Extract DC Component Mean Value]
    redAC --> ratioTop[Numerator ACred divided by DCred]
    redDC --> ratioTop
    irAC --> ratioBot[Denominator ACir divided by DCir]
    irDC --> ratioBot
    ratioTop --> rComp[Ratio of Ratios R]
    ratioBot --> rComp
    rComp --> calib[Empirical SpO2 Calibration Curve]
    calib --> spo2Out[SpO2 Percentage Output]
    redFilt --> peakDet[Peak Detector Heart Rate]
    irFilt --> peakDet
    peakDet --> hrOut[Heart Rate BPM Output]
```

---

## 4.3 Pulse Oximeter Probe Structure — Cross-Sectional Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProbeFinger[Fingertip Clip Probe]
        L1[Red LED 660 nm]:::ledStyle
        L2[Infrared LED 940 nm]:::ledStyle
        PD[Photodetector Silicon PIN]:::pdStyle
        TISS[Fingertip Tissue Skin Bone Arterial Blood]
    end
    L1 --> TISS
    L2 --> TISS
    TISS --> PD
    classDef ledStyle fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    classDef pdStyle fill:#ccccff,stroke:#0000cc,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.4 System-Level IoT Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorLayer[Sensor Layer Pulse Oximeter Probe] --> edgeLayer[Edge Layer MCU BLE]
    edgeLayer --> gatewayLayer[Gateway Layer Smartphone or Raspberry Pi]
    gatewayLayer --> cloudLayer[Cloud Layer AWS Azure MQTT Broker]
    cloudLayer --> appLayer[Application Layer Doctor Dashboard Mobile App EHR]
    cloudLayer --> alertLayer[Alert Layer SMS Email Emergency Services]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Context:** This four-layer architecture directly demonstrates how a pulse oximeter fits into a **biomedical IoT (Bio-IoT)** ecosystem — a core topic in Module 1 of PCBRT402. Expect 7-mark questions asking you to draw or describe this layered architecture.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define the term "Pulse Oximeter." State its clinical significance and the normal range of SpO₂.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pulse oximeter** is a non-invasive, photoelectric medical device used to measure **arterial oxygen saturation (SpO₂)** and **pulse rate** by analyzing the differential absorption of Red (660 nm) and Infrared (940 nm) light by oxygenated and deoxygenated hemoglobin in pulsatile arterial blood.

**Clinical Significance:**
- Early detection of **hypoxemia** (SpO₂ < 90%) in ICU, OT, and emergency settings.
- Continuous patient monitoring without blood sampling (non-invasive, pain-free).
- Critical in **COVID-19, pneumonia, anesthesia, and neonatal care**.

**Normal SpO₂ Range:** **95% – 100%** for a healthy adult at sea level.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Why are exactly two wavelengths (660 nm and 940 nm) used in a pulse oximeter? Justify the choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two wavelengths are required because there are **two unknowns** in arterial blood — the concentrations of **Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)** and **Deoxyhemoglobin (HHb)**. Each wavelength provides **one independent Beer–Lambert equation**, and two equations are needed to solve for two unknowns.

**Choice of 660 nm (Red) and 940 nm (Infrared):**
- At **660 nm**, HHb absorbs strongly while HbO₂ absorbs weakly → high contrast for deoxygenated blood.
- At **940 nm**, HbO₂ absorbs strongly while HHb absorbs weakly → high contrast for oxygenated blood.
- These wavelengths lie on either side of the **isosbestic point (~805 nm)** where both species absorb equally, maximizing the differential signal $R$.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice Rule:** In Part B, students answer **ONE full question** (either Choice A or Choice B). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Both sub-parts must be answered completely** — skipping one sub-part typically costs 5–7 marks (a full letter grade).

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a pulse oximeter. Describe the function of each block.**

**Model Solution:**

A pulse oximeter consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Probe with Dual LEDs (Red 660 nm + IR 940 nm):**
   Emits time-multiplexed light pulses through the fingertip.

2. **Photodetector (Silicon PIN Diode):**
   Detects transmitted light and converts optical intensity into a small electrical current (~µA level).

3. **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA):**
   Converts the photodetector current into a proportional voltage with high gain.

4. **Bandpass Filter (0.5 – 5 Hz):**
   Removes DC offsets and high-frequency noise (mains interference, ambient light), passing only the pulsatile PPG band.

5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC, 12-bit, ≥100 Hz sampling):**
   Digitizes the analog PPG signal for digital processing.

6. **Microcontroller / DSP:**
   Performs AC/DC separation, Ratio of Ratios ($R$) computation, and SpO₂ calibration via lookup table.

7. **Display (LCD/OLED):**
   Shows SpO₂ (%) and Pulse Rate (bpm) numerically and as a PPG waveform.

8. **Buzzer / Alarm:**
   Alerts the clinician when SpO₂ < 90% or pulse rate is out of range.

**Working Summary:** The device illuminates the fingertip alternately with Red and IR light, measures the transmitted intensity, separates the pulsatile (AC) and steady (DC) components, computes $R = (AC_{660}/DC_{660})/(AC_{940}/DC_{940})$, and maps $R$ to SpO₂ through an empirical calibration curve stored in firmware. The same PPG waveform is used to compute heart rate via peak-to-peak interval measurement.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for the "Ratio of Ratios" $R$ starting from Beer–Lambert's Law. Explain how $R$ is used to compute SpO₂.**

**Model Solution:**

Apply Beer–Lambert's Law at the two wavelengths:

$$A_{660} = \log_{10}(I_0^{660}/I^{660}) = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} c_{HbO_2} l + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} c_{HHb} l$$

$$A_{940} = \log_{10}(I_0^{940}/I^{940}) = \varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} c_{HbO_2} l + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} c_{HHb} l$$

The **pulsatile change** in absorbance (AC component) corresponds to arterial blood volume change $\Delta l$. Taking the ratio of AC absorbances cancels $\Delta l$:

$$\frac{\Delta A_{660}}{\Delta A_{940}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{660} c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{660} c_{HHb}}{\varepsilon_{HbO_2}^{940} c_{HbO_2} + \varepsilon_{HHb}^{940} c_{HHb}}$$

Normalizing each by its DC component (baseline tissue absorption) yields the **Ratio of Ratios**:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}}$$

$R$ is mapped to SpO₂ through the empirical calibration:

$$\boxed{\text{SpO}_2 \;[\%] = 110 - 25 \cdot R}$$

Lower $R$ ⇒ higher SpO₂; Higher $R$ ⇒ lower SpO₂. The dimensionless nature of $R$ makes the measurement robust against variations in skin color, tissue thickness, and LED intensity drift.

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the principle of photoplethysmography (PPG). How is the PPG signal used to compute pulse rate and SpO₂ in a pulse oximeter?**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of PPG:**
Photoplethysmography (PPG) is an optical technique that detects **changes in blood volume** in microvascular tissue (typically the fingertip) by illuminating the skin with light and measuring the transmitted or reflected intensity. As arterial blood volume pulsates with each heartbeat, the light absorption changes, producing a time-varying PPG waveform with a clear pulsatile (AC) and steady (DC) component.

**Use of PPG for Pulse Rate:**
- The PPG waveform contains a clear systolic peak for each heartbeat.
- Peak detection algorithms identify successive peak times $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$.
- The instantaneous heart rate is:
$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{\Delta t_{peak-peak}} \; \text{[bpm]}$$
- Averaging over multiple peaks provides a stable display value.

**Use of PPG for SpO₂:**
- Two PPG signals are recorded simultaneously — one at **Red (660 nm)** and one at **IR (940 nm)**.
- The AC and DC components of each are extracted.
- The **Ratio of Ratios** $R$ is computed and mapped to SpO₂ via empirical calibration:
$$\text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PPG signal is **doubly informative** — it yields **both** heart rate (from periodicity) and oxygen saturation (from dual-wavelength amplitude analysis).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the sources of error in pulse oximetry. How do modern pulse oximeters overcome motion artifact and low perfusion problems?**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Error:**

1. **Motion Artifact:** Patient movement causes ambient light changes and probe displacement, contaminating the AC component.
2. **Low Peripheral Perfusion:** Cold extremities or shock reduce PPG amplitude, lowering signal-to-noise ratio.
3. **Dysfunctional Hemoglobins:** Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) and methemoglobin (MetHb) absorb at similar wavelengths, causing overestimation of SpO₂.
4. **Skin Pigmentation:** High melanin content slightly alters light absorption, causing minor inaccuracies.
5. **Nail Polish / Artificial Nails:** Block red light transmission, especially at 660 nm.
6. **Ambient Light:** Sunlight or surgical lamps introduce noise into the photodetector.
7. **Venous Pulsation:** Non-arterial pulsations (e.g., venous) may inflate the AC component.
8. **Probe Misplacement:** Misalignment between LEDs and photodetector reduces signal.

**Modern Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Signal Extraction Technology (SET) — Masimo Algorithm:**
   Uses parallel processing with **adaptive filters** and a **discrete saturation transform** to isolate the arterial PPG signal from noise.
2. **Motion-Resistant Algorithms:** Nellcor OxiMax uses **pattern recognition** to identify and discard motion-corrupted beats.
3. **Accelerometer-Based Noise Cancellation:** Built-in 3-axis accelerometers detect motion; the algorithm adaptively cancels motion-correlated noise.
4. **Multi-Wavelength Sensors (8+ LEDs):** Distinguish COHb, MetHb, and total hemoglobin, providing more accurate readings.
5. **Reflectance-Mode Probes:** Used on forehead or ear where perfusion is better than fingertips.
6. **Synchronous Detection:** LEDs modulated at a unique frequency; the receiver locks onto this frequency, rejecting ambient light.
7. **Signal Quality Index (SQI):** Real-time indicator shown on display to warn the clinician of unreliable readings.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pulse Oximeter Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Forgetting to state BOTH wavelengths with their values** — Writing only "red and infrared" without **660 nm** and **940 nm** loses **1 mark** instantly. (Frequently seen in Part A 3-mark questions.)
>
> 2. **❌ Confusing SaO₂ with SpO₂** — SaO₂ is measured by **invasive arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis**; SpO₂ is measured by **pulse oximetry (non-invasive, peripheral)**. The "p" and "a" subscripts matter and examiners check them.
>
> 3. **❌ Writing the Ratio of Ratios with incorrect normalization** — Many students incorrectly write $R = AC_{660}/AC_{940}$ instead of $R = (AC_{660}/DC_{660})/(AC_{940}/DC_{940})$. The DC normalization is **mandatory** and worth **2 marks** in derivations.
>
> 4. **❌ Stating the Beer–Lambert equation incorrectly** — Writing $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ without specifying that this is the **natural log form** (different from the base-10 form used in absorbance) confuses examiners. Always write $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$.
>
> 5. **❌ Skipping the AC/DC explanation** — In Part B derivations, you MUST explain what AC (pulsatile arterial blood) and DC (steady tissue, venous blood) represent physically. Skipping this costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 6. **❌ Not mentioning isosbestic point** — When justifying the choice of 660/940 nm, mentioning the **isosbestic point ~805 nm** earns a bonus **1 mark** and signals deep understanding.
>
> 7. **❌ Omitting IoT context** — Since this is PCBRT402 (IoT & Biomedical Applications), at least one sentence connecting the pulse oximeter to **IoT/cloud/wearable** architecture is expected in Part B for full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This!)**

- ✅ A **pulse oximeter** measures **SpO₂** (oxygen saturation) and **pulse rate** **non-invasively** using **two wavelengths**: **660 nm (Red)** and **940 nm (IR)**.
- ✅ It works on the principle of **spectrophotometry** + **photoplethysmography (PPG)**.
- ✅ **Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)** absorbs more **IR**; **Deoxyhemoglobin (HHb)** absorbs more **Red**.
- ✅ **Beer–Lambert Law:** $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = \varepsilon c l$ — the foundation of optical biosensing.
- ✅ **PPG waveform** has two components: **AC** (pulsatile, arterial) and **DC** (steady, tissue/venous).
- ✅ **Ratio of Ratios:** $R = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}}$ — dimensionless, robust against drift.
- ✅ **Empirical SpO₂ formula:** $\text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25 \cdot R$ — lower $R$ ⇒ higher SpO₂.
- ✅ **Normal SpO₂:** **95–100%**; **Hypoxemia:** **< 90%**.
- ✅ **Heart rate** is computed from **peak-to-peak interval** in the PPG waveform: $\text{HR} = 60/\Delta t$.
- ✅ **Two wavelengths are needed** because there are **two unknowns** (HbO₂ and HHb concentrations).
- ✅ **Isosbestic point** of hemoglobin is at **~805 nm** — wavelengths are chosen on either side of it.
- ✅ **Modern error mitigation** uses Masimo SET, accelerometer-based motion rejection, and multi-wavelength sensors.
- ✅ **IoT integration** is achieved via **BLE → Gateway → Cloud → Dashboard** architecture (relevant for PCBRT402).
- ✅ **Reflectance vs Transmittance probes:** Transmittance used on fingertips; Reflectance used on forehead/wrist (wrist wearables).
- ✅ **Dysfunctional hemoglobins** (COHb, MetHb) cause oximeter errors — co-oximetry is the gold-standard reference.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
