# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Protocol Standardization in IoT. List any three standardization bodies involved in IoT.**

**Model Answer:**
Protocol Standardization in IoT is the process of defining and adopting common communication rules, message formats, and interoperability procedures that allow heterogeneous devices to exchange data seamlessly across different networks. It ensures that devices from different manufacturers can communicate without custom translation layers.

The three standardization bodies are:

1. **IETF** (Internet Engineering Task Force) — Defines core IoT protocols such as **CoAP, MQTT, 6LoWPAN, and RPL**.
2. **IEEE** — Defines physical and MAC layer standards such as **IEEE 802.15.4** (used by Zigbee, Thread, 6LoWPAN) and **IEEE 11073** for medical device communication.
3. **W3C** (World Wide Web Consortium) — Defines the **Web of Things (WoT)** architecture and **Thing Description (TD)** for semantic interoperability.

*(Other acceptable answers: ITU-T, ETSI, 3GPP, oneM2M, OCF, OASIS, OMA.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Compare MQTT and CoAP with respect to transport, message header size, and Quality of Service mechanisms.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683/5684) |
| **Header Size** | 2-byte minimum fixed header + variable | 4-byte fixed header + token + options |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe with broker | Request/Response (HTTP-like) |
| **QoS Mechanism** | 3 levels: QoS 0, 1, 2 | CON, NON, ACK, RST message types + Observe |
| **Best Suited For** | Reliable telemetry, alarms, fan-out | Constrained devices, multicast, low-power |

MQTT uses a **centralized broker** for message distribution, while CoAP uses a **direct request/response** model similar to HTTP. MQTT's **QoS 2** provides exactly-once delivery via a 4-way handshake, whereas CoAP provides reliability at the message level via **Confirmable (CON)** messages with retransmission. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 2 carries a 14-mark question. The student has an **internal choice** between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of MQTT with a neat diagram. Discuss all three Quality of Service levels in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**MQTT Architecture:**

MQTT follows the **Publish/Subscribe** pattern and uses a central **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX). The architecture has three components:

1. **Publisher** — A sensor or device that publishes messages to the broker on a specific **topic** (e.g., `hospital/icu/bed12/hr`).
2. **Broker** — A server that receives all messages, filters them by topic, and distributes them to interested subscribers. The broker maintains a **topic tree** and a **subscription list**.
3. **Subscriber** — A client (dashboard, EHR server, mobile app) that subscribes to one or more topics and receives matching messages.

The broker is the **decoupling point** — publishers and subscribers do not need to know each other's IP addresses, only the topic name and broker endpoint.

**Diagrammatic representation (text):**

```
PUBLISHER 1 (ECG)   PUBLISHER 2 (BP)   PUBLISHER 3 (SpO2)
      \                  |                  /
       \                 |                 /
        -------> MQTT BROKER <-------
                       |
        -----------------------------
        |              |             |
   SUBSCRIBER 1    SUBSCRIBER 2   SUBSCRIBER 3
   (Dashboard)     (EHR Server)   (Mobile App)
```

**Quality of Service (QoS) Levels:**

**QoS 0 — At Most Once:**
- Fire-and-forget. Publisher sends a PUBLISH packet with QoS = 0.
- The broker does not send an acknowledgment.
- No retransmission logic. Message may be lost.
- Use case: non-critical, high-frequency telemetry where occasional loss is acceptable.

**QoS 1 — At Least Once:**
- Publisher sends PUBLISH with QoS = 1 and a **Packet ID**.
- Broker responds with **PUBACK** (Publish Acknowledgment).
- If the PUBACK is not received within a timeout, the publisher retransmits (DUP = 1).
- Possible duplicate delivery at the subscriber.
- Use case: vital signs telemetry where duplicate is harmless but loss is not.

**QoS 2 — Exactly Once:**
- Four-way handshake ensures the message is delivered **exactly once**:
  1. PUBLISH (QoS = 2, PacketID = P)
  2. PUBREC (PacketID = P) — broker acknowledges receipt.
  3. PUBREL (PacketID = P) — publisher releases the message for delivery.
  4. PUBCOMP (PacketID = P) — broker confirms delivery complete.
- The broker uses state tracking to discard duplicate PUBLISH packets and re-send the appropriate ACK (PUBREC or PUBCOMP) based on its current state.
- Use case: critical alarms (e.g., asystole, ventilator disconnect) where duplicates could cause confusion and loss is unacceptable. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** What is 6LoWPAN? Explain its role in the IoT protocol stack. Derive the worst-case and best-case header compression ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks) is an **IETF standard (RFC 4944, updated by RFC 6282)** that enables **IPv6 packets to be transmitted over IEEE 802.15.4** networks. It performs **header compression, fragmentation, and adaptation** between IPv6 (large, 128-bit addresses) and IEEE 802.15.4 (small, 81-byte MAC payload).

**Role in the IoT Protocol Stack:**
6LoWPAN sits between the **Network Layer (IPv6)** and the **Link Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)**. It enables low-power, low-rate wireless devices to participate in the **global IPv6 Internet** without requiring IPv4 or higher-overhead networking stacks. It is the **enabling layer** for IoT devices to use the same addressing scheme as the rest of the Internet.

**Derivation — Worst-Case Header Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{6LoWPAN, max}} &= L_{\text{dispatch}} + L_{\text{IPHC}} + L_{\text{inline-TC-FL}} + L_{\text{inline-NH}} + L_{\text{inline-HL}} + L_{\text{Src-full}} + L_{\text{Dst-full}} \\
&= 1 + 1 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 16 + 16 \\
&= 40 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation — Best-Case Header Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{6LoWPAN, min}} &= L_{\text{dispatch}} + L_{\text{IPHC}} + L_{\text{inline-NH}} \\
&= 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 3 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compression Ratios:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{c, \text{min}} &= \frac{L_{\text{IPv6}}}{L_{\text{6LoWPAN, max}}} = \frac{40}{40} = 1.00 \\
R_{c, \text{max}} &= \frac{L_{\text{IPv6}}}{L_{\text{6LoWPAN, min}}} = \frac{40}{3} \approx 13.33
\end{aligned}
$$

In a typical mesh deployment with link-local addresses, the practical compression ratio is **8 to 13**, which is the primary reason 6LoWPAN is the de-facto network adaptation layer for IoT. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) message format. Explain the Confirmable, Non-confirmable, Acknowledgement, and Reset message types. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**CoAP Message Format (RFC 7252):**
A CoAP message consists of:

1. **Fixed Header (4 bytes):**
   - **Ver (Version)** — 2 bits — Set to 1.
   - **T (Type)** — 2 bits — CON = 0, NON = 1, ACK = 2, RST = 3.
   - **TKL (Token Length)** — 4 bits — 0 to 8 bytes.
   - **Code** — 8 bits — Class (3 bits) + Detail (5 bits); e.g., `0.01` = GET, `0.02` = POST, `2.05` = Content.
   - **Message ID** — 16 bits — Used for matching ACK/RST to CON/NON, and for duplicate detection.

2. **Token (0 to 8 bytes):** Opaque identifier for correlating request/response.

3. **Options (variable):** URI-Path, Uri-Host, Content-Format, Observe, etc. Each option is Type-Length-Value (TLV) encoded.

4. **Payload (optional):** Prefixed by a 1-byte payload marker `0xFF`.

**Message Types:**

- **CON (Confirmable):** A CON message requires an ACK from the receiver. Used when reliable delivery is required. If the ACK is not received, the sender retransmits with exponential backoff (default 4 retransmissions).

- **NON (Non-confirmable):** A NON message is fire-and-forget. No ACK is expected. Used for periodic readings where the next reading supersedes the previous (e.g., temperature updates every 30 seconds).

- **ACK (Acknowledgement):** A positive acknowledgment of a received CON message. Contains the same Message ID as the CON.

- **RST (Reset):** Sent when the receiver cannot process the message (e.g., context missing, server restart). Indicates "I have received this Message ID but I cannot process the request; please stop retransmitting."

This lightweight reliability model allows CoAP to provide TCP-like guarantees over UDP with minimal overhead. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the W3C Web of Things (WoT) architecture. How does the Thing Description (TD) enable semantic interoperability in biomedical IoT? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**W3C Web of Things Architecture:**

The W3C WoT architecture (W3C Recommendation 2020) decouples **IoT devices** from **applications** using Web standards. The architecture consists of four key components:

1. **Thing:** A physical or virtual entity whose state and capabilities are exposed via a **WoT Interface**.

2. **WoT Thing Description (TD):** A structured JSON-LD document that describes a Thing's metadata, properties, actions, events, security schemes, and protocol bindings (e.g., CoAP, HTTP, MQTT).

3. **WoT Servient:** A runtime that can act as a Thing (expose TD) or a Consumer (consume TD). Servients are the software components embedded in devices, gateways, or cloud services.

4. **WoT Scripting API:** A JavaScript-like API for servients to interact with TDs and underlying protocols uniformly.

**Diagrammatic Representation (text):**

```
+-------------------+        +---------------------+
|  WoT Servient 1   |        |   WoT Servient 2    |
|  (Sensor Device)  |        |   (Cloud Consumer)  |
|                   |        |                     |
|  Exposes TD JSON  | -----> |  Reads TD, Discovers|
|  via HTTP / CoAP  |        |  Properties/Actions |
+-------------------+        +---------------------+
        |                              |
        v                              v
   (Sensor Data)               (Sends Commands)
```

**Role of Thing Description in Semantic Interoperability:**

The TD uses **JSON-LD** (a JSON-based serialization of Linked Data) which embeds **semantic annotations** using standard vocabularies (e.g., **SAREF** for smart appliances, **SSN/SOSA** for sensors, **FHIR** for healthcare).

In **biomedical IoT**, this enables:
- A pulse oximeter from **Vendor A** exposes a property `spo2` with semantic type `saref:Measurement` and unit `unit:PERCENT`.
- An EHR consumer from **Vendor B** automatically understands the meaning, unit, and validity period of the data without custom code.
- New devices can be **discovered** (via `/.well-known/wot` URI) and **integrated automatically** into the hospital's monitoring system.

Thus, the TD acts as a **"digital passport"** for the device, providing a standardized, machine-readable description that bridges vendor-specific implementations and the open Web ecosystem. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Protocol Standardization Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MQTT and CoAP transport:** Many students incorrectly state that CoAP runs over TCP. **Correct: CoAP is UDP-based (RFC 7252)**. MQTT is TCP-based. Mixing these up costs 1 mark immediately.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the broker in MQTT architecture:** When asked to draw the MQTT architecture, students often show direct device-to-server communication, missing the **central broker** — which is the heart of the publish/subscribe model. Loss: 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing QoS levels of MQTT with CoAP message types:** MQTT has 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2). CoAP has 4 message types (CON, NON, ACK, RST). Do not interchange them.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state units and quantitative numbers:** Examiners reward answers that mention specific byte counts (e.g., "CoAP 4-byte header", "MQTT 2-byte fixed header") and standards (RFC numbers, IEEE 802.15.4). Vague answers like "lightweight protocol" lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A neatly labelled diagram of the MQTT pub/sub model or CoAP message format is **mandatory** and typically carries 2–3 marks. Skipping it is the single biggest avoidable loss.
>
> 6. **Misattributing standards to wrong bodies:** W3C does NOT define CoAP (that is IETF). IEEE does NOT define MQTT (that is OASIS/ISO). Examiners test this specifically.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Protocol Standardization for IoT**

- **Protocol Standardization** = unified rules for IoT device communication → ensures **interoperability, security, scalability**.

- **5-Layer IoT Stack:** Perception → MAC/Link → Network (6LoWPAN, RPL) → Transport (TCP/UDP) → Application (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP).

- **Key Standardization Bodies:** IETF, IEEE, ITU-T, ETSI, W3C, OCF, oneM2M, 3GPP, OASIS, OMA.

- **MQTT** — TCP, port 1883/8883, pub/sub, **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2), central broker, low overhead (~2-byte fixed header).

- **CoAP** — UDP, port 5683/5684, request/response (HTTP-like), **4 message types** (CON, NON, ACK, RST), **4-byte header**, supports Observe pattern.

- **6LoWPAN** — IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4, header compression ratio **1:1 to 13.33:1**, RFC 4944 / RFC 6282.

- **RPL** — Routing for Low-Power Lossy Networks (LLNs), RFC 6550, uses **DODAG** (Destination Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph).

- **AMQP** — ISO/IEC 19464, transactional messaging, zero-loss guarantee, broker-based.

- **DDS** — OMG, real-time pub/sub, no broker, **deadline and liveliness QoS**, used in surgical robots and ICU networks.

- **LwM2M** — OMA SpecWorks, device management + telemetry, runs over CoAP, popular with NB-IoT/LTE-M.

- **OPC-UA** — IEC 62541, industrial + medical (MRI, lab analyzers), information modeling, end-to-end secure.

- **W3C WoT** — Web of Things, **Thing Description (TD)** in JSON-LD, semantic interoperability, uses vocabularies like SAREF, SSN/SOSA, FHIR.

- **HL7 FHIR** — Healthcare-specific, RESTful API, JSON/XML, mandatory for EHR integration in many regions.

- **IEEE 11073** — Medical device communication, personal health devices, point-of-care.

- **DICOM** — Medical imaging (CT, MRI, X-ray) communication standard.

- **Header Compression Math:** $L_{\text{6LoWPAN}} = L_{\text{dispatch}} + L_{\text{IPHC}} + L_{\text{inline-fields}}$.

- **Duty Cycle Target:** $D < 1\%$ for multi-year battery life on constrained nodes.

- **Biomedical Protocol Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - *Periodic vitals* → MQTT QoS 1
  - *Wearable low-power* → CoAP NON
  - *Critical alarms* → MQTT QoS 2
  - *Real-time control* → DDS
  - *EHR integration* → HL7 FHIR over HTTP
  - *Imaging equipment* → DICOM
  - *Semantic integration* → W3C WoT TD

- **Golden Mnemonic for IoT Stack (top to bottom):** **"A-T-N-L-P"** → **A**pplication, **T**ransport, **N**etwork, **L**ink, **P**hysical.

- **Exam Tip:** Always cite the **RFC number** (e.g., RFC 7252 for CoAP, RFC 4944 for 6LoWPAN, RFC 6550 for RPL) — it shows examiner-readiness and earns bonus marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efforts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efforts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **biomedical instrumentation and IoT-based physiological monitoring**, the term **"Effort"** refers to the **physiological work performed by the body’s muscular system to execute a vital function** — most commonly the mechanical work done by the **respiratory muscles (diaphragm and intercostals)** to expand the thoracic cavity and drive airflow into/out of the lungs. In a broader sense, it also encompasses the **exertion produced by skeletal muscles (physical effort)** and the **pumping action of cardiac muscle (cardiac effort)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBRT402 — Module 2):**
> *Effort* is the quantifiable biomechanical or bioelectrical manifestation of muscular work expended by the human body, captured through transducers (effort belts, piezoelectric bands, RIP, EMG, impedance pneumograph) and processed to yield clinically meaningful parameters such as **respiratory rate, tidal volume, work of breathing, and perceived exertion**.

Mathematically, physiological **Effort (E)** is expressed as the product of the **force (F)** generated by the muscle and the **displacement (d)** through which that force is applied:

$$
E = F \times d
$$

For respiratory effort, this translates to the **pressure–volume work** done by the respiratory muscles:

$$
W_{resp} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{mus} \, dV
$$

where $P_{mus}$ is the **muscular pressure** generated by respiratory muscles and $V$ is the lung volume change.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Balloon-in-a-Box Experiment"**

Imagine a **balloon sealed inside a rigid box**, with a small pipe connecting the balloon to the outside. To inflate the balloon, you must pull the box walls apart (expand the cavity). The **pulling force** is the *effort*, and the **expansion of the balloon** is the *result (tidal volume)*.

- When you pull the walls apart **gently** → low effort → shallow breathing.
- When you pull the walls apart **forcefully** → high effort → deep, labored breathing.
- A **sensor wrapped around the box walls** that detects how far they move is essentially a **respiratory effort belt** — it does not measure airflow directly; it measures the **mechanical effort** the body puts in to breathe.

In the same way, when a doctor straps an **effort belt** around your chest while you sleep, they are not measuring air entering your nose — they are measuring **how hard your chest and abdominal muscles are working** to breathe. This is *crucial* for diagnosing **Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)** versus **Central Sleep Apnea (CSA)** — both involve paused airflow, but only one shows ongoing (and rising) effort.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Adult Value |
|---|---|---|
| Respiratory Rate | RR | **12 – 20 breaths/min** |
| Tidal Volume | $V_T$ | **500 mL** (at rest) |
| Work of Breathing | $W_{OB}$ | **0.5 J/L** (at rest) |
| Inspiratory Muscle Pressure | $P_{mus}$ | **5 – 10 cmH₂O** (quiet breathing) |
| Borg RPE Scale | RPE | **6 (no exertion) – 20 (maximal exertion)** |
| Heart Rate Reserve | HRR | **HRmax − HRrest** |
| Karvonen Target HR | THR | **60–80 % of HRR** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Borg Scale (6–20)** and the **Karvonen Formula** are *guaranteed high-yield* items in KTU viva and 3-mark questions for this module.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Physiological Effort

Effort in biomedical applications is classified into **three principal domains**:

1. **Respiratory Effort** — effort of breathing muscles (diaphragm, external intercostals).
2. **Cardiac Effort** — work performed by the myocardium (Stroke Work × Heart Rate).
3. **Skeletal/Muscular Effort** — voluntary physical exertion (measured via EMG, RPE, accelerometry).

> [!NOTE]
> In **IoT-based wearable devices**, all three are continuously monitored using embedded biosensors, with data transmitted via **BLE / Wi-Fi** to cloud platforms for analytics — the central theme of PCBRT402.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physiological Basis of Effort

### 2.1.1 The Respiratory Pump as a Mechanical System

The lungs and chest wall form a **two-compartment elastic system**. During inspiration:

1. The **diaphragm contracts** (innervated by the phrenic nerve, C3–C5).
2. **Intrapleural pressure** drops from approximately **−5 cmH₂O** to **−8 cmH₂O**.
3. **Alveolar pressure** falls below atmospheric pressure → air flows in.
4. The **rib cage expands** (pump-handle and bucket-handle motions).

The **effort** is therefore quantifiable in **two independent but coupled ways**:

- **Mechanically** — by tracking chest wall circumference change (RIP belts, piezoelectric bands).
- **Electrically** — by recording the EMG activity of respiratory muscles.

### 2.1.2 Effort vs. Flow — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effort ≠ Airflow.** This is the *single most important concept* in sleep-medicine instrumentation.

| Parameter | What it Measures | Sensor Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Effort** | Muscular work / chest expansion | RIP belt, piezo band, EMG |
| **Flow** | Actual air movement at nose/mouth | Nasal cannula, pneumotachograph |
| **Snore** | Vibration of upper airway | Microphone, piezo snore sensor |

A patient can have **zero flow** (apnea) but **high, rising effort** (obstructive event) — this is the hallmark of **OSA**.

---

## 2.2 Sensing Modalities for Effort

### 2.2.1 Respiratory Inductive Plethysmography (RIP)

- An **insulated sinusoidal wire** is sewn into an elastic band around the chest/abdomen.
- Cross-sectional area **A** of the band forms a single-loop coil of inductance:

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}
$$

- As the patient breathes, **A changes** (chest expands/contracts) → **L changes** → frequency of an **LC oscillator** shifts.
- The change in inductance is linearly proportional to lung volume change.

### 2.2.2 Piezoelectric Effort Belts

- A **piezoelectric polymer (PVDF)** or ceramic element is embedded in a band.
- Mechanical strain $\epsilon$ on the band produces a charge:

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \epsilon
$$

where $d_{33}$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** and $k$ is the band stiffness.

- Output voltage:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_p}
$$

### 2.2.3 Impedance Pneumography

- Two surface electrodes inject a **high-frequency (50–100 kHz), low-amplitude (1–5 mA) AC current** across the thorax.
- The **thoracic impedance** $Z(t)$ modulates with respiration:

$$
Z(t) = \frac{V(t)}{I_{AC}}
$$

- Respiration causes $\Delta Z$ of approximately **0.1 – 1 Ω** superimposed on the baseline impedance of **~500 Ω** (at 50 kHz).

### 2.2.4 Surface EMG (sEMG) for Effort Quantification

- The **RMS amplitude** of the EMG signal is proportional to muscular effort (muscle force):

$$
EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T [s(t)]^2 \, dt}
$$

- This gives a direct, **calibrated** index of muscle recruitment → effort level.

### 2.2.5 Borg's Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE)

- A **psychophysical** scale (6 to 20) where the subject subjectively rates their effort.
- **RPE × 10 ≈ Heart Rate** (a useful clinical rule of thumb).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it is the single fastest revision tool for Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = F \times d$ | Mechanical effort (work) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $W_{resp} = \int P_{mus} \, dV$ | Respiratory work of breathing | J or J/L |
| 3 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$ | Inductance of RIP coil loop | Henry (H) |
| 4 | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge generation | Coulombs (C) |
| 5 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p}$ | Piezo sensor output voltage | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $Z(t) = \dfrac{V(t)}{I_{AC}}$ | Thoracic impedance (impedance pneumography) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $EMG_{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T s^2(t) \, dt}$ | Muscle effort from sEMG | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $THR = \left[ (HR_{max} - HR_{rest}) \times \%\,intensity \right] + HR_{rest}$ | Karvonen target heart rate | bpm |
| 9 | $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{age}$ | Maximum predicted HR (Tanaka correction: 208 − 0.7×age) | bpm |
| 10 | $RPE \times 10 \approx HR$ | Borg scale approximation | bpm |
| 11 | $P_{mus} = P_{ao} - P_{mus,relax}$ | Muscle pressure (Campbell diagram) | cmH₂O |
| 12 | $P_{aw} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot R_{aw}}{1} $ | Airway pressure-flow relation | cmH₂O |
| 13 | $V_T = V_D + V_A$ | Tidal volume = dead-space + alveolar | mL |
| 14 | $\dot{V}_E = V_T \times f$ | Minute ventilation | L/min |
| 15 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_0} \cdot k$ | Impedance pneumograph volume estimate | L/s |

> **Memory Aid:** *"Effort is Force × Displacement; Work of Breathing is Pressure × Volume."*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Sensor Used |
|---|---|---|
| Sleep Medicine (PSG) | OSA vs CSA differentiation | RIP belts, piezo effort |
| ICU Ventilator Monitoring | Patient–ventilator synchrony | Impedance pneumography |
| Wearable Fitness Trackers | Effort-based calorie estimation | RIP + accelerometer + HR |
| Rehabilitation | Effort progression tracking | sEMG, Borg RPE |
| Sports Physiology | Training zone prescription | Karvonen THR via HR strap |
| IoT Smart Beds | Apnea alarming for infants | piezo band + cloud analytics |
| Astronaut Health (NASA) | Effort telemetry in microgravity | RIP, EMG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **IoT biomedical systems**, the effort signal is one of the **four canonical physiological streams** (along with ECG, SpO₂, and temperature) that form the backbone of any wearable patient-monitoring platform.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance Change in a Respiratory Inductive Plethysmograph (RIP) Belt

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A sinusoidal wire is coiled around a flexible band. When the patient inhales, the chest circumference increases by $\Delta C$, and the cross-sectional area of the band loop changes by $\Delta A$. Derive the relationship between the change in inductance and the change in chest volume.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Starting Equation (Inductance of a single-turn loop):**

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, A}{l}
$$

where $N$ is the number of turns, $A$ is the enclosed area, and $l$ is the magnetic path length (approximately the band circumference for a tightly-wound band).

**Step 2 — Differentiate L with respect to A** (assuming $N$, $l$, $\mu_0$ are constant):

$$
\frac{dL}{dA} = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2}{l}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the differential change in inductance:**

$$
dL = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2}{l} \, dA
$$

**Step 4 — Relate area change to chest circumference change.** For a roughly elliptical chest cross-section, the area is:

$$
A \approx \frac{\pi \, a \, b}{4}
$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the major and minor axes. For small changes, $dA$ is proportional to $dC$ (circumference change). Empirically:

$$
dA = k_{cal} \, dC
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\Delta L = \frac{\mu_0 \, N^2 \, k_{cal}}{l} \cdot \Delta C
$$

**Step 6 — Relate circumference change to lung volume change.** From the **Konno–Mead two-compartment model** of the chest wall, lung volume is partitioned into **rib-cage ($V_{rc}$)** and **abdominal ($V_{ab}$)** contributions:

$$
V_L = \alpha \, V_{rc} + \beta \, V_{ab}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **isovolume calibration coefficients** determined by an **isovolume maneuver** (chest and abdomen move out of phase).

**Step 7 — Final linearized form:**

$$
\Delta L = K \cdot \Delta V_L
$$

where $K$ is the **calibration constant** of the RIP belt (units: H/L). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoelectric Effort-Band Output Voltage

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A PVDF piezoelectric band of stiffness $k$ is stretched by a strain $\epsilon$ due to chest expansion. The piezoelectric charge coefficient is $d_{33}$ and the sensor capacitance is $C_p$. Derive the output voltage.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force generated by band deformation:**

$$
F = k \cdot \Delta x
$$

where $\Delta x = \epsilon \cdot L_0$ ($L_0$ is the original band length).

**Step 2 — Piezoelectric charge generated:**

$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \Delta x
$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage on the sensor capacitance:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \Delta x}{C_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of strain:**

$$
V_{out} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \epsilon \cdot L_0}{C_p}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the piezoelectric voltage constant $g_{33} = d_{33}/(\epsilon_r \epsilon_0)$:**

$$
V_{out} = g_{33} \cdot \epsilon \cdot L_0 \cdot k / C_p'
$$

The key result: **the output voltage is directly proportional to the applied strain** (and hence to chest expansion, i.e., respiratory effort). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Karvonen Formula for Target Heart Rate (Exercise Effort Prescription)

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A 30-year-old patient has a resting heart rate of 70 bpm. The cardiologist prescribes aerobic exercise at **70 % intensity** of the Heart Rate Reserve (HRR). Derive the target heart rate (THR).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the predicted maximum heart rate (Tanaka formula, more accurate than 220−age):**

$$
HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \times \text{age}
$$

$$
HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \times 30 = 208 - 21 = 187 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Heart Rate Reserve (HRR):**

$$
HRR = HR_{max} - HR_{rest}
$$

$$
HRR = 187 - 70 = 117 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Target Heart Rate (THR) at 70 % intensity:**

$$
THR = \left( HRR \times \%\,intensity \right) + HR_{rest}
$$

$$
THR = \left( 117 \times 0.70 \right) + 70
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
THR = 81.9 + 70 = 151.9 \approx 152 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Final Answer:** The patient should exercise at a target HR of **152 bpm** to achieve 70 % aerobic effort. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Work of Breathing from Pressure–Volume Loop

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

The inspiratory muscles generate a pressure of $P_{mus}(t) = 5 + 2\sin(\omega t)$ cmH₂O during one breath, while lung volume changes by $V(t) = 0.5 + 0.3\sin(\omega t)$ L. Compute the work done per breath.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Work integral over one inspiration:**

$$
W_{OB} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{mus} \, dV
$$

**Step 2 — Express in parametric form** (assume $\omega$ is such that one full inspiration occurs as $t$ goes from 0 to $\pi/\omega$):

$$
V(t) = 0.5 + 0.3 \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = 0.3\omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
W_{OB} = \int_0^{\pi/\omega} P_{mus}(t) \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} \, dt
$$

$$
W_{OB} = \int_0^{\pi/\omega} \left( 5 + 2\sin(\omega t) \right) \left( 0.3\omega \cos(\omega t) \right) dt
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the integrand:**

$$
W_{OB} = 0.3\omega \int_0^{\pi/\omega} 5\cos(\omega t) \, dt + 0.3\omega \int_0^{\pi/\omega} 2\sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$
I_1 = 5 \int_0^{\pi/\omega} \cos(\omega t) \, dt = 5 \left[ \frac{\sin(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_0^{\pi/\omega} = \frac{5}{\omega} \left( \sin\pi - \sin 0 \right) = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second integral** using $\sin(2x) = 2\sin x \cos x$:

$$
I_2 = 2 \int_0^{\pi/\omega} \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) \, dt = \int_0^{\pi/\omega} \sin(2\omega t) \, dt = \left[ -\frac{\cos(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_0^{\pi/\omega}
$$

$$
I_2 = -\frac{1}{2\omega} \left[ \cos(2\pi) - \cos 0 \right] = -\frac{1}{2\omega} (1 - 1) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$
W_{OB} = 0.3\omega \left( 0 \cdot I_1 \right) + 0.3\omega \left( 0 \cdot I_2 \right) = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Physical Insight:** The integral evaluates to **zero** because, for a *sinusoidal* pressure and *sinusoidal* volume that are 90° out of phase (the case for purely elastic work), the area of the P–V loop is zero. In reality, breathing involves **hysteresis** (energy lost to tissue resistance), and the pressure–volume loop is *not* a closed line of zero area — it forms the **Campbell diagram** with a finite enclosed area = **real work of breathing**. For a resistive work scenario, the loop area is non-zero and represents $\sim 0.5$ J/L at rest. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Effort Sensing Technologies

| Parameter | RIP Belt | Piezo Band | Impedance Pneumograph | Surface EMG |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured Quantity** | Lung volume change | Strain / chest wall motion | Thoracic impedance change | Muscle electrical activity |
| **Output Signal** | Frequency shift | Voltage | Voltage (modulated) | Bipolar EMG waveform |
| **Linearity** | High (after calibration) | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Sensitivity to Motion Artifact** | Low | High | Moderate | High |
| **Calibration Required** | Yes (isovolume maneuver) | No (relative measure) | No | No |
| **Use in PSG** | Gold standard | Common | Limited (research) | Research / specific muscles |
| **IoT Integration Suitability** | High (compact, low-power) | Very High | High | Moderate (high sampling rate) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low | Low | High |
| **KTU Exam Likelihood** | **Very High** | High | High | Moderate |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IoT-Based Respiratory Effort Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Chest and Abdomen] --> B[Effort Sensor Layer]
    B --> B1[RIP Thoracic Band]
    B --> B2[RIP Abdominal Band]
    B --> B3[Piezo Effort Sensor]
    B1 --> C[Analog Front End]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    C1 --> C2[Bandpass Filter 0.05 to 15 Hz]
    C2 --> C3[ADC 16 bit 100 Hz]
    C3 --> D[Digital Signal Processing Unit]
    D --> D1[Baseline Drift Removal]
    D --> D2[RMS Energy Computation]
    D --> D3[Breath Detection Algorithm]
    D3 --> E[Feature Extraction]
    E --> E1[Respiratory Rate]
    E --> E2[Tidal Volume Estimate]
    E --> E3[Work of Breathing]
    E --> E4[Apnea Hypopnea Index]
    E1 --> F[IoT Communication Module]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[BLE Transmitter]
    F --> F2[WiFi Module]
    F1 --> G[Cloud Server]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Clinician Dashboard]
    G --> I[Mobile App Notification]
    G --> J[ML Based Apnea Classifier]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The patient's chest and abdomen expansion is captured by **three parallel effort sensors** (two RIP bands and one piezo sensor), conditioned by the **analog front-end**, digitized by a **16-bit ADC**, processed by the **DSP unit** to extract clinically relevant features, and finally transmitted to the **cloud** via **BLE / Wi-Fi** for clinician dashboards and ML-based apnea classification.

---

## 4.2 Effort Signal Acquisition — Detailed Sensor Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Diaphragm Contraction] --> A2[Thoracic Cavity Expansion]
    A2 --> A3[Chest Wall Circumference Increases]
    A3 --> A4[RIP Belt Area A increases]
    A4 --> A5[Inductance L increases]
    A5 --> A6[LC Oscillator Frequency f decreases]
    A6 --> A7[Frequency to Voltage Converter]
    A7 --> A8[Microcontroller ADC]
    A8 --> A9[Digital Effort Waveform]
```

---

## 4.3 Effort vs. Flow — Decision Flow in Sleep Apnea Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Acquire Flow and Effort Signals] --> S1{Airflow Present?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Normal Breath]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Effort Signal Present?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Obstructive Apnea Event]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Central Apnea Event]
    S4 --> S6[Flag Hypopnea Increment AHI]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Transmit to Cloud via IoT]
    S7 --> S8[Alert Clinician]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This decision tree is the heart of every PSG (Polysomnography) report and is a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU ESE.** The three branches correspond to the three clinical states: *Normal*, *Obstructive (effort present)*, *Central (no effort)*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define physiological effort. Mention any two sensing modalities used to measure respiratory effort.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam KTU 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Physiological **effort** is the **mechanical work performed by the body's muscular system** to execute a vital function such as breathing. In biomedical instrumentation, it most commonly refers to the **work done by the respiratory muscles (diaphragm and intercostals)** to expand the thoracic cavity.

Two sensing modalities for respiratory effort:

1. **Respiratory Inductive Plethysmography (RIP)** — measures change in thoracic/abdominal circumference via inductance change of a coiled band.
2. **Piezoelectric Effort Belt** — converts chest-wall strain into a voltage signal using a PVDF piezo element.

*(Equally acceptable: Impedance pneumography, Surface EMG of respiratory muscles.)*

---

### **Question 2: State the Karvonen formula for Target Heart Rate and explain the significance of HRR.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series Exam 1]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Karvonen formula** computes the prescribed target heart rate for a given exercise intensity:

$$
THR = \left[ \left( HR_{max} - HR_{rest} \right) \times \%\,intensity \right] + HR_{rest}
$$

**Heart Rate Reserve (HRR)** is the difference between maximum and resting heart rate and represents the **available cardiac workload capacity** for exercise. The Karvonen formula uses HRR to prescribe a **personalized training zone** based on the individual's fitness level — more accurate than a fixed % of $HR_{max}$ because it accounts for the resting baseline.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q.A.(a)** Explain the principle of **Respiratory Inductive Plethysmography (RIP)**. Derive the relationship between inductance change and lung volume change. Mention the role of the **isovolume calibration maneuver**. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
RIP is a non-invasive respiratory effort measurement technique that uses two elastic bands — one around the **thorax (RC band)** and one around the **abdomen (AB band)**. Each band contains a sinusoidally-wound insulated wire that forms a single-loop inductor. The cross-sectional area $A$ enclosed by the loop changes as the patient breathes, modulating the loop's inductance $L$.

The inductance of a single-turn loop is:

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}
$$

For small changes, $\Delta L \propto \Delta A \propto \Delta C$ (circumference change).

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Differentiating and substituting the proportionality $\Delta A = k_{cal} \Delta C$:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 \, k_{cal}}{l} \cdot \Delta C
$$

Lung volume $V_L$ is the sum of rib-cage and abdominal contributions (Konno–Mead two-compartment model):

$$
V_L = \alpha \cdot \Delta C_{RC} + \beta \cdot \Delta C_{AB}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the calibration coefficients.

**Isovolume Maneuver (2 marks):**
The **isovolume maneuver** is performed by having the patient shift air between the thorax and abdomen (mouth closed, using a small valve). The total lung volume remains constant while the RC and AB compartments exchange volume out of phase. By recording both band signals, the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are solved so that the **sum gives a constant volume** — yielding a calibrated, absolute volume waveform from RIP.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q.A.(b)** A 45-year-old male has $HR_{rest} = 72$ bpm. The cardiologist prescribes aerobic exercise at **75 % intensity** of HRR. Using the **Tanaka formula** for $HR_{max}$, calculate the **Target Heart Rate (THR)** and the **Karvonen-prescribed effort zone**. Comment on how an **IoT wearable HR strap** can monitor and enforce this zone. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $HR_{max}$ (Tanaka):**

$$
HR_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \times 45 = 208 - 31.5 = 176.5 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute HRR:**

$$
HRR = 176.5 - 72 = 104.5 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute THR at 75 % intensity:**

$$
THR = (0.75 \times 104.5) + 72 = 78.375 + 72 = 150.375 \approx 150 \; \text{bpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Effort zone:** The patient must maintain HR between **~145 bpm and ~155 bpm** (i.e., $\pm 5$ bpm around the THR) to remain in the prescribed 75 % effort zone.

**IoT Wearable Implementation (3 marks):**
A chest-strap **IoT HR monitor** (e.g., Polar H10) acquires ECG, detects R-peaks, and computes **instantaneous HR (IHR)** at 1 Hz. The IHR is transmitted via **BLE** to a smartphone app, where it is compared in real-time against the prescribed THR window. If IHR < lower bound, a **vibration/audio cue** prompts increased effort. If IHR > upper bound, a **rest cue** is issued. Session data is uploaded to a **cloud dashboard** for the cardiologist to review longitudinal compliance — a closed-loop IoT effort-monitoring ecosystem.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q.B.(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **impedance pneumography technique** for respiratory effort monitoring. Derive the expression relating thoracic impedance change to lung volume change. Mention its limitations compared to RIP. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Oscillator 50 kHz] → [Current Source I_AC 1–5 mA]
        ↓
[Surface Electrodes (2 pairs on thorax)]
        ↓
[Differential Amplifier] → [Bandpass Filter] → [Demodulator] → [ADC] → [DSP → RR, V_T]
```

**Principle (2 marks):**
A high-frequency (50–100 kHz), low-magnitude (1–5 mA) AC current is passed between two outer electrodes on the thorax. The voltage between the two inner electrodes is measured. The thoracic impedance $Z$ modulates with respiration because inspired air (a poor conductor) replaces conductive tissue in the lungs.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The baseline thoracic impedance is:

$$
Z_0 \approx 500 \; \Omega \quad (\text{at 50 kHz})
$$

Respiration modulates $Z$ by $\Delta Z \approx 0.1 - 1 \; \Omega$. The fractional change is:

$$
\frac{\Delta Z}{Z_0} = \frac{\Delta V_T}{V_{thoracic}}
$$

Hence:

$$
\Delta V_T = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_0} \cdot V_{thoracic}
$$

**Limitations vs RIP (1 mark):**
1. Highly susceptible to **cardiac artifact** (the heart's motion also changes thoracic impedance).
2. Sensitive to **electrode-skin contact impedance** and motion.
3. Cannot be used during **MRI** (RIP is MRI-conditional).
4. Difficult to separate rib-cage and abdominal contributions.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q.B.(b)** Explain the **Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale**. A patient reports an RPE of 14 during a treadmill stress test with concurrent HR of 145 bpm. Verify the **Borg × 10 ≈ HR rule**, and discuss how IoT-based **subjective-effort logging apps** complement objective biosensor data in cardiac rehabilitation. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Borg RPE Scale (2 marks):**
The Borg RPE is a **psychophysical scale from 6 to 20** (anchor points: 6 = no exertion at all, 20 = maximal exertion) developed by Gunnar Borg in 1970. It captures the **subjective perception of effort** during physical work, exercise, or rehabilitation.

**Verification of the rule (2 marks):**
Given RPE = 14 and observed HR = 145 bpm:

$$
RPE \times 10 = 14 \times 10 = 140 \; \text{bpm}
$$

Observed HR = 145 bpm.
Error: $\vert 145 - 140 \vert = 5$ bpm $\Rightarrow$ error = **3.4 %**.

The Borg × 10 ≈ HR rule is verified within clinically acceptable limits.

**IoT-Based Subjective Effort Logging (3 marks):**
1. **Mobile app** prompts the patient to log RPE at prescribed intervals (e.g., every 5 min during exercise).
2. Data is **time-stamped** and **geo-tagged**, then uploaded to a **cloud EHR**.
3. The cloud platform **fuses subjective RPE** with **objective biosensor streams** (HR from BLE chest strap, SpO₂, accelerometer step count) using ML models.
4. The fused "effort index" enables the **cardiologist to verify the patient's perceived exertion** matches the prescribed Karvonen zone — useful for patients on **beta-blockers** whose HR is pharmacologically suppressed and unreliable.
5. Real-time feedback loops: if RPE > 17 (very hard) but HR is in zone → **patient may be over-reporting**; if RPE < 11 but HR is high → **patient may be under-reporting**, both flagging for clinician review.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE for "Efforts" topic:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating "effort" with "flow"** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the answer does not clearly distinguish *muscular work* (effort) from *air movement* (flow).
> 2. **Forgetting the isovolume calibration** in RIP derivations — this is a **mandatory 2-mark step**; skipping it = −2 marks.
> 3. **Using the outdated 220 − age formula for $HR_{max}$** without mentioning Tanaka's correction (208 − 0.7×age) — KTU 2024 syllabus expects the *Tanaka* formula as the current standard.
> 4. **Not showing units** in final numerical answers (e.g., writing "152" without "bpm") — lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing $W_{OB}$ with $V_T \times P_{aw}$** — work of breathing is an **integral** ($\int P \, dV$), not a simple product.
> 6. **Omitting the IoT/block diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU 2024 scheme awards 2–3 marks for a **neatly labelled system-level block diagram**; always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density recap — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Effort** = mechanical work of muscles (mainly respiratory); quantified as $E = F \times d$ or $W_{resp} = \int P_{mus} \, dV$.
- **Effort ≠ Flow** — the cardinal distinction in sleep medicine.
- Three main effort sensors: **RIP, Piezo belt, Impedance pneumograph** (plus **sEMG** for direct muscle electrical activity).
- RIP uses **inductance change** $L = \mu_0 N^2 A / l$; calibration is via the **isovolume maneuver**; Konno–Mead two-compartment model applies.
- Piezo belt output: $V_{out} = d_{33} \cdot k \cdot \epsilon \cdot L_0 / C_p$.
- Impedance pneumography: $Z(t) = V(t)/I_{AC}$; $\Delta Z / Z_0 = \Delta V_T / V_{thoracic}$.
- **sEMG RMS** is a direct, calibrated index of muscle recruitment → effort.
- **Borg RPE** (6–20) is a psychophysical scale; the rule **RPE × 10 ≈ HR** is approximate.
- **Karvonen formula**: $THR = (HRR \times \%\,intensity) + HR_{rest}$; HRR = $HR_{max} - HR_{rest}$.
- **Tanaka formula** for $HR_{max}$: $208 - 0.7 \times \text{age}$ (preferred over 220 − age in 2024 syllabus).
- **Work of breathing** is read from the **Campbell P–V diagram** (area enclosed = work).
- Obstructive apnea: **flow absent, effort present (rising)**. Central apnea: **flow absent, effort absent**.
- IoT integration transmits effort signals via **BLE/Wi-Fi** to cloud for **AHI computation, ML apnea classification, and clinician dashboards**.
- Always draw a **neat block diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU rewards it with 2–3 marks.
- Final numerical answers **must carry units** (bpm, J/L, mL, cmH₂O).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: M2M and WSN Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** M2M and WSN Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Communication — Formal Definition

**Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Communication** refers to the direct, autonomous exchange of data between physical devices (sensors, actuators, embedded systems, smart meters, vehicles, industrial machines) over wired or wireless communication channels **without requiring direct human intervention**. M2M is considered the foundational substrate of the Internet of Things (IoT), where each "thing" possesses bidirectional intelligence to sense, process, and act upon information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** M2M is a broad label that can be used to describe any technology that enables networked devices to exchange information and perform actions without manual human assistance. In the IoT stack, M2M typically forms the *Perception/Edge* layer, where devices self-organize and report to a middleware or application server.

**Key Operational Metrics of M2M Systems:**
- **Scalability:** Support for **thousands to millions of devices** in a single deployment.
- **Heterogeneity:** Ability to integrate devices across **multiple vendors, protocols, and radio technologies**.
- **Autonomy:** Communication sessions triggered by **event, schedule, or query** without a human operator.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Devices may operate on **battery lifetimes of 5–10 years**.
- **Latency Tolerance:** Often ranges from **sub-millisecond (industrial control)** to **minutes (metering)**.

### 1.2 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) Protocols — Formal Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed network of autonomous, resource-constrained sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, humidity, bio-signals) and communicate wirelessly. **WSN Protocols** are the standardized rule-sets governing how these nodes perform *routing, medium access, synchronization, security, and data aggregation* across the network stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** WSN Protocols are typically organized into a **5-layer protocol stack** analogous to the TCP/IP model: *Physical → Data Link → Network → Transport → Application*, with additional *cross-layer* concerns such as *power management, mobility management, and task management*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Vitals Ward

Imagine a **neonatal ICU** where each incubator is fitted with a tiny wireless monitor recording an infant's heart rate, oxygen saturation, and temperature.

- **Each monitor** is a *sensor node* — a *Thing* in IoT.
- The monitors **talk to each other** (relay data when one loses signal) — this is the **M2M** communication.
- The **standardized language** the monitors use to relay alarms, share bandwidth, and synchronize their clocks is the **WSN Protocol** (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 at radio level, RPL for routing, CoAP for application queries).
- The **nurse's central dashboard** is the *sink/gateway*, and the doctor making decisions based on aggregated data is the *application layer*.

Just as nurses cannot afford monitors that drop readings, WSN protocols are designed to be **loss-tolerant, energy-aware, and self-healing**.

### 1.4 Relationship between M2M and WSN

| Aspect | M2M | WSN |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Generic device-to-device data exchange | Specialized network of sensor nodes |
| **Topology** | Often star or point-to-point | Mesh, cluster, or hybrid |
| **Data Volume** | Variable; can be high (video) | Usually low-bandwidth scalars |
| **Energy Profile** | Sometimes mains-powered | Strictly battery/energy-harvested |
| **Standards Body** | ETSI M2M, OneM2M | IEEE 802.15.4, IETF, ZigBee Alliance |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** M2M is a *conceptual paradigm*, while WSN is a *specific technology instance* of M2M. WSN protocols (ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RPL) are the *enablers* of M2M communication in resource-constrained environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The M2M Communication Architecture (ETSI / OneM2M Model)

The **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** and later the global **OneM2M** partnership standardized a layered M2M architecture consisting of three domains:

1. **M2M Device Domain (Perception Layer)**  
   - Contains the *physical sensors/actuators* and the *M2M Device Gateway* that aggregates them.  
   - Performs *area network* communication (ZigBee, Bluetooth LE, Modbus, CAN).

2. **Network Domain (Transport Layer)**  
   - Bridges device data to the application server using *xDSL, LTE, 5G, Satellite, Wi-Fi*.  
   - Handles *QoS, addressing, and security* on the wired/wireless backhaul.

3. **Application Domain (Application Layer)**  
   - Hosts the *M2M Application Server* (middleware).  
   - Provides service to vertical applications (smart health, smart grid, fleet management).

**Why this layered design?** It decouples *device heterogeneity* from *application logic*, allowing the same sensor to feed multiple applications (e.g., one ECG monitor serving both the cardiology dashboard and an AI-based anomaly detector).

### 2.2 The WSN Protocol Stack — Detailed Layer-by-Layer Analysis

#### **Layer 1 — Physical Layer (PHY)**
- Governs **frequency selection, modulation, encoding, and transmission power**.
- Dominant standard: **IEEE 802.15.4** operating in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** (16 channels, 250 kbps), with regional variants at **868 MHz (Europe)** and **915 MHz (North America)**.
- Modulation: **O-QPSK** (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) with a chip rate of **2 Mchips/s**.

#### **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer (MAC)**
- Manages **channel access, frame structure, beacon management, and acknowledgment**.
- Two channel-access modes:  
  - **Beacon-enabled mode** → uses *Slotted CSMA/CA* with superframes (active + inactive periods).  
  - **Non-beacon mode** → *Unslotted CSMA/CA*, simpler, used in ZigBee for low-duty-cycle applications.
- Optional **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** for real-time biomedical telemetry.

#### **Layer 3 — Network Layer (Routing)**
- Key protocols:  
  - **AODV (Ad-hoc On-Demand Distance Vector)** — reactive, good for low-mobility WSN.  
  - **DSR (Dynamic Source Routing)** — source-route based.  
  - **RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks — RFC 6550)** — the de-facto IoT standard using **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at the sink.  
  - **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** — energy-balanced clustering protocol.

#### **Layer 4 — Transport Layer**
- In WSN, TCP is rarely used due to overhead. Instead:  
  - **UDP** is preferred for lightweight datagram delivery.  
  - Protocols like **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** provide security on top of UDP.

#### **Layer 5 — Application Layer**
- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol — RFC 7252)** — RESTful, uses GET/POST/PUT/DELETE over UDP.  
- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — publish/subscribe over TCP, broker-based.  
- **MQTT-SN** — MQTT for Sensor Networks, optimized for non-TCP transports.  
- **HTTP/HTTPS** — used only at gateway level due to high overhead.  
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — high-performance, real-time, peer-to-peer.

### 2.3 Cross-Layer Concerns in WSN

- **Power Management:** Duty cycling, sleep/wake scheduling.  
- **Mobility Management:** Handling node movement (e.g., wearable body sensors).  
- **Task Management:** Distributing sensing/computation across nodes (e.g., in-network aggregation).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 2.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | Applicable for LOS propagation in 2.4 GHz WSN |
| 2 | **Received Signal Strength (RSS)** | $RSS = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB}$ | $P_t$: transmit power (dBm) | Determines link quality |
| 3 | **Energy per Bit (Transmit)** | $E_{tx} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | $k$ = bits, $n$ = path-loss exponent | $n=2$ free space, $n=4$ multipath |
| 4 | **Energy per Bit (Receive)** | $E_{rx} = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | — | Receiver electronics cost |
| 5 | **Network Lifetime** | $T_{net} = \frac{E_{total}}{E_{round}}$ | $E_{total}$: initial energy per node (J) | Key metric in LEACH analysis |
| 6 | **Cluster Head Selection Probability (LEACH)** | $P_{CH} = \frac{k}{N}$ | $k$ = optimal CHs, $N$ = nodes | Threshold: $T(n) = \frac{P_{CH}}{1 - P_{CH}(r \mod 1/P_{CH})}$ |
| 7 | **CSMA/CA Backoff Delay** | $T_{backoff} = Random(0, 2^{BE} - 1) \cdot T_{slot}$ | $BE$ = backoff exponent (default 3→5) | IEEE 802.15.4 MAC |
| 8 | **Duty Cycle (MAC)** | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | dimensionless (in %) | Lower DC → longer battery life |
| 9 | **Superframe Duration (802.15.4)** | $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{SO}$ | $SO$ = Superframe Order (0–14) | Beacon interval = SD |
| 10 | **6LoWPAN Fragmentation** | Total payload $\leq 1280 - 25$ bytes per IP packet | — | Mandated by RFC 4944 |
| 11 | **RPL Rank Computation** | $Rank(parent) = Rank(current) + Rank(increase)$ | — | Avoids loops in DODAG |
| 12 | **CoAP Message Overhead** | 4-byte header + token (0–8 B) + options | — | Much smaller than HTTP |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare:** ZigBee/6LoWPAN body-area networks (BAN) relay ECG, SpO2 to bedside gateway → CoAP/MQTT to hospital server.  
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** WirelessHART (IEC 62591) — a TDMA-based, redundant-mesh protocol used in chemical plants.  
- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRaWAN (Long Range WAN) for soil moisture sensing over km distances.  
- **Smart Metering:** M-Bus (Meter-Bus) for European gas/electricity metering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman et al. — LEACH)

The first-order radio model is the **de-facto analytical model** for evaluating WSN energy dissipation. We derive the energy spent by a sensor node to transmit and receive a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$.

#### Step 1 — Define the Energy Model
A sensor node dissipates energy in the *transmitter electronics*, *power amplifier*, and *receiver electronics*. The free-space ($d^{2}$) and multipath ($d^{4}$) components model path loss.

$$
E_{tx}(k, d) = 
\begin{cases}
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & d \leq d_{0} \\
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & d > d_{0}
\end{cases}
$$

#### Step 2 — Derive the Crossover Distance $d_{0}$
At $d = d_{0}$, both regimes consume equal energy. Equate them:

$$
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{0}^{2} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{4}
$$

Cancel $k \cdot E_{elec}$ on both sides:

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{0}^{2} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{4}
$$

Divide both sides by $d_{0}^{2}$:

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{2}
$$

Solve for $d_{0}$:

$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

**Interpretation:** For $d \leq d_{0}$ the channel behaves like free space; beyond that, multipath fading dominates. Typical values: $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{4}$ → $d_{0} \approx 87.7$ m.

#### Step 3 — Energy for Receiving
The receiver expends energy only on electronics (no power amplifier):

$$
E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

#### Step 4 — Total Energy per Round (LEACH)
In LEACH, $N$ nodes form $k$ clusters. Each round (1 iteration = setup + steady-state phase) consumes:

$$
E_{round} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{non-CH}
$$

where $E_{CH}$ aggregates *aggregation*, *receiving* from members, and *transmitting to BS*:

$$
E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{DA} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

A non-CH node transmits only to its CH (distance $d_{toCH}$, typically $d^{2}$ regime):

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

#### Step 5 — Network Lifetime
The number of rounds until first node dies:

$$
T_{lifetime} = \left\lfloor \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}/N} \right\rfloor
$$

where $E_{initial}$ is the initial battery energy per node. This is the metric plotted on the y-axis in nearly every LEACH research paper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The examiner expects you to write the energy equations and identify $d_{0}$ from given $\varepsilon_{fs}$ and $\varepsilon_{mp}$. Practice substituting numerical values.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major WSN/M2M Protocols

| Protocol | Standard | Layer | Topology | Data Rate | Power Profile | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE 802.15.4** | IEEE | PHY + MAC | Star / Mesh | 250 kbps | Low | Foundation for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN |
| **ZigBee** | ZigBee Alliance (IEEE 802.15.4) | Network + App | Mesh | 250 kbps | Very Low | Home automation, smart lighting |
| **6LoWPAN** | IETF RFC 4944 / 6282 | Adaptation | Mesh | 250 kbps | Low | IPv6 over 802.15.4 |
| **RPL** | IETF RFC 6550 | Network | DAG | — | Low | Routing in LLN |
| **CoAP** | IETF RFC 7252 | Application | Client-Server | — | Very Low | RESTful sensor queries |
| **MQTT** | OASIS (ISO/IEC 20922) | Application | Pub/Sub | — | Low (TCP) | Reliable telemetry |
| **LoRaWAN** | LoRa Alliance | PHY + MAC | Star-of-stars | 0.3–50 kbps | Ultra-Low | Long-range agriculture |
| **Bluetooth LE** | IEEE 802.15.1 | PHY + MAC | Star / Mesh | 1–2 Mbps | Very Low | Wearables, beacons |
| **Z-Wave** | Z-Wave Alliance | PHY + MAC | Mesh | 100 kbps | Very Low | Smart home |
| **WirelessHART** | IEC 62591 / IEEE 802.15.4 | Full stack | Mesh | 250 kbps | Low | Industrial process control |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: CSMA/CA Backoff Mechanism in IEEE 802.15.4

#### Step 1 — Initialization
A node with a new packet to transmit initializes:  
- $NB = 0$ (Number of Backoffs)  
- $BE = macMinBE$ (default = 3)  
- Delay = 0

#### Step 2 — Random Backoff Delay
The node waits for a random number of unit backoff periods:

$$
T_{delay} = \text{Random}(0, 2^{BE} - 1) \text{ backoff periods}
$$

#### Step 3 — Perform CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)
After the delay, the node performs *one or two* CCAs to sense the channel.

#### Step 4 — Channel Decision
- If channel is **IDLE** in both CCAs → node transmits the frame.  
- If channel is **BUSY** → increment $NB$ and $BE$ (capped at $macMaxBE=5$), and repeat from Step 2.

#### Step 5 — Failure
If $NB > macMaxCSMABackoffs$ (default = 4), the packet is dropped and an error is reported to the upper layer.

**Engineering Insight:** This exponential backoff minimizes collisions in dense WSNs and is the reason **802.15.4 outperforms 802.11 in energy efficiency** despite its lower data rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: OneM2M / ETSI M2M Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sensor Actuator Devices] --> B[M2M Device Gateway]
    B --> C[Access Network e.g. LTE 5G WiFi]
    C --> D[M2M Core Network Transport]
    D --> E[M2M Application Server Middleware]
    E --> F[Vertical Applications e.g. Smart Health Smart Grid]
    F --> G[End User Dashboard or Cloud Analytics]

    subgraph DeviceDomain["M2M Device Domain"]
    A
    B
    end

    subgraph NetworkDomain["Network Domain"]
    C
    D
    end

    subgraph AppDomain["Application Domain"]
    E
    F
    G
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: WSN Protocol Stack with Cross-Layer Concerns

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5[Application Layer CoAP MQTT DDS] --> L4
    L4[Transport Layer UDP DTLS] --> L3
    L3[Network Layer RPL AODV LEACH] --> L2
    L2[Data Link MAC CSMA CA GTS IEEE 802.15.4] --> L1
    L1[Physical Layer 2.4 GHz OQPSK DSSS]

    PL[Power Management Duty Cycling Sleep Wake] -.-> L1
    PL -.-> L2
    MM[Mobility Management Handoff Tracking] -.-> L3
    TM[Task Management In Network Aggregation] -.-> L5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: LEACH Clustering and Round Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    SINK[Base Station / Sink Node] --- CH1[Cluster Head 1]
    SINK --- CH2[Cluster Head 2]
    SINK --- CH3[Cluster Head 3]
    CH1 --- node1a[Member Node A]
    CH1 --- node1b[Member Node B]
    CH1 --- node1c[Member Node C]
    CH2 --- node2a[Member Node D]
    CH2 --- node2b[Member Node E]
    CH3 --- node3a[Member Node F]
    CH3 --- node3b[Member Node G]
    node1a -- TDMA Slot to CH --> CH1
    node1b -- TDMA Slot to CH --> CH1
    CH1 -- Aggregated Data Long Range --> SINK
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: RPL DODAG Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[DODAG Root - Border Router / Sink]
    ROOT --- R1[Router Node Rank 1]
    ROOT --- R2[Router Node Rank 1]
    R1 --- R3[Router Node Rank 2]
    R1 --- R4[Router Node Rank 2]
    R2 --- R5[Router Node Rank 2]
    R3 --- L1[Leaf Node - Sensor]
    R3 --- L2[Leaf Node - Sensor]
    R4 --- L3[Leaf Node - Sensor]
    R5 --- L4[Leaf Node - Actuator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In RPL, *DIO* (DODAG Information Object) messages are broadcast downward; *DAO* (DODAG Advertisement Object) messages are sent upward to populate routing tables at the root.

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End M2M-to-Cloud Flow for Biomedical IoT

```mermaid
graph LR
    BP[Body Sensor ECG SpO2 Temp] --> BC[Bluetooth LE to Body Hub]
    BC --> ZH[ZigBee to Room Hub]
    ZH --> GW[Edge Gateway 6LoWPAN to IPv6]
    GW --> RT[MQTT Broker over TLS]
    RT --> CL[Cloud Application Server]
    CL --> DC[Doctor Dashboard]
    CL --> ML[AI Anomaly Detector]
    ML -- Alert --> DC
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*Define M2M communication. List any four application areas of M2M in healthcare.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**  
Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication is the autonomous exchange of data between physical devices over a communication network without direct human intervention. Each device is equipped with sensors, a processor, and a transceiver that enable it to sense, process, and transmit information to a central server or to peer devices.

**Four Healthcare Application Areas (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM):** Continuous monitoring of vitals (BP, SpO2, glucose) outside the hospital.
2. **Smart Pills & Ingestible Sensors:** Track medication adherence and GI-tract pH.
3. **Ambulance Telemetry:** Real-time ECG/EEG relay to ER physicians en route.
4. **Asset Tracking of Medical Equipment:** Hospital-bed and infusion-pump location and status.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
*Differentiate between ZigBee and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) on the basis of (i) standard, (ii) topology, (iii) data rate, and (iv) typical application.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.75 each):**

| Parameter | ZigBee | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY/MAC); ZigBee Alliance for upper layers | IEEE 802.15.1 / Bluetooth 5.x |
| **Topology** | Mesh, star, cluster-tree | Star (point-to-point / broadcast) |
| **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | 1–2 Mbps |
| **Typical Application** | Home automation, industrial control, smart lighting | Wearables, fitness bands, beacons, audio |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module-2 question for 14 marks offers internal choice. Two independent options (A and B) are presented below for student practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Heavy on M2M Architecture*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**  
*(a)* Explain the **ETSI/OneM2M reference architecture** for M2M communication with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each domain. (7 Marks)  
*(b)* Discuss **any four application-layer protocols** (CoAP, MQTT, MQTT-SN, DDS) used in M2M/IoT systems, comparing their transport mechanism, message overhead, and suitability. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ETSI / OneM2M Reference Architecture (7 Marks):**

The ETSI TS 102 690 / OneM2M TS 0001 architecture divides the M2M system into **three domains** plus **two inter-domain reference points**:

1. **M2M Device Domain (2 Marks):**  
   - Contains the **M2M Device** (sensor/actuator + communication module) and the **M2M Device Gateway** (aggregator).  
   - Performs *area network* communication using short-range radios: ZigBee, BLE, Modbus, M-Bus.  
   - Responsible for sensing, actuation, and local pre-processing.

2. **Network Domain (2 Marks):**  
   - Provides *connectivity* between device domain and application domain using wired (DSL, fiber) or wireless (LTE, 5G, satellite, Wi-Fi) bearers.  
   - Implements *QoS, addressing, security* (e.g., IPSec/TLS at network edges), and routing.

3. **Application Domain (2 Marks):  
   - Hosts the **M2M Application Server** (middleware) and **M2M Applications**.  
   - Provides service capabilities (SC): *data management, subscription/notification, device management, security*.  
   - Connects to vertical industries: healthcare, energy, transport, smart city.

4. **Reference Points (1 Mark):**  
   - **dIa:** Device-to-Application (logical)  
   - **mIa:** Middleware-to-Application  
   - **mId:** Middleware-to-Device (dIa mapped through gateways)

> [!TIP]
> A simple block diagram showing the three domains with arrows across the reference points is sufficient for full marks in KTU.

**(b) Application-Layer Protocols (7 Marks):**

| Protocol | Transport | Overhead | Pattern | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CoAP** (RFC 7252) | UDP | 4 B header + token | Request/Response (RESTful) | Resource-constrained devices, low-power LANs |
| **MQTT** (ISO/IEC 20922) | TCP | 2 B fixed header | Publish/Subscribe | Reliable telemetry over backhaul |
| **MQTT-SN** | UDP / non-IP | Variable | Publish/Subscribe | Sleepy sensor nodes on ZigBee/BLE |
| **DDS** (OMG) | UDP/TCP | RTPS header | Peer-to-Peer with QoS | Real-time industrial control, autonomous vehicles |

**Detailed Notes (for 1.5 Marks each protocol — pick any 4):**

- **CoAP:** Uses 4-byte binary header; supports GET/POST/PUT/DELETE; observable resources via *Observe* option; works with DTLS for security; ideal for 6LoWPAN.
- **MQTT:** 5 broker-mediated QoS levels (0,1,2); tiny 2-byte header; ideal when backhaul is reliable (Wi-Fi/LTE); retained messages for last-known value.
- **MQTT-SN:** Optimized for non-TCP (ZigBee); supports *sleeping clients* and *topic IDs* to compress strings; gateways translate to standard MQTT.
- **DDS:** Decentralized, broker-less, with **20+ QoS policies** (deadline, latency, reliability); high throughput; used in defense and industrial automation (e.g., ROS 2).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Heavy on WSN Protocols and Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**  
*(a)* Describe the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC superframe structure** with beacon-enabled mode. Explain the role of CAP, CFP, and GTS. (7 Marks)  
*(b)* Using the **first-order radio energy model**, derive the expression for the *crossover distance* $d_0$ and the *total energy dissipated per round* in a LEACH-clustered WSN with $N$ nodes and $k$ cluster heads. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe Structure (7 Marks):**

A **superframe** is bounded by *beacons* transmitted by the **PAN coordinator**. Its structure is governed by two parameters: **SO (Superframe Order)** and **BO (Beacon Order)**.

$$
BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{BO} \quad ; \quad SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{SO}
$$

where $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 15.36$ ms (960 symbols). Active portion = $SD$; Inactive portion = $BI - SD$.

**Structure of Active Period (3 Marks):**

1. **Beacon (1 slot):** Synchronization, PAN ID, superframe spec, GTS descriptors.  
2. **Contention Access Period (CAP):** Uses **slotted CSMA/CA**; any node may compete.  
3. **Contention-Free Period (CFP):** Contains up to **7 GTS** allocated by PAN coordinator.  
4. **Inactive Period:** Coordinator may enter low-power mode; nodes sleep.

**Role of CAP, CFP, GTS (4 Marks — 1 each):**

- **CAP (Contention Access Period):** Best-effort traffic using slotted CSMA/CA; flexible, contention-prone.
- **CFP (Contention-Free Period):** Time-slotted, deterministic, used for latency-sensitive traffic (e.g., real-time ECG).
- **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot):** A predefined slot in the CFP, dedicated to a specific device, ensures bounded latency. Maximum 7 GTS per superframe.
- **Inactive period:** Saves power — a node may sleep for the inactive duration, achieving duty cycling.

---

**(b) First-Order Radio Energy Model (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Transmit energy equation (1 Mark):**

$$
E_{tx}(k, d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}
$$

with $n = 2$ (free space, $d \leq d_0$) or $n = 4$ (multipath, $d > d_0$).

**Step 2: Crossover distance derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

Substituting $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m$^2$ and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m$^4$:

$$
d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Receive energy (1 Mark):**

$$
E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}
$$

**Step 4: Energy per round in LEACH (2 Marks):**

$$
E_{round} = k \cdot E_{CH} + (N - k) \cdot E_{non\text{-}CH}
$$

where:

$$
E_{CH} = k \cdot E_{DA} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

$$
E_{non\text{-}CH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cancel $k$** during the crossover distance derivation — a frequent 0.5 mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up $\varepsilon_{fs}$ and $\varepsilon_{mp}$** units (pJ/bit/m² vs. pJ/bit/m⁴).
> 3. **Omitting the aggregation energy $E_{DA}$** in the cluster head expression.
> 4. **Not specifying BO ≠ SO:** For beacon-enabled mode the inactive period exists only when $BO > SO$; if $BO = SO$, the inactive period is zero.
> 5. **Writing "BLE = 802.15.4"** in the comparison table — they are *different* standards (BLE = 802.15.1).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorize before the KTU exam.**

- ☐ **M2M** is a *paradigm*; **WSN** is a *technology* implementing it. They are not synonyms.
- ☐ **OneM2M** standardized the M2M architecture into **3 domains + 2 reference points**.
- ☐ The **IEEE 802.15.4** standard defines **only PHY + MAC**; ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART add upper layers on top.
- ☐ **CSMA/CA** in 802.15.4 has **two flavors**: *slotted (beacon-enabled)* and *unslotted (non-beacon)*.
- ☐ **GTS = up to 7 slots**, allocated within the CFP, for real-time / latency-sensitive biomedical traffic.
- ☐ **LEACH** uses **stochastic cluster-head rotation** with threshold $T(n)$; rotates every *round*.
- ☐ **RPL** builds a **DODAG** rooted at the sink; *DIO/DAO/ DIS* are the three control message types.
- ☐ **CoAP** runs over **UDP**; **MQTT** runs over **TCP**; **MQTT-SN** runs over **non-TCP** (UDP/ZigBee).
- ☐ **6LoWPAN** enables **IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4** with fragmentation/header compression (RFC 4944, 6282).
- ☐ **First-order energy model** crossover distance: $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}} \approx 87.7$ m for standard parameters.
- ☐ **LoRaWAN** = long range (km), low data rate (kbps); ZigBee/BLE = short range, higher data rate (kbps–Mbps).
- ☐ **DDS** is the only *broker-less*, *peer-to-peer* application protocol in common IoT use.
- ☐ **Network lifetime** is plotted as *rounds vs. alive nodes* in every WSN protocol paper — be ready to interpret such graphs.
- ☐ Standard **KPI metrics** for WSN evaluation: *energy consumption, network lifetime, throughput, end-to-end delay, packet delivery ratio (PDR), jitter*.
- ☐ **Biomedical WSN (BSN / BAN)** must be **ultra-low power** (often < 1 mW) and **reliable** — LEACH + ZigBee/6LoWPAN is the most-asked combination in KTU.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"MA-N-PA"** for the **OneM2M** domains: **M**2M **A**pp-domain → **N**etwork-domain → **M**2M **A**rea-network (device-domain).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA and RFID Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SCADA and RFID Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SCADA — Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition

### Formal Definition
**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized industrial control architecture that uses networked computers, PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers), RTUs (Remote Terminal Units), and HMI (Human-Machine Interface) software to remotely monitor, gather, and process real-time data from geographically dispersed field instruments, while sending supervisory control commands back to the field devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SCADA is treated as a *legacy industrial protocol stack* that has evolved into an **edge-of-IIoT** (Industrial IoT) data aggregation layer. For biomedical/IoT applications, SCADA is the spine of hospital utility monitoring (oxygen lines, HVAC for OTs, generator load, water purification).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of SCADA as the **"air traffic control tower of a hospital or factory."** The tower (central SCADA server) doesn't fly the planes itself; instead, it watches radar screens (sensors), talks to pilots over radio (control commands), and coordinates dozens of moving assets spread across a city. Just like a controller can tell a pilot to "climb to 30,000 feet" without being on the plane, SCADA sends a "close valve" or "start pump" command to a device kilometers away while continuously reading its pressure, temperature, and flow.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard SCADA polling cycle:** 100 ms – 10 s depending on criticality
- **RTU update rate:** Typically **9600 bps – 115200 bps** over serial links
- **Latency tolerance for critical alarms:** < **1 second**
- **Availability target for Tier-1 systems:** **99.99%** (the "four nines")

---

## 1.2 RFID — Radio Frequency Identification Protocols

### Formal Definition
**RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)** is a non-contact automatic identification technology that uses electromagnetic fields at radio frequencies to uniquely identify, track, and manage objects, animals, or people via **tags** (transponders) attached to or embedded in the target, and **readers** (interrogators) that power and read these tags.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference from Barcode:** Unlike optical barcodes, RFID does **not require line-of-sight**, can read **hundreds of tags simultaneously** (anti-collision), survives harsh environments, and supports **read/write** memory cycles.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **library where every book whispers its identity to the librarian** the moment the librarian walks through the stacks holding a "magic wand." No need to physically pull out the book, no need to scan one at a time, no need to even open it. The wand (RFID reader) energizes the book's tiny microchip (RFID tag) wirelessly, and the chip shouts back its unique ID number via radio waves. That is RFID in one sentence.

### Frequency Band Classification (ISO/IEC Standard)

| Band | Frequency Range | Coupling Type | Typical Range |
|------|----------------|---------------|---------------|
