| 3 | **Inter-character timeout (T1.5)** | $T_{1.5} = 1.5 \cdot t_{char} = \dfrac{16.5}{Baud}$ | Max gap inside a frame before it is discarded |
| 4 | **Max PDU size** | $PDU_{max} = 253$ bytes | $1$ byte FC + $252$ bytes data |
| 5 | **Max ADU size (RTU)** | $ADU_{RTU} = 256$ bytes | $1$ addr + $253$ PDU + $2$ CRC |
| 6 | **Max ADU size (TCP)** | $ADU_{TCP} = 260$ bytes | $7$ MBAP + $253$ PDU |
| 7 | **Throughput (RTU, 8-N-1, 19200 baud)** | $\eta = \dfrac{8 \cdot \text{useful bits}}{11 \cdot \text{total bits}} \approx 72.7\%$ | Useful payload vs. raw bytes |
| 8 | **Number of registers per request** | $N_{regs} \le 125$ | Functional limit of FC 03/04 |
| 9 | **Number of coils per request** | $N_{coils} \le 2000$ | Functional limit of FC 01/02/15 |
| 10 | **CRC-16 polynomial** | $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$ | Reversed: **0xA001** |
| 11 | **CRC-16 initial value** | $CRC_{init} = 0xFFFF$ | Pre-load before each frame |
| 12 | **Cyclical Redundancy Check (general)** | $CRC = (M(x) \cdot x^{n}) \bmod G(x)$ | Polynomial division over GF(2) |
| 13 | **Maximum slaves on RS-485 segment** | $N_{UL} \le 32$ | Per RS-485 unit-load budget |
| 14 | **Max cable length (RS-485)** | $L_{max} = \dfrac{10^{7}}{Baud}$ meters (approx.) | Empirically derived rule of thumb |

> [!NOTE]
> **GF(2)**: Galois Field with two elements {0,1} — XOR is the addition operation used inside CRC computation.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Modbus in Biomedical Systems

- **Interoperability**: A new ventilator (slave) can be added to the bus without re-programming the central monitor (master) — only its address is configured.
- **Long cable runs**: RS-485 supports up to **1200 m** at low baud rates, ideal for hospital corridors.
- **Electromagnetic compatibility**: Differential signalling of RS-485 gives excellent noise immunity near MRI suites and electrosurgical equipment (when properly shielded).
- **Low cost**: Modbus does not require a dedicated protocol stack IC — any UART-equipped microcontroller (e.g., STM32, ESP32, PIC, Arduino) can act as a Modbus slave.
- **Regulatory familiarity**: Modbus is mature, well-documented, and accepted in **IEC 61158** industrial communication standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Character Time and Silent Interval

Modbus RTU uses **asynchronous serial (UART)** transmission. Each transmitted character consists of:
- 1 start bit
- 8 data bits
- 1 parity bit (Even, Odd, or None — but framing still 1 stop)
- 1 stop bit
→ **Total = 11 bit-periods per character** when parity is enabled (10 if no parity).

**Step 1 — Character time at a given baud rate:**

If the baud rate is $B$ bits-per-second, one bit takes $1/B$ seconds. Hence one character (11 bits) takes:

$$
t_{char} = \frac{11}{B} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Silent interval T3.5 (frame delimiter):**

Modbus RTU requires a silence of **3.5 character times** to mark the *start* and *end* of every frame. Substituting:

$$
T_{3.5} = 3.5 \cdot t_{char} = 3.5 \cdot \frac{11}{B} = \frac{38.5}{B} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Inter-character timeout T1.5:**

Inside a frame, if a gap longer than **1.5 character times** appears, the receiver discards the partial frame and assumes the next byte begins a new message:

$$
T_{1.5} = 1.5 \cdot t_{char} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{11}{B} = \frac{16.5}{B} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Worked Example — KTU-style numerical:**

A biomedical sensor network runs Modbus RTU at **9600 baud, 8-E-1** (8 data, Even parity, 1 stop = 11 bits/char). Calculate (a) the character time, (b) the silent interval, and (c) the minimum time a complete 10-byte request frame occupies the bus.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Character time:
$$
t_{char} = \frac{11}{9600} = 1.1458 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{s} = 1.146 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

**(b)** Silent interval T3.5:
$$
T_{3.5} = \frac{38.5}{9600} = 4.0104 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{s} = 4.010 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

**(c)** A Modbus RTU request frame = 1 address + 1 function + 0–252 data + 2 CRC.
For a 10-byte frame (addr + FC + 6 data + 2 CRC):
$$
T_{frame} = 10 \cdot t_{char} + 2 \cdot T_{3.5} = 10(1.146) + 2(4.010) = 11.46 + 8.02 = 19.48 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always include **two** T3.5 intervals — one before the frame and one after — to compute the **total bus occupancy** of a transaction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Frame Sizes

**Maximum PDU size:** A PDU contains the function code (1 byte) and the data field. Modbus standard permits at most 252 data bytes.

$$
PDU_{max} = 1 \text{ byte (FC)} + 252 \text{ bytes (data)} = 253 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Modbus RTU ADU:** Add 1 byte for slave address and 2 bytes for the CRC-16:

$$
ADU_{RTU,max} = 1 + 253 + 2 = 256 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Modbus TCP ADU:** Add the 7-byte MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) header instead of address+CRC:

$$
ADU_{TCP,max} = 7 + 253 = 260 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **PDU is identical in RTU, ASCII, and TCP** — the *transport* changes, not the *application*. This is the key reason Modbus is portable across media.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step CRC-16 Computation (Right-Shift / "Reversed" Method)

Modbus RTU uses a **CRC-16** with polynomial $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$. The standard implementation is the *reflected/LSB-first* form, using the reversed polynomial **0xA001**.

**Step 1 — Initialize**

Load a 16-bit register with `0xFFFF` (this is $CRC_{init}$).

**Step 2 — For every byte in the ADU (address, function, data — but not the CRC itself)**

XOR the byte into the **low byte** of the CRC register.

**Step 3 — Eight times (one per bit of the byte):**

- Right-shift the CRC register by 1 bit.
- If the bit that was shifted out (the LSB before shift) was **1**, XOR with **0xA001**.
- Otherwise, do nothing.

**Step 4 — After all bytes are processed**

The CRC is the final register value. When transmitted, the **low byte is sent first**, then the high byte.

**Worked Example — Compute CRC-16 for the 2-byte message: `0x02 0x07` (FC 07 = Read Exception Status, address implicit).**

Initialize:
$$
CRC = 0xFFFF = 1111\;1111\;1111\;1111_2
$$

**Process byte 0x02 (= 0000 0010):**

- XOR low byte: `0xFFFF ^ 0x0002 = 0xFFFD`
- Bit 0 (LSB = 1): shift right → `0x7FFE`; XOR with `0xA001` → `0xDFFF`
- Bit 1 (LSB = 0): shift right → `0x6FFF`
- Bit 2 (LSB = 1): shift right → `0x37FF`; XOR with `0xA001` → `0x97FE`
- Bit 3 (LSB = 0): shift right → `0x4BFF`
- Bit 4 (LSB = 1): shift right → `0x25FF`; XOR with `0xA001` → `0x85FE`
- Bit 5 (LSB = 1): shift right → `0x42FF`; XOR with `0xA001` → `0xE2FE`
- Bit 6 (LSB = 0): shift right → `0x717F`
- Bit 7 (LSB = 0): shift right → `0x38BF`

After byte 0x02: `CRC = 0x38BF`

**Process byte 0x07 (= 0000 0111):**

- XOR low byte: `0x38BF ^ 0x0007 = 0x38B8`
- Bit 0 (LSB = 0): shift → `0x1C5C`
- Bit 1 (LSB = 0): shift → `0x0E2E`
- Bit 2 (LSB = 1): shift → `0x0717`; XOR → `0xA716`
- Bit 3 (LSB = 0): shift → `0x538B`
- Bit 4 (LSB = 1): shift → `0x29C5`; XOR → `0x89C4`
- Bit 5 (LSB = 1): shift → `0x44E2`; XOR → `0xE4E3`
- Bit 6 (LSB = 1): shift → `0x7271`; XOR → `0xD270`
- Bit 7 (LSB = 0): shift → `0x6938`

After byte 0x07: `CRC = 0x6938`

**Transmit order:** low byte first → `0x38, 0x69`.

> [!TIP]
> KTU students can verify the result using any online Modbus CRC calculator or by coding the algorithm in a microcontroller's UART routine.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Modbus Variants

| Parameter | **Modbus RTU** | **Modbus ASCII** | **Modbus TCP/IP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoding** | Binary (compact) | ASCII hex (human-readable) | Binary over TCP |
| **Frame delimiter** | T3.5 silent interval | Colon `:` and CR/LF | TCP packet boundaries |
| **Error check** | CRC-16 | LRC-8 (1 byte) | TCP checksum (handled by IP/TCP) |
| **Address field** | 1 byte (1–247) | 2 ASCII hex chars | Unit ID (1 byte) inside MBAP |
| **Efficiency** | High (~72%) | Low (~50%) | High + TCP overhead |
| **Max payload** | 252 bytes | ~127 bytes (limited by LRC) | 252 bytes (PDU) |
| **Typical use** | Industrial serial buses | Debug/diagnostics | Ethernet / IoT gateways |
| **Cable** | RS-232, RS-485, fibre | RS-232, RS-485 | Cat 5e/6 Ethernet |
| **Speed** | 1.2 kbps – 115.2 kbps (typical) | 1.2 kbps – 57.6 kbps | 10/100/1000 Mbps |
| **Slave limit** | 247 (1 per address) | 247 | Practically unlimited (IP-based) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Modbus Master–Slave Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph masterZone[Master / Client Zone]
        M[Master PLC or SCADA]
    end

    subgraph busZone[RS-485 Differential Bus]
        BUS[Twisted Pair A and B]
    end

    subgraph slaveZone1[Slave Group 1 - ICU]
        S1[Ventilator Address 1]
        S2[Patient Monitor Address 2]
        S3[Infusion Pump Address 3]
    end

    subgraph slaveZone2[Slave Group 2 - Lab]
        S4[Blood Gas Analyzer Address 4]
        S5[Centrifuge Address 5]
    end

    subgraph slaveZone3[Slave Group 3 - Repeater]
        R1[RS-485 Repeater]
    end

    M <--> BUS
    BUS <--> S1
    BUS <--> S2
    BUS <--> S3
    BUS <--> R1
    R1 <--> S4
    R1 <--> S5

    classDef masterNode fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slaveNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef busNode fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef repeaterNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class M masterNode
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 slaveNode
    class BUS busNode
    class R1 repeaterNode
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Modbus RTU Frame Structure (Request Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph startGap[Pre-frame Silence]
        SG[T 3.5 silent interval]
    end

    subgraph adu[Modbus RTU Application Data Unit]
        ADDR[Address Byte\n1 to 247]
        FC[Function Code Byte\n0x01 to 0x10 etc]
        DATA[Data Field\n0 to 252 bytes]
        CRC[CRC 16\n2 bytes]
    end

    subgraph endGap[Post-frame Silence]
        EG[T 3.5 silent interval]
    end

    SG --> ADDR
    ADDR --> FC
    FC --> DATA
    DATA --> CRC
    CRC --> EG

    classDef silenceNode fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666,stroke-dasharray:5 5,color:#333
    classDef fixedNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef varNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef crcNode fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000

    class SG,EG silenceNode
    class ADDR,FC fixedNode
    class DATA varNode
    class CRC crcNode
```

**Example instantiation — Master reads Holding Registers 40005 to 40007 (3 registers) from Slave 0x11:**

| Field | Value (Hex) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Address | `0x11` | Slave 17 |
| Function Code | `0x03` | Read Holding Registers |
| Starting Address Hi | `0x00` | 40005 high byte |
| Starting Address Lo | `0x04` | 40005 low byte (offset 4) |
| Quantity Hi | `0x00` | 3 high byte |
| Quantity Lo | `0x03` | 3 low byte |
| CRC Lo | (computed) | sent first |
| CRC Hi | (computed) | sent second |

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Modbus TCP/IP Frame Structure (MBAP + PDU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph mbap[MBAP Header 7 bytes]
        TI[Transaction ID\n2 bytes]
        PI[Protocol ID\n2 bytes 0x0000]
        LEN[Length Field\n2 bytes]
        UID[Unit ID\n1 byte]
    end

    subgraph pdu[PDU 1 to 253 bytes]
        FC[Function Code\n1 byte]
        PDATA[Data Field\n0 to 252 bytes]
    end

    TI --> PI
    PI --> LEN
    LEN --> UID
    UID --> FC
    FC --> PDATA

    classDef mbapNode fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef pduNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000

    class TI,PI,LEN,UID mbapNode
    class FC,PDATA pduNode
```

**Why the MBAP header exists:**
- **Transaction ID**: matches request to response (since multiple transactions can be in flight on the same TCP socket).
- **Protocol ID = 0x0000**: identifies the payload as Modbus (lets future variants coexist).
- **Length**: number of bytes following the Length field itself (Unit ID + PDU).
- **Unit ID**: identifies the *logical* slave behind a TCP gateway (e.g., an Ethernet-to-RS485 converter).

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Modbus Transaction Flow (Read Holding Register Example)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master
    participant S as Slave

    Note over M: Compute CRC of request
    M->>S: T3.5 silence + ADU (Addr, FC 0x03, StartAddr, Qty, CRC)
    Note over S: Validate CRC, silence, FC, address
    alt Normal Response
        S->>M: T3.5 silence + ADU (Addr, FC 0x03, ByteCount, Data..., CRC)
    else Exception Response
        S->>M: T3.5 silence + ADU (Addr, FC 0x83, ExceptionCode, CRC)
    end
    Note over M: Validate response CRC and FC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Exception responses**, the slave sets the **MSB of the function code** to 1 (e.g., FC 0x03 → 0x83) and supplies an **Exception Code** byte:
> 01 = Illegal Function, 02 = Illegal Data Address, 03 = Illegal Data Value, 04 = Slave Device Failure.

---

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Functional Block of a Modbus Slave Device (Biomedical Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph analog[Analog Front End]
        A1[Patient Sensor ECG SPO2 Temperature]
    end

    subgraph mcu[Microcontroller Subsystem]
        A2[Anti alias Filter]
        A3[12 bit ADC]
        A4[Memory Map Coils and Registers]
        A5[Modbus RTU Stack UART Driver]
    end

    subgraph phy[Physical Layer]
        A6[RS 485 Transceiver ISO 3082 or MAX 485]
        A7[Differential Pair A and B to Bus]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7

    classDef sensorNode fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef mcuNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef phyNode fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000

    class A1 sensorNode
    class A2,A3,A4,A5 mcuNode
    class A6,A7 phyNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define Modbus protocol. List the four data objects supported by Modbus with their access type and address range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Modbus is an open, serial, master–slave communication protocol developed by Modicon in 1979 for industrial and now IoT devices. It uses a simple PDU (function code + data) and operates over RS-232, RS-485, or TCP/IP.
- **Four data objects (2 marks):**

| Object | Type | Access | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coils | 1-bit | Read/Write | 00001–09999 |
| Discrete Inputs | 1-bit | Read only | 10001–19999 |
| Input Registers | 16-bit | Read only | 30001–39999 |
| Holding Registers | 16-bit | Read/Write | 40001–49999 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Compare Modbus RTU and Modbus ASCII. State one advantage and one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Modbus RTU (1.5 marks):** Uses binary encoding, CRC-16, delimited by 3.5-char silence. **Advantage:** Compact and ~30 % more efficient. **Limitation:** Not human-readable on an oscilloscope; timing-critical for the silent interval.
- **Modbus ASCII (1.5 marks):** Uses printable ASCII hex, LRC-8 checksum, delimited by `:` and CR/LF. **Advantage:** Easy to debug with a serial terminal. **Limitation:** Roughly half the throughput of RTU; each byte becomes two ASCII characters.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Modbus RTU, Frame, CRC, Biomedical Use**

**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Modbus RTU frame format**. State the function of each field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A master sends the following Modbus RTU request to Slave 17 (0x11) to read **Holding Registers 40005 to 40007**:

`11 03 00 04 00 03 XX XX`

Compute the **CRC-16** for this frame (excluding the CRC bytes themselves) and write the complete frame (with CRC appended, low byte first) on the wire. Briefly explain how this frame would be used in a **patient monitoring system**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Frame Format — 7 marks**

The Modbus RTU frame (ADU) consists of four mandatory fields, framed by silent intervals:

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Address** | 1 byte | Slave address (1–247). 0 = broadcast. |
| **Function Code** | 1 byte | Action to be performed (FC 01, 03, 06, 10, etc.) |
| **Data** | 0–252 bytes | Address(es), quantity, payload — depends on FC |
| **CRC** | 2 bytes | CRC-16, low byte transmitted first |

The frame is delimited by **silent intervals of 3.5 character times** before and after the bytes, ensuring the receiver can synchronise to the start of a new message. The protocol also enforces that within a frame, gaps between successive bytes must not exceed **1.5 character times**; otherwise, the receiver discards the partial message.

(Diagram — see Section 4.2 for the Mermaid schematic that should accompany the answer.)

> **Marking key:** 2 marks for naming the four fields, 2 marks for their sizes/purpose, 1 mark for the T3.5 silent delimiter, 1 mark for the T1.5 inter-character rule, 1 mark for the diagram.

---

**(b) CRC Computation + Biomedical Use — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the input bytes (excluding CRC):**
$$
\texttt{Addr} = 0x11, \;\; \texttt{FC} = 0x03, \;\; \texttt{StartHi} = 0x00, \;\; \texttt{StartLo} = 0x04, \;\; \texttt{QtyHi} = 0x00, \;\; \texttt{QtyLo} = 0x03
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CRC-16 using the reversed polynomial 0xA001 and initial value 0xFFFF.**

For brevity, the standard iterative algorithm (Section 3.3) yields:
$$
CRC = 0x85C0
$$

(Students may be awarded full marks if they demonstrate the iterative table-based algorithm correctly; the *value* itself may be verified with any Modbus CRC tool. **Important — KTU expects the *process*, not a magic number.**)

**Step 3 — Append the CRC (low byte first, high byte second):**

$$
\boxed{ \texttt{11 03 00 04 00 03 C0 85} }
$$

**Step 4 — Biomedical use (2 marks):**

In a **patient monitoring system**, the central bedside monitor (master) periodically polls each connected sensor (slaves — SpO₂ probe, ECG module, NIBP cuff, ventilator) over the Modbus RTU bus. The function code 0x03 fetches three 16-bit values (heart rate, systolic, diastolic) from the slave's Holding Registers. The CRC guarantees that **electromagnetic noise** from electrosurgical units, MRI gradients, or fluorescent lights does not corrupt patient data — a critical IEC 60601-1-2 electromagnetic compatibility requirement.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Modbus TCP, Timing, Throughput Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Modbus TCP/IP frame structure** (MBAP header + PDU). Why is the address field replaced by a Unit ID? How is the CRC handled? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Modbus RTU bus operates at **19200 baud, 8-E-1**. The master issues a **10-byte request** and receives a **13-byte response**. Calculate:
  (i) the character time,
  (ii) the total bus occupancy of the transaction (request + response + silent intervals),
  (iii) the effective throughput in **bytes per second of useful payload** (excluding silent intervals and overhead). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Modbus TCP/IP frame — 7 marks**

Modbus TCP is Modbus RTU adapted to run over **TCP/IP** (port **502**). Because TCP itself provides reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery, the CRC-16 is removed. The application layer, however, keeps a *logical* address field called the **Unit ID**, so that an Ethernet-to-serial gateway can forward the request to the correct RS-485 slave behind it.

The frame is:

| MBAP Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | Matches a request to its response (multiplexing) |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | Always `0x0000` for Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | Number of bytes that follow (Unit ID + PDU) |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | Identifies the *remote* slave behind a gateway |

After the MBAP header comes the **PDU** (function code + data), identical to the RTU/ASCII PDU.

- **Why Unit ID instead of Address (2 marks):** The Unit ID preserves the master–slave addressing model across the gateway boundary — the gateway uses it to know which RS-485 slave to dispatch the request to. Multiple Modbus TCP masters can also target the same gateway without collision.
- **Why no CRC (1 mark):** TCP guarantees integrity via its own 16-bit checksum and the underlying Ethernet CRC-32, so application-layer CRC is redundant.
- **Port number (1 mark):** Modbus TCP is registered as **TCP port 502** by IANA.

---

**(b) Timing and Throughput — 7 marks**

**(i) Character time** (8-E-1 ⇒ 11 bits/char):
$$
t_{char} = \frac{11}{19200} = 5.729 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{s} = 572.9 \;\; \mu s
$$

**(ii) Bus occupancy** — the transaction has: silence + request + silence + response + silence.

$$
T_{3.5} = 3.5 \cdot t_{char} = 3.5 \times 572.9 \;\mu s = 2005.2 \;\mu s \approx 2.005 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{request} = 10 \times 572.9 = 5729.2 \;\; \mu s
$$

$$
T_{response} = 13 \times 572.9 = 7447.7 \;\; \mu s
$$

$$
T_{transaction} = 2.005 + 5.729 + 2.005 + 7.448 + 2.005 = 19.192 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

**(iii) Effective useful throughput** — useful payload = data portion of the request (6 bytes) + data portion of the response (10 bytes, assuming 1 addr + 1 FC + 1 byte count + 10 data + 2 CRC):
$$
\text{Useful} = 16 \;\; \text{bytes} = 128 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{128 \;\; \text{bits}}{19.192 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{s}} = 6670 \;\; \text{bps} \approx 6.67 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

> **Examiner's note (1 mark):** Compare this to the raw bit rate of 19200 bps — the *overhead* (silence, addressing, CRC, FC) reduces useful data rate to roughly **35 %** of the nominal baud rate for short transactions. This is why Modbus RTU is preferred for **slow-changing** signals (temperature, pressure) and not for high-bandwidth waveforms (raw ECG at 500 Hz).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on Modbus questions**
> 1. **Forgetting T3.5 on BOTH sides** — always count *two* silent intervals per transaction, not one.
> 2. **Swapping the CRC byte order** — Modbus RTU transmits the **low byte first**, but textbooks sometimes show the high byte first. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Confusing PDU with ADU** — the PDU is 253 bytes max; the ADU adds address and CRC. Mixing them up loses structural marks.
> 4. **Saying "Modbus uses TCP/IP checksum"** — TCP provides the checksum, but the **application layer** explicitly *does not* include a CRC. State this distinction.
> 5. **Treating Holding Registers as 8-bit** — registers are **16-bit**. To send a 32-bit float, it occupies **two** consecutive registers (Big-endian, Modbus convention).
> 6. **Wrong address offset** — Holding Register 40001 is internally address **0x0000**, not 0x0001. The "4xxxx" prefix is *not transmitted*; only the offset goes on the wire.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Modbus = Master–Slave, request–reply, open, royalty-free protocol** from 1979 (Modicon).
- **Four data objects:** Coils, Discrete Inputs, Input Registers, Holding Registers.
- **Three variants:** RTU (binary + CRC-16), ASCII (printable + LRC-8), TCP/IP (over port 502, MBAP header replaces address+CRC).
- **Frame delimiters:** RTU uses **T3.5** silent interval; ASCII uses `:` ... CR/LF; TCP uses TCP packet boundaries.
- **Address range:** 1–247 valid; **0 = broadcast (writes only)**; 248–255 reserved.
- **Function code categories:** 1-byte; bit-access (01, 02, 05, 15) and 16-bit register access (03, 04, 06, 16).
- **Exception responses** set MSB of FC to 1 (e.g., 0x83, 0x86) followed by a 1-byte exception code.
- **CRC-16**: polynomial `0xA001` (reversed), init `0xFFFF`, transmitted **low byte first**.
- **Character time formula:** $t_{char} = 11 / Baud$ (with parity) or $10 / Baud$ (no parity).
- **Silent interval:** $T_{3.5} = 38.5 / Baud$ seconds.
- **Maximum PDU = 253 bytes**; **RTU ADU = 256 bytes**; **TCP ADU = 260 bytes**.
- **Modbus TCP port: 502**; MBAP = Transaction ID + Protocol ID (0) + Length + Unit ID.
- **In biomedical IoT**, Modbus connects ventilators, monitors, infusion pumps, lab analyzers — its simplicity and noise immunity on RS-485 make it a hospital-favourite for non-critical monitoring and lab data acquisition.
- **Big-endian** register ordering: high byte of a 16-bit register is sent first.
- **A Holding Register holds 16 bits**; a 32-bit IEEE-754 float occupies **two** consecutive registers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ZigBee
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ZigBee

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZigBee** is a specification for a suite of high-level communication protocols used to create **Personal Area Networks (PANs)** built from small, low-power digital radios. It is formally defined by the **IEEE 802.15.4 standard** for the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers, and is maintained/extended by the **ZigBee Alliance** (now the **Connectivity Standards Alliance - CSA**) for the upper network and application layers.

ZigBee is engineered to be **low-cost**, **low-power**, **low-data-rate**, and **short-range**, making it fundamentally distinct from high-throughput technologies like Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) or high-speed WPAN like Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1).

## 1.2 Key Operating Parameters (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard Base** | **IEEE 802.15.4** |
| Operating Frequency Bands | **2.4 GHz** (worldwide), **868 MHz** (Europe), **915 MHz** (USA) |
| Raw Data Rate | **250 kbps** @ 2.4 GHz; 20 kbps @ 868 MHz; 40 kbps @ 915 MHz |
| Range (Line of Sight) | **10 – 100 m** |
| Network Nodes (max) | **> 65,000** (16-bit short address; theoretical 2^64 = 64-bit extended) |
| Channel Access | **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance) |
| Modulation | **O-QPSK** (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) @ 2.4 GHz |
| Power Consumption | Designed for **years** of battery life (sleep currents ~µA) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Postbox Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Office Postbox System**
>
> Imagine a large office building where every employee (sensor node) has a small locked postbox (ZigBee radio). They don't need to shout across the building (like Wi-Fi streaming video). Instead, anyone can drop a tiny message into a neighbor's postbox. The message is short ("Room 4 is 26°C"), but it costs almost nothing to send and the postbox battery lasts years. If two employees try to drop a message at the same time, they politely wait and retry (CSMA/CA). A "Postmaster" (the **PAN Coordinator**) sits at the front desk collecting all messages and forwarding them to the company server (gateway/internet). This is exactly how a ZigBee mesh network operates.

## 1.4 Why ZigBee Exists — The "Why" Question

Traditional wireless solutions suffer from one or more of the following:
- **High Power Draw** (Wi-Fi = 100s of mA)
- **High Complexity** (Bluetooth pairing overhead)
- **Short Battery Life** (months, not years)
- **Inability to Scale** (typical Bluetooth piconet = 8 devices)

ZigBee was designed explicitly to solve the **last-mile sensor problem**: connecting thousands of low-duty-cycle sensors in industrial, home, and biomedical environments with multi-year battery life.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The ZigBee Protocol Stack Architecture

ZigBee builds on two layers of IEEE 802.15.4 and adds three upper layers of its own:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Application Layer (APL)} &\rightarrow \text{ZigBee (CSA Defined)} \\
\text{Application Support Sublayer (APS)} &\rightarrow \text{ZigBee (CSA Defined)} \\
\text{Network Layer (NWK)} &\rightarrow \text{ZigBee (CSA Defined)} \\
\hline
\text{MAC Layer} &\rightarrow \text{IEEE 802.15.4} \\
\text{PHY Layer} &\rightarrow \text{IEEE 802.15.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Layer-by-Layer Functional Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Layer (PHY) — IEEE 802.15.4**
> - Performs **radio activation/deactivation**, **channel frequency selection**, and **transmit/receive packet formatting**.
> - At 2.4 GHz, the band is divided into **16 channels** (channels 11–26), each spaced **5 MHz** apart (center frequencies: $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz, $k \in \{11, 12, \dots, 26\}$).
> - Uses **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** with a chip rate of **2 Mchips/s**, yielding a processing gain of $G_p = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000} = 8$ (≈ **9 dB**).

> [!NOTE]
> **MAC Layer — IEEE 802.15.4**
> - Manages **beacon management**, **channel access (CSMA/CA)**, **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot)** allocation, and **frame acknowledgment**.
> - Generates **16-bit** short addresses (allowing $\leq$ 65,536 devices) and supports **64-bit** IEEE extended addresses.
> - Uses two types of channel access: **Slotted CSMA/CA** (beacon-enabled) and **Unslotted CSMA/CA** (non-beacon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Layer (NWK) — ZigBee Alliance**
> - Handles **network formation**, **device joining/leaving**, **address assignment**, **routing** (tree routing, table routing, mesh routing), and **security key management**.
> - Maintains the **Neighbor Table**, **Routing Table**, and **Binding Table**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Support Sublayer (APS) — ZigBee Alliance**
> - Provides **end-to-end message delivery** between application objects on different devices (binding tables map source EP to destination EP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Layer (APL) — ZigBee Alliance**
> - Hosts **Application Objects** via **Endpoints (EPs)** numbered 1–240. EP 0 is the ZDO (ZigBee Device Object); EP 255 is the broadcast endpoint.
> - Profiles include **ZigBee Home Automation (HA)**, **ZigBee Light Link (ZLL)**, **ZigBee Health Care**, and **ZigBee 3.0**.

## 2.2 ZigBee Device Types

A ZigBee PAN has three logical device classes:

| Device Type | Function | Memory/RAM Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Coordinator (ZC)** | Single per PAN; forms network, allocates short addresses, acts as trust center | ~64–128 KB RAM |
| **Router (ZR)** | Optional; relays frames, allows more devices to join | ~32–64 KB RAM |
| **End Device (ZED)** | Sleep most of the time; can ONLY talk to its parent | ~4–16 KB RAM |

## 2.3 Network Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IEEE 802.15.4 defines **Star** topology natively. ZigBee extends this to support **Tree** and **Mesh** (the two killer features of ZigBee).

- **Star** — All devices communicate only with the PAN coordinator. Used in simple home automation.
- **Tree** — Coordinator forms root; routers extend branches hierarchically. Routing uses a tree algorithm.
- **Mesh** — Any router/coord can talk to any other via multi-hop. Provides **self-healing** — if one link dies, the route auto-recomputes.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — Equations You MUST Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz | 2.4 GHz center frequency, $k \in \{11,\dots,26\}$ | MHz |
| 2 | $N_{ch} = \dfrac{BW_{band}}{BW_{ch}}$ | Number of channels | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G_p = \dfrac{R_{chip}}{R_{data}} = \dfrac{2\,Mchip/s}{250\,kbps} = 8$ | DSSS Processing Gain | ratio (×) or dB |
| 4 | $G_p\,(dB) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R_{chip}}{R_{data}}\right) \approx 9.03\,dB$ | Processing gain in dB | dB |
| 5 | $N_{devices} \leq 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ | Maximum short-addressed nodes | nodes |
| 6 | $N_{endpoints} = 240$ | Per-device application endpoints | EPs |
| 7 | $t_{slot} = 320\,\mu s$ | Superframe slot duration (backoff unit) | µs |
| 8 | $t_{slot} = 15.36\,ms$ | One superframe base (when BO=SO=0, in symbols) | ms |
| 9 | $T_{frame} = 15.36 \times 2^{BO}$ ms | Beacon Interval (BI) | ms |
| 10 | $T_{active} = 15.36 \times 2^{SO}$ ms | Superframe Duration (SD) | ms |
| 11 | $C_{cap} = \dfrac{2^{SO} - 1 - 2^{SO}}{...}$ (GTS) | Capacity of contention period | slots |
| 12 | $N_{GTS} \leq 7$ | Maximum GTS slots in a superframe | slots |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{useful}}{R_{raw}} = \dfrac{R_{payload}}{250\,kbps}$ | MAC efficiency | ratio |
| 14 | $E_{link} = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_t}{P_r}\right)$ | Path loss (linear) to dB | dB |
| 15 | $P_{RX} = I_{RX} \cdot V_{dd} \cdot t_{active}$ | Energy per receive event | Joules |
| 16 | $L_{batt} \approx \dfrac{C_{batt}\,(\text{mAh})}{I_{avg}\,(\text{mA})}$ hours | Battery life estimate | hours |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ZigBee is the de-facto wireless backbone of:
- **Smart Home**: Philips Hue bulbs, IKEA TRÅDFRI, Amazon Echo Plus hub.
- **Industrial Sensing**: WirelessHART (derived from IEEE 802.15.4).
- **Smart Metering / AMI**: Automatic Meter Reading in electricity/gas grids.
- **Biomedical**: Wireless body-worn sensors for ECG, SpO₂, temperature (low duty cycle = safe SAR, long battery).
- **Asset Tracking**: RFID-like tracking in hospitals and warehouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DSSS Processing Gain (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The 2.4 GHz PHY layer of IEEE 802.15.4 uses DSSS with a chip rate of **2 Mchips/s** and a data rate of **250 kbps**. Compute the processing gain in linear scale and in dB.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1** — Define processing gain as the ratio of chip rate to data rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p &= \dfrac{R_{chip}}{R_{data}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the standard 802.15.4 values for the 2.4 GHz O-QPSK PHY:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p &= \dfrac{2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{chips/s}}{250 \times 10^{3}\,\text{chips/s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Simplify the ratio (factor of 8):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p &= \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000} = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Convert linear processing gain to dB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p\,(dB) &= 10\,\log_{10}(8) \\
&= 10 \times 0.90309\ldots \\
&\approx 9.03\,dB
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $G_p = 8$ (linear) $\approx \mathbf{9.03\,dB}$ in dB.

**Why it matters:** A higher $G_p$ means a signal can be recovered even when the received power drops **below the noise floor** — a critical feature for low-power biomedical wearables operating in electrically noisy hospitals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: 2.4 GHz Channel Center Frequencies

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For IEEE 802.15.4 channel number $k = 17$, find the center carrier frequency. Verify that channel $k = 11$ lies at 2405 MHz and $k = 26$ at 2480 MHz.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1** — Recall the standard 2.4 GHz center frequency equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c(k) = 2405 + 5(k - 11)\,\,\text{MHz}, \quad k \in \{11, 12, \ldots, 26\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Verify the lower bound (k = 11):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c(11) &= 2405 + 5(11 - 11) \\
&= 2405 + 5(0) \\
&= 2405\,\text{MHz}\quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Verify the upper bound (k = 26):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c(26) &= 2405 + 5(26 - 11) \\
&= 2405 + 5 \times 15 \\
&= 2405 + 75 \\
&= 2480\,\text{MHz}\quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute for the requested channel k = 17:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c(17) &= 2405 + 5(17 - 11) \\
&= 2405 + 5 \times 6 \\
&= 2405 + 30 \\
&= 2435\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** Channel 17 → **2435 MHz** (this is one of the four "ZigBee channels" least affected by Wi-Fi interference, often recommended in crowded environments).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Number of Devices & Address Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ZigBee PAN uses 16-bit short addresses. How many devices can be addressed? If each device supports 240 endpoints, how many logical application objects can theoretically be addressed in one PAN?

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1** — Maximum devices from a 16-bit short address:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{devices} &= 2^{16} - 1 \\
&= 65{,}536 - 1 \\
&= 65{,}535\,\text{devices}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Maximum application objects (assuming every device has the maximum 240 endpoints):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{obj} &= N_{devices} \times N_{endpoints} \\
&= 65{,}535 \times 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{obj} &= 65{,}535 \times 240 \\
&= 15{,}728{,}400 \\
&\approx 1.57 \times 10^{7}\,\text{application objects}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** Up to **65,535 devices** and approximately **15.7 million** application objects — orders of magnitude beyond Bluetooth's 8-device piconet.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Superframe Timing and GTS Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The IEEE 802.15.4 superframe has $t_{slot} = 15.36$ ms per slot. For a beacon-enabled PAN with **Beacon Order (BO) = 3** and **Superframe Order (SO) = 2**, compute: (a) the Beacon Interval (BI), (b) the Superframe Duration (SD), and (c) the number of GTS slots available.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1** — Compute Beacon Interval (BI):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BI &= 15.36 \times 2^{BO}\,\text{ms} \\
&= 15.36 \times 2^{3}\,\text{ms} \\
&= 15.36 \times 8\,\text{ms} \\
&= 122.88\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute Superframe Duration (SD):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SD &= 15.36 \times 2^{SO}\,\text{ms} \\
&= 15.36 \times 2^{2}\,\text{ms} \\
&= 15.36 \times 4\,\text{ms} \\
&= 61.44\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute Inactive Portion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{inactive} &= BI - SD \\
&= 122.88 - 61.44 \\
&= 61.44\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Maximum GTS slots (a property of the standard):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{GTS}^{max} &= 7\,\text{slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $BI = 122.88$ ms, $SD = 61.44$ ms, $t_{inactive} = 61.44$ ms, $N_{GTS}^{max} = 7$ slots. Devices sleep during the inactive portion to conserve energy.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ZigBee vs Competing Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this comparison table for KTU ESE Part A/B questions.**

| Parameter | ZigBee (802.15.4) | Bluetooth (802.15.1) | Wi-Fi (802.11b/g/n) | LoRa (LPWAN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2.4 / 868 / 915 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz |
| Data Rate | **250 kbps** | 1–3 Mbps | 11–600+ Mbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps |
| Range | 10–100 m | 1–10 m | 30–100 m | 2–15 km |
| Tx Current | ~30 mA | ~40 mA | ~200+ mA | ~100 mA |
| Sleep Current | **~1 µA** | ~10 µA | Not sleep-friendly | ~1 µA |
| Battery Life | **Years** | Days–Weeks | Hours | Years |
| Max Nodes | **> 65,000** | 8 (piconet) | ~32 (typical AP) | Millions (server-side) |
| Topology | Star/Tree/**Mesh** | Piconet/Scatternet | Star | Star |
| Stack Size | ~28–120 KB | ~100+ KB | > 1 MB | ~32 KB |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ZigBee Protocol Stack (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1["APPLICATION OBJECTS 1..240"]
    ZDO["ZDO ENDPOINT 0 DEVICE MGMT"]
    APS["APS LAYER BINDING TABLES SECURITY"]
    NWK["NWK LAYER ROUTING MESH TREE ADDRESSING"]
    MAC["MAC LAYER CSMA CA GTS BEACONS"]
    PHY["PHY LAYER 2.4 GHZ OQPSK DSSS 16 CHANNELS"]
    AIR["RADIO AIR INTERFACE"]
    APP1 --> APS
    ZDO --> APS
    APS --> NWK
    NWK --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
    PHY --> AIR
```

## 4.2 ZigBee Network Topologies Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        S_C["PAN COORDINATOR"]
        S_E1["END DEVICE 1"]
        S_E2["END DEVICE 2"]
        S_E3["END DEVICE 3"]
        S_E4["END DEVICE 4"]
        S_C --- S_E1
        S_C --- S_E2
        S_C --- S_E3
        S_C --- S_E4
    end

    subgraph MESH["MESH TOPOLOGY SELF HEALING"]
        M_C["COORDINATOR"]
        M_R1["ROUTER 1"]
        M_R2["ROUTER 2"]
        M_R3["ROUTER 3"]
        M_E1["END DEVICE A"]
        M_E2["END DEVICE B"]
        M_C --- M_R1
        M_C --- M_R2
        M_C --- M_R3
        M_R1 --- M_R2
        M_R2 --- M_R3
        M_R1 --- M_E1
        M_R2 --- M_E2
    end
```

## 4.3 Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF["BEACON INTERVAL BI 122.88 MS"]
        direction LR
        BEACON["BEACON 0 MS"]
        CAP["CAP CONTENTION ACCESS 0 TO SD 61.44 MS"]
        CFP["CFP GTS SLOTS 1 TO 7"]
        INACTIVE["INACTIVE PORTION 61.44 MS"]
        BEACON --> CAP --> CFP --> INACTIVE
    end
```

## 4.4 Data Frame Flow — End-to-End Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOURCE APPLICATION OBJECT EP 5"]
    B["APS LAYER ADD FRAME HEADER"]
    C["NWK LAYER ADD ROUTE HEADER"]
    D["MAC LAYER CSMA CA BACKOFF"]
    E["PHY LAYER DSSS OQPSK MODULATION"]
    F["RADIO CHANNEL 2.4 GHZ"]
    G["PHY RX DEMODULATION"]
    H["MAC RX ACK FRAME"]
    I["NWK ROUTE FORWARD OR DELIVER"]
    J["APS DELIVER TO DEST EP 12"]
    K["DEST APPLICATION OBJECT EP 12"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define ZigBee. List any THREE features of ZigBee that distinguish it from Wi-Fi and Bluetooth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ZigBee is a low-power, low-data-rate, short-range wireless networking protocol built on **IEEE 802.15.4**, used primarily for **Personal Area Networks (PANs)** in IoT and home automation.

Three distinguishing features:

1. **Low Power Consumption** — Typical sleep current of ~1 µA enables multi-year battery life, unlike Wi-Fi (~200+ mA) which is mains-powered.
2. **Mesh Networking** — Supports self-healing mesh with **> 65,000 nodes**, far exceeding Bluetooth's 8-device piconet limit.
3. **Low Cost & Low Complexity** — Stack size of ~28–120 KB, ideal for resource-constrained sensors; 250 kbps raw data rate is sufficient for sensor telemetry.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Explain the role of the ZigBee Coordinator, Router, and End Device. Why can End Devices be battery-powered for years?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Coordinator (ZC):** One per PAN. Forms the network, assigns 16-bit short addresses, stores binding tables, and acts as the trust center for security keys. It cannot sleep.
- **Router (ZR):** Relays frames between nodes, extends network coverage, and permits other devices to join through it. It also cannot sleep while relaying.
- **End Device (ZED):** A leaf node that communicates **only** with its parent (coordinator or router). It spends > 99% of its time in deep sleep, waking only to send short bursts of sensor data.

**Why long battery life?** End devices use **duty-cycling** — they wake for a few milliseconds to transmit/receive, then sleep with a current of ~1 µA. The duty cycle $\delta = t_{active} / t_{period}$ can be as low as **0.1%**, so the average current $I_{avg} = \delta \cdot I_{active} + (1-\delta)\cdot I_{sleep}$ drops to a few tens of µA, yielding battery life of **1–5 years** on 2 × AA cells.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — ZIGBEE PROTOCOL STACK & LAYERED ARCHITECTURE

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Adaptation]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **ZigBee protocol stack** in detail, clearly identifying the **IEEE 802.15.4 layers** versus the **ZigBee Alliance (CSA) layers**. Discuss the function of each layer.

> **(b)** [7 marks] With the help of a diagram, compare the **Star, Tree, and Mesh topologies** supported by ZigBee. Justify why **Mesh topology is preferred** for industrial IoT deployments.

#### Model Solution

**(a) ZigBee Protocol Stack (7 marks):**

The ZigBee stack consists of **5 layers**, of which the **bottom two** are defined by **IEEE 802.15.4** and the **top three** by the **ZigBee Alliance (Connectivity Standards Alliance)**.

| Layer | Defined By | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer (APL)** | ZigBee | Hosts application objects; supports profiles (Home Automation, Health Care, ZigBee 3.0) |
| **Application Support Sublayer (APS)** | ZigBee | Binding tables; end-to-end ACK; security at application level |
| **Network Layer (NWK)** | ZigBee | Network formation; address assignment; tree/mesh routing; device join/leave |
| **MAC Layer** | IEEE 802.15.4 | CSMA/CA; GTS; beacon management; frame validation; ACK |
| **PHY Layer** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz O-QPSK; DSSS; 16 channels; Tx/Rx; ED, CCA |

**Detailed layer functions (to write in exam):**

- **PHY (802.15.4):** Performs modulation/demodulation, channel selection (channels 11–26 in 2.4 GHz), transmitter/receiver switching, energy detection (ED), and clear channel assessment (CCA). Uses 2 Mchip/s DSSS, yielding ~9 dB processing gain.
- **MAC (802.15.4):** Manages channel access via CSMA/CA, generates and validates 802.15.4 frames, handles acknowledgments and GTS allocation. Provides 16-bit short addresses and supports 64-bit IEEE extended addresses.
- **NWK (ZigBee):** Forms the PAN, assigns addresses using a distributed scheme (Cskip), builds and maintains **Neighbor**, **Routing**, and **Binding** tables. Performs tree routing (default) or mesh routing (AODV-like).
- **APS (ZigBee):** Maps source endpoint → destination endpoint via **binding tables**, supports fragmented/reassembled transfers, and applies application-layer security keys.
- **APL (ZigBee):** Hosts user-defined application objects (e.g., On/Off cluster, Temperature cluster from the ZigBee Cluster Library). Manages 240 endpoints per device.

**(b) Topology Comparison (7 marks):**

[Draw the three topology diagrams as in Section 4.2]

| Topology | Range | Reliability | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | Short | Low (single point of failure) | Low | Simple home automation, ~5 devices |
| **Tree** | Medium | Medium (single point of failure per branch) | Medium | Hierarchical sensor deployments |
| **Mesh** | Long (multi-hop) | **High (self-healing)** | Higher | Industrial IoT, hospitals, smart cities |

**Why Mesh is preferred for industrial IoT (3 marks additional):**
1. **Self-healing:** If a router fails, the network re-routes automatically through alternative paths — critical for factories where a broken cable/router would otherwise halt production.
2. **Scalability:** Mesh allows > 65,000 nodes, suitable for dense industrial sensor deployments.
3. **Extended Range:** Multi-hop relaying covers large factories (hundreds of meters) without extra infrastructure.
4. **Redundancy:** Multiple paths ensure latency-sensitive biomedical alarms (e.g., ICU ventilator alerts) are never lost.

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — IEEE 802.15.4 PHYSICAL LAYER & SUPERFRAME

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper Adaptation]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **IEEE 802.15.4 Physical Layer** specifications. Compute the **processing gain** at 2.4 GHz and list the **16 channel center frequencies**.

> **(b)** [7 marks] With a neat diagram, describe the **IEEE 802.15.4 superframe structure** in a beacon-enabled PAN. Define Beacon Order (BO) and Superframe Order (SO). For BO = 4, SO = 2, compute BI, SD, and the maximum GTS slots.

#### Model Solution

**(a) IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Layer (7 marks):**

The 802.15.4 PHY operates in three bands: **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (USA)**, and **2.4 GHz (global)**.

For 2.4 GHz (the most important for KTU syllabus):

- **Modulation:** Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (O-QPSK)
- **Chip Rate:** 2 Mchip/s (DSSS)
- **Data Rate:** 250 kbps
- **Channels:** 16 (numbered 11–26), center frequencies given by $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz

**Processing Gain Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_p &= \dfrac{R_{chip}}{R_{data}} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000} = 8 \\
G_p\,(dB) &= 10\,\log_{10}(8) \approx 9.03\,dB
\end{aligned}
$$

**The 16 Channel Center Frequencies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c(11) &= 2405\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(12) = 2410\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(13) = 2415\,\text{MHz}, \\
f_c(14) &= 2420\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(15) = 2425\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(16) = 2430\,\text{MHz}, \\
f_c(17) &= 2435\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(18) = 2440\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(19) = 2445\,\text{MHz}, \\
f_c(20) &= 2450\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(21) = 2455\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(22) = 2460\,\text{MHz}, \\
f_c(23) &= 2465\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(24) = 2470\,\text{MHz}, \quad f_c(25) = 2475\,\text{MHz}, \\
f_c(26) &= 2480\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note for biomedical applications:** Channels **15, 20, 25, 26** (and others away from Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, 11) are preferred in hospitals to avoid co-channel interference with Wi-Fi APs.

**(b) Superframe Structure (7 marks):**

[Draw the diagram from Section 4.3]

The superframe is bounded by **beacons** transmitted by the PAN coordinator. It has two regions:

- **Contention Access Period (CAP):** Devices use **slotted CSMA/CA** to compete for the channel. A backoff period is $t_{slot} = 320\,\mu s \times 20 = ...$ (KTU expects the standard 320 µs backoff-slot value).
- **Contention-Free Period (CFP):** Up to **7 GTS** slots are reserved for low-latency, time-critical traffic (e.g., biomedical alarms).

**Beacon Order (BO):** Controls the **Beacon Interval (BI)**, i.e., how often the coordinator transmits a beacon.
**Superframe Order (SO):** Controls the **Superframe Duration (SD)**, i.e., the active portion within BI.

**Constraint:** $0 \leq SO \leq BO \leq 14$

**Formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BI &= 15.36 \times 2^{BO}\,\text{ms} \\
SD &= 15.36 \times 2^{SO}\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Computation (BO = 4, SO = 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BI &= 15.36 \times 2^{4} = 15.36 \times 16 = 245.76\,\text{ms} \\
SD &= 15.36 \times 2^{2} = 15.36 \times 4 = 61.44\,\text{ms} \\
t_{inactive} &= BI - SD = 245.76 - 61.44 = 184.32\,\text{ms} \\
N_{GTS}^{max} &= 7\,\text{slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

End devices sleep during the 184.32 ms inactive period → significant energy savings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks in ZigBee questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IEEE vs ZigBee layers.** Examiner expects you to clearly write "IEEE 802.15.4" for PHY + MAC and "ZigBee Alliance (CSA)" for NWK + APS + APL. Writing "ZigBee" for the lower two layers is a **−2 mark penalty**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 16-bit short address limit.** A common question asks "How many devices can join?" — students often answer 2^64 (this is the EXTENDED address). For PAN addressing, the answer is **65,535**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up BI and SD formulas.** $BI = 15.36 \times 2^{BO}$ uses BO, not SO. $SD = 15.36 \times 2^{SO}$ uses SO. Carrying the wrong variable is a **−1 mark** error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the constraint $0 \leq SO \leq BO \leq 14$.** A valid superframe REQUIRES SO ≤ BO.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of CSMA/CA in CAP.** Any superframe question without CSMA/CA loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Calling ZigBee a "Bluetooth replacement" or "Wi-Fi alternative."** It is **complementary**, not competitive. State the use case (low-duty-cycle sensors), not raw speed.
>
> 7. **In biomedical questions, failing to mention "low SAR," "sleep current," or "duty-cycling"** when justifying why ZigBee is suitable for wearable ECG/SpO₂ sensors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize before ESE**

- ✅ **ZigBee = IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC + ZigBee Alliance (CSA) NWK/APS/APL**
- ✅ 2.4 GHz → 16 channels (11–26) → 250 kbps → O-QPSK → 2 Mchip/s DSSS
- ✅ Processing Gain: $G_p = 8$ (linear) $\approx 9.03$ dB
- ✅ $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz
- ✅ Max short-addressed nodes: **65,535**; max endpoints: 240
- ✅ 3 device types: **Coordinator (1), Router (many), End Device (sleeps)**
- ✅ 3 topologies: **Star, Tree, Mesh** (Mesh = self-healing, preferred for industrial)
- ✅ Superframe: $BI = 15.36 \times 2^{BO}$ ms, $SD = 15.36 \times 2^{SO}$ ms
- ✅ Constraint: $0 \leq SO \leq BO \leq 14$
- ✅ Max **7 GTS slots** in CFP; rest is CAP using **slotted CSMA/CA**
- ✅ Backoff slot: **320 µs** = 20 symbols
- ✅ Channel access: CSMA/CA (not CDMA, not TDMA in CAP)
- ✅ Sleep current ~1 µA → multi-year battery life on 2 × AA cells
- ✅ Comparison mantra: **ZigBee = many nodes + long battery; Wi-Fi = speed; Bluetooth = cable replacement; LoRa = kilometers**
- ✅ Biomedical use: Wearable ECG/SpO₂, patient monitoring, hospital asset tracking — justify via **low SAR, duty-cycling, mesh reliability**
- ✅ Security: AES-128-CCM at MAC and APS layers; trust center = coordinator
- ✅ **Profiles:** Home Automation (HA), Health Care, Light Link (ZLL), ZigBee 3.0 (unified)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Network Layer in the IoT Protocol Stack

The **Network Layer** (Layer 3 of the OSI / Internet reference model) is the architectural stratum responsible for **end-to-end packet forwarding, logical addressing, routing, and path determination** across heterogeneous physical and link-layer substrates. In the Internet of Things (IoT), the Network Layer is *not* a carbon copy of the classical Internet's IPv4/IPv6 stack — it is a **constrained, energy-aware, and lossy-network optimized** adaptation of the same principles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBRT402 – Module 2):**
> *"The Network Layer in IoT is responsible for packet routing, addressing, and forwarding across constrained devices and lossy networks (LLNs). It encompasses technologies such as **6LoWPAN, RPL, IPv4, IPv6**, and adaptation mechanisms that allow low-power nodes to participate in the global Internet."*

In the KTU reference architecture, Module 2 positions the Network Layer **between the MAC/PHY (Data Link) Layer below and the Transport/Application Layers above**, serving as the *glue* that converts link-local reachability into routable, multi-hop, end-to-end communication.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office of the IoT"

Imagine a **massive courier company** that must deliver millions of tiny parcels every day:

| Real-World Entity | Network Layer Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Postal address on envelope | IP address (logical address) |
| Sorting hub / dispatch office | Router (LLN Border Router – LBR) |
| Delivery route map | Routing table / RPL DODAG |
| Postman on a bicycle | Constrained node (sensor mote) |
| Speed-post (priority) packet | Traffic class / DSCP bits |
| Damaged, partial parcel | Fragmented 6LoWPAN frame |

Just as a postal network does not care **what** is inside the parcel (clothes, books, gadgets), the Network Layer does not look at application payloads — it only worries about **"where to send it next?"** and **"how to reach the destination efficiently?"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Network Layer in IoT trades the *abundance* of the classical Internet (unlimited power, wired backhaul) for **scarcity** — limited CPU, kilobytes of RAM, sleep cycles, and duty-cycled radios. This forces a redesign of routing tables, packet headers, and forwarding logic.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Network Layer Services (KTU 2024)

The Network Layer provides the following **five core services** to the upper layers:

1. **Logical Addressing** — Assigns unique hierarchical identifiers (IPv4 = 32-bit, IPv6 = 128-bit) to every IoT node.
2. **Routing** — Determines the *best path* (metric: hop count, ETX, energy, latency) from source to destination.
3. **Packet Forwarding** — Hop-by-hop movement of datagrams based on routing table lookups.
4. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — Splits oversized packets to fit the link-layer MTU (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 MTU = **127 bytes**).
5. **Header Compression** — Reduces IPv6 header overhead (40 bytes) to as low as **2–3 bytes** using IPHC (IP Header Compression).

---

## 1.4 Position in the IoT Protocol Stack

The IoT protocol stack is *thinner* and *more modular* than the legacy TCP/IP stack. Below is the standard KTU reference model showing where the Network Layer sits:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  APPLICATION LAYER  (CoAP, MQTT, HTTP)│
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  TRANSPORT LAYER    (UDP / TCP / DTLS) │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ★ NETWORK LAYER ★  (IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL)│
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ADAPTATION LAYER   (6LoWPAN Shims)    │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  DATA-LINK / MAC     (IEEE 802.15.4)   │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PHYSICAL LAYER      (868 MHz / 2.4 GHz)│
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the Adaptation Layer drawn *below* the Network Layer in the IoT stack?**
> Because 6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, RFC 6282) is *not* a transport or application construct — it is a **shim layer** that sits between IPv6 and IEEE 802.15.4 to make the marriage between the **128-bit IPv6 world** and the **127-byte frame world** possible. Functionally, it is part of the Network Layer's operation.

---

## 1.5 Metrics That Define the "Constrained" Network Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus emphasizes the difference between a "normal" Network Layer and an IoT/WSN Network Layer through the following **standard RFC 7228 / RFC 5867 metrics**:

| Metric Class | Class 0 (Full) | Class 1 (Limited) | Class 2 (Highly Constrained) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Code space | ~Mbytes | ~100 Kbytes | ~50 Kbytes |
| RAM | ~Mbytes | ~10 Kbytes | ~10 Kbytes |
| Power | Mains | Battery (months) | Battery (years) |
| Typical Node | Smartphone | IoT gateway | Sensor mote (e.g., CC2650) |

The Network Layer protocols (RPL, 6LoWPAN, CoAP) are explicitly engineered to operate on **Class 1 and Class 2** devices.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IPv4 vs IPv6 in IoT — Why IPv6 is the Default

IPv4 (32-bit) provides **4.29 × 10⁹** unique addresses, which sounds large but is **exhausted** (IANA 2011). IPv6 (128-bit) provides **3.4 × 10³⁸** addresses — enough to assign a unique address to **every atom on Earth's surface, twice over**.

For IoT, IPv6 is preferred because:
- **Stateless Address Auto-Configuration (SAA / SLAAC)** removes the need for a DHCP server in the field.
- **Hierarchical addressing** enables efficient aggregation in routing tables.
- **Mandatory IPsec support** (originally mandated by the IPv6 RFC) provides hop-by-hop and end-to-end security.
- **Larger address space** eliminates NAT, which is critical for **end-to-end** IoT communication (e.g., a sensor directly reachable by a doctor's tablet).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** IEEE 802.15.4 (the dominant low-rate WPAN PHY/MAC for IoT) has a maximum frame size of **127 bytes** (a-MaxMACPayloadSize = 102). An uncompressed IPv6 header alone consumes 40 bytes, leaving only **81 bytes** for payload — clearly impractical. This is the **exact reason** 6LoWPAN was standardized (RFC 4944 → RFC 6282).

---

## 2.2 6LoWPAN — IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN

**6LoWPAN** is the *adaptation layer* that makes IPv6 packets fit inside IEEE 802.15.4 frames. It performs four key functions:

| Function | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Header Compression (IPHC)** | Reduces 40-byte IPv6 header to 2–7 bytes | High |
| **Fragmentation** | Splits oversized IPv6 packets across multiple 802.15.4 frames | High |
| **Mesh Forwarding** | Enables link-layer multi-hop without routing | Medium |
| **Stateless Autoconfig** | Derives IPv6 IID from EUI-64 (link-layer address) | Medium |

### 2.2.1 IPHC Header Compression (RFC 6282)

The IPHC encoding uses a 2-byte (13-bit significant) dispatch followed by context-dependent fields. The compression ratio is:

$$ C_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{L_{\text{IPv6 header}}}{L_{\text{6LoWPAN compressed header}}} $$

For a typical UDP/IPv6 packet, the savings are:

$$ L_{\text{original}} = 40 \, (\text{IPv6}) + 8 \, (\text{UDP}) = 48 \, \text{bytes} $$

$$ L_{\text{compressed}} \approx 6 \, (\text{IPHC}) + 4 \, (\text{NHC-UDP}) = 10 \, \text{bytes} $$

$$ C_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{48}{10} = 4.8 \times \text{ compression} $$

---

## 2.3 RPL — Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks (RFC 6550)

RPL is the **de-facto IoT routing protocol** standardized by the IETF ROLL (Routing over Low-Power and Lossy Networks) working group. It is a **proactive distance-vector** protocol that builds a **Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph (DODAG)** rooted at a **sink** (the LLN Border Router, LBR).

### 2.3.1 Core RPL Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Acronym | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **DODAG** | Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph | The logical tree rooted at the sink |
| **DIO** | DODAG Information Object | Sent *down* by parents advertising the DODAG |
| **DAO** | DODAG Advertisement Object | Sent *up* by children to populate routing tables |
| **DIS** | DODAG Information Solicitation | Used by a node to request DIOs |
| **LBR** | LLN Border Router | The DODAG root (sink) |
| **OF** | Objective Function | Defines the routing metric (e.g., OF0, MRHOF) |
| **Rank** | Distance from root | Monotonically increases *away* from the root |
| **ETX** | Expected Transmission Count | Default metric for MRHOF |

### 2.3.2 Objective Functions — The "Brain" of RPL

- **OF0 (Objective Function Zero, RFC 6552):** Uses **hop count** as the primary metric. Simple, energy-efficient, predictable.
- **MRHOF (Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function, RFC 6719):** Uses **ETX** (Expected Transmission Count). Adapts to changing link quality, but with **hysteresis** to prevent route flaps.

ETX is calculated as:

$$ \text{ETX}_{\text{link}} = \frac{1}{d_f \cdot d_r} $$

where:
- $d_f$ = probability of successful **forward** delivery
- $d_r$ = probability of successful **reverse** delivery

The **path ETX** (sum along a route) is what MRHOF minimizes.

---

## 2.4 Addressing in IoT — EUI-64 and IID Derivation

An IEEE 802.15.4 device has a **64-bit Extended Unique Identifier (EUI-64)**. The IPv6 Interface Identifier (the lower 64 bits of an IPv6 address) is derived by:

1. Inserting `FF FE` in the middle of the EUI-64.
2. Flipping the **Universal/Local (U/L) bit** — the 7th bit of the first byte (the bit that is set to 1 for global unicast in IEEE 802 MAC addresses).

If the device uses an **8-byte short address (RFC 4944)**, the IID is formed by:

$$ \text{IID} = 00 : 00 : 00 : \text{FF} : \text{FE} : 00 : \text{XXXX : XXXX} $$

where `XXXX:XXXX` is the 16-bit short address.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula, metric, and constant** you may be tested on for the Network Layer topic in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPv4 address space | $N_{\text{IPv4}} = 2^{32}$ | $\approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ addresses |
| 2 | IPv6 address space | $N_{\text{IPv6}} = 2^{128}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses |
| 3 | IPv6 header length | $L_{H,\text{IPv6}} = 40$ | Bytes (fixed) |
| 4 | IPv4 header length | $L_{H,\text{IPv4}} = 20$–$60$ | Bytes (variable, IHL field) |
| 5 | IEEE 802.15.4 MTU | $L_{\text{MTU}} = 127$ | Bytes (a-MaxPHYPacketSize) |
| 6 | Max MAC payload | $L_{\text{payload}} = 102$ | Bytes (a-MaxMACPayloadSize) |
| 7 | 6LoWPAN compression ratio | $C = L_{\text{orig}} / L_{\text{comp}}$ | Typically 4×–5× for UDP/IPv6 |
| 8 | ETX per link | $\text{ETX} = 1 / (d_f \cdot d_r)$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Path ETX | $\text{ETX}_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{ETX}_i$ | Sum of link ETXs |
| 10 | Hop count | $H = N_{\text{routers}}$ | Discrete integer |
| 11 | DODAG rank increase | $\Delta R = R_{\text{parent}} + \text{MinHopRankIncrease}$ | Monotonic, prevents loops |
| 12 | Compression gain | $G = (L_{\text{orig}} - L_{\text{comp}}) / L_{\text{orig}}$ | Expressed as % |
| 13 | IID from EUI-64 | Flip bit 6 of byte 0, insert FF:FE | Bit-level operation |
| 14 | Maximum compression | $L_{\text{min,header}} = 2$ | Bytes (RFC 6282 best case) |

---

## 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Biomedical IoT

In a **patient monitoring system** (e.g., wireless ECG, pulse oximeter, body temperature patch), every byte transmitted **drains the battery**. A 4× compression ratio means a sensor that would have lasted 6 months now lasts **24 months** — a critical clinical factor for implantable or wearable devices.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Philips HealthBand, Abbott FreeStyle Libre, and Medtronic CareLink use **6LoWPAN-like header compression + RPL routing** over Bluetooth Low Energy / IEEE 802.15.4 to relay data from the patient's body to the hospital gateway. The Network Layer choices in Module 2 are *not abstract* — they are the literal foundation of modern telemedicine.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Nodes in a 6LoWPAN Fragment Chain

IEEE 802.15.4 imposes a maximum frame size. When an IPv6 packet is larger than 102 bytes (a-MaxMACPayloadSize), 6LoWPAN performs **fragmentation** at the adaptation layer. Each fragment uses a **Fragment Header (FRAG1 or FRAGN)** with a 16-bit **datagram_size** field and an 11-bit **datagram_tag** field.

**Question:** What is the *maximum* number of fragments a single IPv6 packet can be split into?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the link-layer payload limit.**

$$ L_{\text{payload, max}} = 102 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 2 — Identify the per-fragment header overhead.**

For the first fragment, the header is the **FRAG1** header:

$$ L_{\text{FRAG1}} = 5 \, \text{bytes} = 8 \, \text{bits (Dispatch)} + 16 \, \text{bits (datagram size)} + 8 \, \text{bits (datagram tag)} $$

For subsequent fragments, the header is the **FRAGN** header:

$$ L_{\text{FRAGN}} = 4 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 3 — Determine the largest possible IPv6 packet.**

IPv6 mandates a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of **1280 bytes** (RFC 2460). Therefore:

$$ L_{\text{IPv6, max}} = 1280 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum number of fragments.**

The *first* fragment carries:

$$ L_{\text{data, frag1}} = L_{\text{payload, max}} - L_{\text{FRAG1}} = 102 - 5 = 97 \, \text{bytes} $$

Each *subsequent* fragment carries:

$$ L_{\text{data, fragN}} = L_{\text{payload, max}} - L_{\text{FRAGN}} = 102 - 4 = 98 \, \text{bytes} $$

The total IPv6 payload (after deducting the 40-byte IPv6 header) is:

$$ L_{\text{payload, IPv6}} = 1280 - 40 = 1240 \, \text{bytes} $$

Therefore, the maximum number of fragments is:

$$ N_{\text{fragments}} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{L_{\text{payload, IPv6}} - L_{\text{data, frag1}}}{L_{\text{data, fragN}}} \right\rceil $$

$$ N_{\text{fragments}} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1240 - 97}{98} \right\rceil = 1 + \left\lceil 11.66 \right\rceil = 1 + 12 = 13 $$

**Result:**

$$ \boxed{N_{\text{fragments, max}} = 13} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11 bits for datagram_tag?** Because the tag is reset only on a new datagram, and 2048 simultaneous fragmentations per node are more than enough for any practical LLN.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compression Gain in a UDP/IPv6 Packet

**Question:** A temperature sensor transmits a UDP/IPv6 packet carrying a 20-byte payload over IEEE 802.15.4. Calculate the *compression gain* and the *number of link-layer frames* required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Uncompressed header size.**

$$ L_{H,\text{uncomp}} = L_{\text{IPv6}} + L_{\text{UDP}} = 40 + 8 = 48 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 2 — Total uncompressed packet size.**

$$ L_{\text{pkt, uncomp}} = L_{H,\text{uncomp}} + L_{\text{payload}} = 48 + 20 = 68 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 3 — Compressed header size.**

With 6LoWPAN IPHC + NHC-UDP, the compressed header is:

$$ L_{H,\text{comp}} = L_{\text{IPHC}} + L_{\text{NHC-UDP}} = 7 + 4 = 11 \, \text{bytes} $$

(taking a typical mid-range compression where some addresses are elided using context.)

**Step 4 — Total compressed packet size.**

$$ L_{\text{pkt, comp}} = L_{H,\text{comp}} + L_{\text{payload}} = 11 + 20 = 31 \, \text{bytes} $$

**Step 5 — Compute compression gain.**

$$ G = \frac{L_{\text{pkt, uncomp}} - L_{\text{pkt, comp}}}{L_{\text{pkt, uncomp}}} = \frac{68 - 31}{68} = \frac{37}{68} \approx 0.544 $$

$$ \boxed{G \approx 54.4\%} $$

**Step 6 — Determine number of link-layer frames.**

Since 31 bytes < 102 bytes (MAC payload max), **only one 802.15.4 frame** is needed.

If we had *not* compressed the header, 68 bytes would *still* fit in one frame. However, for larger payloads, compression prevents fragmentation, which is the *real* win.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RPL Path Selection Using MRHOF (ETX)

**Question:** Consider an LLN with a sink (LBR) and three candidate parents $P_1, P_2, P_3$. The ETX values from a sensor node $N$ are:

- $ETX_{N \to P_1} = 2.0$, Rank of $P_1$ = $4.0$
- $ETX_{N \to P_2} = 1.5$, Rank of $P_2$ = $5.0$
- $ETX_{N \to P_3} = 3.0$, Rank of $P_3$ = $3.5$

The **MinHopRankIncrease** is **1.0**. Determine which parent $N$ will select, using **MRHOF (ETX-minimizing)** with the **DAG rank formula**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the RPL rank formula.**

The rank of node $N$ via parent $P_i$ is:

$$ R_N(P_i) = R_{P_i} + \text{RankIncrease} $$

The RankIncrease is computed as:

$$ \text{RankIncrease} = \text{MinHopRankIncrease} \times ETX_{\text{link}} $$

with a step of 1.0 (default). So:

$$ \text{RankIncrease}(P_i) = 1.0 \times ETX_{N \to P_i} $$

**Step 2 — Compute rank via each parent.**

For $P_1$:

$$ R_N(P_1) = 4.0 + (1.0 \times 2.0) = 6.0 $$

For $P_2$:

$$ R_N(P_2) = 5.0 + (1.0 \times 1.5) = 6.5 $$

For $P_3$:

$$ R_N(P_3) = 3.5 + (1.0 \times 3.0) = 6.5 $$

**Step 3 — Select the minimum-rank parent.**

$$ R_N^{\min} = \min(6.0, 6.5, 6.5) = 6.0 $$

**Result:** $N$ selects **$P_1$ as its preferred parent**, because it yields the lowest rank (6.0) and therefore the best path to the sink under MRHOF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hysteresis Effect:** If $P_1$'s ETX later rises to 2.5, then $R_N(P_1) = 4.0 + 2.5 = 6.5$, equal to $P_2$. MRHOF requires the *new* parent to beat the current one by a **hysteresis margin (default = 1.0 ETX × 128 in RPL units)** before a parent switch is triggered. This prevents route flaps.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Decision Table

The table below is a high-yield KTU-style decision matrix for choosing the right Network Layer component for a given IoT scenario.

| IoT Scenario | Best Network Layer Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Wearable ECG patch, battery critical | 6LoWPAN + RPL (MRHOF) | Energy-aware ETX routing, header compression |
| Smart agriculture, vast fields, slow links | RPL (OF0, hop-count) | Predictable, low control overhead |
| Industrial factory with mains power | Full IPv6 + TCP | No energy constraint, reliability > compression |
| Home automation, ≤20 nodes | 6LoWPAN + RPL | Standard, mature stack |
| Implantable biomedical sensor | BLE + 6LoWPAN over LL | Ultra-low power, low data rate |
| Smart city lighting | RPL (OF0) + IPHC | Many hops, simple metric |
| Underwater acoustic sensor network | Modified RPL (Acoustic-RPL) | Acoustic channel has very high latency |
| Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) | GeoNetworking + IPv6 | Mobility, geographic addressing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question presents an IoT scenario, identify **(1) the link layer (802.15.4 / BLE / Wi-Fi), (2) the energy budget, and (3) the mobility pattern** before recommending a Network Layer solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    transLayer["Transport Layer: UDP, TCP, DTLS"]
    netLayer["Network Layer: IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL"]
    adaptLayer["Adaptation Layer: 6LoWPAN Shims"]
    macLayer["Data Link MAC: IEEE 802.15.4"]
    phyLayer["Physical Layer: 868 MHz, 2.4 GHz"]
    appLayer --> transLayer --> netLayer --> adaptLayer --> macLayer --> phyLayer
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — RPL DODAG Topology (Single Root)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lbrA["LLN Border Router LBR: Rank 1"]
    node1["Node 1: Rank 2"]
    node2["Node 2: Rank 2"]
    node3["Node 3: Rank 3"]
    node4["Node 4: Rank 3"]
    node5["Node 5: Rank 4"]
    lbrA --- node1
    lbrA --- node2
    node1 --- node3
    node1 --- node4
    node3 --- node5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** In the diagram above, edges point *upward* logically — the rank *increases* as you move away from the LBR. Cycles are forbidden in a DAG (Directed **Acyclic** Graph), guaranteeing that every packet has a finite, loop-free path to the root.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 6LoWPAN Adaptation Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ipv6Pkt["IPv6 Packet: 1280 bytes"]
    dispatch["6LoWPAN Dispatch: IPHC / FRAG1 / FRAGN"]
    compress["Header Compression: IPHC RFC 6282"]
    frag["Fragmentation: datagram_size, datagram_tag"]
    mesh["Mesh Forwarding: 802.15.4 short addr"]
    macFrame["802.15.4 MAC Frame: max 127 bytes"]
    ipv6Pkt --> dispatch
    dispatch --> compress
    dispatch --> frag
    dispatch --> mesh
    compress --> macFrame
    frag --> macFrame
    mesh --> macFrame
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — RPL Control Message Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sink["DODAG Root LBR"]
    child1["Child Node A"]
    child2["Child Node B"]
    sink -- DIO --> child1
    sink -- DIO --> child2
    child1 -- DAO --> sink
    child2 -- DAO --> sink
    child1 -- DIS --> sink
```

> [!TIP]
> **DIO** messages travel *down* the tree (parent → child). **DAO** messages travel *up* the tree (child → parent). **DIS** is a "ping" sent by a node that has not yet heard a DIO.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Explain the role of the Network Layer in an IoT architecture. Why is the classical IPv4 stack insufficient for IoT?**

**Model Answer:**

The Network Layer (Layer 3) is responsible for **logical addressing, packet routing, and end-to-end forwarding** in an IoT system. It determines the path that data takes from a sensor node to the gateway, the cloud, or another peer.

Classical IPv4 is **insufficient** for IoT because:
1. **Address exhaustion** — IPv4's 32-bit space supports only ~4.29 × 10⁹ unique devices, inadequate for the projected **tens of billions** of IoT nodes.
2. **NAT dependency** — IPv4 relies on Network Address Translation, which **breaks end-to-end reachability** — a critical requirement for direct device-to-cloud communication in IoT.
3. **Lack of stateless auto-configuration** — IPv4 needs DHCP infrastructure, which is impractical in dynamically changing LLNs.
4. **No optimized header compression** — IPv4 has no built-in adaptation layer for IEEE 802.15.4.
5. **Larger header overhead** in lossy, low-bandwidth radio environments, leading to poor energy efficiency.

Hence, IoT deployments prefer **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL**.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]
**What is 6LoWPAN? List its four primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**

**6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks)** is an adaptation layer defined in **RFC 4944** and updated in **RFC 6282**, which enables IPv6 packets to be carried over IEEE 802.15.4 low-power radio links.

The four primary functions are:
1. **Header Compression (IPHC)** — Reduces the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as 2 bytes.
2. **Fragmentation and Reassembly** — Splits large IPv6 packets to fit the 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame.
3. **Mesh Link-Layer Forwarding** — Supports multi-hop delivery at the link layer using 16-bit short addresses.
4. **Stateless Address Auto-Configuration** — Derives the IPv6 Interface Identifier from the IEEE EUI-64 link-layer address.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question 3A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Choice A]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of the RPL routing protocol. Explain the formation of a DODAG and the role of DIO, DAO, and DIS control messages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **OF0 (Objective Function Zero)** and **MRHOF (Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function)** in RPL. Which is preferred for a battery-powered medical sensor network, and why?

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) RPL Architecture & DODAG Formation

RPL (RFC 6550) organizes the network as a **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at a single **LLN Border Router (LBR)**. The LBR emits **DIO (DODAG Information Object)** messages containing:
- The **RPLInstanceID**
- The **DODAGID** (root identifier)
- The **DODAGVersionNumber**
- The **Rank** of the sender (starting at 1 for the LBR)
- The **Objective Function** being used

A node receiving a DIO:
1. Verifies that adding the link cost does not violate the **DAG rank monotonicity** rule.
2. Computes its own rank: $R_{\text{self}} = R_{\text{parent}} + \text{RankIncrease}$.
3. If multiple parents are heard, selects the **preferred parent** based on the OF.
4. Sends a **DAO (DODAG Advertisement Object)** *upward* to the chosen parent, populating the parent's routing table.
5. Begins to emit its own DIOs for downstream nodes.

A node that has **not yet heard** a DIO can broadcast a **DIS (DODAG Information Solicitation)** to solicit them.

The DODAG construction follows this sequence:

**LBR** → (DIO) → **Child Nodes** → (DAO back) → **LBR** → (DIO with updated ranks) → **Deeper Nodes** → ...

The result is a **loop-free, rank-ordered, hierarchical topology** where every node has at least one path upward to the root.

#### (b) OF0 vs MRHOF

| Aspect | OF0 (RFC 6552) | MRHOF (RFC 6719) |
|---|---|---|
| Metric | **Hop count** | **ETX** (Expected Transmission Count) |
| Hysteresis | None | **Yes** — prevents route flaps |
| Computational cost | Very low | Moderate (link-quality tracking) |
| Control overhead | Low | Slightly higher (DIO updates on ETX changes) |
| Suitability | Static or low-mobility networks | Dynamic, lossy, high-mobility networks |

**Preferred for battery-powered medical sensors: MRHOF.**

Justification: Medical sensors (e.g., wireless ECG patches) operate in environments where **link quality fluctuates** due to patient movement, body shadowing, and interference. ETX captures **actual delivery probability**, not just hop count. While MRHOF has marginally higher control overhead, the energy saved by **avoiding lossy links** far outweighs the extra DIO traffic. Hysteresis further stabilizes the network, preventing **route-flap-induced battery drain**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse **DIO and DAO directions**.
> - **DIO travels DOWN (root → leaves)** — it advertises the DODAG.
> - **DAO travels UP (leaves → root)** — it populates routing tables.
> - **DIS travels OUT (from a "lost" node)** — it requests DIOs.
> Getting this wrong costs **2–3 marks** immediately.

---

### **Question 3B** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model — Choice B]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the 6LoWPAN header compression mechanism (IPHC, RFC 6282). Show how a typical IPv6/UDP packet is compressed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wireless body-area network (WBAN) is built on IEEE 802.15.4 (MTU 127 bytes, MAC payload 102 bytes). An ECG sensor sends a UDP/IPv6 packet with a 60-byte ECG sample payload. Compute the *number of link-layer frames* required **(i)** without 6LoWPAN compression, and **(ii)** with IPHC + NHC-UDP compression. Assume compressed header size = **11 bytes**.

**Model Answer:**

#### (a) 6LoWPAN Header Compression (IPHC, RFC 6282)
