Each item is encoded as `(major_type << 5) | value`. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Map header (4 items)} \rightarrow 0\text{xA4} \quad (1010\ 0100) \\ &\text{Key "rt" (2 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x62}\ 0\text{x72}\ 0\text{x74} \\ &\text{Value "oic.r.light" (12 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x6C}\ \text{chars} \\ &\text{Key "id" (2 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x62}\ \text{chars} \\ &\text{Value "light-001" (9 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x69}\ \text{chars} \\ &\text{Key "dimmingSetting" (14 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x6E}\ \text{chars} \\ &\text{Value 75 (1 byte, unsigned int)} \rightarrow 0\text{x18}\ 0\text{x4B} \\ &\text{Key "state" (5 bytes)} \rightarrow 0\text{x65}\ \text{chars} \\ &\text{Value true (1 byte)} \rightarrow 0\text{xF5} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total CBOR Size:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CBOR}_{\text{size}} &= 1 + (1+2) + (1+12) + (1+2) + (1+9) + (1+14) + 2 + (1+5) + 1 \\ &= 1 + 3 + 13 + 3 + 10 + 15 + 2 + 6 + 1 \\ &= 54\ \text{bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compression Ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ratio} &= \frac{\text{CBOR}}{\text{JSON}} = \frac{54}{86} \approx 0.628 \\ \text{Savings} &= (1 - 0.628) \times 100\% \approx 37.2\% \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** CBOR saves ~37% of bandwidth, critical for constrained devices transmitting over BLE or LoRa. The savings scale with the number of repeated keys (which are common in IoT telemetry streams). --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: OIC Layer-to-IoTivity Mapping | OIC Logical Layer | IoTivity Implementation | Primary API/Module | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Application** | User App (Android/iOS) | `OCPlatform`, `OCResource` | UI / business logic | | **Framework** | `resource/` directory | `OCResourceRequest`, `OCResourceResponse` | CRUDN handler | | **Base Abstraction** | `stack/` directory | `OCConnectivity`, `OCSecurity` | Transport & security | | **Communication** | `connectivity/` directory | `CATCPAdapter`, `CABLEAdapter`, `CAUDPAdapter` | Wire protocols | | **Platform/OS** | `porting/` directory | OS-specific ports (Linux, Tizen, Arduino) | HAL | --- ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: OIC Device Discovery Process **Step 1 — Device Boot:** A new OIC device (e.g., a smart bulb) powers on and registers its resources in the local resource database. **Step 2 — Multicast Announcement:** The device sends a **CoAP NON-confirmable POST** to the multicast address `224.0.1.187` (or `[FF02::FD]` for IPv6) on UDP port `5683`: $$ \text{Request} = \begin{cases} \text{Method} = \text{POST} \\ \text{URI} = \text{"/oic/res"} \\ \text{Payload} = \{\text{rt:}\ \text{"oic.r.light", id:}\ \text{"bulb-42", dimmingSetting:}\ 0\} \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 — Client Discovery:** A client (e.g., a phone app) sends a **CoAP GET** to the same multicast group: $$ \text{Request} = \begin{cases} \text{Method} = \text{GET} \\ \text{URI} = \text{"/oic/res"} \\ \text{Accept} = \text{application/cbor} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Response Aggregation:** All OIC devices in the network respond with their resource lists. The client now knows the **device ID, resource URIs, and supported interfaces** of every nearby device. **Step 5 — Secure Onboarding:** The client (acting as **Mediator**) provisions the device into a **Security Domain** by exchanging DeviceID, Access Tokens, and Owner Credentials via DTLS-protected channel. **Step 6 — Application Interaction:** The client can now `PUT /oic/r/light/1 {dimmingSetting: 80}` to control the bulb. --- ### 3.4 Mathematical Analysis: CoAP Message Structure A CoAP message (used by OIC) is structured as: $$ \text{CoAP}_{\text{header}} = \begin{Bmatrix} \text{Ver} (2\ \text{bits}) \vert \text{T} (2\ \text{bits}) \vert \text{TKL} (4\ \text{bits}) \vert \text{Code} (8\ \text{bits}) \vert \text{MessageID} (16\ \text{bits}) \end{Bmatrix} $$ **Where:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ver} &= 1\ (\text{CoAP version}) \\ T &\in \{0:\ \text{CON}, 1:\ \text{NON}, 2:\ \text{ACK}, 3:\ \text{RST}\} \\ TKL &\in [0, 8]\ \text{tokens} \\ \text{Code} &= 4 \cdot \text{Class} + \text{Detail} \\ \text{MessageID} &= \text{unique per sender for deduplication} \end{aligned} $$ For example, a **GET** request has: $$ \text{Code} = 4 \times 0 + 1 = 0.01 \rightarrow 0\text{x01} $$ A **2.05 Content** response has: $$ \text{Code} = 4 \times 2 + 5 = 0.25 \rightarrow 0\text{x45} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OIC/OCF Layered Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[Application Layer
Mobile Apps, Cloud Services] --> B[Framework Layer
OCResource, OCConnectivity API] B --> C[Base Abstraction Layer
Security, Serialization, Routing] C --> D[Communication Layer
CoAP over UDP TCP BLE ZigBee] D --> E[Platform and OS Layer
Linux, Tizen, Contiki, RIOT] E --> F[Hardware Sensors and Actuators] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This top-down flow represents how a user request (e.g., "dim the light to 50%") traverses the OIC stack. The Application Layer invokes the Framework, which constructs a CoAP PUT request, the Base Abstraction Layer encrypts it with DTLS, the Communication Layer transmits over BLE/Wi-Fi, the OS manages the radio, and the Hardware executes the PWM dimming. --- ### 4.2 IoTivity Software Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph APP[Application Layer] APP1[Smart Home App] APP2[Health Monitor App] APP3[Industrial Dashboard] end subgraph SDK[SDK Layer] SDK1[IoTivity Java] SDK2[IoTivity C plus plus] SDK3[IoTivity Node JS] end subgraph CORE[IoTivity Core Engine] CORE1[Resource Manager] CORE2[Connectivity Abstraction] CORE3[Security Manager] CORE4[Discovery Engine] end subgraph PLAT[Platform Adapters] PLAT1[Linux Port] PLAT2[Android Port] PLAT3[Tizen Port] PLAT4[Arduino Port] end subgraph HW[Hardware] HW1[WiFi Radio] HW2[BLE Radio] HW3[ZigBee Radio] HW4[Sensors and Actuators] end APP1 --> SDK1 APP2 --> SDK1 APP3 --> SDK3 SDK1 --> CORE1 SDK2 --> CORE1 SDK3 --> CORE1 CORE1 --> CORE2 CORE1 --> CORE3 CORE1 --> CORE4 CORE2 --> PLAT1 CORE2 --> PLAT2 CORE2 --> PLAT3 CORE2 --> PLAT4 PLAT1 --> HW1 PLAT2 --> HW2 PLAT3 --> HW3 PLAT4 --> HW4 ``` --- ### 4.3 OIC Resource Discovery Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Client Device Phone participant Network as Multicast Group FF02 FD participant Server as OIC Server Smart Bulb Client->>Network: GET /oic/res (multicast) Network->>Server: Forward GET Server-->>Network: 2.05 Content with resource list Network-->>Client: Aggregate response Client->>Server: GET /oic/r/light/1 Server-->>Client: 2.05 Content with light properties Client->>Server: PUT /oic/r/light/1 (dimmingSetting: 80) Server-->>Client: 2.04 Changed ``` --- ### 4.4 IoT Deployment Models Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph CLOUD[Cloud Deployment Model] C1[Devices] --> C2[Cloud Gateway] C2 --> C3[Cloud Server] C3 --> C4[Data Analytics] end subgraph EDGE[Edge Deployment Model] E1[Devices] --> E2[Edge Gateway] E2 --> E3[Local Analytics] end subgraph FOG[Fog Deployment Model] F1[Devices] --> F2[Fog Node] F2 --> F3[Local Processing] F2 --> F4[Cloud Sync] end subgraph HYBRID[Hybrid Deployment Model] H1[Devices] --> H2[Edge Gateway] H2 --> H3[Edge Processing] H2 --> H4[Cloud Backup] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The diagrams above represent the major IoT deployment models. Cloud offers scalability but suffers latency. Edge minimizes latency but has limited compute. Fog distributes compute across the network. Hybrid combines the best of both — local processing for time-critical tasks and cloud for heavy analytics. **KTU frequently asks to compare these in 7-mark questions.** ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Explain the OIC Resource Model with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** The OIC Resource Model represents every IoT entity as a **resource** identified by a **URI**. A resource has four components: - **URI** — Unique address (e.g., `/oic/r/light/1`) - **Properties** — Key-value pairs (e.g., `dimmingSetting: 50`) - **Representation** — Serialized form (JSON or CBOR) - **Interface** — Allowed operations (e.g., `oic.if.rw`) **Example:** A smart bulb's light resource would expose `state` (boolean) and `brightness` (integer 0–100). Any OCF client can interact with it using standardized GET/PUT requests, regardless of manufacturer. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List and briefly explain any three design principles of the OIC/OCF architecture.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Interoperability:** Ensures devices from different vendors can communicate using common data models and protocols. 2. **Security by Design:** Implements DTLS encryption, device identity (UUID), and per-resource Access Control Lists (ACLs) to protect data and resources. 3. **Discoverability:** Devices automatically announce their presence via multicast CoAP requests, allowing clients to find them without manual configuration. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a) Describe the OIC architecture in detail. Explain the function of each layer with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The OIC (Open Interconnect Consortium) architecture, now maintained by the Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF), is a five-layer reference model designed for seamless device-to-device communication. **Layer 1 — Application Layer:** This is the topmost layer where end-user applications reside. It includes mobile apps (Android/iOS), cloud services, and web dashboards. Applications interact with the Framework Layer via the **Application Framework Interface (AFI)**, which exposes high-level functions for resource discovery, observation, and control. Example: A mobile app that lets a user turn off a smart light from a remote location. **Layer 2 — Framework Layer:** This layer provides a unified API set to applications, abstracting the complexity of lower layers. It implements the **CRUDN operations** (Create, Retrieve, Update, Delete, Notify) and the **Resource Model**. Key components include the Resource Manager, the Discovery Engine, and the Request/Response handler. The Framework also handles **Observers** — clients that subscribe to resource changes and receive automatic notifications. **Layer 3 — Base Abstraction Layer:** This is the most critical layer. It provides: - **Serialization/Deserialization** between JSON (rich devices) and CBOR (constrained devices). - **Security primitives** — DTLS handshake, encryption, decryption, ACL enforcement. - **Message routing** — Multiplexing multiple requests over a single transport. - **Device identity management** — UUID generation, certificate storage. **Layer 4 — Communication Layer:** Defines the **Generic Transport Interface (GTI)** and provides adapter plugins for: - CoAP over UDP (default for IP networks) - CoAP over TCP - CoAP over BLE GATT - CoAP over NFC - ZigBee IP (future) **Layer 5 — Platform/OS Layer:** The lowest layer, providing OS services and hardware abstraction. Supports Linux, Tizen, Android, Contiki, RIOT, FreeRTOS, and bare-metal. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 in this handout) --- **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the IoTivity software architecture. List the main components of IoTivity. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **IoTivity Architecture:** IoTivity is the open-source reference implementation of the OIC/OCF specification. Its software architecture consists of the following blocks: **1. SDK Layer (Top):** - **IoTivity-Java:** SDK for Android and Linux desktop, written in Java. Provides high-level API for building OCF clients and servers. - **IoTivity-C++:** High-performance C++ wrapper around the C SDK. Used for Linux, Windows, and macOS desktops. - **IoTivity-Node:** Node.js binding for cloud-side services and web servers. **2. IoTivity Core Engine (Middle):** - **Resource Manager:** Maintains the local resource database; handles CRUDN operations. - **Connectivity Abstraction (CA):** Pluggable transport adapters; abstracts UDP, TCP, BLE, NFC. - **Security Manager:** Handles DTLS, provisioning, ACL, and Onboarding. - **Discovery Engine:** Sends and processes multicast discovery messages. - **Message Queue:** Asynchronous message handling for high throughput. **3. Platform Adapters (Bottom):** - Linux Port, Android Port, Tizen Port, Arduino Port, etc. **4. Hardware (Bottom):** - Radios (Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee), Sensors, Actuators. **Main Components of IoTivity:** - `iotivity-base` (C SDK) - `iotivity-lite` (C SDK for MCUs) - `iotivity-constrained` (C SDK for OpenOCF) - `iotivity-java` - `iotivity-node` - `iotivity-cpp` - Reference Implementations of resources (e.g., smart home, healthcare) **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 in this handout) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) Explain the different IoT deployment models with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** IoT deployment models describe how devices, gateways, and compute resources are organized. The four main models are: **1. Cloud Deployment Model:** All data and processing occur in the cloud. Devices connect to a cloud gateway, which forwards data to cloud servers. - **Advantages:** Unlimited scalability, centralized analytics, easy updates. - **Disadvantages:** High latency, requires constant internet, privacy concerns. **2. Edge Deployment Model:** Processing happens at the edge, close to the data source (e.g., on the gateway device itself). - **Advantages:** Low latency, works offline, preserves privacy. - **Disadvantages:** Limited compute, higher device cost, difficult to scale. **3. Fog Deployment Model:** A hierarchical model where compute is distributed across multiple tiers — devices, fog nodes, and cloud. - **Advantages:** Balanced latency and scalability, fault tolerance. - **Disadvantages:** Complex orchestration, requires careful resource management. **4. Hybrid Deployment Model:** Combines edge and cloud. Time-critical processing is at the edge; heavy analytics in the cloud. - **Advantages:** Best of both worlds, flexibility. - **Disadvantages:** Higher architectural complexity, synchronization overhead. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4 in this handout) --- **(b) What is the Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)? Explain its design principles and how it evolved into the Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF). (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **OIC Definition:** The **Open Interconnect Consortium (OIC)** was founded in **2014** by Intel, Samsung, and Dell to define a common communications framework for the Internet of Things. **Evolution to OCF:** In **February 2016**, OIC merged with the **AllSeen Alliance** and rebranded as the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)**. The goal was to unify competing IoT standards (OIC's CoAP-based stack and AllJoyn's D-Bus-based stack) under a single specification. OCF absorbed AllJoyn and continued the OIC specification as its core. **Design Principles of OIC/OCF:** 1. **Interoperability** — A single protocol suite for all device types. 2. **Discoverability** — Automatic device discovery via multicast. 3. **Security by Design** — End-to-end encryption, device identity, and access control. 4. **Resource Orientation** — All entities are modeled as discoverable resources with URIs. 5. **Transport Agnosticism** — Works over multiple physical transports. 6. **Scalability** — Designed for billions of devices. 7. **Open Standards** — Based on IETF and IEEE standards (CoAP, DTLS, IPv6, 6LoWPAN). 8. **Open Source Reference Implementation** — IoTivity provided as a free open-source stack. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "OIC and OCF are two different, competing standards." They are the **same organization, rebranded**. Students lose 2 marks for this. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **OIC** with **OCF** or with the older **AllJoyn** standard. Remember: OIC (2014) → OCF (2016, post AllJoyn merger). AllJoyn is **deprecated** since 2017. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing that OIC uses HTTP — it uses **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol) over UDP. Writing HTTP costs 1 mark. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention **DTLS** when asked about security. OIC mandates **DTLS 1.2+** for all secure communication. > - **Common Mistake 5:** For IoTivity, students often miss listing the **lite** version — which is specifically for **microcontrollers** (constrained devices). Examiners consider this a key differentiator. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **OIC** was founded in **2014** by Intel, Samsung, Dell → became **OCF** in **2016**. - **IoTivity** is the **open-source reference implementation** of OIC, hosted by the **Linux Foundation**. - The OIC architecture has **5 layers**: Application, Framework, Base Abstraction, Communication, Platform/OS. - The **Resource Model** is the heart of OIC: every entity is a resource with **URI + Properties + Representation + Interface**. - The default transport is **CoAP over UDP** on port **5683**. - Security uses **DTLS 1.2+** with **AES-CCM**, **UUID-based device IDs**, and per-resource **ACLs**. - Discovery uses **multicast** at `224.0.1.187` (IPv4) or `FF02::FD` (IPv6). - Resource representations use **JSON** (rich devices) or **CBOR** (constrained devices) — CBOR is ~30–40% smaller. - CRUDN operations: **C**reate, **R**etrieve, **U**pdate, **D**elete, **N**otify. - **IoTivity** components: `iotivity-base`, `iotivity-lite` (for MCUs), `iotivity-constrained`, `iotivity-java`, `iotivity-cpp`, `iotivity-node`. - IoT deployment models: **Cloud, Edge, Fog, Hybrid** — Hybrid is the most common in production. - OIC is transport-agnostic: supports **Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, NFC, Ethernet, Thread**. - The **AllSeen Alliance's AllJoyn** merged into OCF in 2016 and is now **deprecated**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2:** "OIC = Standards Body, IoTivity = Open Source Stack. OIC = 2014, OCF = 2016, AllJoyn = Deprecated. CoAP + DTLS = Secure Transport. Resource Model = URI + JSON/CBOR + CRUDN." Memorize this line for the 3-mark short answers — examiners love awarding full marks when the student hits these keywords.

topics.title: Overview - IoTivity Stack Architecture - Resource Model and Abstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview - IoTivity Stack Architecture - Resource Model and Abstraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IoTivity – Formal Academic Definition

**IoTivity** is an **open-source, vendor-neutral software framework** sponsored by the **Open Connectivity Foundation (OCF)** that enables seamless **device-to-device communication** for the Internet of Things (IoT). It provides a uniform application programming interface (API) to discover, configure, control, monitor, and manage heterogeneous devices, sensors, and services across diverse operating systems and communication transports (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, Z-Wave, NFC, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *“IoTivity is a layered, reference-constrained, open-source middleware architecture that abstracts IoT devices as addressable network resources using a RESTful (REpresentational State Transfer) paradigm over CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol), thereby enabling interoperability across heterogeneous IoT ecosystems.”*

## 1.2 Stack Architecture – What Does "Stack" Mean Here?

A **software stack** is a layered set of software components that cooperate to deliver a service. The IoTivity stack is a **vertically integrated protocol-and-service stack** where each layer hands a well-defined interface to the layer above it, and consumes the services of the layer below. This guarantees **portability** and **modularity**.

## 1.3 Resource Model & Abstraction – The Core Idea

The **Resource Model** is the **fundamental data abstraction** in IoTivity. Every physical/digital IoT entity (a heart-rate sensor, a smart bulb, a thermostat) is represented as a **Resource** — a *virtual, network-addressable, addressable-by-URI, stateful object* with **properties**, **attributes**, and exposed **CRUD (Create, Retrieve, Update, Delete)** interfaces.

**Resource Abstraction** is the engineering practice of **hiding the underlying hardware, OS, and transport specifics** behind a uniform REST-like API.

## 1.4 Real-World Analogy (The "Universal Remote" Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – A Smart Universal Remote Control**
>
> Imagine you walk into a room containing a Samsung TV, a Sony soundbar, an LG air-conditioner, and a Philips light. Normally, you would need **four different remote controls** with **four different button layouts** and **four different signalling technologies** (IR vs. RF vs. Wi-Fi).
>
> Now imagine a **single smartphone app** that:
> - **Auto-discovers** every appliance in the room.
> - **Translates** your taps ("increase volume", "dim lights") into the device-specific binary codes each gadget understands.
> - Lets you group gadgets into logical **rooms/scenes** ("Movie Mode" = TV ON + lights DIM + AC 24°C).
>
> That smartphone app is **IoTivity**. The translation layer is the **IoTivity Stack Architecture**. The way each appliance is represented inside the app as an object with **name, type, properties, and methods** is the **Resource Model**. The fact that the app never bothers you with *how* communication happens (Wi-Fi / BLE / ZigBee) is **Resource Abstraction**.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Syllabus High-Yield)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Resource** | A virtual representation of a device/service state, identified by a **URI**. |
| **Resource Type** | A string identifier declaring *what* a resource is (e.g., `oic.r.light`). |
| **Interface** | Defines *how* a resource can be interacted with (e.g., `oic.if.a` = actuator, `oic.if.s` = sensor). |
| **Property / Attribute** | A named, typed data field of a resource (e.g., `brightness = 75`). |
| **Collection Resource** | A parent resource that groups multiple child resources (a “Room” containing bulbs + fans). |
| **CoAP** | **Constrained Application Protocol** — the REST-style UDP transport IoTivity rides on. |
| **OCF** | **Open Connectivity Foundation** — the standards body governing IoTivity. |
| **Discovery** | Process by which a client finds server resources on the network. |
| **LwM2M** | Lightweight M2M — a related (but different) device-management standard. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of IoTivity (The Vertical Stack)

The IoTivity architecture is structured into **six logical layers**, conceptually similar to the OSI model but tailored for constrained IoT environments. From top to bottom:

### Layer 1 – Application / Use-Case Layer
- The end-user smart application (e.g., a healthcare dashboard, a home-automation app).
- Uses the **C/C++/Java/JavaScript IoTivity APIs** (`OCConnectivity`, `OCResource`, `OCPlatform`).
- Issues high-level intents: *“turn on the bulb in Room 102.”*

### Layer 2 – Framework Layer
- Provides the **Resource Hosting** engine — turns a hardware capability into a discoverable, addressable resource.
- Implements the **CRUD Manager** (handles `GET`, `PUT`, `POST`, `DELETE` requests).
- Offers **Discovery APIs** (multicast/unicast), **Group APIs**, **Observe/Notify** semantics.
- Performs **Serialization/Deserialization** using **CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation)** — a compact binary alternative to JSON, optimized for low-bandwidth, low-memory devices.

### Layer 3 – Resource Model & Abstraction Layer ⭐ *(Focus of this lecture)*
- Defines the **resource type system** (e.g., `oic.r.temperature`, `oic.r.heartrate`).
- Implements the **Property Bag** model — every resource is a key-value dictionary.
- Provides a **Device Model** (`oic.d.*`) and a **Vertical Resource Model** (e.g., `oic.r.energy.consumption`).
- Exposes the **Interface Set** (`oic.if.baseline`, `oic.if.rw`, `oic.if.s`).
- Encapsulates **Security** metadata (Access Control Lists / ACLs).

### Layer 4 – Communication Protocol Stack (CoAP + HTTP)
- **CoAP (RFC 7252)** — a UDP-based RESTful protocol with **4-byte headers** and **method semantics mirroring HTTP** (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`).
- **HTTP** fallback for non-constrained devices.
- **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** at this layer provides mutual authentication and encryption for CoAP.
- **Observe pattern** (RFC 7641) — server-push subscriptions (one-time `GET` ⇒ ongoing updates).

### Layer 5 – Transport Layer
- **UDP** (default for CoAP).
- **TCP** (when reliability & ordering outweigh energy cost).
- **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** GATT profile.
- **NFC**, **ZigBee IP**, **Z-Wave IP**, **LoRaWAN** through plug-in interfaces.

### Layer 6 – Network & Hardware Layer
- IPv4 / IPv6, **6LoWPAN** (IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN), **Thread**, Wi-Fi, Ethernet.
- Sensors, microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, Arduino-class), actuators, smart SoCs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Tip:** When asked to “explain the IoTivity architecture,” always draw the **layered diagram** (Top→Bottom: Application → Framework → Resource Model → Communication → Transport → Network/HW) and label **which standard is used at each layer** (e.g., CoAP at L4, UDP at L5, 6LoWPAN at L6). Half the marks go to a correctly labelled diagram.

## 2.2 Why the Stack Is Designed This Way — The “Why”

| Design Principle | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| **Layered separation of concerns** | A smart-light firmware written today can switch from Wi-Fi to Thread tomorrow *without rewriting the application code*. |
| **RESTful / URI addressing** | Reuses 30+ years of web paradigms; engineers don’t need to learn a new paradigm. |
| **CoAP over UDP** | UDP consumes **~80 % less energy** than TCP, critical for battery-powered sensors. |
| **CBOR instead of JSON** | A JSON message of 200 bytes shrinks to ~60 bytes in CBOR — directly extends battery life. |
| **Observe/Notify** | Eliminates the need for the client to *poll* the sensor; saves bandwidth and energy. |
| **Resource abstraction** | A cardiologist’s tablet and a hospital server interact with the *same* heart-rate resource object — device details are invisible. |

## 2.3 Deep Dive – The Resource Model

A **Resource** in IoTivity is formally defined by the tuple:

$$R = \{URI,\ T,\ I,\ P,\ M\}$$

Where:
- $URI$ = Uniform Resource Identifier (e.g., `coap://192.168.1.42:5683/ocf/lite/heartrate/1`).
- $T$ = **Resource Type** (e.g., `oic.r.heartrate`).
- $I$ = **Interface Set** (e.g., `oic.if.s`, `oic.if.baseline`).
- $P$ = **Properties** (key-value bag — `{ "bpm": 72, "unit": "bpm", "range": "30-220" }`).
- $M$ = **Method Bindings** (the CRUD verbs accepted).

### 2.3.1 Resource Types (RT)
Resource Types are namespaced, reverse-DNS strings, e.g.:
- `oic.r.switch.binary` — a binary on/off switch.
- `oic.r.sensor.temperature` — a temperature sensor.
- `oic.r.heartrate` — *(biomedical)* a heart-rate sensor.
- `oic.r.energy.consumption` — a smart-meter reading.

### 2.3.2 Interfaces (IF)
Interfaces define *how* a client may talk to a resource. Standard interfaces include:

| Interface | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `oic.if.baseline` | Default — every resource exposes this; gives `n`, `id`, `rt`, `if` metadata. |
| `oic.if.rw` | Read/Write — the client can GET and PUT. |
| `oic.if.s` | Sensor — read-only auto-updated values. |
| `oic.if.a` | Actuator — control a state (e.g., turn on). |
| `oic.if.ll` | Linked List — exposes collection of linked child URIs. |

### 2.3.3 Property Model
Properties are **strongly typed** using JSON/CBOR data types — integers, floats, booleans, strings, arrays, nested objects. Every property has:
- A **name** (key).
- A **type** (`integer`, `string`, `boolean`, `number`, `array`, `object`).
- A **value**.
- An optional **unit** (SI standard — `°C`, `Pa`, `bpm`, `kWh`).
- An optional **range** (e.g., `"30-220"` for heart rate in beats-per-minute).

### 2.3.4 Collection Resources & Linking
A `Collection Resource` is a parent resource that aggregates children via **Web Linking (RFC 5988)** semantics. Each link element has a `rel`, `href`, `rt`, and `if`. Example representation:

```json
{
  "rt": ["oic.r.icu"],
  "if": ["oic.if.baseline", "oic.if.ll"],
  "links": [
    { "rel": "contains", "href": "/ecg/1", "rt": "oic.r.ecg" },
    { "rel": "contains", "href": "/spo2/1", "rt": "oic.r.spo2" },
    { "rel": "contains", "href": "/temp/1", "rt": "oic.r.temperature" }
  ]
}
```

## 2.4 The Four CRUD Operations over Resources

| Verb | CoAP Code | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `GET` | 0.01 | Retrieve the current representation (state) of a resource. |
| `POST` | 0.02 | Create a new resource or trigger an action. |
| `PUT` | 0.03 | Update an existing resource’s property set. |
| `DELETE` | 0.04 | Remove a resource from a collection. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for biomedical apps:** A wearable ECG patch (e.g., `oic.r.ecg`) continuously **OBSERVES** — a cardiologist’s app **GETs** historical beats; an alert engine uses `oic.if.a` (actuator) on a `oic.r.buzzer` resource to **POST** a “vibrate now” command if arrhythmias are detected.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept | Standard / Equation / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resource definition tuple | $R = \{URI,\ T,\ I,\ P,\ M\}$ |
| 2 | OCF Namespacing | `oic.<category>.<subtype>` (e.g., `oic.r.sensor.temperature`) |
| 3 | CoAP Default Port | **UDP/5683** (unsecured), **UDP/5684** (DTLS-secured) |
| 4 | CoAP Method Codes | `0.01=GET,\ 0.02=POST,\ 0.03=PUT,\ 0.04=DELETE` |
| 5 | Header size | CoAP = **4 bytes** (vs. HTTP = ~300 bytes) |
| 6 | Wire format | **CBOR** (IETF RFC 8949) |
| 7 | Discovery multicast address | `coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res` (IPv4); `ff03::fd` (IPv6) |
| 8 | Observe pattern | `Observe: 0` in GET ⇒ server pushes updates until Observe cancel. |
| 9 | Security | **DTLS 1.2 / TLS 1.2** + **OCF ACE** (Authorization Constrained EST) for OAuth-like access tokens. |
| 10 | Serialization | JSON over HTTP (rich devices) ↔ CBOR over CoAP (constrained devices) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Homes (Residential):** A single app on your phone can control lights (Philips Hue), locks (August), and thermostats (Nest) because each vendor *exposes* its products as OCF-compliant resources.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Siemens, Bosch, and ABB PLCs adopt OCF resource modelling so that a factory MES (Manufacturing Execution System) can poll a uniform `oic.r.sensor.vibration` resource regardless of the motor brand.
- **Healthcare (Biomedical IoT — *direct relevance to PCBRT402*):** A bedside monitor (server) hosts a `oic.r.heartrate` resource; a tablet, an EHR system, and an alarm-LED all `GET` the same URI — **device-vendor-agnostic interoperability**, mandated by regulations like **FDA 21 CFR Part 11** and **ISO 27001**.
- **Smart Cities:** A streetlight collection resource groups individual lamp resources; maintenance crews query one URI to find all malfunctioning lamps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The IoTivity Resource Addressing & Routing Logic

When a client sends a request, the IoTivity framework must **resolve** a logical resource identifier to a *physical network endpoint*. The derivation below formalizes this resolution.

### Step 1 — Client issues a logical request
The application invokes an API call:

```
OCResource.get(URI="/oic/lite/heartrate/1", interface="oic.if.s")
```

This is converted to a CoAP request internally:

```
Code  = 0.01 (GET)
URI   = coap://<host>/oic/lite/heartrate/1
Accept = application/cbor
Observe = 0  (for continuous monitoring)
```

### Step 2 — Discovery → Host Resolution
If `<host>` is unknown, the framework fires a **multicast discovery** to the OCF well-known URI:

$$D_{req} = \text{coap://}\langle 224.0.1.187\ /\ ff03::fd\ \rangle:5683/oic/res$$

Each server on the LAN replies with its resource directory listing the resources it hosts. The framework builds a lookup table:

$$\text{Cache:}\ \{\ URI_i \rightarrow \{host_j,\ port_k,\ path_m\}\ }$$

### Step 3 — URI Canonicalization
A resource may be referenced by:
- A **fully qualified URI** (absolute).
- A **relative URI** within a known collection.
- A **wildcard URI** (`/oic/res?di=<device-id>`).

Canonicalization produces the *absolute CoAP URI*:

$$URI_{abs} = \text{scheme} + "://" + \text{authority} + \text{path} + "?" + \text{query}$$

For IoTivity: `scheme = "coap"` or `"coaps"` (secured).

### Step 4 — Security Binding (DTLS)
If the resource carries an ACL (Access Control List) entry, the request must be sent over a **secure** channel:

$$S_{mode} = \begin{cases} \text{coap (no DTLS)} & \text{if } ACL_{entry}.access\ =\ \text{“public”} \\ \text{coaps (DTLS 1.2)} & \text{if } ACL_{entry}.access\ =\ \text{“authenticated”} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5 — Serialization (JSON ↔ CBOR)
The property bag $P$ is encoded:

$$E(P) = \text{CBOR}(P) \quad \text{for constrained devices}$$
$$E(P) = \text{JSON}(P) \quad \text{for non-constrained devices}$$

### Step 6 — Transmission
The encoded request is sent over the chosen transport $T$:

$$T \in \{ UDP_{coap},\ TCP_{http},\ BLE_{GATT},\ NFC,\ ZigBeeIP,\ Thread \}$$

### Step 7 — Server-side Decoding
On receipt, the server:
1. Decodes the CBOR payload: $D = CBOR^{-1}(E(P))$.
2. Authenticates via DTLS handshake (if `coaps`).
3. Matches `URI` to a registered `ResourceHandle`.
4. Invokes the bound **EntityHandler** (callback) that reads from hardware (e.g., SPI from an ECG ADC).
5. Encodes the response and sends it back.

### Step 8 — Client Receives & Updates Cache
The client updates the **Property Bag cache**:

$$\text{New } P = \{ "bpm":\ 72,\ "unit":\ "bpm" \}$$

If `Observe: 0` is set, the client will keep receiving updates until cancelled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Summary of Resource Resolution:**
> $$\boxed{\text{ClientIntent} \xrightarrow{\text{Discovery}} \text{URI}_{\text{abs}} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec}} \text{coap(s) URI} \xrightarrow{\text{Encode}} \text{CBOR} \xrightarrow{\text{Transport}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{EntityHandler}} \text{Reply}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Biomedical Heart-Rate Resource

A wrist-worn heart-rate sensor advertises the following OCF resource:

```json
{
  "rt": ["oic.r.heartrate"],
  "if": ["oic.if.s", "oic.if.baseline"],
  "n": "Wrist HR Sensor",
  "id": "uuid:7d2f-4a",
  "href": "/heartrate/1",
  "p": { "bpm": 78, "unit": "bpm", "range": "30-220" }
}
```

**Question:** A hospital dashboard issues an Observe subscription. Show the CoAP message exchange and derive the network energy cost.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — The dashboard (client) issues a `GET` with the `Observe` option set to `0` (register subscription):

```
Client → Server:
  CoAP 0.01 (GET)  URI = "/heartrate/1"
  CoAP-Option Observe: 0
  CoAP-Option Accept: application/cbor
  → Token: 0x4A2F
```

Step 2 — Server responds with current state and a **non-zero Observe option** to indicate acceptance of the subscription:

```
Server → Client:
  CoAP 2.05 (Content)
  Observe: 12345
  Payload (CBOR): A2 65 "bpm" 18 4E 64 "unit" 63 "bpm"
  → Token: 0x4A2F
```

Decoded payload = `{"bpm": 78, "unit": "bpm"}`.

Step 3 — When the sensor detects a new BPM (e.g., 82), the server pushes a notification:

```
Server → Client:
  CoAP 2.05 (Content)
  Observe: 12346  (incremented sequence number)
  Payload (CBOR): A2 65 "bpm" 18 52 64 "unit" 63 "bpm"
```

**Energy-cost estimation:**

Let the radio active-mode current $I_{tx} = 12 \text{ mA}$, supply voltage $V = 3.3 \text{ V}$, transmission time $t_{tx} = 5 \text{ ms}$ per CoAP packet.

$$E_{packet} = V \times I_{tx} \times t_{tx} = 3.3 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$E_{packet} = 1.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 198\ \mu\text{J}$$

For 60 heart-rate updates per minute (1 Hz sampling):

$$E_{per\ hour} = 60 \times 60 \times 1.98 \times 10^{-4} = 0.7128\ \text{J}$$

Battery capacity (typical CR2032) = **620 mAh × 3 V = 1.86 × 10³ J**.

$$T_{battery} = \frac{1.86 \times 10^3}{0.7128} \approx 2609\ \text{hours} \approx 108.7\ \text{days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because CoAP+CBOR keeps each packet small and UDP eliminates the TCP ACK overhead, the **Observe pattern** drastically improves battery life compared to polled HTTP/JSON, which would last only a few days on the same battery.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Resource Models Across IoTivity and Related Frameworks

| Feature | **OCF / IoTivity** | **oneM2M** | **LwM2M** | **AllJoyn (legacy)** | **Web of Things (W3C WoT)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource ID** | URI (CoAP) | AE/AE-ID | `/object/instance/resource` | Object Path | TD (Thing Description) JSON |
| **Data Format** | CBOR / JSON | JSON / XML | TLV / JSON | BBML (binary) | JSON-LD |
| **Transport** | CoAP / HTTP | HTTP / CoAP / MQTT | CoAP | TCP/UDP proprietary | HTTP / CoAP / MQTT |
| **Discovery** | Multicast `oic/res` | CSE-based registry | CoAP `/rd` | Multicast | TD directory |
| **Security** | DTLS + ACE OAuth | TLS + ACL | DTLS + PSK / X.509 | SASL + ECDHE | OAuth 2.0 |
| **Biomedical use** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ⚠️ Declining | ✅ Emerging |
| **Open source reference impl.** | IoTivity / Iotivity-Lite | OM2M (Eclipse) | Wakaama / Leshan | AllSeen Alliance (archived) | W3C TD spec |

## 3.4 Derivation — URI Composition for an IoTivity Resource

A canonical OCF URI is constructed hierarchically:

$$URI = \text{scheme} :// \text{host} : \text{port} / \text{path} ; \text{?query}$$

For a biomedical device:

- **Scheme:** `coap://` (or `coaps://` for secure)
- **Host:** device IP (e.g., `192.168.1.42` for Wi-Fi, `[fe80::1]` for 6LoWPAN link-local)
- **Port:** `5683` (unsecured) or `5684` (secured)
- **Path:** `/oic/<device-id>/<resource-type>/<instance>`
- **Query:** optional `?if=<interface>`

**Example URI for the heart-rate sensor of device `7d2f-4a`:**

$$URI = \text{coaps://[fe80::1abcd]}:5684 / oic / 7d2f-4a / heartrate / 1\ ? \text{if}=oic.if.s$$

This single, uniformly-structured URI means *any* OCF-compliant client (a phone, a server, another MCU) can find and consume the resource.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IoTivity Layered Stack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Layer 6: Application & Use-Case"]
        APP1["Healthcare Dashboard App"]
        APP2["Home Automation App"]
        APP3["Industrial SCADA App"]
    end

    subgraph L2["Layer 5: Framework Layer"]
        FW1["Resource Hosting & Entity Handlers"]
        FW2["Discovery Manager"]
        FW3["Group / Scene Manager"]
        FW4["CRUD Manager (GET/PUT/POST/DELETE)"]
        FW5["Observe/Notify Manager"]
    end

    subgraph L3["Layer 4: Resource Model and Abstraction"]
        RM1["Resource Type System oic.r.*"]
        RM2["Property Bag Model"]
        RM3["Interface Set oic.if.*"]
        RM4["Collection & Web Linking"]
        RM5["Security ACL / ACE"]
    end

    subgraph L4["Layer 3: Communication Protocols"]
        CP1["CoAP RFC 7252 over UDP"]
        CP2["HTTP REST API"]
        CP3["DTLS 1.2 / TLS 1.2"]
        CP4["CBOR Encoding RFC 8949"]
    end

    subgraph L5["Layer 2: Transport"]
        TR1["UDP"]
        TR2["TCP"]
        TR3["Bluetooth LE GATT"]
        TR4["ZigBee IP / Thread / NFC"]
    end

    subgraph L6["Layer 1: Network and Hardware"]
        NH1["IPv4 / IPv6 / 6LoWPAN"]
        NH2["Wi-Fi / Ethernet"]
        NH3["Sensors / Actuators / MCU"]
    end

    APP1 --> FW1
    APP2 --> FW2
    APP3 --> FW3
    FW1 --> RM1
    FW2 --> RM3
    FW3 --> RM4
    FW4 --> RM2
    FW5 --> RM5
    RM1 --> CP1
    RM2 --> CP4
    RM3 --> CP2
    RM4 --> CP3
    RM5 --> CP3
    CP1 --> TR1
    CP2 --> TR2
    CP3 --> TR3
    CP4 --> TR4
    TR1 --> NH1
    TR2 --> NH2
    TR3 --> NH3
    TR4 --> NH3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the diagram:** Each layer is a *subgraph* (visually grouped). Arrows point *downward* from the top-most user-facing layer to the bottom-most hardware layer, indicating the direction of *service consumption* (the layer above consumes services of the layer below).

## 4.2 Resource Model — Object Composition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DR["Device (oic.d.*)"]
        D1["Device ID uuid:7d2f-4a"]
        D2["Device Name Wrist HR Sensor"]
        D3["Manufacturer Acme MedTech"]
        D4["Model No. HR-2025"]
    end

    subgraph COLL["Collection Resource (oic.r.icu)"]
        CR1["links array"]
    end

    subgraph R1["Resource 1: oic.r.heartrate"]
        R1A["URI coap://[fe80::1abcd]:5683/heartrate/1"]
        R1B["Interfaces oic.if.s, oic.if.baseline"]
        R1C["Property: bpm 78"]
        R1D["Property: unit bpm"]
    end

    subgraph R2["Resource 2: oic.r.spo2"]
        R2A["URI coap://[fe80::1abcd]:5683/spo2/1"]
        R2B["Interfaces oic.if.s, oic.if.baseline"]
        R2C["Property: spo2 98 percent"]
    end

    subgraph R3["Resource 3: oic.r.temperature"]
        R3A["URI coap://[fe80::1abcd]:5683/temp/1"]
        R3B["Interfaces oic.if.s, oic.if.baseline"]
        R3C["Property: celsius 36.8"]
    end

    subgraph ACL["Access Control List"]
        ACL1["Entry: dashboard READ allowed"]
        ACL2["Entry: phone APP READ + WRITE allowed"]
        ACL3["Entry: anonymous no access"]
    end

    DR --- COLL
    COLL --> R1
    COLL --> R2
    COLL --> R3
    R1 --- ACL
    R2 --- ACL
    R3 --- ACL
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **structural anatomy** of a typical biomedical IoT device. Notice the **three concentric containment relationships**:
> 1. A **Device** contains **one or more Collection Resources**.
> 2. A **Collection Resource** contains **linked child Resources**.
> 3. Each **Resource** has its own **Property Bag** + **Interface set** + **ACL**.

## 4.3 CRUD Operation Flow – Observe / Notify Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client App
    participant F as IoTivity Framework
    participant S as Server (Sensor)

    C->>F: 1. discover(oic.res) — multicast
    F->>S: 2. CoAP GET /oic/res
    S-->>F: 3. Resource directory listing
    F-->>C: 4. List of URIs and types

    C->>F: 5. observe("/heartrate/1", if=oic.if.s)
    F->>S: 6. CoAP GET + Observe:0
    S-->>F: 7. CoAP 2.05 + Observe:1 + CBOR{bpm:78}
    F-->>C: 8. Decoded property bag (bpm=78)

    loop Every 1 second
        S->>F: 9. CoAP 2.05 + Observe:2 + CBOR{bpm:80}
        F->>C: 10. Notify: bpm changed to 80
    end

    C->>F: 11. cancelObservation()
    F->>S: 12. CoAP GET + Observe:1 (deregister)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Time-axis diagram (sequenceDiagram):** Time flows from top to bottom. The vertical lines represent the three actors (Client, Framework, Server). The numbered horizontal arrows show the **Observe pattern** in action — step 6 is the single subscription, and the loop (steps 9–10) shows the server-pushed updates with **incrementing Observe sequence numbers**.

## 4.4 Discovery & Multicast Routing Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NET["Local IoT Network 192.168.1.0/24"]
        subgraph MCAST["Multicast Group 224.0.1.187 :5683"]
            MQ["Discovery Request /oic/res"]
        end
        S1["Server A: Heart Rate Sensor coap://192.168.1.21"]
        S2["Server B: Smart Bulb coap://192.168.1.22"]
        S3["Server C: Temperature Sensor coap://192.168.1.23"]
        C1["Client: Hospital Tablet"]
        C2["Client: Smartphone App"]
    end

    C1 --> MQ
    C2 --> MQ
    MQ --> S1
    MQ --> S2
    MQ --> S3
    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The **dashed central node** represents the **multicast group address** through which the discovery packet is fanned out. All three servers respond with their resource directory entries. Clients maintain a URI→host cache and may then communicate **unicast** for subsequent CRUD operations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term “Resource” as used in the IoTivity resource model. List any two components of a resource.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Resource** in IoTivity is a **virtual, network-addressable representation of an IoT device capability or service state**, uniquely identified by a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** and accessible via RESTful CRUD semantics over CoAP.

**Two components of a resource:**

1. **Resource Type (RT):** A namespaced identifier (e.g., `oic.r.heartrate`, `oic.r.switch.binary`) describing *what* the resource is.
2. **Interface Set (IF):** A set of operation interfaces (e.g., `oic.if.s` for sensor, `oic.if.rw` for read/write) describing *how* the resource can be interacted with.

*(Acceptable alternative components: Property Bag, URI, Method Bindings, ACL — 1 mark for correct definition, ½ mark for each component × 2 = 1 mark)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the role of CBOR in IoTivity? Why is it preferred over JSON for constrained devices?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role:** CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation, RFC 8949) is the **default on-the-wire serialization format** for IoTivity resource representations. It encodes the property bag of a resource into a compact binary form before transmission over CoAP.

**Why preferred over JSON (1 mark per reason, up to 2 marks):**

1. **Compactness:** A typical JSON object of 200 bytes shrinks to ~60 bytes in CBOR — reducing radio-on time and energy consumption.
2. **Parse efficiency:** CBOR’s deterministic binary grammar can be parsed 5–10× faster than JSON on resource-constrained MCUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+ with 32 kB RAM).
3. **Schema-agnostic but typed:** CBOR supports integers, floats, byte strings, and arrays natively, allowing a faithful round-trip of the property bag without type coercion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A** — `(14 Marks)` — Comprehensive Long-Answer

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`**

**(a)** *Explain the layered architecture of the IoTivity stack with a neat diagram. Discuss the function of each layer. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Describe the IoTivity Resource Model in detail. With a suitable biomedical example, explain Resource Type, Interface, Property, and Collection Resource. (7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Introduction (1 mark):**
The IoTivity stack is a **modular, layered middleware** designed to enable device-to-device communication across heterogeneous IoT ecosystems. It is governed by the OCF (Open Connectivity Foundation) and follows a **bottom-up hardware abstraction to top-down service exposure** design philosophy.

**Step 2 – The Six Layers (5 marks, ~1 mark per layer with crisp explanation):**

1. **Application / Use-Case Layer:** Hosts the end-user smart apps. Examples: a healthcare dashboard, a smart-home app. Uses high-level IoTivity APIs (`OCPlatform`, `OCResource`).

2. **Framework Layer:** Provides discovery (`OCDoResource`), CRUD dispatch, observe/notify, group/scene management, and the CBOR encode/decode pipeline.

3. **Resource Model & Abstraction Layer:** Defines the resource type system, the property bag, the interface set, and the ACL/security metadata. *(This is the focus layer of the current topic.)*

4. **Communication Protocol Layer:** Uses **CoAP (UDP, port 5683)** for constrained devices and **HTTP (TCP, port 80)** for richer devices. **DTLS** provides mutual authentication and confidentiality.

5. **Transport Layer:** **UDP/TCP/BLE/NFC/ZigBee-IP**. Plug-in architecture allows new transports to be added without altering upper layers.

6. **Network & Hardware Layer:** **IPv4, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, Thread, Wi-Fi, Ethernet** + the actual sensor/actuator hardware and MCUs.

**Step 3 – Neat Diagram (1 mark):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1, IoTivity Layered Stack diagram.)*

**Step 4 – Conclusion (0 marks, may be implicit):**
Emphasize that this layered design ensures **interoperability**, **portability**, and **energy efficiency**, making IoTivity suitable for smart homes, industrial IoT, and biomedical wearables.

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – The Resource Model Concept (2 marks):**
The IoTivity **Resource Model** abstracts every IoT entity as a *stateful, addressable, REST-like object* defined by the tuple $R = \{URI,\ T,\ I,\ P,\ M\}$. It conceals hardware/firmware details behind a uniform CRUD interface.

**Step 2 – Resource Type (RT) — 1 mark:**
A reverse-DNS namespaced identifier declaring the *kind* of resource. E.g., `oic.r.heartrate` for a heart-rate sensor, `oic.r.thermostat` for a thermostat.

**Step 3 – Interface (IF) — 1 mark:**
The set of operation patterns supported. Common interfaces:
- `oic.if.s` — sensor (read-only auto-updated)
- `oic.if.a` — actuator (control output)
- `oic.if.rw` — generic read/write
- `oic.if.baseline` — minimum metadata (`n`, `id`, `rt`, `if`)
- `oic.if.ll` — linked-list / collection of children

**Step 4 – Property (P) — 1 mark:**
A typed, named data field. Strongly typed, may include unit and range metadata.

**Step 5 – Collection Resource — 1 mark:**
A *parent* resource that aggregates multiple *child* resources via Web Linking (`rel`, `href`, `rt`, `if` keys). Used to model devices with multiple sensors.

**Step 6 – Biomedical Example (1 mark):**
A wrist-worn ICU monitor exposes:
- **Device ID:** `uuid:7d2f-4a`
- **Collection resource:** `oic.r.icu` with `if = ["oic.if.ll", "oic.if.baseline"]`
- **Child resources:** `oic.r.heartrate` (bpm), `oic.r.spo2` (oxygen saturation), `oic.r.temperature` (°C), `oic.r.ecg` (waveform array).
- A cardiology tablet **discovers** the collection URI, **GETs** `oic.if.s` for live vitals, and **PUTs** alarm thresholds to `oic.if.rw` actuators on the device.

---

### **QUESTION B** — `(14 Marks)` — Alternative Comprehensive Long-Answer

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest Paper]`**

**(a)** *Compare and contrast the resource models of IoTivity and oneM2M / LwM2M. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With reference to the IoTivity resource abstraction, explain how a clinician’s tablet discovers, observes, and controls a wearable ECG patch. Draw the necessary message sequence. (7 marks)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Introduction (1 mark):**
Both IoTivity (OCF) and LwM2M (OMA Specworks) are **RESTful, URI-based** resource-modelling standards for IoT, but they differ in naming conventions, transports, security models, and adoption domains.

**Step 2 – Comparison (5 marks, 5 dimensions × 1 mark each):**

| Dimension | **IoTivity (OCF)** | **oneM2M** | **LwM2M** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource ID** | URI path + namespaced RT | AE/CSE hierarchical path | `/Object/Instance/Resource` numeric path |
| **Wire format** | CBOR over CoAP / JSON over HTTP | XML or JSON over HTTP/CoAP/MQTT | TLV or JSON over CoAP |
| **Transport** | CoAP (UDP) + HTTP | HTTP, CoAP, MQTT | CoAP (UDP/DTLS) |
| **Security** | DTLS 1.2 + OCF ACE (OAuth-like) | TLS + ACP (Access Control Policies) | DTLS with PSK / X.509 + OSCORE |
| **Discovery** | Multicast `oic/res` | CSE-based directory | CoAP `/rd` lookup |

**Step 3 – Conclusion (1 mark):**
IoTivity is best suited for **consumer-electronics and biomedical** smart ecosystems; oneM2M excels in **telco-grade** smart-city deployments; LwM2M dominates **cellular LPWAN** device-management (NB-IoT, LTE-M).

#### MODEL SOLUTION – Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 – Scenario (1 mark):**
A wearable ECG patch is attached to a patient. A clinician’s tablet on the same Wi-Fi LAN must (i) discover the device, (ii) subscribe to live ECG waveform updates, and (iii) remotely control a buzzer on the patch to alert the patient.

**Step 2 – Discovery (2 marks):**
The tablet invokes `OCDoResource("/oic/res", RT="oic.r.ecg")`. IoTivity multicasts a CoAP GET to `coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res`. The patch responds with:

```json
{
  "rt": ["oic.r.ecg"],
  "if": ["oic.if.s", "oic.if.baseline"],
  "href": "/ecg/1",
  "p": { "samplingHz": 250, "leads": 2, "unit": "mV" }
}
```

The tablet caches the URI → `coap://[patch-IP]:5683/ecg/1`.

**Step 3 – Observation (2 marks):**
The tablet issues a `GET` with `Observe: 0`. The patch responds with the first 5-second waveform window and increments `Observe: 1`. On every new sample buffer (e.g., every 1 s), the patch sends `Observe: 2, 3, 4, …` with the latest CBOR-encoded waveform array. The clinician’s UI plots a real-time ECG.

**Step 4 – Control (2 marks):**
For the buzzer actuator, the tablet issues a CoAP `POST` to `/buzzer/1` with payload `{"state": "ON", "pattern": "long"}`. The patch’s entity handler activates the haptic buzzer.

**Step 5 – Message Sequence Diagram (Bonus for clarity):**
*(Refer to Section 4.3 Observe/Notify sequence diagram.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing “Resource” with “Device.”** A *Device* hosts one or more *Resources*. Students who equate the two lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the namespace.** Writing just `heartrate` instead of `oic.r.heartrate` is considered a *conceptual error*; examiners deduct ½ mark per instance.
> 3. **Missing the Observe pattern.** A model answer on biomedical IoT that does not mention the **Observe/Notify** pattern is marked down — it is a *high-weightage KTU topic*.
> 4. **Not stating the CoAP port numbers.** Always quote **UDP/5683** (insecure) and **UDP/5684** (DTLS-secured) — examiners often award a free 1 mark for this.
> 5. **Drawing the architecture without labels.** A diagram without the *standard used at each layer* scores only ~50 % of the diagram marks. Always annotate “CoAP,” “UDP,” “CBOR,” “DTLS,” etc.
> 6. **Mixing up CRUD codes.** `POST` is *0.02*, not `0.03`; `PUT` is `0.03`. The wrong code is a factual error and loses ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **IoTivity** is an OCF-sponsored, open-source, layered middleware for **interoperable, device-to-device IoT communication**.
- ⭐ The architecture is **6-layered**: Application → Framework → Resource Model & Abstraction → Communication (CoAP/HTTP) → Transport (UDP/TCP/BLE) → Network/Hardware.
- ⭐ **Resource Model** = the abstraction that maps every physical/digital IoT entity to a **URI-addressable, REST-style object** with a type, interface, and property bag.
- ⭐ A Resource is defined by the tuple $R = \{URI,\ T,\ I,\ P,\ M\}$ — URI + Type + Interfaces + Properties + Methods.
- ⭐ **Resource Type (RT)** is reverse-DNS namespaced: `oic.r.<category>.<subtype>` (e.g., `oic.r.heartrate`).
- ⭐ **Interface (IF)** declares access pattern: `oic.if.s` (sensor), `oic.if.a` (actuator), `oic.if.rw` (read/write), `oic.if.baseline` (metadata), `oic.if.ll` (linked-list collection).
- ⭐ **Property Bag** = typed key-value dictionary; may carry unit + range metadata.
- ⭐ **Collection Resource** aggregates child resources via Web Linking (`rel`, `href`, `rt`, `if`).
- ⭐ **CRUD ↔ CoAP codes:** GET=0.01, POST=0.02, PUT=0.03, DELETE=0.04.
- ⭐ **CoAP port:** **5683 (plain)** / **5684 (DTLS)**.
- ⭐ **Wire format:** **CBOR** (compact binary) for constrained devices; **JSON** for richer ones.
- ⭐ **Discovery** uses **multicast** to `coap://224.0.1.187:5683/oic/res` (IPv4) or `ff03::fd` (IPv6).
- ⭐ **Observe pattern** lets a server *push* updates to a client without polling — critical for biomedical and energy-constrained devices.
- ⭐ **Security:** **DTLS 1.2** + **OCF ACE** (Authorization for Constrained Environments) using OAuth-like access tokens.
- ⭐ For **biomedical applications**, the resource model guarantees **device-vendor-agnostic interoperability** — a heart-rate monitor, ECG patch, or SpO₂ probe can be consumed by any OCF-compliant app.
- ⭐ **Energy advantage of CoAP+CBOR** is decisive: a typical CoAP packet is **4 bytes of header** vs. ~300 bytes for HTTP, drastically reducing radio-on time and extending battery life of wearables.
- ⭐ **Comparison-ready one-liners:** IoTivity = OCF, REST, CBOR, Observe; oneM2M = Telco, CSE-based; LwM2M = OMA, CoAP/TLV, LPWAN.
- ⭐ **Key acronyms to memorize:** OCF, CoAP, CBOR, DTLS, ACE, URI, CRUD, 6LoWPAN, BLE, GATT.
- ⭐ Always **state the standard** used at each architecture layer in a diagram-answer — half the marks depend on it.
- ⭐ Always **use the fully qualified namespaced RT and IF** (`oic.r.heartrate`, `oic.if.s`) — never the bare name.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS) - Basics of Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS) - Basics of Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS) # MODULE 3: WEARABLE HEALTH MONITORING SYSTEM (WHMS) — BASICS ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS):** A *non-invasive, body-worn electronic system* that integrates **biosensors**, **signal conditioning units**, a **microcontroller/processor**, **wireless communication modules**, and **energy harvesting/storage units** to continuously or periodically acquire, process, transmit, and display physiological parameters of a user for preventive healthcare, clinical diagnostics, fitness tracking, or remote patient monitoring. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a WHMS is a sub-class of **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** that operates at the **tier-1 (perception layer)** of the IoT architecture stack, with data typically pushed to **tier-2 (network layer)** gateways and **tier-3 (application layer)** cloud platforms. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a WHMS as a "Personal Health Guardian on Your Wrist":** > Imagine a tiny, invisible doctor riding on your body 24 × 7. Every time your heart skips a beat, every time your body temperature rises by even 0.5 °C, every time your blood oxygen drops while climbing stairs — this mini-doctor quietly notes it down, makes sense of it, and instantly alerts you *or* your actual doctor sitting 1,000 km away. A smartwatch, a smart ECG patch, a CGM (Continuous Glucose Monitor), or even a smart T-shirt that reads your ECG — all are physical avatars of this guardian. ### 1.3 Standard Physiological Metrics Acquired by WHMS The following are the **canonical biomedical signals** a typical WHMS tracks (these names are **high-yield for KTU exams**): | S.No | Parameter | Typical Range (Adult) | Sensor Type | |:----:|:----------|:---------------------|:------------| | 1 | Heart Rate (HR) | **60 – 100 bpm** | PPG / ECG electrodes | | 2 | Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂) | **95 – 100 %** | Pulse oximeter (PPG) | | 3 | Body Temperature | **36.1 – 37.2 °C** | Thermistor / RTD | | 4 | Blood Pressure (SBP/DBP) | **120 / 80 mmHg** | PTT-based cuff-less | | 5 | Respiratory Rate (RR) | **12 – 20 brpm** | Chest strain / nasal airflow | | 6 | Electrocardiogram (ECG) | mV range, **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Ag/AgCl wet or dry electrodes | | 7 | Electromyogram (EMG) | µV–mV, **20 – 500 Hz** | Surface electrodes | | 8 | Electroencephalogram (EEG) | µV range, **0.5 – 100 Hz** | Scalp electrodes | | 9 | Blood Glucose | **70 – 110 mg/dL (fasting)** | Enzymatic electrochemical | | 10 | Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) | **0.1 – 5 µS** | Two-electrode conductance | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick — "HEART-BEES":** **H**R, **E**CG, **A**ccelerometer (gait/fall), **R**espiration, **T**emperature, **B**P, **E**EG/EMG, **S**pO₂ — these 8 parameters cover ~90% of exam questions on WHMS. ### 1.4 Classification of Wearable Health Devices (by Form Factor) * **Wrist-worn:** Smartwatches, fitness bands (e.g., Apple Watch, Fitbit). * **Patch-type:** Adhesive skin patches (e.g., ECG Holter patches, CGM). * **Textile-integrated (e-textiles):** Smart shirts, smart socks with embroidered electrodes. * **Head-mounted:** EEG headsets, AR/VR health displays. * **Implantable (invasive WHMS):** Pacemakers, implantable loop recorders. * **Accessory-based:** Rings (Oura), earbuds (in-ear PPG), chest straps (HRM). # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Generic Architecture of a WHMS — The 5-Layer Functional Stack A WHMS is best understood as a **closed-loop signal chain**. Every wearable, from a ₹500 fitness band to a ₹5,00,000 clinical Holter, follows the same five blocks: 1. **Sensor / Transduction Layer** — Converts the biophysical/ biochemical variable into an electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioning & Analog Front-End (AFE)** — Amplification, filtering, isolation. 3. **Data Acquisition & Processing Layer** — ADC, MCU/DSP, feature extraction. 4. **Communication Layer** — Wireless transmission to gateway/cloud. 5. **Power & Energy Management Layer** — Battery, energy harvesting, power budgeting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** The above 5 layers directly correspond to the *Perception → Network → Application* model of IoT, with the **processing and communication layers forming the "edge node"** of the IoMT hierarchy. ### 2.2 Sensor Layer — Deep Dive #### 2.2.1 Physical Sensors * **PPG (Photoplethysmography):** Measures volumetric blood changes using **LED (λ = 530 nm green, 660 nm red, 940 nm IR)** and a photodetector. Green light → surface capillaries (wrist); Red/IR → deep tissue. * **ECG Electrodes:** Ag/AgCl wet electrodes (high SNR), dry capacitive electrodes (convenient but noisy). * **MEMS Accelerometer / Gyroscope:** 3-axis, range **±2 g to ±16 g**, used for activity recognition, fall detection, gait analysis. * **Thermistor (NTC):** Resistance varies inversely with temperature; **β-equation** governs the curve. * **Strain Gauge (respiratory belt):** Wheatstone bridge configuration. #### 2.2.2 Biochemical Sensors * **Enzymatic amperometric biosensor** (e.g., glucose oxidase — GOx for CGM). * **ISFET (Ion-Sensitive Field-Effect Transistor)** for pH/sweat ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻). * **Aptamer-based electrochemical** sensors for emerging biomarkers. ### 2.3 Signal Conditioning & AFE | Stage | Purpose | Typical IC / Topology | |:------|:--------|:----------------------| | Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) | Differential gain **G = 1 + (2R₁ / R_g)** | AD620, INA128, ADS1292 | | High-Pass Filter (HPF) | Remove DC electrode offset (< 0.5 Hz) | 1st-order RC, fc = 1/(2πRC) | | Low-Pass Filter (LPF) | Anti-aliasing, remove HF noise | Sallen-Key, 2nd-order Butterworth | | Notch Filter | 50/60 Hz mains rejection | Twin-T, Q ≈ 30 | | Right-Leg Driver (RLD) | Common-mode noise reduction in ECG | Driven-shield topology | > [!NOTE] > **Why an Instrumentation Amplifier?** Biopotentials (ECG, EEG, EMG) are *tiny* (µV–mV), *floating* (high source impedance, **Z ≈ 1 – 10 MΩ**), and *buried in noise*. A standard op-amp cannot reject the large common-mode 50 Hz hum. An IA provides **high CMRR (> 100 dB)**, **high input impedance (> 1 GΩ)**, and **adjustable gain via a single resistor R_g**. ### 2.4 Data Acquisition & Processing * **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist):** **f_s ≥ 2 × f_max** of the biopotential. For ECG (f_max ≈ 150 Hz clinically, 40 Hz diagnostic bandwidth), f_s = 250 – 500 Hz. For EEG (f_max ≈ 100 Hz), f_s = 250 – 500 Hz. * **ADC Resolution:** ECG typically needs **12 – 16 bits**; sEMG needs **16 bits** to resolve µV signals. * **On-node processing:** Pan-Tompkins (QRS detection), wavelet denoising, peak detection for SpO₂. ### 2.5 Communication Layer — Wireless Protocols | Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Best Suited For | |:---------|:------|:----------|:------|:----------------| | **BLE 5.0** | ~ 50 – 100 m | 2 Mbps | Very low | Smartwatches, fitness bands | | Classic Bluetooth | ~ 10 m | 1 – 3 Mbps | Medium | Older Holter monitors | | Wi-Fi (802.11) | ~ 50 m | 11 – 600 Mbps | High | Home gateways, in-hospital | | ZigBee (802.15.4) | ~ 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Body Area Network (BAN) mesh | | NFC | < 10 cm | 106 – 424 kbps | Passive | Tag-based ID, payment | | LoRaWAN | 2 – 10 km | 0.3 – 50 kbps | Low | Outdoor / farm wearables | | NB-IoT / LTE-M | km range | < 200 kbps | Medium | Cellular, on-the-go | > [!TIP] > **KTU Punchline:** *Short-range, high-rate → BLE; Long-range, low-rate → LoRa/NB-IoT; In-body, ultra-low-power → ZigBee/NFC.* ### 2.6 Power Management * **Primary battery:** Li-Poly / Li-ion coin cell (e.g., CR2032 = 3 V, 220 mAh). * **Energy harvesting:** Thermoelectric (body heat), piezoelectric (motion), photovoltaic (skin-exposed), RF harvest. * **Average current budget for a fitness band:** **≈ 5 – 15 mA** active, **< 50 µA** sleep. * **Battery life target:** **5 – 14 days** (consumer), **> 30 days** (medical). ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Boundary | |:--|:-----------------------|:--------------|:----------------| | 1 | **G_IA = 1 + (2 R₁ / R_g)** | Instrumentation amp gain | dimensionless | | 2 | **f_c = 1 / (2 π R C)** | RC filter cutoff | Hz | | 3 | **f_s ≥ 2 f_max** | Nyquist rate | Hz | | 4 | **SNR = 20 log₁₀ (V_signal / V_noise)** | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | | 5 | **CMRR = 20 log₁₀ (A_d / A_cm)** | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB, **> 100 dB** desired | | 6 | **P_avg = V_supply × I_avg** | Power budget | mW | | 7 | **Battery life (h) = (C_mAh × V) / P_mW** | Endurance | hours | | 8 | **HR = 60 / R-R interval (s)** | Heart Rate from ECG | bpm | | 9 | **SpO₂ ≈ f(R = (AC_red / DC_red) / (AC_IR / DC_IR))** | Beer–Lambert ratio | % | | 10 | **R_T = R₀ exp [β (1/T − 1/T₀)]** | NTC thermistor | Kelvin | | 11 | **P_total = P_sense + P_proc + P_tx + P_idle** | Node power | mW | | 12 | **E_harvested = P_density × t × η** | Harvested energy | mJ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** When asked for "WHMS architecture", most students draw *only* the sensor block. Examiners award full 7 marks only when **all 5 layers** (sensor → AFE → processing → communication → power) are explicitly drawn *with labels and example components*. ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Preventive Cardiology:** WHMS detects silent atrial fibrillation up to **weeks before** a stroke (Apple Heart Study, 2019 — > 400,000 participants). * **Diabetic Management:** CGMs reduce HbA1c by **0.5 – 1 %**, lowering long-term complications. * **Elderly Fall Detection:** Accelerometer + gyroscope fusion → 95%+ fall-detection accuracy in < 1 s. * **Post-COVID Remote Monitoring:** SpO₂ and respiratory rate alerts flag "happy hypoxia" at home. * **Sports Medicine:** EMG textile shirts monitor muscle fatigue in real time for athletes. * **Pandemic Surveillance:** Population-level WHMS data predicts flu/COVID outbreaks **7 – 14 days** earlier than hospital admissions. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!NOTE] > The derivations below are mandatory derivations that frequently appear as **sub-parts of 14-mark KTU questions**. We work them out exhaustively. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Life of a WHMS Node **Problem (typical 7-mark part-a):** A wearable health monitoring node has the following average current consumption profile per second of operation: * **Sensing (PPG + temperature):** 8 mA for 50 ms * **Processing (MCU active):** 12 mA for 150 ms * **Wireless TX (BLE):** 20 mA for 30 ms * **Sleep mode (rest of the second):** 50 µA for 770 ms If the device is powered by a **3.7 V, 400 mAh Li-Po battery**, find the **average current**, **average power**, and **battery life in days**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Compute the charge consumed in 1 s cycle.** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{sense}} &= I_{\text{sense}} \times t_{\text{sense}} = 8\ \text{mA} \times 0.050\ \text{s} = 0.400\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{proc}} &= 12\ \text{mA} \times 0.150\ \text{s} = 1.800\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{tx}} &= 20\ \text{mA} \times 0.030\ \text{s} = 0.600\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{sleep}} &= 0.050\ \text{mA} \times 0.770\ \text{s} = 0.0385\ \text{mC} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total charge per cycle.** $$ Q_{\text{total}} = 0.400 + 1.800 + 0.600 + 0.0385 = 2.8385\ \text{mC} $$ **Step 3 — Average current over 1 s.** $$ I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{1\ \text{s}} = 2.8385\ \text{mA} $$ **Step 4 — Average power.** $$ P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{supply}} \times I_{\text{avg}} = 3.7\ \text{V} \times 2.8385\ \text{mA} = 10.50\ \text{mW} $$ **Step 5 — Battery life in hours.** $$ T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{C_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{400\ \text{mAh}}{2.8385\ \text{mA}} = 140.92\ \text{h} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to days.** $$ T_{\text{days}} = \frac{140.92}{24} = 5.87\ \text{days} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The wearable lasts **≈ 5.9 days** on a single charge — typical for a modern fitness band. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Threshold (Conceptual Energy Derivation) The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is the *gold standard* for real-time ECG QRS detection on a wearable. It cascades five filters: 1. Band-pass filter (5 – 15 Hz) → removes baseline wander and HF noise. 2. Differentiator → emphasises QRS slope. 3. Squarer → makes everything positive and amplifies large differences. 4. Moving-window integrator (window width **N = 0.15 × f_s** samples) → smooths. 5. Adaptive thresholding → decides "R-peak detected" when integrated signal > threshold. **Derivation of window length N:** $$ N_{\text{window}} = 0.15 \times f_s $$ **For f_s = 250 Hz:** $$ N = 0.15 \times 250 = 37.5 \approx 38\ \text{samples} $$ **Rationale:** A normal QRS complex lasts **70 – 110 ms**. Choosing 150 ms (= 0.15 s) ensures *one and only one* QRS peak contributes to the integrator output per heartbeat, preventing double-counting. **Adaptive threshold update rule:** $$ \text{Threshold}_k = \text{Threshold}_{k-1} + \alpha \cdot (P_k - \text{Threshold}_{k-1}) $$ where **α ≈ 0.05** is the learning rate and **P_k** is the latest peak value. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SpO₂ from PPG (Beer–Lambert Simplified) The pulse oximeter uses **two wavelengths** — Red (660 nm) and Infrared (940 nm). The "ratio of ratios" R is: $$ R = \frac{\left( \dfrac{AC_{\text{red}}}{DC_{\text{red}}} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{AC_{\text{IR}}}{DC_{\text{IR}}} \right)} $$ Empirically calibrated linear approximation: $$ \text{SpO}_2\ (\%) = 110 - 25 \times R $$ **For a healthy subject, R ≈ 0.4:** $$ \text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25(0.4) = 110 - 10 = 100\ \% $$ **For a hypoxic subject, R ≈ 1.6:** $$ \text{SpO}_2 = 110 - 25(1.6) = 110 - 40 = 70\ \% $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU 1-liner:** SpO₂ is *inversely* proportional to R because oxy-haemoglobin absorbs *less* red and *more* IR light than deoxy-haemoglobin. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs in WHMS | Design Parameter | Trade-off | Typical Optimization | |:-----------------|:----------|:---------------------| | Sampling Rate ↑ | Power ↑, Data ↑ | Choose 2 × f_max (Nyquist) only | | Resolution (bits) ↑ | Power ↑, Memory ↑ | 12-bit ECG; 16-bit sEMG | | TX Power ↑ | Battery life ↓ | BLE adaptive TX, +4 dBm typical | | Form factor ↓ | Sensor accuracy ↓ | Use dry electrodes w/ DSP denoise | | Number of sensors ↑ | Comfort ↓, Cost ↑ | Multi-modal fusion w/ shared AFE | | Cloud upload ↑ | Privacy risk ↑ | On-node edge AI, transmit features not raw | # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 WHMS Overall Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSIOLOGICAL SOURCE
Heart, Lungs, Skin, Muscles] --> B[SENSOR LAYER
PPG, ECG, Temp, Accel] B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING
IA, Filters, Notch 50Hz] C --> D[ADC AND MCU
12-16 bit, DSP, Pan-Tompkins] D --> E[BLE 5.0 MODULE
Wireless TX to Gateway] D --> F[LOCAL DISPLAY
OLED TFT] D --> G[EDGE AI
Anomaly Detection] E --> H[SMARTPHONE GATEWAY] H --> I[CLOUD EMR
AWS IoT, Azure] I --> J[DOCTOR DASHBOARD
Alerts and Analytics] K[POWER MANAGEMENT
Li-Po 3.7V 400mAh] -.-> B K -.-> C K -.-> D K -.-> E L[ENERGY HARVESTING
Thermoelectric Piezo] -.-> K ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows → data/signal flow. **Dashed arrows** → power supply rails. The **5 functional blocks** (sensor, AFE, processing, communication, power) are clearly visible — exactly the format KTU examiners expect in 7-mark "architecture" questions. --- ## 4.2 ECG Signal Conditioning Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[BODY
Skin Electrodes] --> A2[IA Stage 1
Gain 10] A2 --> A3[HPF 0.5 Hz
Removes DC Offset] A3 --> A4[IA Stage 2
Gain 100] A4 --> A5[LPF 150 Hz
Anti-aliasing] A5 --> A6[Notch 50 Hz
Mains Rejection] A6 --> A7[ADC 12 bit
500 Hz Sampling] A7 --> A8[MCU
QRS Detection] A8 --> A9[BLE TX
to Phone] A10[Right Leg Driver
Common Mode Feedback] -.-> A2 A10 -.-> A4 ``` --- ## 4.3 Wearable Device Decision Tree — Choosing a Communication Protocol ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Choose Wireless Protocol] --> B{Indoor or Outdoor?} B -- Indoor --> C{Data Rate Needed} B -- Outdoor --> D{Network Coverage} C -- > 1 Mbps --> E[WiFi 802.11] C -- 100 kbps to 1 Mbps --> F[BLE 5.0] C -- < 100 kbps --> G{ZigBee Mesh?} G -- Yes --> H[ZigBee 802.15.4] G -- No --> I[BLE] D -- Cellular Available --> J[NB-IoT or LTE-M] D -- No Cellular --> K[LoRaWAN] D -- Short Range Body --> F ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Cue:** Examiners love asking "Which protocol would you choose for an **outdoor marathon runner's** WHMS?" — the correct answer is **LoRaWAN** (long range, low power) with **BLE as fallback** to a runner's phone. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sem 4]** **Define a Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS). List any four physiological parameters that can be monitored using a WHMS.** **Model Answer:** A **Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** is a non-invasive, body-worn electronic system that integrates sensors, signal processing, and wireless communication to continuously or periodically acquire, process, and transmit physiological signals of a user for healthcare, fitness, or clinical applications. **Four physiological parameters:** 1. **Heart Rate (HR)** — using PPG / ECG. 2. **Blood Oxygen Saturation (SpO₂)** — using dual-wavelength PPG. 3. **Body Temperature** — using NTC thermistor. 4. **Blood Pressure** — using PTT (Pulse Transit Time) cuff-less method. *(Equivalent answers with EEG, EMG, respiration, glucose are also valid.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sem 4]** **Compare wired and wireless wearable health monitoring systems in terms of mobility, infection risk, and patient comfort.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Wired WHMS | Wireless WHMS | |:----------|:-----------|:--------------| | Mobility | Restricted by cable length | Full freedom of movement | | Infection risk | Higher (cables trap pathogens) | Lower (no cable junctions) | | Patient comfort | Lower (cables restrict posture) | High, enables 24×7 monitoring | | Data integrity | Higher immunity to interference | Needs robust RF/encryption | | Power | Mains powered | Battery + harvesting | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer this as a "definition" instead of a "**comparison**". The question demands a **3-row minimum table**; merely listing points sequentially costs 1 mark. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Sem 4, Module 3]* **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Wearable Health Monitoring System**. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The architecture of a WHMS consists of **five major functional blocks** connected sequentially to form a closed-loop signal chain: 1. **Sensor / Transduction Layer** — Converts physiological variable (pressure, temperature, ion concentration) into an electrical signal. *Example:* PPG photodiode converts blood-volume pulsations into a varying current. 2. **Signal Conditioning (Analog Front-End)** — Amplifies weak biopotentials (µV–mV) and filters out-of-band noise. Uses **Instrumentation Amplifier (CMRR > 100 dB)**, **HPF (0.5 Hz)**, **LPF (40–150 Hz)**, and **50 Hz notch filter**. 3. **Data Acquisition & Processing Layer** — ADC samples the conditioned signal at f_s = 2 × f_max (Nyquist). MCU/DSP runs algorithms like **Pan-Tompkins** for ECG QRS detection, **peak detection** for SpO₂, **FFT** for HRV. 4. **Communication Layer** — Wireless TX via **BLE 5.0 / ZigBee / Wi-Fi** to a gateway (smartphone) which forwards to cloud. 5. **Power Management Layer** — Li-Po battery, energy harvesting (thermoelectric, piezoelectric), and sleep-mode duty cycling. > **Block diagram:** See **Section 4.1** (reproduce in exam). --- **(b)** Explain **PPG-based heart rate and SpO₂ monitoring** with the necessary formulas. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle:** Photoplethysmography (PPG) measures the **volumetric change of blood in micro-vessels** using a light source (LED) and photodetector. The PPG waveform has a **DC component** (tissue, bone, venous blood) and an **AC component** (pulsatile arterial blood). **Heart Rate derivation:** $$ \text{HR (bpm)} = \frac{60}{T_{\text{peak-to-peak}}\ \text{(s)}} $$ **Algorithm:** Detect each PPG peak; measure time between consecutive peaks → invert and multiply by 60. **SpO₂ calculation — Beer–Lambert law:** Two wavelengths are used (Red 660 nm, IR 940 nm). Define the **ratio of ratios**: $$ R = \frac{\left( \dfrac{AC_{\text{red}}}{DC_{\text{red}}} \right)}{\left( \dfrac{AC_{\text{IR}}}{DC_{\text{IR}}} \right)} $$ Empirical calibration: $$ \text{SpO}_2\ (\%) = 110 - 25 R $$ **Numerical example:** If R = 0.4, SpO₂ = 110 − 10 = **100%** (healthy). If R = 1.6, SpO₂ = 110 − 40 = **70%** (severe hypoxia). **KTU 1-liner:** *Oxy-Hb absorbs less red and more IR; deoxy-Hb does the opposite. Hence, R increases as oxygenation drops.* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Sem 4, Module 3, Alternative]* **Q. (a)** Discuss the **different types of sensors** used in WHMS. Compare **PPG, ECG, and bio-impedance** for heart-rate monitoring. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Types of sensors in WHMS:** 1. **Optical (PPG)** — LED + photodetector; non-contact with skin. 2. **Electrochemical (CGM)** — GOx enzyme + electrode; measures glucose. 3. **Electrical (ECG/EEG/EMG electrodes)** — Ag/AgCl wet, dry, capacitive. 4. **Mechanical (MEMS accelerometer, gyroscope, strain gauge)** — Motion, respiration, gait. 5. **Thermal (NTC thermistor, RTD)** — Body/skin temperature. 6. **Biochemical (ISFET, aptamer-based)** — pH, electrolytes, lactate in sweat. **Comparison for heart-rate monitoring:** | Property | PPG | ECG | Bio-impedance | |:---------|:----|:----|:--------------| | Contact | Optical, near-skin | Skin electrodes | 4 electrodes | | Signal | Arterial pulse | Electrical (R-peak) | Thoracic impedance change | | Accuracy | ± 2 bpm (rest) | ± 1 bpm (gold standard) | ± 3 bpm | | Comfort | High (wrist) | Medium (chest strap) | Medium | | Power | Low | Low–Medium | Low | | Form factor | Wrist, ring, ear | Patch, strap | Strap, patch | | Best for | Continuous lifestyle | Clinical / Holter | Respiration + HR combo | **Conclusion:** **ECG** is the clinical gold standard; **PPG** is the most comfortable for 24×7 use; **bio-impedance** is excellent when respiration is also required. --- **(b)** A wearable ECG patch samples the ECG at **f_s = 500 Hz** with a **12-bit ADC** and transmits the data via **BLE 5.0**. The patch uses a **3.7 V, 250 mAh Li-Po** battery.
