The current profile is: * Sense + AFE: 6 mA for 100 ms * MCU active: 10 mA for 50 ms * BLE TX: 18 mA for 20 ms * Sleep: 40 µA for 830 ms **Compute (i) the data rate in kbps and (ii) the battery life in days.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Data rate:** $$ \text{Bits/sample} = 12\ \text{bits} $$ $$ \text{Data rate} = 12 \times 500 = 6000\ \text{bps} = 6\ \text{kbps} $$ *(Add 20% BLE protocol overhead for full link budget — 7.2 kbps, but raw payload = 6 kbps.)* **(ii) Battery life:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{sense}} &= 6\ \text{mA} \times 0.100 = 0.600\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{proc}} &= 10\ \text{mA} \times 0.050 = 0.500\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{tx}} &= 18\ \text{mA} \times 0.020 = 0.360\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{sleep}} &= 0.040\ \text{mA} \times 0.830 = 0.0332\ \text{mC} \\ Q_{\text{total}} &= 0.600 + 0.500 + 0.360 + 0.0332 = 1.4932\ \text{mC} \\ I_{\text{avg}} &= 1.4932\ \text{mA} \\ T_{\text{hours}} &= \frac{250}{1.4932} = 167.43\ \text{h} \\ T_{\text{days}} &= \frac{167.43}{24} = 6.98\ \text{days} \end{aligned} $$ > **Battery life ≈ 7 days.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Top 5 Mistakes on WHMS Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in current calculations (mA vs A) — instant 1-mark loss. > 2. **Drawing sensor block only** when "architecture" is asked — must show **all 5 blocks**. > 3. **Confusing ECG and EEG bandwidths** — ECG = 0.05–150 Hz, EEG = 0.5–100 Hz. > 4. **Not stating CMRR value** when explaining IA — examiners expect **> 100 dB**. > 5. **Mentioning Wi-Fi as default** for outdoor WHMS — should be **LoRa / NB-IoT** for long range, low power. --- ## 6. TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this 1-page summary the night before your exam.** * ✅ **WHMS = 5 layers:** Sensor → AFE → Processing → Communication → Power. * ✅ **Key parameters:** HR, SpO₂, BP, Temp, ECG, EEG, EMG, Respiration, Glucose. * ✅ **PPG formula:** SpO₂ = 110 − 25 R, where R = (AC_red/DC_red) / (AC_IR/DC_IR). * ✅ **Heart rate:** HR = 60 / R-R interval (s). * ✅ **Nyquist:** f_s ≥ 2 × f_max. ECG f_s = 250–500 Hz; EEG f_s = 250–500 Hz. * ✅ **IA gain:** G = 1 + 2R₁/R_g. CMRR > 100 dB. RLD for common-mode rejection. * ✅ **Filter cutoffs:** HPF 0.5 Hz (ECG), LPF 40 Hz diagnostic / 150 Hz monitoring, Notch 50 Hz. * ✅ **Pan-Tompkins:** 5-stage cascade; integrator window N = 0.15 × f_s. * ✅ **Protocols:** BLE 5.0 (wrist), ZigBee (BAN), Wi-Fi (gateway), LoRa/NB-IoT (outdoor). * ✅ **Power budget:** P_total = P_sense + P_proc + P_tx + P_idle; battery life = (C × V) / P. * ✅ **Acronyms:** WHMS, IoMT, PPG, ECG, EMG, EEG, AFE, CMRR, IA, CGM, RLD, BLE, BAN, ISFET, GOx, SpO₂. * ✅ **Heimlich of the WHMS exam:** "If asked to *compare*, **always use a 3-column table**; if asked to *design*, **always draw all 5 layers**; if asked a *numerical*, **always show the charge-balance step**."

topics.title: Need for Wearable Systems, Applications of WHMS - Home Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need for Wearable Systems, Applications of WHMS - Home Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Wearable System

A **Wearable System** (also called a *Wearable Computing Device* or *Wearable Biosensor*) is a class of miniaturized, body-mounted electronic device that integrates **sensors, microprocessors, wireless communication modules, and power sources** to continuously or semi-continuously monitor physiological, biochemical, or motion-related parameters of the wearer without restricting normal ambulatory activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBRT402 - Module 3):**
> A wearable system is a smart electronic device worn on the human body as an accessory or as an implant that integrates sensors, actuators, software, and connectivity to enable real-time data acquisition, processing, and transmission of health parameters for clinical and non-clinical decision-making.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of WHMS (Wearable Health Monitoring System)

A **Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** is a closed-loop, patient-centric IoT-enabled system in which wearable sensors capture vital physiological signals (e.g., ECG, SpO₂, body temperature, blood pressure, glucose) and transmit them—via a wireless personal area network (WPAN) or body area network (WBAN)—to a local hub (smartphone/gateway) or a remote cloud server for storage, analysis, and clinical feedback.

> [!NOTE]
> **WHMS is a subset of IoMT (Internet of Medical Things).** It is characterized by the triad: **SENSE → ANALYZE → ACT.** The "act" part may be a clinician alert, an automated drug delivery, or a patient notification.

## 1.3 What is Home Healthcare in the WHMS Context?

**Home Healthcare** refers to the delivery of medical care, monitoring, and rehabilitation to a patient **within their residence**, supported by remote patient monitoring (RPM) technologies. In a WHMS-enabled home healthcare model, the hospital is *virtually extended* into the patient's home.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Virtual Nurse on Your Wrist"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine a chronic heart patient, Mr. Krishnan, who lives alone in Kochi. Earlier, he had to travel 25 km to the KTU-affiliated medical college every week for an ECG and BP checkup, costing him ₹1500 per visit in travel + wages lost. Now, he wears a smartwatch-like device. While he sleeps, drinks tea, or walks to the temple, the device silently records his heart rhythm, blood oxygen, and step count. If his heart rate crosses **120 bpm at rest**, the watch immediately pushes an SMS to his daughter (a software engineer in Bengaluru) and emails his cardiologist at Aster Medcity. The cardiologist reviews the ECG strip on his tablet and says, "Reduce the Beta-blocker dose." This entire episode—**without any hospital visit**—is the magic of WHMS for Home Healthcare. Mr. Krishnan's watch is the **Virtual Nurse**.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Need (Why Wearables?)

| Pillar | Plain Meaning | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Demographic Pressure** | Aging population, more chronic patients | By **2050**, **22%** of the world's population will be over 60 (WHO). |
| **Economic Burden** | Hospital readmissions are expensive | US healthcare spends **$26 B/year** on avoidable readmissions. |
| **Data Continuity** | A clinic visit is one snapshot; a wearable is a movie | Sampling at **≥ 250 Hz** for ECG vs. intermittent 1-min measurements |

---

## 1.6 Wearable vs. Portable vs. Implantable — A Clear Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Comparison:**
>
> - **Wearable** → Worn *on* the body (skin surface). Example: Smartwatch, fitness band, ECG patch.
> - **Portable** → Carried *near* the body but can be removed easily. Example: Holter monitor, handheld pulse oximeter.
> - **Implantable** → Placed *inside* the body surgically. Example: Pacemaker, implantable loop recorder, continuous glucose sensor (subcutaneous).
>
> The KTU textbook draws the line at *invasion depth*: **Epidermal / Skin-surface = Wearable**.

## 1.7 Classification of Wearable Devices (By Form Factor)

1. **Wrist-worn** — Smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit), smart bands.
2. **Patch-type** — Adhesive ECG patches (e.g., Zio Patch), temperature patches.
3. **Chest-strap / Textile-integrated** — Smart shirts with woven electrodes (e.g., Hexoskin, Myant).
4. **Head-mounted** — EEG headsets (Emotiv EPOC), smart glasses.
5. **Footwear / Insole** — Pressure-sensor insoles for gait analysis in diabetic neuropathy.
6. **Eyewear** — Smart contact lenses for glucose / intraocular pressure.
7. **Implantable (borderline)** — Subcutaneous continuous glucose monitors (Dexcom G7).

---

# SECTION 1 SUMMARY
We have formally defined the Wearable System, the WHMS, and Home Healthcare. We used the **"Virtual Nurse"** analogy to make the concept intuitive. We also established the **three pillars of need** and the *Wearable / Portable / Implantable* distinction—high-yield content for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Need for Wearable Systems" — A Rigorous Justification

The clinical, technological, and socio-economic drivers for wearable systems can be grouped into **seven engineering rationale points**. KTU examiners frequently ask 7-mark questions asking to "Discuss the need for wearable systems."

### RATIONALE 1: The Chronic Disease Epidemic
- **Diabetes:** Globally **537 million** adults (IDF Atlas 2021); projected **643 M by 2030**.
- **Hypertension:** Affects **1.28 billion** adults (WHO 2023).
- **Cardiovascular Disease (CVD):** **17.9 million deaths/year** — the #1 cause of death globally.
- These conditions require **continuous**, not episodic, monitoring.

### RATIONALE 2: Aging Population & Geriatric Care Gap
- The old-age dependency ratio in Kerala is one of India's highest (~21%).
- **50%** of elders in India have at least one chronic ailment (Lancet, 2019).
- Wearables enable *aging in place* — reducing institutionalization.

### RATIONALE 3: Hospital Readmission Reduction
- In the US, **1 in 5 Medicare patients** is readmitted within 30 days.
- A WHMS with automated post-discharge monitoring reduces 30-day heart-failure readmission by **~38%** (meta-analysis, npj Digital Medicine 2020).

### RATIONALE 4: Real-Time Alerts & Preventive Care
- Continuous monitoring can detect **pre-symptomatic** events:
  - Atrial Fibrillation (AFib) episodes lasting only minutes.
  - Nocturnal hypoglycemia in diabetics.
  - Apnea-hypopnea events during sleep.
- Episodic clinical visits **miss** these transient events.

### RATIONALE 5: Personalized & Predictive Medicine
- Wearables generate **longitudinal big data** for each patient.
- Combined with **AI/ML**, this enables personalized baselines and predictive risk scoring.

### RATIONALE 6: Miniaturization & Energy Efficiency
- Moore's Law: Transistor density doubles every **18–24 months**.
- Modern MCUs (e.g., Nordic nRF5340) draw **~5 µA/MHz**.
- Solid-state micro-batteries + energy harvesting (body heat, motion) extend life to **weeks/months**.

### RATIONALE 7: IoT & 5G Connectivity
- Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT) and **5G mMTC** support **1 million devices/km²**.
- Edge computing on the wearable reduces latency to **< 10 ms** for alarms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** When asked "Discuss the need for wearable systems," present the answer in **7 distinct points**, each with a *technical driver + one quantitative statistic*. This scores full marks.

---

## 2.2 WHMS Functional Architecture — Block-Level View

A WHMS, in its canonical form, has **5 layers**:

| Layer | Function | Example Components |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Sensing Layer** | Acquire physiological signals | PPG, ECG electrodes, thermistor, MEMS accelerometer |
| **L2 — Signal Conditioning** | Amplify, filter, digitize | Instrumentation amp (AD8232), analog filters, 16-bit ADC |
| **L3 — Processing Layer** | DSP, feature extraction | Cortex-M4 MCU, FFT, QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins) |
| **L4 — Communication Layer** | Wireless transmission | BLE 5.0, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT |
| **L5 — Application Layer** | Visualization, alerting | Smartphone app, cloud dashboard, clinician portal |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum formula set** expected by the KTU 2024 examiner for any calculation-based sub-question in this module. All quantities are **SI units** unless stated.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** | $f_s$ = sampling freq (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq (Hz) |
| 2 | $T_{QT} = \frac{60}{HR}$ | Cardiac cycle period | $T_{QT}$ in seconds, $HR$ in bpm |
| 3 | $HR = 60 \cdot f_{peak}$ | Heart rate from ECG peak frequency | $f_{peak}$ in Hz |
| 4 | $R = \frac{V_{out}}{I_{in}}$ | Sensor trans-impedance / gain | $R$ in ohms |
| 5 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (voltage) | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 6 | $P_{avg} = I_{avg} \cdot V_{supply}$ | Average power consumption | $P$ in W, $I$ in A, $V$ in V |
| 7 | $E_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt}$ | Stored battery energy | $E$ in Wh, $V$ in V, $C$ in Ah |
| 8 | $T_{life} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$ | Battery life (hours) | $C$ in Ah, $I_{avg}$ in A |
| 9 | $D = \frac{P_{tx}}{P_{rx}}$ | Link loss / path factor | Dimensionless (dB) |
| 10 | $f_{doppler} = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos\theta$ | Doppler shift (motion artifact) | $f_c$ in Hz, $v$ in m/s |
| 11 | $P_{path} = P_{0} \cdot 10^{-\alpha d}$ | Indoor path loss model | $\alpha$ in dB/m, $d$ in m |
| 12 | $Q = C \cdot V$ | Charge stored in capacitor | $Q$ in C, $C$ in F, $V$ in V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Notice formula **#5 uses 20·log₁₀ for voltage** and would use **10·log₁₀ for power**. Many KTU students write 10·log₁₀ for voltage and lose 1 mark.

---

## 2.4 Application: Home Healthcare with WHMS

**Home Healthcare** is the single largest *use-case* of WHMS. Let us define its operational scope:

### 2.4.1 What is Monitored at Home?
1. **Cardiac Monitoring** — 12-lead equivalent via patch, AFib detection, QT-prolongation alerts.
2. **Respiratory Monitoring** — SpO₂, respiratory rate, sleep apnea screening.
3. **Metabolic Monitoring** — Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM), ketone sensors.
4. **Mobility & Fall Detection** — 6-axis IMU for elderly fall alerts.
5. **Medication Adherence** — Smart pill bottles with weight sensors.
6. **Body Temperature** — Continuous fever monitoring (post-op, sepsis watch).
7. **Blood Pressure** — Cuffless BP estimation via PPG + PWV (Pulse Wave Velocity).

### 2.4.2 The Home Healthcare Workflow (Engineered)
- **Step 1:** Patient dons wearable sensors at home (self-application, single-use adhesive).
- **Step 2:** Data is buffered on the wearable and transmitted to a **home gateway** (smart speaker, smart TV, or dedicated hub).
- **Step 3:** The home gateway forwards data to the **cloud** (HIPAA-compliant server).
- **Step 4:** Cloud-based analytics run anomaly detection (e.g., AFib onset).
- **Step 5:** On anomaly, an **alert** is dispatched to caregiver + clinician.
- **Step 6:** Clinician initiates a **tele-consultation** (video) and decides on intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Home healthcare via WHMS is the *practical realization* of the **"Hospital-at-Home" (HaH)** model—a care model proven to reduce 30-day mortality by **~26%** for select conditions (NEJM, 2020 — Levine et al.).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

WHMS in home healthcare enables:
- **Tele-ICU at home** (continuous ICU-grade vitals without ICU bed).
- **Smart Aging** (independent living for seniors).
- **Pandemic-era remote triage** (COVID-19 SpO₂ monitoring at home).
- **Clinical trial decentralization** (e.g., Apple Heart Study enrolled **400,000+** participants remotely).
- **Insurance-linked wellness** (premium discounts for active users).

---

# SECTION 2 SUMMARY
We dissected the **7 rationale points** justifying wearables, mapped the **5-layer WHMS architecture**, listed the **12 critical formulas**, and described the **6-step home healthcare workflow**. This is a high-density section that directly supports KTU's 7-mark and 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Wearable Sensors Used in Home Healthcare

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matrix Style:** KTU examiners award full marks when students present sensor information in a comparative tabular matrix. The matrix below is **exam-ready**.

| Sensor Type | Physiological Signal | Output Range | Frequency Band | Wearable Form Factor | Home Healthcare Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PPG (Photoplethysmogram)** | Blood volume pulse | $0$ – $5$ V (after conditioning) | $0.5$ – $4$ Hz | Wrist watch, ring, earlobe clip | HR, SpO₂, BP (cuffless) |
| **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** | Cardiac electrical potential | $0.5$ – $5$ mV (raw) | $0.05$ – $150$ Hz | Chest patch, smart shirt | Arrhythmia, AFib, QT |
| **MEMS Accelerometer (3-axis)** | Body acceleration | $\pm 2$ g to $\pm 16$ g | DC – $500$ Hz | Wrist, patch, insole | Fall detection, gait |
| **Gyroscope** | Angular velocity | $\pm 2000$ °/s | DC – $200$ Hz | Wrist, patch | Tremor (Parkinson's) |
| **Thermistor / RTD** | Skin temperature | $30$ – $42$ °C | DC – $0.1$ Hz | Patch, skin-adhesive | Fever, sepsis watch |
| **Galvanic Skin Response (GSR)** | Skin conductance | $0.01$ – $100$ µS | $0$ – $5$ Hz | Wrist band | Stress, sleep |
| **CGM (Electrochemical)** | Interstitial glucose | $40$ – $400$ mg/dL | $0$ – $0.05$ Hz | Subcutaneous patch | Diabetes management |
| **Capnography (EtCO₂)** | End-tidal CO₂ | $0$ – $10$ % | $0.1$ – $2$ Hz | Nasal cannula | COPD, sleep apnea |
| **Bio-impedance (BIA)** | Tissue impedance | $200$ – $700$ $\Omega$ | $10$ kHz – $1$ MHz | Patch, smart band | Hydration, body fat |

---

## 3.2 Derivations — KTU Exam Standard (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Multi-line equations use `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks. Each derivation has an explanation row.

---

### DERIVATION 1: Minimum Sampling Rate for ECG

> **Problem:** The ECG signal has useful frequency content from $0.05$ Hz to $150$ Hz. Determine the **minimum sampling rate** required for lossless digitization per the Nyquist criterion.

**Given:** $f_{max} = 150$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem:**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the value of $f_{max}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_s &\geq 2 \cdot 150 \text{ Hz} \\
f_s &\geq 300 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the engineering safety margin of 10%:**

In practice, biomedical engineers oversample to allow for the **anti-aliasing filter roll-off**. A 10% margin gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{s,practical} &= 1.1 \cdot f_{s,min} \\
&= 1.1 \cdot 300 \\
f_{s,practical} &= 330 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering note:** Real KTU-grade ECG wearables (e.g., Apple Watch) sample at **512 Hz** — well above the minimum to give headroom for the Pan-Tompkins QRS detector.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{f_{s,min} = 300 \text{ Hz}; \; f_{s,practical} = 330 \text{ Hz}}$

---

### DERIVATION 2: Heart-Rate Calculation from ECG R-R Interval

> **Problem:** A wearable ECG patch records five consecutive R-peaks at times $t_1 = 0.40$ s, $t_2 = 1.18$ s, $t_3 = 1.95$ s, $t_4 = 2.72$ s, $t_5 = 3.50$ s. Calculate the **average heart rate in bpm**.

**Step 1 — Compute the four R-R intervals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
RR_1 &= t_2 - t_1 = 1.18 - 0.40 = 0.78 \text{ s} \\
RR_2 &= t_3 - t_2 = 1.95 - 1.18 = 0.77 \text{ s} \\
RR_3 &= t_4 - t_3 = 2.72 - 1.95 = 0.77 \text{ s} \\
RR_4 &= t_5 - t_4 = 3.50 - 2.72 = 0.78 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean R-R interval:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{RR} &= \frac{RR_1 + RR_2 + RR_3 + RR_4}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.78 + 0.77 + 0.77 + 0.78}{4} \\
\overline{RR} &= \frac{3.10}{4} = 0.775 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert R-R interval to heart rate using $HR = 60 / \overline{RR}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
HR &= \frac{60}{\overline{RR}} = \frac{60}{0.775} \\
HR &= 77.42 \text{ bpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{HR \approx 77 \text{ bpm}}$

---

### DERIVATION 3: Battery Life of a Wearable

> **Problem:** A wearable health patch uses a **3.7 V, 240 mAh Li-Po battery**. The system draws an average current of **1.2 mA** (active sensing + BLE transmission cycle). Calculate the **battery life in days**.

**Step 1 — Recall the battery-life formula:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $I_{avg}$ to mA (consistent units):**

$$I_{avg} = 1.2 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the life in hours:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \frac{240 \text{ mAh}}{1.2 \text{ mA}} \\
T_{life} &= 200 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:**

$$T_{life,days} = \frac{200}{24} = 8.33 \text{ days}$$

> **Engineering note:** The result (~8 days) matches real-world patches like the Zio XT (up to 14 days). Real devices achieve this by entering deep-sleep modes between transmissions, which our simple $I_{avg}$ model approximates.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_{life} \approx 8.33 \text{ days}}$

---

### DERIVATION 4: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of a PPG Sensor

> **Problem:** A PPG sensor at the wrist produces a clean signal of **$V_{signal} = 2.0$ V peak** and a noise floor of **$V_{noise} = 5$ mV RMS**. Compute the **SNR in dB**.

**Step 1 — Recall the voltage-SNR formula:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Convert units consistently (mV ↔ V):**

$$V_{noise} = 5 \text{ mV} = 0.005 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} = \frac{2.0}{0.005} = 400$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SNR_{dB} &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}(400) \\
&= 20 \cdot (\log_{10}(4) + 2) \\
&= 20 \cdot (0.6021 + 2) \\
&= 20 \cdot 2.6021 \\
SNR_{dB} &= 52.04 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering note:** An SNR > 50 dB is the *clinical-grade benchmark* for ambulatory PPG. Consumer smartwatches typically achieve 35–45 dB.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{SNR = 52.04 \text{ dB}}$

---

### DERIVATION 5: Cuffless Blood Pressure from Pulse Transit Time (PTT)

> **Problem:** A wearable measures the **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** from the ECG R-wave to the PPG foot at the finger as $PTT = 75$ ms. Using the empirical model $SBP = 120 - 0.5 \cdot PTT$ (in appropriate units), estimate the **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)**.

**Step 1 — Substitute PTT in the model:**

$$SBP = 120 - 0.5 \cdot 75$$

$$SBP = 120 - 37.5$$

$$SBP = 82.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

> **Engineering note:** The realistic models are more complex ($SBP = a + b \cdot PTT$ with subject-specific calibration), but KTU problems use a simplified linear form.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{SBP = 82.5 \text{ mmHg}}$

---

# SECTION 3 SUMMARY
We built a **9-row sensor matrix**, then performed **5 full derivations** spanning sampling theory, heart-rate calculation, battery life, SNR, and cuffless BP. Every algebraic transition is shown step-by-step — this section is the **highest-weightage KTU 2024 asset** for Part B numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagrams below use **alphanumeric node IDs only** (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`) and **plain text inside double quotes** to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safe-rendering rules.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a WHMS for Home Healthcare

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeA["Wearable Sensors Layer ECG PPG SpO2 Temp IMU"] --> nodeB["On-Body Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter ADC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Local Processing MCU DSP Feature Extraction"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Wireless Communication BLE 5.0 Wi-Fi LoRa NB-IoT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Home Gateway Smartphone Smart Speaker Dedicated Hub"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Cloud Platform HIPAA Compliant Server Database"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Analytics AI ML Anomaly Detection Risk Score"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Alert Decision Threshold Breached?"}
    nodeH -->|"Yes"| nodeI["Notification Patient Caregiver Clinician SMS App Push"]
    nodeH -->|"No"| nodeJ["Continue Monitoring Log Data"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Tele Consultation Intervention Hospital at Home"]
    nodeJ --> nodeE
    nodeK --> nodeL["Clinical Outcome Reduced Readmission Better QoL"]
```

## 4.2 Use-Case Flow — Elderly Fall Detection at Home

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Elderly Patient at Home Wearing Wrist IMU"] --> node2["Continuous Accelerometer Gyro Data DC to 200 Hz"]
    node2 --> node3{"Fall Signature Detected Acceleration greater than 2.5 g and Post-Impact Inactivity greater than 5 s"}
    node3 -->|"Yes"| node4["Trigger BLE Alert from Watch to Smartphone"]
    node3 -->|"No"| node2
    node4 --> node5["Smartphone App Confirms Event with Audio Prompt"]
    node5 --> node6{"Patient Response within 60 seconds"}
    node6 -->|"No Response"| node7["Auto Escalate to Caregiver SMS Phone Call"]
    node6 -->|"Yes Response"| node8["Log False Alarm Cancel Escalation"]
    node7 --> node9["Emergency Services Dispatched Ambulance Family Notified"]
    node9 --> node10["Hospital Admission Clinical Documentation"]
```

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Cloud Data Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Sensor Electrode or Optical"] --> nodeB["Analog Front End AFE Gain Bandwidth 0.05 to 150 Hz"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["ADC 16 bit Sampling 500 Hz"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["MCU Buffer 256 kB SRAM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["BLE Radio Transmit Burst 1 ms every 1 s"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Gateway Phone"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["MQTT Broker Cloud"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Time Series Database InfluxDB"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Dashboard Clinician App"]
```

## 4.4 Home-Healthcare WHMS Use-Case Matrix (Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Chronic_Disease_Management
        nodeA1["Cardiac AFib Patch"]
        nodeA2["CGM Diabetes"]
        nodeA3["Smart Inhaler Asthma COPD"]
        nodeA4["BP Monitor Hypertension"]
    end
    subgraph Geriatric_Care
        nodeB1["Fall Detection IMU"]
        nodeB2["Medication Adherence Smart Pill Box"]
        nodeB3["Sleep Monitor EEG Mattress"]
        nodeB4["Cognitive Decline Tracker"]
    end
    subgraph Post_Operative_Care
        nodeC1["Temperature Patch Sepsis"]
        nodeC2["Wound Drainage Sensor"]
        nodeC3["Mobility Counter Steps"]
        nodeC4["Pain Score App"]
    end
    subgraph Maternal_and_Child
        nodeD1["Fetal Heart Rate Belt"]
        nodeD2["Neonatal Apnea Mat"]
        nodeD3["Postpartum Mood Wearable"]
        nodeD4["Lactation Tracking"]
    end
    Chronic_Disease_Management --> nodeX["Unified Cloud Data Lake"]
    Geriatric_Care --> nodeX
    Post_Operative_Care --> nodeX
    Maternal_and_Child --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY["Clinician Decision Support AI"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Personalized Treatment Plan"]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — When Does the WHMS Trigger an Alert?

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["New Vital Sample Received HR SpO2 BP Temp"] --> nodeB["Run Rule Based Classifier AND ML Anomaly Model"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Any Parameter Out of Safe Range HR less than 50 or greater than 120 bpm SpO2 less than 92 percent Temp greater than 38.5 C"}
    nodeC -->|"No"| nodeD["Store in DB and Continue"]
    nodeC -->|"Yes"| nodeE["Severity Classify Low Medium High"]
    nodeE -->|"High"| nodeF["Immediate Alert to Clinician Emergency Call"]
    nodeE -->|"Medium"| nodeG["Alert to Caregiver Schedule Tele Consult within 4 hours"]
    nodeE -->|"Low"| nodeH["Patient Self Notification App Banner"]
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeI["Document Outcome and Close Loop"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

# SECTION 4 SUMMARY
We rendered **5 detailed Mermaid diagrams** mapping the WHMS architecture, fall-detection flow, sensor-to-cloud pipeline, use-case matrix, and alert decision tree. These diagrams are **exam-figure substitutes**—students can redraw them in the answer sheet to score full marks on any descriptive 7-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, or short-justification. Answers should be **3–4 sentences** with one example or one statistic. Below are 2 KTU-model questions with model answers.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)**
**Define a Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS). List any two applications of WHMS in Home Healthcare.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** is an IoT-enabled, body-mounted system that uses sensors, microcontrollers, and wireless modules to continuously capture physiological signals and transmit them to a local or cloud-based platform for real-time monitoring and clinical decision support.

**Two applications in Home Healthcare:**

1. **Continuous cardiac monitoring** — Wearable ECG patches (e.g., Zio Patch) detect arrhythmias such as Atrial Fibrillation at home, enabling early intervention and reducing hospital readmissions.

2. **Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM)** — Subcutaneous wearable sensors (e.g., Dexcom G7) track interstitial glucose every few minutes, sending data to the patient's smartphone and alerting both the patient and endocrinologist to hypo-/hyperglycemic events.

> **[Examiner's Note]:** A *definition* + *2 applications* = 3 marks. Including a *brand name* or *statistic* earns an extra conceptual mark (i.e., perfect score).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**
**State the need for wearable systems in modern healthcare. Mention any two socio-economic drivers.**

**Model Answer:**

Modern healthcare demands **continuous, real-time, and personalized** monitoring of patients—something episodic clinical visits cannot provide. Wearable systems fulfill this need by enabling *remote patient monitoring* outside hospital walls, especially for chronic and elderly patients.

**Two socio-economic drivers:**

1. **Aging population** — Globally, the population over 60 years is projected to reach **2.1 billion by 2050** (WHO), creating a massive demand for *aging-in-place* solutions.

2. **High cost of hospital readmissions** — In the US alone, avoidable 30-day hospital readmissions cost an estimated **$26 billion annually**; WHMS reduces these costs by enabling proactive post-discharge monitoring.

> **[Examiner's Note]:** Use the **statistic + mechanism** structure for full marks. Avoid vague answers like "people are sick now."

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** (descriptive) and **(b) 7 marks** (analytical/numerical/case-study).

---

### **QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **need for wearable systems** in modern healthcare. Cover at least five technical and socio-economic drivers with supporting data.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Wearable Health Monitoring System (WHMS)** for **home healthcare**. Identify the role of each layer.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The need for wearable systems arises from a confluence of **clinical, technological, and socio-economic** factors:

1. **Rising burden of chronic diseases** — Cardiovascular disease (17.9 M deaths/year, WHO), diabetes (537 M adults, IDF), and hypertension (1.28 B adults, WHO) demand continuous rather than episodic monitoring. Wearables provide this continuity at scale.

2. **Aging global population** — By 2050, 22% of the world's population will be over 60. Wearables enable *aging in place*, reducing institutionalization and caregiver burden.

3. **Reducing hospital readmissions** — Programs integrating wearables with telehealth have shown 30–40% reduction in 30-day readmission rates for heart failure (npj Digital Medicine, 2020), saving billions globally.

4. **Real-time anomaly detection** — Transient events like paroxysmal AFib, nocturnal hypoglycemia, and sleep apnea can be missed in clinic visits. Wearables capture them 24/7.

5. **Miniaturization & low-power electronics** — Modern MCUs (sub-µA/MHz), solid-state batteries, and energy harvesting enable multi-day wearability without compromising form factor.

6. **IoT and 5G connectivity** — Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (NB-IoT, LoRaWAN) and 5G mMTC support massive device densities with reliable, low-latency uplink—critical for emergency alerts.

7. **Personalized, predictive, and preventive medicine** — Continuous longitudinal data from wearables train AI/ML models for individual risk prediction, moving healthcare from *reactive* to *preventive*.

> **[Score Boost Tip]:** Each driver with a **quantitative statistic** typically earns 1 mark. Mentioning **AI/ML personalization** often earns the 7th bonus mark.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

A **WHMS for home healthcare** is structured as a **5-layer functional architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Sensing Layer:** Body-worn sensors (PPG, ECG electrodes, thermistors, MEMS accelerometers) acquire raw physiological signals. *Example:* A chest patch captures single-lead ECG at 250 Hz.

**Layer 2 — Signal Conditioning Layer:** An analog front-end (AFE) amplifies and filters the raw signals (e.g., AD8232 instrumentation amplifier with 0.05–150 Hz bandpass) and an ADC digitizes them (typically 16-bit resolution).

**Layer 3 — Processing Layer:** A low-power MCU (e.g., Cortex-M4) runs DSP algorithms—QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins), SpO₂ computation, FFT for HRV—compressing the data stream and extracting clinically meaningful features.

**Layer 4 — Communication Layer:** Wireless modules (BLE 5.0, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT) transmit the processed data to a local gateway or directly to the cloud. BLE is preferred for body-area networks due to its **~1 mW** transmit power.

**Layer 5 — Application Layer:** A smartphone app, clinician dashboard, and AI analytics engine visualize data, generate alerts, and feed decision support. The loop closes via tele-consultation or automated caregiver notifications.

For **home healthcare** specifically, the wearable is paired with a **home gateway** (smart speaker, smart TV, or dedicated hub) that acts as a relay to the cloud, ensuring **continuous connectivity** even when the patient is not near their phone.

> **[Score Boost Tip]:** A neat labelled block diagram + one *example component* per layer + a *closing sentence on the home healthcare loop* = full 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **any four major applications of WHMS in home healthcare**, specifying the sensor, the monitored parameter, and the clinical benefit for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable ECG patch operates on a **3.7 V, 240 mAh battery** and draws an average current of **0.8 mA** in a duty-cycled mode (1 minute active, 9 minutes sleep). Calculate the **effective average current**, the **battery life in days**, and the **energy in watt-hours** stored in the battery.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**1. Continuous Cardiac Monitoring (AFib & Arrhythmia Detection)**
- **Sensor:** Single-lead ECG patch (e.g., Zio Patch).
- **Monitored parameter:** R-R interval variability, QRS morphology.
- **Clinical benefit:** Early detection of silent Atrial Fibrillation, reducing stroke risk by enabling timely anticoagulation therapy.

**2. Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) for Diabetes**
- **Sensor:** Subcutaneous electrochemical sensor (e.g., Dexcom G7).
- **Monitored parameter:** Interstitial glucose concentration (mg/dL) every 1–5 minutes.
- **Clinical benefit:** Prevents hypoglycemic events, improves HbA1c by ~1%, and enables automated insulin pump titration.

**3. Sleep Apnea & Respiratory Monitoring**
- **Sensor:** Wrist-worn PPG + nasal airflow cannula.
- **Monitored parameter:** SpO₂ desaturation events, apnea-hypopnea index (AHI).
- **Clinical benefit:** Home-based diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea without overnight polysomnography, enabling CPAP titration at home.

**4. Fall Detection in Elderly**
- **Sensor:** 6-axis IMU (3-axis accelerometer + gyroscope) worn on the wrist.
- **Monitored parameter:** Acceleration magnitude (g) and angular velocity spikes.
- **Clinical benefit:** Automatic alert to caregivers and emergency services within seconds, reducing "long-lie" complications and mortality.

**5. (Optional) Post-Operative Recovery Monitoring**
- **Sensor:** Adhesive temperature patch + accelerometer.
- **Monitored parameter:** Continuous body temperature + mobility (step count).
- **Clinical benefit:** Early detection of post-surgical infection (fever spike) and tracking recovery trajectory at home.

> **[Score Boost Tip]:** Always use the **Sensor → Parameter → Benefit** triplet structure. Each application = ~1.5 marks. Mentioning a real brand/model (e.g., Dexcom G7) demonstrates deep understanding.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Battery: $V_{batt} = 3.7$ V, $C_{batt} = 240$ mAh.
- Duty cycle: 1 min active, 9 min sleep → total cycle = 10 min.
- Active current (typical) = $I_{active} = 4$ mA (estimated standard for active ECG patch).
- Sleep current (negligible) = $I_{sleep} \approx 0$ mA.

> **Note:** The "average current of 0.8 mA" in the problem is the *pre-computed effective average*, but the duty cycle allows us to *derive* it.

**Step 1 — Compute the duty ratio:**

$$D = \frac{t_{active}}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{1 \text{ min}}{10 \text{ min}} = 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective average current:**

Using $I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= 0.1 \cdot 4 \text{ mA} + 0.9 \cdot 0 \text{ mA} \\
I_{avg} &= 0.4 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}$$

> **However, the problem states $I_{avg} = 0.8$ mA — using this directly:**

**Step 3 — Compute battery life in hours:**

$$T_{life} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{240 \text{ mAh}}{0.8 \text{ mA}} = 300 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days:**

$$T_{life,days} = \frac{300}{24} = 12.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the stored energy in watt-hours:**

$$E_{batt} = V_{batt} \cdot C_{batt} = 3.7 \text{ V} \cdot 0.240 \text{ Ah}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{batt} &= 0.888 \text{ Wh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $I_{avg} = 0.4$ mA *(derived from duty cycle)* or $0.8$ mA *(problem-stated)*
- $T_{life} = 12.5$ days
- $E_{batt} = 0.888$ Wh

> **[Score Boost Tip]:** Show **all unit conversions explicitly** (mAh → Ah). Students often lose 1 mark by writing "mAh" in the Wh calculation.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Wearable vs. Implantable** — A subcutaneous CGM is *implantable*, not strictly *wearable*. KTU strictly classifies *epidermal/skin-surface* devices as wearables. Lose 1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong log formula for SNR** — Voltage-SNR uses $20 \log_{10}$, power-SNR uses $10 \log_{10}$. Mixing them loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in the battery-life calculation** — Writing $T = 240/0.8$ without "hours" units costs 0.5 mark. Always annotate.
>
> 4. **Vague "Need for Wearables" answers** — Listing only "easy to use" and "comfortable" without quantitative drivers (cost, statistics, mortality data) scores 4 out of 7.
>
> 5. **Missing the home-gateway layer** — In a WHMS-for-home-healthcare diagram, students often jump from wearable → cloud, missing the *home gateway* (smart speaker/phone). This is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Mixing up sampling theorem inequality direction** — Writing $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$ instead of $\geq$ is an instant 0.5-mark loss.
>
> 7. **Not closing the loop** — A WHMS answer is incomplete without explaining *what happens after the alert* (tele-consult, intervention). KTU examiners award a closing-the-loop mark.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Wearable System** is body-mounted (skin-surface) and integrates *sensors + processing + communication + power*.
- [x] **WHMS = Wearable Health Monitoring System** is a *sense → analyze → act* closed-loop IoMT system.
- [x] **Home Healthcare** is the delivery of medical care at a patient's residence, supported by WHMS.
- [x] The **7 drivers** of wearable need: chronic disease, aging, readmission, real-time alerts, personalization, miniaturization, IoT/5G.
- [x] **WHMS Architecture** = Sensing → Signal Conditioning → Processing → Communication → Application (5 layers).
- [x] **Nyquist rate** for ECG: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot 150 = 300$ Hz; practical $f_s = 500$ Hz.
- [x] **Heart rate** $HR = 60 / \overline{RR}$ (in bpm, with R-R in seconds).
- [x] **Battery life** $T_{life} = C_{batt} / I_{avg}$.
- [x] **Voltage SNR** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$.
- [x] **Sensor triplet** for any application: *Sensor type → Parameter measured → Clinical benefit*.
- [x] Major **Home Healthcare applications**: Cardiac (AFib), CGM (diabetes), SpO₂ (sleep apnea), Fall detection (elderly), Temperature (post-op), BP (cuffless).
- [x] **Home gateway** is a critical intermediate layer between the wearable and the cloud.
- [x] **Real-world examples**: Zio Patch (ECG), Dexcom G7 (CGM), Apple Watch (AFib), Fitbit (HR/SpO₂), Hexoskin (smart shirt).
- [x] Standard benchmark: **SNR > 50 dB** = clinical-grade ambulatory PPG.
- [x] Standard benchmark: **30-day readmission reduced by ~38%** with WHMS.
- [x] Standard benchmark: **CGM improves HbA1c by ~1%** in type-1 diabetes.
- [x] **Hospital-at-Home (HaH)** is the macro-care-model enabled by WHMS.
- [x] KTU examiner tip: Use **statistics + mechanism** in every descriptive answer.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sports/Fitness IoT (S-FIT)** refers to the integration of **wearable biosensors, ambient sensors, edge computing devices, and cloud-based analytics platforms** to continuously monitor, quantify, and enhance human physical performance, athletic training, rehabilitation outcomes, and overall well-being.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Sports/Fitness applications** sit at the intersection of:
- **Biomedical Instrumentation** (acquiring physiological signals such as ECG, PPG, EMG)
- **Internet of Things** (wireless transmission, cloud storage, edge processing)
- **Data Science / Analytics** (extracting actionable insights from biosignals)

### Sub-Domains of Sports/Fitness IoT

| Sub-Domain | Primary Goal | Typical Sensor Suite |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumer Fitness** | Track daily activity, calories, sleep | Accelerometer, PPG, SpO₂ |
| **Sports Performance** | Optimize training, prevent injury | IMU (6/9-axis), GPS, EMG, force plate |
| **Rehabilitation** | Restore function post-injury | IMU, goniometer, EMG, force sensor |
| **Clinical Fitness** | Manage chronic disease via exercise | ECG, SpO₂, blood pressure, glucose |
| **Team Sports Analytics** | Tactics, player load monitoring | GPS vests, HR straps, IMU, RFID |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Coach on Your Wrist"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a personal coach who never sleeps, never gets distracted, and remembers every heartbeat, every stride, and every breath you take. That coach cannot speak, but constantly writes tiny notes about your body. A **fitness IoT system is exactly that coach** — a *sensor* writes the notes (physiological signal), a *microcontroller* translates them (edge processing), a *radio* mails them to a big notebook in the sky (cloud), and a *smartphone app* shows you the highlights reel and tells you what to improve.

### Why "Sports/Fitness" Matters in IoT & Biomedical Engineering
- Global wearable market exceeds **USD 70+ billion** (industry standard metric).
- Real-time biosignal monitoring enables **preventive healthcare** (NEP 2020 alignment).
- Bridges **personalized medicine** and **everyday lifestyle engineering**.

---

## 1.3 Core Components of a Sports/Fitness IoT System

A typical S-FIT pipeline has **four functional layers** aligned with the standard IoT reference architecture:

1. **Perception / Sensor Layer** → Acquires raw biosignals and motion data.
2. **Network / Communication Layer** → Transmits data via BLE, ANT+, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT.
3. **Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer** → Filters, aggregates, runs AI/ML models.
4. **Application Layer** → Mobile apps, dashboards, alerts, coach interfaces.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **BPM** (Beats Per Minute) — heart rate unit
> - **MET** (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) — **1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min**
> - **VO₂ max** — maximal oxygen uptake (mL/kg/min)
> - **HRV** (Heart Rate Variability) — ms
> - **IMU** (Inertial Measurement Unit) — accelerometer + gyroscope (±g, ±°/s)
> - **PPG** (Photoplethysmogram) — optical blood-volume pulse

---

## 1.4 Physiological Signals Used in Sports/Fitness

| Signal | What it Measures | Sensor Type | Placement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** | Electrical heart activity | Ag/AgCl electrodes | Chest strap |
| **PPG** | Blood volume pulse (optical) | LED + Photodiode | Wrist, earlobe |
| **Accelerometer (ACC)** | Linear acceleration | MEMS 3-axis | Wrist, waist, shoe |
| **Gyroscope (GYRO)** | Angular velocity | MEMS 3-axis | Wrist, shoe insole |
| **Magnetometer** | Heading/orientation | Hall-effect / anisotropic magnetoresistive | Wrist, chest |
| **GPS** | Outdoor position & speed | GNSS receiver | Watch, vest |
| **EMG** | Muscle electrical activity | Surface electrodes | Skin over muscle belly |
| **Temperature** | Skin/core temperature | Thermistor, RTD | Skin patch, ingestible |
| **Galvanic Skin Response (GSR)** | Sweat/electrodermal activity | Ag/AgCl electrodes | Fingers, wrist |
| **Spirometry** | Respiration volume/flow | Pressure transducer | Mouthpiece |

---

## 1.5 The Five-Step Signal Journey in S-FIT

```
[1] ACQUISITION  →  [2] CONDITIONING  →  [3] DIGITIZATION  →  [4] PROCESSING  →  [5] DECISION
   (sensor)          (filter, amp)         (ADC)               (MCU/edge)        (alert, display)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *raw data* (e.g., 50 Hz accelerometer stream) with *derived metrics* (e.g., step count = 8,432). Always clarify in exams **which layer** produces which output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Sports/Fitness IoT System

A modern S-FIT system follows a **three-tier architecture** with a feedback loop for *closed-loop training optimization*:

### Tier 1 — Wearable Node (Edge Sensor)
- Houses biosensors, analog front-end, **ADC** (typically 12–24-bit), and an ultra-low-power **MCU** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4).
- Performs **on-node preprocessing**: filtering, peak detection, step counting.
- Communicates via **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — standard protocol for wearables.

### Tier 2 — Edge Gateway (Smartphone / Hub)
- Aggregates data from multiple nodes (watch + HR strap + shoe pod).
- Performs heavier processing: ML inference, activity classification.
- Provides user interface and pushes data upstream.

### Tier 3 — Cloud Platform
- Stores long-term data, runs population-level analytics.
- Returns personalized insights (e.g., recovery score, training load).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Understand the trade-off between *edge processing* (low latency, privacy, battery) and *cloud processing* (high compute, scalable). Examiners frequently test this.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1: Signal Acquisition
- **Accelerometer** samples at **50 Hz** (typical) producing 3-axis `ax, ay, az` in `g` or `m/s²`.
- **PPG** samples at **100–250 Hz** to capture blood-volume pulse waveform.

### Step 2: Magnitude Computation
Combine the three accelerometer axes to remove orientation dependence:
```
|a| = √(ax² + ay² + az²)
```

### Step 3: Gravity Removal (High-Pass Filter)
Subtract the slow-varying gravity component using a high-pass filter or a low-pass estimate:
```
a_dyn(t) = |a(t)| − g_lp(t)
```
where `g_lp(t)` is the low-pass-filtered magnitude (~0.5 Hz cutoff).

### Step 4: Step Detection (Peak Counting)
- Apply a **dynamic threshold** (e.g., 0.1 g above baseline).
- A *step* is registered when `a_dyn` crosses threshold upward, then downward, with a **minimum refractory period** of ~250 ms (to avoid double counting).

### Step 5: Derived Metrics
- **Cadence** (steps/min) = step count in last 60 s.
- **Distance** = step count × stride length.
- **Energy expenditure** via METs.

### Step 6: Wireless Transmission
- BLE packet contains: timestamp, HR, steps, cadence, battery %.
- **Latency target:** < 2 s for live coaching.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — these formulas appear repeatedly in KTU University Exams and are the highest-yield items in Module 3 Sports/Fitness.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accelerometer magnitude | $\vert a \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$ | m/s² or g |
| 2 | Dynamic acceleration | $a_{dyn}(t) = \vert a(t) \vert - g_{lp}(t)$ | Removes gravity |
| 3 | Heart rate (BPM) | $\text{BPM} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | $T_{RR}$ = R-R interval (s) |
| 4 | Heart rate from PPG | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{f_s \cdot 60}{N_{peaks}}$ | $f_s$ = sample rate, $N_{peaks}$ = peaks in window |
| 5 | Energy expenditure (EE) | $\text{EE} = \text{METs} \times W \times t$ | kcal; W = weight (kg), t = time (h) |
| 6 | MET definition | $1\ \text{MET} = 3.5\ \dfrac{\text{mL O}_2}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{min}}$ | Basal metabolic rate |
| 7 | VO₂ max (Cooper test) | $\text{VO}_2\max \approx \dfrac{D - 504.9}{44.73}$ | D = 12-min run distance (m) |
| 8 | VO₂ max (Rockport walk) | $\text{VO}_2\max = 132.853 - 0.0769 W - 0.3877 A + 6.315 G - 3.2649 T$ | W=weight(lb), A=age, G=gender, T=time(min) |
| 9 | HRV (RMSSD) | $\text{RMSSD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1}-RR_i)^2}$ | ms |
| 10 | Stride length | $L_{stride} = h \times k$ | h = height, k ≈ 0.4 (walk), 0.7 (run) |
| 11 | Distance (GPS) | Haversine — see §3 | km / m |
| 12 | Walking speed | $v = L_{stride} \times f_{step}$ | m/s |
| 13 | Training Impulse (TRIMP) | $\text{TRIMP} = D \times \Delta HR \times e^{b \cdot \Delta HR}$ | b ≈ 1.92 (men), 1.67 (women) |
| 14 | Training Load | $\text{Load} = \text{sRPE} \times \text{duration}$ | sRPE = session Rating of Perceived Exertion (0–10) |
| 15 | Resting HR range | $60 \le \text{HR}_{rest} \le 100\ \text{bpm}$ | Bradycardia < 60, Tachycardia > 100 |
| 16 | Max HR (Tanaka) | $\text{HR}_{max} = 208 - 0.7 \times \text{age}$ | bpm |
| 17 | HR Reserve (Karvonen) | $\text{HRR} = \text{HR}_{max} - \text{HR}_{rest}$ | bpm |
| 18 | Target HR zone | $\text{HR}_{target} = \text{HR}_{rest} + k \cdot \text{HRR}$, $k \in [0.5, 0.85]$ | bpm |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Examiners often ask "Why not just divide peaks by 60 instead of multiply by 60/divide?" — answer: peaks are *counted in a window of N samples*, so you must scale by `f_s × 60 / N_peaks` to get BPM. Show the derivation!

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injury Prevention:** Sudden drop in HRV signals overtraining → adjust load.
- **Performance Optimization:** VO₂ max trends guide periodization.
- **Clinical Use:** Continuous ECG monitors (AliveCor, Apple Watch) detect atrial fibrillation.
- **Team Sports:** GPS vests measure *PlayerLoad™* — total body acceleration summed, used to prevent soft-tissue injuries.
- **Rehabilitation:** IMU-based gait analysis quantifies post-stroke recovery.

---

## 2.5 Communication Protocols in S-FIT (Network Layer)

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE 5.0** | ~50–200 m | 2 Mbps | Very low | Wrist ↔ phone |
| **ANT+** | ~30 m | 1 Mbps | Low | HR strap ↔ watch |
| **Wi-Fi** | ~50 m | 100+ Mbps | High | Watch ↔ cloud direct |
| **NFC** | < 10 cm | 424 kbps | Passive | Tap-to-pair |
| **LoRa** | 2–10 km | < 50 kbps | Ultra-low | Remote athlete tracking |
| **NB-IoT** | km scale | 200 kbps | Low | Cellular wearables |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Heart Rate from PPG (Pulse Peak Detection Method)

> **Goal:** Derive the BPM formula given a sampled PPG signal.

### Setup
- PPG sampled at $f_s$ Hz, producing $N$ samples over observation window $T = N / f_s$ seconds.
- Number of systolic peaks detected = $M$.
- Each peak corresponds to one heartbeat.

### Step 1 — Compute the mean inter-beat interval

The total elapsed time covers $M$ heartbeats (if $M$ peaks are detected, there are $M$ intervals between consecutive beats within the window, assuming steady state).

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T}{M} = \frac{N}{M \cdot f_s} \quad \text{[seconds per beat]}$$

**Logic:** The window of $N$ samples spans $T$ seconds, during which $M$ beats occurred, so the average beat period is total time divided by beat count.

### Step 2 — Convert period to frequency (BPM)

$$\text{HR} = \frac{1}{T_{avg}} \times 60$$

$$\text{HR} = \frac{M \cdot f_s}{N} \times 60 \quad \text{[bpm]}$$

**Logic:** Beats per second times 60 gives beats per minute. Multiply numerator and denominator to expose the sampling rate scaling.

### Step 3 — Equivalent compact form

$$\boxed{\;\text{HR}_{bpm} = \frac{60 \cdot M \cdot f_s}{N}\;}$$

**Example:** $f_s = 100$ Hz, $N = 1000$ samples (10 s window), $M = 13$ peaks.

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60 \times 13 \times 100}{1000} = 78\ \text{bpm}$$

This matches a normal resting adult heart rate perfectly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Step Count and Stride-Based Distance

### Step 1 — Magnitude vector

$$|a(t)| = \sqrt{a_x(t)^2 + a_y(t)^2 + a_z(t)^2}$$

**Logic:** Combine all three axes so that the result is invariant to wrist rotation.

### Step 2 — Gravity estimation via moving average

$$g_{lp}(t) = \frac{1}{W} \sum_{k=0}^{W-1} |a(t-k)|$$

where $W$ is a window of $\sim 1$ second.

**Logic:** Acceleration due to gravity changes slowly (≤ 1 Hz) compared to step impact (1–3 Hz). A short-window average approximates it.

### Step 3 — Dynamic component

$$a_{dyn}(t) = |a(t)| - g_{lp}(t)$$

**Logic:** Subtracting the low-frequency gravity component isolates the high-frequency step impacts.

### Step 4 — Step event detection

A step is registered at time $t^*$ if:
- $a_{dyn}(t^*) > \theta_{up}$ (e.g., $\theta_{up} = 0.15\ g$)
- $a_{dyn}(t^* + \Delta t) < \theta_{down}$ within 250 ms
- Minimum gap from previous step $\geq 250$ ms

### Step 5 — Distance

$$D = N_{steps} \times L_{stride} = N_{steps} \times h \times k$$

where $h$ is the user's height and $k \in [0.4, 0.7]$ depending on activity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Energy Expenditure via METs

### Step 1 — MET definition
$$1\ \text{MET} = 3.5\ \frac{\text{mL O}_2}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{min}}$$

### Step 2 — Activity-specific MET lookup

| Activity | METs |
|---|---|
| Sleeping | 0.9 |
| Sitting | 1.3 |
| Walking (3 mph) | 3.5 |
| Brisk walking | 4.3 |
| Running (6 mph) | 9.8 |
| Cycling (moderate) | 6.8 |
| Swimming (moderate) | 7.0 |

### Step 3 — Convert to energy

The caloric equivalent of 1 L of O₂ $\approx 5$ kcal (≈ 20.9 kJ).

$$\text{EE (kcal)} = \text{METs} \times 3.5 \times W \times t \times \frac{5}{1000}$$

Simplified:
$$\text{EE} = 0.0175 \times \text{METs} \times W \times t$$

where $t$ is in **minutes**, $W$ in **kg**.

$$\boxed{\;\text{EE} = 0.0175 \cdot \text{METs} \cdot W \cdot t_{min}\;}$$

**Example:** A 70 kg person runs for 30 minutes at 9.8 METs:

$$\text{EE} = 0.0175 \times 9.8 \times 70 \times 30 = 360.15\ \text{kcal}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation — VO₂ max from Cooper 12-Minute Run Test

The Cooper test is empirical. Distance $D$ (in metres) covered in 12 minutes is linearly correlated with VO₂ max.

$$\text{VO}_2\max = \frac{D - 504.9}{44.73} \quad \text{[mL/kg/min]}$$

**Example:** A runner covers 2400 m in 12 minutes.

$$\text{VO}_2\max = \frac{2400 - 504.9}{44.73} = \frac{1895.1}{44.73} \approx 42.4\ \text{mL/kg/min}$$

This indicates a **good** cardiorespiratory fitness level.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — GPS Distance via Haversine Formula

When consecutive GPS coordinates $(lat_1, lon_1)$ and $(lat_2, lon_2)$ are obtained, the great-circle distance is:

$$\Delta\phi = (\phi_2 - \phi_1) \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}, \quad \Delta\lambda = (\lambda_2 - \lambda_1) \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$$

$$a = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos(\phi_1)\cos(\phi_2)\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)$$

$$c = 2 \cdot \arctan 2\!\left(\sqrt{a},\ \sqrt{1-a}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;d = R \cdot c\;}$$

where $R = 6371$ km (Earth's mean radius).

**Logic:** Haversine gives *great-circle* distance on a sphere, accurate enough for sports tracking.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Method vs Metric

| Derived Metric | Primary Sensor | Algorithm | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heart rate | PPG / ECG | Peak detection | BPM |
| HRV | ECG / PPG | RMSSD / SDNN | ms |
| Steps | Accelerometer | Dynamic peak count | integer count |
| Cadence | Accelerometer | Steps / minute | spm |
| Stride length | Acc + height | Empirical `k × h` | m |
| Distance | GPS or steps × stride | Haversine / cumulative | m / km |
| Speed | GPS derivative or cadence × stride | $v = ds/dt$ | m/s or km/h |
| Calories | Acc + HR | MET lookup or HR-based | kcal |
| VO₂ max | HR + speed/grade | Cooper / Rockport | mL/kg/min |
| Sleep stages | Acc + HR | ML classifier | Awake / Light / Deep / REM |
| Activity class | Acc + gyro | CNN / Random Forest | Walk / Run / Bike / Sit |
| PlayerLoad™ | IMU | $\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$ summed | AU (arbitrary units) |
| Training load | HR + duration | TRIMP / sRPE | dimensionless score |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** List and briefly explain **three physiological signals** commonly used in sports/fitness IoT systems, indicating the sensor for each.

**Model Answer:**
1. **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** Electrical activity of the heart, captured using Ag/AgCl electrodes typically on a chest strap. Used to compute heart rate and HRV.
2. **PPG (Photoplethysmogram):** Optical measurement of blood volume changes using an LED (green/red) and photodiode on the wrist. Used for heart rate and SpO₂.
3. **Accelerometer signal:** 3-axis MEMS sensor measuring linear acceleration. Used to detect steps, cadence, activity type, and sleep.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per signal — name + sensor + application)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Define **VO₂ max**. Mention the standard unit and a **one formula** used to estimate it.

**Model Answer:**
**VO₂ max** is the maximum rate of oxygen uptake by the body during maximal exercise, indicating cardiorespiratory fitness. Standard unit: **mL/kg/min** (millilitres of O₂ per kilogram of body weight per minute).

**Estimation formula (Cooper 12-min Run Test):**
$$\text{VO}_2\max = \frac{D - 504.9}{44.73}\ \text{mL/kg/min}$$
where $D$ is the distance covered (in metres) in 12 minutes.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 unit + 1 formula)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note on KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks, often with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The model answers below are exhaustive.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a wearable sports/fitness IoT system**. List the four major layers and one example sensor/protocol per layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A PPG signal is sampled at $f_s = 125$ Hz for a window of $N = 1500$ samples. Peak detection yields $M = 16$ systolic peaks. Calculate the heart rate in BPM. Show the derivation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture [7 marks]

The wearable sports/fitness IoT system is organized in **four layers** (refer to §4.1 and §4.2 block diagrams):

**1. Perception Layer (1.5 marks)**
- Examples: 3-axis MEMS accelerometer, PPG module, ECG electrodes, GPS receiver, gyroscope, temperature sensor, GSR electrodes.
- Function: Converts physiological or motion phenomena into electrical signals.

**2. Network / Communication Layer (1.5 marks)**
- Examples: **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0)**, ANT+, Wi-Fi, NFC.
- Function: Wireless transmission of packets (e.g., HR, steps, timestamp) to gateway.

**3. Edge Processing Layer (2 marks)**
- Examples: ARM Cortex-M4 MCU, DSP, TinyML engine.
- Function: Filtering, peak detection, step counting, activity classification, on-device alerts.

**4. Cloud / Application Layer (2 marks)**
- Examples: AWS IoT, Google Fit, Apple HealthKit, custom REST APIs.
- Function: Long-term storage, AI analytics, coach dashboards, push notifications.

*(Use a labelled block diagram for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) Heart Rate Calculation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark)**
- $f_s = 125$ Hz
- $N = 1500$ samples
- $M = 16$ peaks

**Step 2 — Compute observation window (1 mark)**

$$T = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{1500}{125} = 12\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Average inter-beat interval (2 marks)**

$$T_{RR} = \frac{T}{M} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75\ \text{s/beat}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to BPM (2 marks)**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} = \frac{60}{0.75} = 80\ \text{bpm}$$

**Step 5 — Verify using the compact formula (1 mark)**

$$\text{HR} = \frac{60 \cdot M \cdot f_s}{N} = \frac{60 \times 16 \times 125}{1500} = \frac{120000}{1500} = 80\ \text{bpm} \checkmark$$

**Result:** Heart rate = **80 BPM** (within the normal resting range of 60–100 bpm).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the **photoplethysmography (PPG) technique** for heart-rate monitoring in wearables. Discuss the role of green vs infrared LEDs and the typical signal processing chain. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 70 kg athlete runs for 45 minutes at an intensity of 9.8 METs. Calculate the **energy expenditure in kcal** using the MET formula. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) PPG Technique [7 marks]

**Principle (2 marks):**
PPG uses an optical method to detect **blood volume changes** in the microvascular bed of tissue. A light source (LED) illuminates the skin; a photodiode detects light either reflected (wrist) or transmitted (fingertip, earlobe). With each heartbeat, blood volume in the capillaries changes, modulating the reflected/transmitted light intensity.

**Green vs Infrared LEDs (2 marks):**
- **Green (520–565 nm):** Strongly absorbed by hemoglobin, gives a high signal-to-noise ratio for **shallow** capillaries near skin surface. **Preferred for wrist-worn** devices during motion.
- **Infrared / Red (650–940 nm):** Penetrates deeper into tissue, less affected by motion artifact, used for **SpO₂** measurement (where two wavelengths are needed).

**Signal Processing Chain (3 marks):**
1. **Transimpedance amplifier** — converts photodiode current to voltage.
2. **Bandpass filter (0.5–8 Hz)** — removes DC baseline and high-frequency noise.
3. **24-bit ADC** (100–250 Hz sampling).
4. **Baseline removal** (moving-average subtraction).
5. **Adaptive threshold peak detector** — robust to motion artifacts.
6. **HR calculation** — `BPM = 60 × M × f_s / N`.

#### Model Solution — (b) Energy Expenditure [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the formula (2 marks)**

$$\text{EE (kcal)} = 0.0175 \times \text{METs} \times W \times t_{min}$$

**Step 2 — Identify given values (1 mark)**
- $\text{METs} = 9.8$
- $W = 70$ kg
- $t = 45$ min

**Step 3 — Substitute (2 marks)**

$$\text{EE} = 0.0175 \times 9.8 \times 70 \times 45$$

**Step 4 — Compute (1 mark)**

$$\text{EE} = 0.0175 \times 9.8 = 0.1715$$
$$0.1715 \times 70 = 12.005$$
$$12.005 \times 45 = 540.225\ \text{kcal}$$

**Step 5 — Final result (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\;\text{EE} \approx 540\ \text{kcal}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert minutes to hours (or hours to minutes) in different formula versions. Memorize the constant `0.0175` version (uses minutes) explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing MET and VO₂ units:** 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min, NOT 1 MET = 3.5 kcal/kg/h. The kcal conversion uses the additional factor 5 kcal/L O₂.
> 3. **HR formula misuse:** Dividing peaks by seconds instead of multiplying by 60, or omitting the $f_s/N$ scaling.
> 4. **Inadequate diagrams:** Block diagrams without arrows showing data flow lose at least 1–2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 5. **PPG answer missing the green/IR distinction** — examiners specifically test this in S-FIT questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Sports/Fitness IoT (Module 3):**

- [ ] **Architecture** has **4 layers**: Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud (Application).
- [ ] **Standard reference protocol** for wearables is **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0)**; **ANT+** is used for HR straps.
- [ ] **PPG** is the optical workhorse of wrist-worn devices; **ECG** is gold standard for HRV.
- [ ] **1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min** — the *anchor* of all energy-expenditure problems.
- [ ] **Energy formula:** `EE = 0.0175 × METs × Weight_kg × Time_min`.
- [ ] **VO₂ max** is expressed in **mL/kg/min**; Cooper test formula uses distance in **metres**.
- [ ] **HR formula:** `BPM = 60 × M × f_s / N` — write it on the exam paper as soon as you see a PPG/ECG problem.
- [ ] **Accelerometer magnitude** formula `|a| = √(ax² + ay² + az²)` removes orientation dependence.
- [ ] **HRV (RMSSD)** in **ms** is the marker of recovery / overtraining.
- [ ] **Stride length** ≈ height × factor (k = 0.4 walk, 0.7 run).
- [ ] **GPS distance** uses the **Haversine formula** with Earth radius R = 6371 km.
- [ ] **Karvonen target HR** uses HRR = HR_max − HR_rest (Tanaka: HR_max = 208 − 0.7·age).
- [ ] **TRIMP and sRPE** quantify training load — a key metric in periodization.
- [ ] **Key sensors to memorise:** Accelerometer, Gyroscope, Magnetometer, PPG, ECG, GPS, EMG, GSR, Temperature.
- [ ] **Memorise activity MET values:** Sleep 0.9, Sit 1.3, Walk 3.5, Run 9.8.
- [ ] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** for 7-mark architecture questions — even a quick sketch earns easy marks.
- [ ] **Differentiate raw data** (50 Hz acc stream) from **derived metrics** (step count, BPM) in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disaster
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Disaster

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Disaster (KTU 2024 Context)

In the context of **PCBRT402 – IoT & Biomedical Applications**, a **Disaster** is formally defined as:

> A serious disruption of the functioning of a community or a society involving widespread human, material, economic, or environmental losses and impacts, which exceeds the community's or society's ability to cope using its own resources. *(Adapted from UNDRR / KTU 2024 Module 3 framework)*

In the **IoT-Biomedical intersection**, a disaster is not merely a natural calamity but a *systemic failure event* where:
- **Mass casualties** overwhelm medical infrastructure.
- **Communication networks** collapse or become saturated.
- **Sensor data streams** spike exponentially (vital signs, environmental, structural).
- **Decision-making** must be decentralized, rapid, and data-driven.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus**: The emphasis is on how **IoT-enabled biomedical systems** act as the *technological nervous system* during disasters — detecting, communicating, triaging, and assisting response.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Disasters

Disasters are broadly classified into the following categories as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| S.No | Category | Sub-Types | Biomedical/IoT Relevance |
|:----:|:---------|:----------|:-------------------------|
| 1 | **Natural Disasters** | Earthquakes, Floods, Tsunamis, Cyclones, Landslides, Droughts | Environmental sensor networks, Search-and-Rescue (SAR) wearables |
| 2 | **Biological / Health Disasters** | Pandemics (COVID-19, SARS), Epidemics, Biological warfare | mHealth surveillance, contact tracing, remote ICU monitoring |
| 3 | **Man-Made / Industrial Disasters** | Chemical spills, Nuclear accidents, Building collapse, Fires | Toxic gas IoT sensors, radiation dosimeters, HazMat wearables |
| 4 | **Complex / Hybrid Disasters** | Climate-induced migration, refugee health crises, Terrorism | Multi-modal IoT fusion, biometric identification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight**: Module 3 particularly stresses **biological disasters and pandemics** because of their direct mapping to *biomedical IoT applications* — namely remote patient monitoring, telehealth, and epidemiological IoT dashboards.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Disaster as a System Shock"

**Real-World Analogy (Intuition Building):**

Imagine your body as a city. A **disaster** is analogous to a **cardiac arrest** — the heart (central command) fails, oxygen (resources) stops reaching organs (population), and within minutes, secondary organs begin to fail. The role of an **IoT-Biomedical system during a disaster** is like the role of an **AED (Automated External Defibrillator) + ambulance telemetry**: it *detects* the shock early, *communicates* the emergency, *monitors* vitals of victims, and *coordinates* response teams in real-time.

Just as a paramedic wearing a smart wearable can relay ECG to a hospital before arrival, IoT sensor nodes in a flood zone can relay water-level, victim heart-rate, and GPS coordinates to a centralized **Disaster Response Cloud**.

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Constants in Disaster IoT Systems

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|:-------|:---------------|:-------------|
| **Golden Hour** | **60 minutes** | Maximum time after injury for life-saving medical intervention |
| **Mass Casualty Threshold** | **≥ 10 victims** triggering MCI protocol | When triage IoT systems activate |
| **START Triage Time** | **≤ 30 seconds per victim** | Standard for field IoT triage devices |
| **Hospital Surge Capacity** | **20% over normal capacity** | Benchmark for IoT load-balancing |
| **Latency Target (Critical Alerts)** | **< 1 second** | For life-critical IoT messages (e.g., wearable vitals) |
| **Bandwidth Priority** | **QoS Class 1** | Reserved for emergency biomedical traffic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Definition**: The **"4 Phases of Disaster Management"** as per KTU 2024 Module 3 are:
> 1. **Mitigation** (Pre-disaster risk reduction)
> 2. **Preparedness** (Planning, training, IoT infrastructure deployment)
> 3. **Response** (Real-time IoT-enabled action)
> 4. **Recovery** (Post-disaster IoT-driven rehabilitation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Phase Disaster Management Cycle (Detailed)

### **Phase 1: Mitigation (Pre-Disaster — IoT in Design)**

- Deploy **environmental IoT sensor grids** (seismic, flood, meteorological).
- Use **digital twins** of cities to simulate disaster scenarios.
- Strengthen **biomedical infrastructure IoT** (smart ICU, automated pharmacies).

### **Phase 2: Preparedness (IoT Infrastructure Standby)**

- **Wearable devices** issued to first responders (vitals + GPS + toxic-gas).
- **Edge computing nodes** pre-loaded with disaster response AI models.
- **Stockpile monitoring** using RFID + IoT (medicines, blood, oxygen).

### **Phase 3: Response (Real-Time IoT Activation)**

- **Real-time victim triage** using smart tags (RFID wristbands).
- **Drones + IoT** for aerial search-and-rescue with thermal cameras.
- **Ad-hoc WSN** (Wireless Sensor Networks) for communication when cellular fails.
- **mHealth apps** for crowdsourced incident reporting.

### **Phase 4: Recovery (Post-Disaster IoT Analytics)**

- **Epidemiological surveillance IoT** to prevent disease outbreaks.
- **Mental health IoT** monitoring PTSD in survivors.
- **Structural health monitoring IoT** for damaged buildings.

---

## 2.2 IoT Architecture Stack for Disaster Management

The KTU 2024 curriculum emphasizes a **5-layer IoT reference architecture** customized for disaster scenarios:

$$\text{Disaster IoT Stack} = \underbrace{\text{Perception}}_{\text{Sensors}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Network}}_{\text{WSN/LoRa/Sat}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Edge}}_{\text{Local AI}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Cloud}}_{\text{Big Data}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Application}}_{\text{Triage/Alert/Map}}$$

Each layer has disaster-specific roles:

| Layer | Disaster Function | Example Devices |
|:------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical/biological data acquisition | Seismic accelerometers, pulse oximeters, gas sensors |
| **Network Layer** | Resilient, low-power, mesh communication | LoRaWAN, ZigBee, Satellite IoT (Iridium) |
| **Edge Layer** | On-site triage decisions (offline-resilient) | Raspberry Pi + TensorFlow Lite triage classifier |
| **Cloud Layer** | Aggregated analytics, ML prediction | AWS IoT Core, Azure Digital Twins |
| **Application Layer** | End-user decision dashboards | Web/mobile apps for incident commanders |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **routinely tested** in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 3.

| Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|:--------|:--------|:----------|:------|
| $EIRP = P_t + G_t - L_c$ | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | $P_t$ = Tx power, $G_t$ = antenna gain, $L_c$ = cable loss | **dBm** |
| $L_{path} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | Free-space path loss (FSPL) | $d$ = distance (km), $f$ = frequency (MHz) | **dB** |
| $SNR = P_{signal} / P_{noise}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Determines reliable sensor comms | **dB** |
| $R_{max} = \sqrt{(P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2) / (P_{min} \cdot (4\pi)^2 \cdot L)}$ | Max IoT link range | $P_{min}$ = receiver sensitivity | **meters** |
| $T_{sensing} = N / f_s$ | Total sensing duration | $N$ = samples, $f_s$ = sampling rate | **seconds** |
| $Q_o = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot q_i$ | Triage priority score | $w_i$ = weight, $q_i$ = vital parameter | **score (0–4)** |
| $B_{req} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} (S_k \cdot f_{tx,k})$ | Required bandwidth in disaster IoT network | $S_k$ = packet size, $f_{tx,k}$ = tx freq | **bps** |
| $E_{node} = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Battery energy of sensor node | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | **Joules** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Memorize the **FSPL formula** and the **EIRP equation** — they appear in nearly every disaster IoT numerical problem.

---

## 2.4 Mathematical Modeling of Disaster IoT Networks

### **2.4.1 Sensor Node Coverage in Disaster Zone**

For a disaster zone approximated as a 2D plane of area $A$, with $N$ IoT sensor nodes each having circular coverage radius $r$:

$$A_{covered} = N \cdot \pi r^2 - A_{overlap}$$

The **overlap correction** for hexagonal deployment (most efficient) is given by:

$$A_{overlap} = N \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{2\pi}{3\sqrt{3}}\right)$$

Thus the **effective covered area** is:

$$A_{eff} = N \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{3\sqrt{3}}$$

### **2.4.2 Network Lifetime Estimation**

For battery-powered IoT nodes:

$$T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{(P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx}) + (P_{idle} \cdot t_{idle}) + (P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep})}$$

In disaster scenarios, transmission duty cycle spikes, hence:

$$T_{lifetime}^{disaster} \ll T_{lifetime}^{normal}$$
