<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define wearable electronics. List any four key characteristics that distinguish a wearable device from a handheld portable device.**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* Wearable electronics are body-conformable smart devices that integrate sensors, processing, communication, and energy modules into clothing or accessories to deliver continuous, unobtrusive monitoring of the wearer's physiology, motion, or environment.

*Four distinguishing characteristics:*

1. **Body conformability** — flexible/stretchable substrate (e.g., polyimide, PDMS, textile).
2. **Always-on, low-power operation** — operates on µW–mW budget to ensure multi-day battery life.
3. **Direct skin or garment integration** — sensor is in contact with body, not held in hand.
4. **Biocompatibility & washability** — must pass ISO 10993 and survive textile wash cycles.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the role of energy harvesting in wearable electronics with two suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

*Role:* Energy harvesting eliminates the need for frequent battery replacement by scavenging ambient energy from the wearer's body, extending operational lifetime and enabling truly *unattended* long-term monitoring.

*Example 1 — Piezoelectric:* A $PVDF$ patch in a shoe insole converts heel-strike mechanical stress into AC voltage, harvested and rectified to charge a thin-film battery.

*Example 2 — Thermoelectric (Seebeck):* A TEG module between skin ($\approx 33°\text{C}$) and ambient air ($\approx 25°\text{C}$) produces $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$, powering low-duty-cycle sensors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a wearable biomedical monitoring system**. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable pulse oximeter uses two LEDs (red 660 nm, IR 940 nm) and a photodiode. Explain the **principle of operation** and the **transmission vs. reflection mode** configurations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture — 7 marks**

A wearable biomedical monitoring system has **five core blocks**:

1. **Sensor / Electrode Layer (2 marks)** — Performs transduction.
   - *Example:* Ag/AgCl electrodes for ECG, optical PPG probe for SpO₂.
   - *Function:* Converts physiological phenomenon (electrical potential, optical absorption) into measurable electrical signal.

2. **Signal Conditioning Unit (1.5 marks)** — Amplifies weak biopotentials ($\mu$V–mV) using instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD8232 with CMRR > 80 dB), filters out 50/60 Hz mains interference via notch filter, and digitizes via 16–24-bit ADC.

3. **Processing / MCU (1.5 marks)** — Runs firmware for feature extraction (R-R interval, heart rate), edge-AI inference, and packetization. Typical SoC: nRF52840 (ARM Cortex-M4, 64 MHz, 256 KB RAM).

4. **Wireless Transceiver (1 mark)** — BLE 5.0 (2.4 GHz, 2 Mbps PHY) for short-range streaming to smartphone gateway.

5. **Energy Module (1 mark)** — Rechargeable Li-Po (3.7 V, 200–500 mAh) supplemented by energy harvester (TENG/TEG).

---

**(b) Pulse Oximeter — 7 marks**

*Principle (4 marks):* Pulse oximetry exploits the differential light absorption of **oxyhaemoglobin (HbO₂)** and **deoxyhaemoglobin (Hb)** at two wavelengths — **660 nm (red)** and **940 nm (infrared)**.

- At 660 nm, Hb absorbs more than HbO₂.
- At 940 nm, HbO₂ absorbs more than Hb.
- Only **arterial (pulsatile) blood** modulates the signal; this AC component divided by the DC baseline yields the **R value**:

$$ R = \dfrac{AC_{660}/DC_{660}}{AC_{940}/DC_{940}} $$

$R$ is then mapped empirically to SpO₂ (%) via a calibration curve.

*Transmission vs. Reflection (3 marks):*

| Aspect | Transmission Mode | Reflection Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Light passes *through* tissue (e.g., fingertip, earlobe) | Light *bounces back* from tissue surface |
| Site | Fingertip, earlobe — thin, well-perfused | Wrist, forehead, chest — thicker sites |
| Wearable suitability | Limited (clips) | **High** — used in smartwatches, patches |
| SNR | Higher (cleaner optical path) | Lower (scattering) — needs careful photodiode placement |

For a **wrist-worn wearable**, reflection mode is mandatory because the device sits on a thick tissue bed with no through-path.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the various **energy harvesting techniques** used in wearable electronics. Compare piezoelectric, thermoelectric, and triboelectric mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable strain sensor has a gauge factor $GF = 2.5$. If the original resistance is $R_0 = 500\,\Omega$ and it undergoes a strain $\varepsilon = 0.04$, find the change in resistance. Comment on the suitability of this sensor for body-motion detection. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Energy Harvesting Techniques — 7 marks**

| Mechanism | Source | Working Principle | Typical Power Density | Wearable Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric** | Body motion / vibration | $V = d_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot t$ — stress on $PVDF$/$PZT$ produces charge | 10–100 µW/cm² | Insole pressure harvester |
| **Thermoelectric (TEG)** | Body-ambient $\Delta T$ | $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$ (Seebeck) | 10–50 µW/cm² | Wristband body-heat harvester |
| **Triboelectric (TENG)** | Skin-clothing friction | Contact-separation charge transfer | 100–1000 µW/cm² (high!) | Glove, jacket motion sensor |
| **Photovoltaic** | Ambient / solar light | Photon → electron-hole pair in Si or organic cell | 1–10 mW/cm² (outdoor) | Smartwatch face |
| **Biofuel cell** | Sweat metabolites | Enzymatic oxidation (glucose + O₂) | 1–10 µW/cm² | Sweat lactate/patch sensor |

*Comparison highlights (2 marks):*
- **TENG** has the highest power density for low-frequency body motion.
- **TEG** is best for *continuous* low-grade harvesting (no motion needed).
- **Piezoelectric** is best for *impulsive* high-force events (heel strike, finger tap).
- **Biofuel cells** are unique in harvesting *chemical* energy — directly relevant to biosensors.

---

**(b) Strain Sensor Calculation — 7 marks**

*Given:* $GF = 2.5$, $R_0 = 500\,\Omega$, $\varepsilon = 0.04$.

**Step 1 — Use the gauge factor formula:**

$$ \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.5 \times 0.04 = 0.10 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta R$:**

$$ \Delta R = 0.10 \times R_0 = 0.10 \times 500 = 50\,\Omega $$

**Step 3 — Final resistance:**

$$ R = R_0 + \Delta R = 500 + 50 = 550\,\Omega $$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 50\,\Omega}$$

*Suitability for body-motion detection (2 marks):*
- Human skin strain during joint flexion can reach **20–30%**.
- A $GF = 2.5$ gives $\Delta R/R = 62.5\%$ at 25% strain — a large, easily detectable signal.
- A standard metallic gauge has $GF \approx 2$; this 25% higher sensitivity is **suitable for finger/wrist motion tracking**, gait analysis, and respiratory chest-belt applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 strain** in the formula leads to wrong $\Delta R$ (a common 2-mark loss).
> 2. **Mixing up R₀ and R** when computing $\Delta R$ — remember, $GF$ always uses the *initial* $R_0$ in the denominator.
> 3. **Skipping units** in Part A answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **Confusing gauge factor** with strain — $GF$ is dimensionless; $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless *but* must be in m/m, not %.
> 5. **Omitting the comparison paragraph** in 7-mark sub-parts — the "comment on suitability" line is mandatory and carries 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Body-conformable smart device integrating sensing, processing, communication, and energy modules.
- **3 W's + 3 S's** framework: *Wearability, Wirelessness, Wear-time* + *Softness, Stretchability, Stability*.
- **Five functional blocks:** Sensor → Conditioning → Processing → Wireless → Energy.
- **Flexible substrates:** PI, PET, PDMS, textile yarns.
- **Gauge Factor formula:** $GF = (\Delta R / R_0) / \varepsilon$ — high yield in KTU numericals.
- **Piezoelectric voltage:** $V = d_{33}\,\sigma\,t$ — body-motion energy harvesting.
- **Seebeck voltage:** $V_{oc} = S\,\Delta T$ — body-heat harvesting ($\Delta T \approx 8°\text{C}$ typical).
- **Battery life formula:** $t_{life} = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$ — always use *time-averaged* current.
- **Pulse oximetry wavelengths:** 660 nm (red) + 940 nm (IR); $R$ ratio mapped to SpO₂.
- **Transmission mode** = through-tissue (fingertip); **Reflection mode** = back-scatter (wrist wearable).
- **BLE 5.0** is the de-facto wearable standard: 2.4 GHz, 2 Mbps, range ≈ 75 m.
- **Design priority order** (from KTU 2024 syllabus): Power → Comfort → Reliability → Performance.
- **Skin-electrode impedance** is modelled as $R_s \parallel C_d$; use gel or dry-electrode tricks.
- **Bending strain:** $\varepsilon = t / (2 r_b)$ — flex-PCBs must keep this below substrate fracture strain.
- **TENG** has the highest power density among body-motion harvesters.
- **Closed-loop wearable example:** CGM → insulin pump = therapeutic wearable.
- **KTU keyword phrases to memorize:** *"smart second skin"*, *"body area network (BAN)"*, *"energy autonomy"*, *"flexible hybrid electronics (FHE)"*.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DRY ELECTRODES: SECTION 1
## Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Dry Electrode** is a biopotential signal acquisition transducer that establishes an electrical coupling between the human body (an ionic conductor) and the front-end electronic measurement circuit (an electronic conductor) **without the use of a conductive electrolytic gel, paste, or fluid interface**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PCBRT402 — IoT & Biomedical Applications), dry electrodes are classified as **non-invasive, gel-free epidermal transducers** that convert ionic bio-currents originating from excitable tissues (cardiac, neural, muscular) into measurable electron flow within an instrumentation amplifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 3.2):** Dry electrodes belong to the broader family of "Electrode-Skin Interfaces" and are a direct enabler of *wearable, ambulatory, and continuous remote patient monitoring (RPM)* systems — the foundational use case of Biomedical IoT (Bio-IoT).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to listen to the whisper of a friend sitting in a swimming pool. If you put your ear right at the water surface, the air–water interface muffles and distorts the sound. So you press your ear *into* the water — but you must first wet your ear to "match" the mediums. A **wet electrode is that wet ear**: we slather gel on the skin to "match" the body's ionic fluid to the electrode's electronic world.

A **dry electrode is like a very sophisticated underwater microphone** placed directly against the wall of the pool — it has specially engineered materials (conductive polymers, micro-spikes, capacitive plates) that can "hear" the ionic whispers *without* any liquid bridge. The catch? It is more sensitive to ambient noise (motion, sweat variability), so the electronics behind it must work harder.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Stratum corneum thickness | $d_{sc}$ | **15–40 µm** (dry); hydrated: **10 µm** |
| Skin-electrode contact impedance (dry, 10 Hz) | $Z_{c}$ | **10 kΩ – 5 MΩ** |
| Skin-electrode contact impedance (wet Ag/AgCl, 10 Hz) | $Z_{c,wet}$ | **1–10 kΩ** |
| Electrode DC half-cell potential | $E_{hc}$ | ideally **0 mV** (matched pair) |
| Standard biopotential bandwidth | $BW$ | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (ECG) |
| Body Temperature | $T$ | **310 K (37 °C)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter at KTU exams:** Examiners love asking why dry electrodes suffer from *higher low-frequency noise*. The answer lies in the **10 kΩ – 5 MΩ impedance range**, which when paired with a 10 GΩ amplifier input impedance still yields a non-trivial voltage divider loss.

### 1.4 The Three Functional Families of Dry Electrodes

> [!NOTE]
> **Taxonomy (must memorize for 2-mark / 3-mark questions):**
> 1. **Contact (Resistive) Dry Electrodes** — physically touch the skin; rely on direct electron transfer via conductive polymer or metallic micro-structures.
> 2. **Capacitive (Non-Contact) Dry Electrodes** — use a thin dielectric layer; no galvanic (DC) path; only AC displacement current is sensed.
> 3. **Microneedle / Quasi-Dry Electrodes** — arrays of micro-scale spikes (50–500 µm tall) that *painlessly* perforate the stratum corneum to lower contact impedance without a wet reservoir.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DRY ELECTRODES: SECTION 2
## Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Skin-Electrode Interface — Operational Breakdown

When a dry electrode is placed on the skin, the following physical layers exist in series from the body to the metal/polymer conductor:

1. **Stratum Corneum (SC):** the outermost, dead, keratinized layer. Its high resistance (10⁵–10⁶ Ω·cm at low frequency) is the *primary* noise and impedance contributor.
2. **Viable Epidermis + Dermis:** living tissue with relatively low impedance due to blood/electrolyte content.
3. **Electrode Surface Layer:** for non-polarizable (wet Ag/AgCl) electrodes, a Helmholtz double layer forms. For dry polarizable electrodes, only an RC interface exists.
4. **Bulk Electrode Material:** the conductor (metal, conductive polymer, carbon composite).

Each layer stores and dissipates energy differently, leading to a **distributed RC ladder** rather than a single resistor. For analysis, this is collapsed into a lumped equivalent circuit (Section 2.3).

### 2.2 Why "Dry" is Hard — The Core Engineering Problem

| Phenomenon | Wet Ag/AgCl | Dry Electrode |
|------------|-------------|---------------|
| Charge transfer mechanism | Faradaic (reversible redox) | Mostly capacitive (non-Faradaic) |
| Half-cell potential stability | ±1 mV drift | ±50–200 mV drift |
| Skin impedance at 10 Hz | ~2 kΩ | ~50 kΩ – 1 MΩ |
| Motion artifact | Low | **High** (ΔZ dominant) |
| Long-term wear (>24 h) | Skin irritation | Possible |
| Setup time | Requires prep | Plug-and-play |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the problem:** A wet electrode establishes a *reversible chemical equilibrium* at the boundary — current can cross without polarization. A dry electrode, by contrast, behaves like a **capacitor**: current can only flow via charging/discharging the interface capacitance. At DC this means **no stable DC bias** is established, hence baseline wander and motion artifacts dominate.

### 2.3 Equivalent Circuit (Lumped Model)

The standard **EDLC (Electrical Double Layer Capacitance) + Charge Transfer Model**:

$$
Z_{se}(\omega) \;=\; R_{s} \;+\; \frac{R_{ct}}{1 \;+\; j\omega R_{ct} C_{d}}
$$

Where:
- $R_{s}$ — series bulk resistance of gel/tissue/electrode
- $R_{ct}$ — charge-transfer resistance (very high for dry, nearly infinite for ideal capacitive)
- $C_{d}$ — double-layer capacitance at the interface
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ — angular frequency

For an **ideal pure capacitive dry electrode**, $R_{ct} \to \infty$, simplifying the model to:

$$
Z_{se}(\omega) \;=\; R_{s} \;+\; \frac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory table — memorize before ESE.** Critical note: the pipe symbol is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $E_{hc} = E^{0} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log[Cl^{-}]$ | Nernst half-cell potential (Ag/AgCl) | Volts |
| 2 | $Z_{se}(\omega) = R_{s} + \dfrac{R_{ct}}{1 + j\omega R_{ct} C_{d}}$ | Skin-electrode interface impedance | Ω |
| 3 | $Z_{cap}(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}$ | Pure capacitive electrode impedance | Ω |
| 4 | $V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_{B} T R \Delta f}$ | Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise | V |
| 5 | $V_{motion} = I_{b} \cdot \Delta Z_{se}$ | Motion-artifact induced voltage | V |
| 6 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 7 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C_{d}}$ | High-pass corner (electrode RC) | Hz |
| 8 | $C_{d} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate double-layer capacitance | F |
| 9 | $V_{out} = V_{in}\!\left(\dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + Z_{se}}\right)$ | Voltage divider loss at amplifier input | V |
| 10 | $R_{ct} = \dfrac{RT}{nF i_{0}}$ | Charge-transfer resistance (Butler-Volmer) | Ω |

**Universal constants used:**
- $k_{B} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Bio-IoT wearables** (Apple Watch ECG, AliveCor KardiaMobile, Empatica E4, Muse EEG headband), dry electrodes are the *only* viable option because:

1. **Continuous wearability** — no gel drying out or skin irritation
2. **Hygiene** — non-contact capacitive variants can be embedded in steering wheels, helmets, or car seats for driver monitoring
3. **Mass-deployment** — no per-use consumables (gel, abrasive paste)
4. **IoT integration** — Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) front-ends connect directly to cloud dashboards via gateways

The cost is **electronic complexity** — high-CMRR (>100 dB) instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD8232, INA128) with active driven-right-leg (DRL) loops and impedance-tracking calibration are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DRY ELECTRODES: SECTION 3
## Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skin-Electrode Interface Impedance Magnitude

**Goal:** Derive the magnitude $\vert Z_{se}(\omega) \vert$ as a function of frequency and prove the *low-frequency roll-off* characteristic that plagues dry electrodes.

**Starting from the generic interface model:**

$$
Z_{se}(\omega) \;=\; R_{s} \;+\; \frac{R_{ct}}{1 \;+\; j\omega R_{ct} C_{d}}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate** $(1 - j\omega R_{ct} C_{d})$:

$$
Z_{se}(\omega) \;=\; R_{s} \;+\; \frac{R_{ct}(1 - j\omega R_{ct} C_{d})}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{d})^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Split into real and imaginary parts:**

$$
Z_{se}(\omega) \;=\; \left[ R_{s} + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{d})^{2}} \right] \;-\; j\left[ \frac{\omega R_{ct}^{2} C_{d}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{d})^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude** $\vert Z_{se} \vert = \sqrt{(\text{Re})^{2} + (\text{Im})^{2}}$:

$$
\vert Z_{se}(\omega) \vert \;=\; \sqrt{ \left( R_{s} + \frac{R_{ct}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{d})^{2}} \right)^{2} \;+\; \left( \frac{\omega R_{ct}^{2} C_{d}}{1 + (\omega R_{ct} C_{d})^{2}} \right)^{2} }
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases (exam favorites):**

$$
\text{(a) At low frequency } (\omega \to 0): \quad \vert Z_{se} \vert \;\to\; R_{s} + R_{ct}
$$

$$
\text{(b) At high frequency } (\omega \to \infty): \quad \vert Z_{se} \vert \;\to\; R_{s}
$$

This proves that the dry electrode interface behaves like a **first-order high-pass filter** with corner frequency:

$$
f_{c} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R_{ct} C_{d}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $R_{ct}$ for a capacitive dry electrode is enormous (10⁸–10⁹ Ω). With $C_{d} \approx 10$ nF, the corner falls to ~0.016 Hz — *inside* the ECG bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz), which is exactly why ECG baseline wander is severe in dry systems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) Noise Voltage

**Goal:** Show the dominant noise floor for a high-impedance dry electrode system.

**Starting from the Johnson-Nyquist relation:**

$$
\overline{V_{n}^{2}} \;=\; 4 k_{B} T R \Delta f
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute representative values** for a wearable ECG dry electrode at body temperature:

- $k_{B} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T = 310$ K
- $R = 100$ kΩ $= 10^{5}$ Ω (mid-range dry contact impedance)
- $\Delta f = 150$ Hz (ECG bandwidth)

$$
\overline{V_{n}^{2}} \;=\; 4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (10^{5}) \times 150
$$

**Step 2 — Arithmetic (step-by-step):**

$$
\overline{V_{n}^{2}} \;=\; 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 10^{-23} \times 10^{5} \times 150
$$

$$
=\; 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 150 \times 10^{-18}
$$

$$
=\; 4 \times 64{,}170 \times 10^{-18}
$$

$$
=\; 2.5668 \times 10^{-13} \; V^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root for RMS voltage:**

$$
V_{n,rms} \;=\; \sqrt{2.5668 \times 10^{-13}} \;\approx\; 5.07 \times 10^{-7} \;V \;=\; 0.507 \; \mu V
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key exam insight:** The thermal noise scales as $\sqrt{R}$. Increasing dry-electrode impedance from 2 kΩ (wet) to 100 kΩ raises noise by a factor of $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07\times$. This is a non-negotiable trade-off of dry systems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motion Artifact Voltage

**Goal:** Quantify baseline wander caused by skin stretching during patient motion.

**Model:** A perturbation $\Delta Z_{se}$ in interface impedance, driven by galvanic bias current $I_{b}$ from the amplifier's DRL or input bias network.

$$
V_{motion} \;=\; I_{b} \cdot \Delta Z_{se}
$$

**Step 1 — Typical bias current** of a FET-input instrumentation amplifier: $I_{b} = 200$ pA $= 2 \times 10^{-10}$ A.

**Step 2 — Motion-induced impedance swing** for a dry electrode: $\Delta Z_{se} \approx 500$ kΩ $= 5 \times 10^{5}$ Ω.

**Step 3 — Compute the artifact voltage:**

$$
V_{motion} \;=\; (2 \times 10^{-10}) \times (5 \times 10^{5}) \;=\; 10^{-4} \;V \;=\; 100 \; \mu V
$$

This **100 µV** sits squarely within the ECG's QRS amplitude range (~1 mV), confirming that dry electrodes suffer a *signal-correlated* artifact roughly **10%** of the QRS amplitude — a critical noise source in ambulatory Bio-IoT systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Dry Electrodes

| Material Class | Example | $\rho$ (Ω·cm) | $C_{d}$ Behaviour | Best Use Case |
|----------------|---------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Noble metal | Au, Pt | $2.2 \times 10^{-6}$ | Low, polarizable | Capacitive |
| Ag/AgCl (textured) | Sintered Ag/AgCl | Bulk metal | High, quasi-reversible | Hybrid wet/dry |
| Conductive polymer | PEDOT:PSS | $10^{2}$–$10^{4}$ | Tunable via doping | Flexible ECG |
| Carbon nanotube (CNT) | Buckypaper | $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$ | Excellent | Wearable patches |
| Graphene | CVD monolayer | $10^{-6}$ | Ultra-high | Research-grade |
| Silver nanowire (AgNW) | Network film | $10^{-4}$ | High | Stretchable electrodes |
| Textile (yarn-coated) | PEDOT:cotton | $10^{1}$–$10^{3}$ | Variable | Smart garments |

### 3.5 KTU Numerical Sample — Solved

**Question:** A capacitive dry ECG electrode has $C_{d} = 22$ nF and the underlying tissue-electrode series resistance is $R_{s} = 50$ kΩ. Compute the impedance magnitude at $f = 10$ Hz and at $f = 100$ Hz.

**Solution:**

For a pure capacitive dry electrode: $Z_{se} = R_{s} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}$

**At f = 10 Hz:**

$$
\omega = 2\pi \times 10 = 62.83 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\omega C_{d}} = \frac{1}{62.83 \times 22 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{1.382 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7.235 \times 10^{5} \; \Omega
$$

$$
\vert Z_{se} \vert = \sqrt{R_{s}^{2} + \left(\frac{1}{\omega C_{d}}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{4})^{2} + (7.235 \times 10^{5})^{2}}
$$

$$
\approx 7.24 \times 10^{5} \; \Omega \;\; (\approx 724 \; \text{k}\Omega)
$$

**At f = 100 Hz:**

$$
\omega = 628.3 \; \text{rad/s}, \quad \frac{1}{\omega C_{d}} \approx 7.235 \times 10^{4} \; \Omega
$$

$$
\vert Z_{se} \vert \approx \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{4})^{2} + (7.235 \times 10^{4})^{2}} \approx 8.79 \times 10^{4} \; \Omega \;\; (\approx 88 \; \text{k}\Omega)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away for the student:** As frequency rises 10×, the impedance falls ~8.2× — proving the capacitive dominance of the dry interface and justifying the **high input-impedance, AC-coupled amplifier front-end** required in dry-electrode Bio-IoT front-ends.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DRY ELECTRODES: SECTION 4
## Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Skin-Electrode Interface (Lumped Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biopotential Source - ECG 1 mV - EEG 50 uV] --> B[Stratum Corneum - Rsc High Z - 15 to 40 um]
    B --> C[Viable Epidermis Dermis - Low Z Tissue - Ionic Conductor]
    C --> D[Dry Electrode Interface - Polarizable RC - Cd High Rct High]
    D --> E[Bulk Electrode Material - PEDOT or AgNW or Metal - Low Resistance]
    E --> F[Instrumentation Amplifier - INA128 or AD8232 - CMRR greater than 100 dB]
    F --> G[ADC 16 bit 500 sps] --> H[BLE Transceiver - IoT Gateway]
    H --> I[Cloud Dashboard - RPM Server]

    B -. High motion artifact .-> D
    C -. Sweat variability .-> D
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Dry Electrode Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1[Cardiac ECG 0.05 to 150 Hz] --> mux
    src2[Neural EEG 0.5 to 100 Hz] --> mux
    src3[Muscular EMG 20 to 500 Hz] --> mux
    mux{Front End Multiplexer} --> ia[INA High Z greater than 10 GOhm]
    ia --> hp[Active High Pass 0.05 Hz]
    hp --> lp[Low Pass Anti Alias 150 Hz]
    lp --> adc[16 bit Sigma Delta ADC]
    adc --> mcu[ARM Cortex M4 MCU]
    mcu --> ble[BLE 5.0 Radio]
    ble --> cloud[IoT Cloud MQTT Broker]
    cloud --> ml[Edge ML Artifact Removal]
    ml --> doc[Clinician Dashboard]

    ia -. DRL Driven Right Leg Loop .-> src1
    ia -. DRL Driven Right Leg Loop .-> src2
    ia -. DRL Driven Right Leg Loop .-> src3
```

### 4.3 Comparative Type Diagram — Three Dry Electrode Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Dry Electrode Taxonomy] --> contact[Contact Resistive Dry]
    root --> cap[Capacitive Non Contact Dry]
    root --> micro[Microneedle Quasi Dry]

    contact --> pedot[Conductive Polymer PEDOT PSS]
    contact --> agnw[Silver Nanowire Film]
    contact --> cnt[Carbon Nanotube Buckypaper]

    cap --> plate[Parallel Plate with Dielectric]
    cap --> tex[Textile Integrated Capacitor]

    micro --> sil[Silicon Microneedle 200 um]
    micro --> pol[Polymer Microneedle Dissolvable]
    micro --> met[Metal Microneedle Coated]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison — Wet vs Dry Electrode Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wet[Wet AgAgCl Electrode] -- Faradaic Reversible Redox --> wetcir["Rct LOW around 1 kOhm || Cd MEDIUM 1 uF"]
    dry[Dry Capacitive Electrode] -- Non Faradaic Displacement Current --> drycir["Rct HIGH around 1 GOhm || Cd LOW 10 nF"]
    wetcir --> amp1[INA Front End]
    drycir --> amp2[INA Front End - Active DRL Required]
    amp1 --> sig1[Clean ECG Signal]
    amp2 --> sig2[Noisy ECG Signal - Needs Adaptive Filtering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked to draw the equivalent circuit, always show **two parallel RC branches**: one for the double-layer ($C_{d} \parallel R_{ct}$), and a series resistor $R_{s}$. For *capacitive* dry electrodes, omit $R_{ct}$ (treat as open circuit). For *microneedle*, add a small $R_{ct}$ in parallel since the spike tips provide micro-Faradaic contact.
