# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Programming & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setting Up the Raspberry Pi for Python IoT Programming

### Step 1: Hardware Prerequisites

1. Raspberry Pi (3B+/4/Zero 2 W recommended)
2. MicroSD card (≥ 16 GB) with Raspberry Pi OS flashed
3. Breadboard, LEDs, resistors (330 Ω), jumper wires
4. Sensors (e.g., DHT11 for temperature/humidity, HC-SR04 for distance)

### Step 2: Enable Hardware Interfaces

Open the Raspberry Pi configuration tool:

```bash
sudo raspi-config
```

Navigate to **Interface Options** → Enable **I2C**, **SPI**, **Serial**, and **1-Wire** as required.

### Step 3: Install Required Python Libraries

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3-pip python3-dev
pip3 install RPi.GPIO
pip3 install smbus2
pip3 install pyserial
pip3 install gpiozero
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Roles:**
> - **RPi.GPIO** → Direct pin-level control (LED, button, PWM).
> - **gpiozero** → High-level abstraction built on top of RPi.GPIO (recommended for beginners).
> - **smbus2** → For I²C device communication.
> - **pyserial** → For UART communication on the RPi.

## 3.2 Program 1 — Blinking an LED (GPIO Output)

This is the *Hello World* of IoT and a common 3-mark question.

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# Step 1: Choose the pin numbering mode (BCM = Broadcom chip numbers)
LED_PIN = 17
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)

# Step 2: Configure the pin as an OUTPUT
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

# Step 3: Loop - Blink the LED 5 times
try:
    for i in range(5):
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # Turn LED ON (3.3V)
        time.sleep(1)                     # Wait 1 second
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # Turn LED OFF (0V)
        time.sleep(1)                     # Wait 1 second

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass  # Allow Ctrl+C to exit cleanly

# Step 4: Cleanup - Reset all pins to a safe state
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

### Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

1. **`import RPi.GPIO as GPIO`** → Loads the GPIO driver library. `as GPIO` is an alias to shorten references.
2. **`GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)`** → Sets numbering to BCM mode (refer to pin by GPIO number, not physical pin position). Alternative: `GPIO.BOARD` uses physical pin numbers.
3. **`GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)`** → Configures GPIO 17 as an output pin. Internal pull-up/pull-down resistors are not configured for outputs.
4. **`GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.HIGH)`** → Drives the pin to 3.3 V (logic 1).
5. **`GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.LOW)`** → Drives the pin to 0 V (logic 0).
6. **`GPIO.cleanup()`** → Resets all pins to their default input state — **critical** to prevent damaging the Pi if the script is rerun.

## 3.3 Program 2 — Reading a Push Button (GPIO Input with Pull-up)

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

BUTTON_PIN = 27
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)

# Configure as input with internal pull-up resistor enabled
GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

print("Press the button (Ctrl+C to exit)")
try:
    while True:
        input_state = GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN)
        if input_state == False:          # Button pressed = LOW (pull-up active)
            print("Button Pressed")
            time.sleep(0.2)               # Debounce delay
        else:
            print("Button Released")
            time.sleep(0.1)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull-up Resistor Logic:** A floating input pin can randomly read HIGH or LOW. The internal pull-up resistor ties the pin to 3.3 V by default, so it reads HIGH when the button is *open*. Pressing the button connects the pin to GND, so it reads LOW. This is why `if input_state == False:` means *pressed*.

## 3.4 Program 3 — UART Serial Communication (RPi ↔ Arduino / PC)

```python
import serial
import time

# Open the serial port
# /dev/ttyS0 = hardware UART, /dev/ttyUSB0 = USB-to-Serial
ser = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyS0', baudrate=9600, timeout=1)

# Flush any stale data in the buffer
ser.flush()

try:
    while True:
        # Write a line of data (must be bytes, with newline as delimiter)
        ser.write(b'Hello from Raspberry Pi!\n')
        print("Sent: Hello from Raspberry Pi!")

        # Read any incoming data
        if ser.in_waiting > 0:
            received = ser.readline().decode('utf-8').strip()
            print(f"Received: {received}")

        time.sleep(2)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Exiting...")
finally:
    ser.close()
    print("Serial port closed")
```

### Key pyserial Functions

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `serial.Serial(port, baudrate, ...)` | Opens the serial port |
| `ser.write(bytes_data)` | Transmits data |
| `ser.read(n)` | Reads n bytes |
| `ser.readline()` | Reads until newline `\n` |
| `ser.in_waiting` | Number of bytes in the receive buffer |
| `ser.close()` | Closes the port |

## 3.5 Program 4 — I²C Communication (Reading Temperature from LM75 / DS18B20)

```python
import smbus2
import time

# Create an I2C bus object
bus = smbus2.SMBus(1)   # Bus 1 is the default I2C bus on modern RPi

# LM75 sensor address (commonly 0x48, with A0-A2 pins tied to GND)
LM75_ADDR = 0x48
TEMP_REG  = 0x00         # Temperature register address

def read_temperature():
    # Read 2 bytes from the temperature register
    raw = bus.read_i2c_block_data(LM75_ADDR, TEMP_REG, 2)

    # LM75 returns temperature in 9-bit two's complement
    # The first byte is the integer part, second byte has fraction (MSB only)
    temp_int = raw[0]
    # Combine and shift to handle negative temperatures
    temp_combined = (raw[0] << 1) | (raw[1] >> 7)

    # Convert to signed integer (two's complement)
    if temp_combined & 0x100:           # Negative (bit 8 set)
        temp_combined -= 512

    # Each LSB = 0.5 °C for LM75
    temperature = temp_combined * 0.5
    return temperature

try:
    while True:
        temp = read_temperature()
        print(f"Temperature: {temp} °C")
        time.sleep(1)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    bus.close()
```

## 3.6 Program 5 — SPI Communication (MCP3008 Analog-to-Digital Converter)

```python
import spidev
import time

# Open SPI bus
spi = spidev.SpiDev()
spi.open(0, 0)              # Bus 0, Device (CS) 0
spi.max_speed_hz = 1350000  # 1.35 MHz

def read_adc(channel):
    """Read from MCP3008 (10-bit ADC), channel 0-7"""
    # Build the command bytes for MCP3008
    # First byte: start bit (1) + single-ended mode (1) << 4
    cmd1 = 0b00000001
    # Second byte: configuration bits
    cmd2 = (0b1000 + channel) << 4

    # Send 3 bytes and receive 3 bytes
    reply = spi.xfer2([cmd1, cmd2, 0x00])

    # Combine bytes to get 10-bit value
    adc_value = ((reply[1] & 0x03) << 8) | reply[2]
    return adc_value

try:
    while True:
        value = read_adc(0)              # Read from channel 0
        voltage = (value * 3.3) / 1023   # Convert to voltage (10-bit, 3.3V ref)
        print(f"ADC Value: {value}, Voltage: {voltage:.2f} V")
        time.sleep(1)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    spi.close()
```

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Interface

| Requirement | Best Protocol | Justification |
|-------------|--------------|---------------|
| Single sensor, low data rate (temperature) | I²C | Few wires, addressable, energy efficient |
| Streaming high-speed data to an SD card | SPI | High bandwidth, full-duplex |
| Debug logs from a microcontroller | UART | Asynchronous, simple, universal |
| Multiple identical sensors | I²C | Each can have a different address |
| Driving a TFT LCD display | SPI | High frame rate needs high bandwidth |
| GPS module output | UART | GPS modules natively output NMEA via UART |

## 4.3 UART Communication Flow (Master-Slave Exchange)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as RPi (Master)
    participant Bus as UART Wire Pair
    participant Slave as Arduino (Slave)

    Note over Master,Slave: Both configured at 9600 baud, 8N1

    Master->>Bus: Send Start Bit (LOW)
    Master->>Bus: Transmit byte "A" (LSB first)
    Master->>Bus: Send Stop Bit (HIGH)
    Bus->>Slave: Deliver frame
    Slave-->>Master: Acknowledge / Reply byte "B"
    Master->>Master: Read response into buffer
```

## 4.4 I²C Master-Slave Handshake Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as RPi Master
    participant Bus as I2C Bus
    participant S as Slave Device (e.g., 0x48)

    M->>Bus: START condition (SDA falls while SCL is HIGH)
    M->>Bus: Send slave address 0x48 + Write bit (0)
    Bus->>S: Address received
    S-->>Bus: ACK (SDA pulled LOW by slave)
    M->>Bus: Send register address (e.g., 0x00)
    S-->>Bus: ACK
    M->>Bus: RESTART condition
    M->>Bus: Send slave address 0x48 + Read bit (1)
    S-->>Bus: ACK
    S->>Bus: Transmit data bytes
    M-->>S: ACK after each byte
    M->>Bus: STOP condition (SDA rises while SCL is HIGH)
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between UART, SPI, and I²C serial communication protocols.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | UART | SPI | I²C |
|---------|------|-----|-----|
| Synchronization | Asynchronous (no clock) | Synchronous | Synchronous |
| Wires | 2 (TX, RX) | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCLK, CS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| Devices Supported | Point-to-point only | Multiple (via CS) | Up to 127 (via addressing) |
| Speed | Up to ~1 Mbps | Very High (>10 Mbps) | Up to 3.4 Mbps (Hs mode) |
| Duplex | Full | Full | Half |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Typical Use | Debug console, GPS | Displays, SD cards | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM |

UART uses a pre-agreed baud rate with start/stop bits. SPI uses a dedicated chip select line per slave. I²C uses 7-bit addressing on a shared 2-wire bus.

---

### **Q2. List any three Python libraries used in Raspberry Pi IoT programming and state their primary use.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **RPi.GPIO** → Provides direct, low-level access to the Raspberry Pi's GPIO pins for digital input/output operations such as reading buttons or controlling LEDs.
2. **smbus2** → A pure-Python implementation used to communicate with devices connected via the **I²C bus** (e.g., temperature sensors, EEPROMs).
3. **pyserial** → Enables **UART serial communication** between the Raspberry Pi and external devices like Arduinos, GPS modules, or PCs over a COM/USB port.
4. *(Optional)* **gpiozero** → A high-level, beginner-friendly abstraction built on top of RPi.GPIO, simplifying common tasks like LED, button, and sensor interfacing.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 4 questions are typically framed as either *design a program*, *explain protocol*, or *build an IoT application* type. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of UART communication. Define baud rate and frame format. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Python program for the Raspberry Pi to read a push button connected to GPIO 17. When the button is pressed, an LED connected to GPIO 27 should toggle. Use the `RPi.GPIO` library and explain each line. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-A (a): UART Architecture**

**UART Block Diagram (ASCII representation):**

```
   TRANSMITTER UART                              RECEIVER UART
   +-------------------+                        +-------------------+
   |  TX Register      |---TX line (serial)---->|  RX Register      |
   +-------------------+                        +-------------------+
   | Baud Rate Gen     |                        | Baud Rate Gen     |
   +-------------------+                        +-------------------+
   | Parity Generator  |<-- SHARED BAUD RATE -->| Parity Checker    |
   +-------------------+                        +-------------------+
   | Shift Register    |                        | Shift Register    |
   +-------------------+                        +-------------------+
```

**Baud Rate Definition (2 Marks):** The *baud rate* is the number of **signal changes (symbols) per second** transmitted over the serial line, expressed in **bits per second (bps)**. For UART, baud rate = bit rate (since each symbol represents 1 bit). Both transmitter and receiver must operate at the **same baud rate** to correctly sample the incoming bit stream. Common baud rates: 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200.

**Frame Format (3 Marks):** A standard UART frame consists of:

$$ \text{Frame} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{Start Bit (0)}} + \underbrace{D}_{\text{Data Bits (5-9, typically 8)}} + \underbrace{P}_{\text{Parity (None/Even/Odd)}} + \underbrace{S}_{\text{Stop Bits (1 or 2)}} $$

Total bits in an 8N1 frame: $1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10$ bits.

**Time to Transmit 1 Byte at 9600 baud:** $t = \frac{10}{9600} = 1.042$ ms.

The least significant bit (LSB) is transmitted first. The start bit synchronizes the receiver, and stop bits provide inter-frame gap.

### **Model Solution for Q-A (b): Button-Controlled LED**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

BUTTON_PIN = 17
LED_PIN    = 27
LED_STATE  = False  # Tracks current LED state

# Use BCM pin numbering
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)

# Configure button as input with internal pull-up
GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

# Configure LED as output, initially OFF
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT, initial=GPIO.LOW)

def toggle_led(channel):
    """Callback function - toggles LED when button is pressed"""
    global LED_STATE
    LED_STATE = not LED_STATE
    GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH if LED_STATE else GPIO.LOW)
    print(f"LED is now {'ON' if LED_STATE else 'OFF'}")

# Add event detection for falling edge (button press)
GPIO.add_event_detect(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.FALLING,
                      callback=toggle_led, bouncetime=300)

try:
    print("Press the button to toggle LED. Press Ctrl+C to exit.")
    while True:
        time.sleep(0.1)   # Keep main thread alive

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\nCleaning up...")
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (2 Marks):**

1. `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` → Enables internal pull-up resistor. Without this, the pin would *float* and produce random readings.
2. `initial=GPIO.LOW` → Sets the LED to OFF state at the start of the program.
3. `GPIO.add_event_detect(...)` → Registers an *interrupt callback* on the falling edge of the button pin. This is **non-blocking** — the CPU doesn't waste cycles polling.
4. `bouncetime=300` → Software debounce. Mechanical buttons "bounce" for a few milliseconds; this prevents the callback from firing multiple times per press.
5. `GPIO.cleanup()` → **Mandatory** to reset pins to a safe state and release the event detection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the I²C protocol in detail. List the start and stop conditions, and describe the addressing mechanism. How does the master select a particular slave? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to interface an LM75 temperature sensor (I²C address 0x48) with a Raspberry Pi using the `smbus2` library. Display the temperature every second. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-B (a): I²C Protocol Deep Dive**

**I²C Bus Lines (1 Mark):**
- **SDA (Serial Data Line)** — Bidirectional data line.
- **SCL (Serial Clock Line)** — Clock generated by the master.

Both lines are **open-drain**, requiring **pull-up resistors** (typically 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ to 3.3 V).

**Start and Stop Conditions (2 Marks):**
- **START (S):** SDA transitions from **HIGH to LOW** *while SCL is HIGH*.
- **STOP (P):** SDA transitions from **LOW to HIGH** *while SCL is HIGH*.
- These conditions are always generated by the **master** and frame every transaction.

**Addressing Mechanism (2 Marks):**
- I²C uses **7-bit addressing** (10-bit addressing is also possible but rare).
- The first byte after START contains the 7-bit slave address plus a 1-bit **R/W̄** flag.
- Example: To write to address 0x48, master sends `0b01001000` (R/W̄ = 0). To read, it sends `0b01001001` (R/W̄ = 1).
- Maximum slave devices: $2^7 = 128$ addresses, 127 usable (address 0 is the *General Call* address).

**Master Selection of a Slave (2 Marks):**
1. Master generates **START** condition.
2. Master transmits the **7-bit slave address + R/W̄ bit** (8 bits total) on SDA, clocked by SCL.
3. All slaves on the bus compare the address.
4. The matching slave pulls SDA **LOW** during the 9th clock pulse to send an **ACK (Acknowledgement)**.
5. All other slaves ignore the rest of the transaction.
6. Data transfer begins.
7. Master generates **STOP** to release the bus.

### **Model Solution for Q-B (b): LM75 Python Program**

```python
import smbus2
import time

# Initialize I2C bus (bus 1 is default on modern Raspberry Pi)
bus = smbus2.SMBus(1)

# LM75 sensor constants
LM75_ADDRESS = 0x48       # 7-bit I2C address
TEMP_REGISTER = 0x00      # Temperature data register

def read_lm75_temperature():
    """
    Reads temperature from LM75 sensor.
    LM75 returns a 9-bit two's complement value.
    Each LSB = 0.5 °C
    """
    # Read 2 bytes from the temperature register
    raw = bus.read_i2c_block_data(LM75_ADDRESS, TEMP_REGISTER, 2)

    # High byte: D8 to D1 of temperature
    # Low byte: D0 (in bit 7) + unused bits
    temp_msb = raw[0]
    temp_lsb = raw[1]

    # Combine to 11-bit raw value, then take top 9 bits
    raw_temp = (temp_msb << 3) | (temp_lsb >> 5)

    # Convert from two's complement (9-bit signed)
    if raw_temp & 0x100:          # If bit 8 is set, it's negative
        raw_temp -= 512

    # Scale to °C (0.5 °C per LSB)
    temperature_c = raw_temp * 0.5
    return temperature_c

def main():
    print("LM75 Temperature Monitor (Press Ctrl+C to exit)")
    print("-" * 45)
    try:
        while True:
            temp = read_lm75_temperature()
            print(f"Temperature: {temp:+.2f} °C  ({temp * 9/5 + 32:+.2f} °F)")
            time.sleep(1)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("\nExiting program...")
    except OSError as e:
        print(f"I2C Error: {e}. Check wiring and I2C address.")
    finally:
        bus.close()
        print("I2C bus closed.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Explanation of Key Logic (2 Marks):**
- `bus.read_i2c_block_data(addr, reg, n)` reads `n` bytes starting from register `reg`.
- The two's complement conversion handles negative temperatures (below 0 °C), which is essential for **biomedical cold-storage monitoring**.
- The `if __name__ == "__main__":` idiom ensures `main()` runs only when the script is executed directly (good Python practice).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** in any GPIO program → loses 1 mark. Always mention cleanup to prevent pin damage.
> 2. **Confusing BCM and BOARD numbering** in pin declarations → 1 mark deduction. Always explicitly state `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` or `GPIO.BOARD`.
> 3. **Forgetting pull-up/pull-down resistors** on input pins → examiners often penalize 1 mark because floating inputs cause unreliable readings.
> 4. **Mixing up 7-bit vs 8-bit I²C addressing** — the 7-bit address in code (e.g., `0x48`) is the *actual* address, but on the wire it becomes `0x48 << 1 = 0x90` (write) / `0x91` (read). State this clearly.
> 5. **No error handling** (no `try/except`) in I²C programs → wire failures cause bus hang; use `try/except OSError` and `bus.close()`.
> 6. **Baud rate assumption without declaration** in UART programs → always declare the baud rate explicitly in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Raspberry Pi GPIO voltage is 3.3 V** — never connect a 5 V signal directly to a GPIO pin or you will destroy the SoC.
- [x] **UART** is **asynchronous**, full-duplex, point-to-point. Pins: **GPIO 14 (TX)**, **GPIO 15 (RX)**.
- [x] **I²C** is **synchronous**, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave (up to 127 devices). Pins: **GPIO 2 (SDA)**, **GPIO 3 (SCL)**.
- [x] **SPI** is **synchronous**, full-duplex, fastest of the three. Pins: **GPIO 9 (MISO)**, **GPIO 10 (MOSI)**, **GPIO 11 (SCLK)**, **GPIO 8 (CE0)**.
- [x] **UART Frame** = 1 Start bit + 8 Data bits (LSB first) + Parity (optional) + 1 Stop bit.
- [x] **I²C START** = SDA falls while SCL is HIGH. **I²C STOP** = SDA rises while SCL is HIGH.
- [x] **I²C addressing** is **7 bits** → 128 possible addresses, **127 usable** (address 0 is General Call).
- [x] **Key Python libraries:** `RPi.GPIO` (low-level GPIO), `gpiozero` (high-level), `smbus2` (I²C), `pyserial` (UART), `spidev` (SPI).
- [x] **Pin numbering modes:** `GPIO.BCM` (chip pin numbers) and `GPIO.BOARD` (physical pin positions).
- [x] **Pull-up resistors** are critical for floating inputs — use `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` or `PUD_DOWN`.
- [x] **Bounce time** in `add_event_detect()` is essential to prevent false triggers from mechanical switch contact bounce.
- [x] **GPIO.cleanup()** must be called in the `finally` block to safely release pins.
- [x] **SPI** requires one **chip select (CS)** line per slave device — that's why it uses more wires.
- [x] **UART baud rates** must match on both communicating devices — mismatch is the #1 cause of *garbled* serial data.
- [x] For **biomedical IoT applications** (e.g., patient temperature monitoring), I²C is preferred for sensors due to low noise and simple wiring.
- [x] The `if __name__ == "__main__":` idiom in Python ensures modules can be imported without running side effects — a best-practice marker examiners look for.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlling Hardware - Controlling AC Power Devices with Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controlling Hardware - Controlling AC Power Devices with Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Electromechanical Relay)**
> An **Electromechanical Relay (EMR)** is a galvanically isolated, electrically operated switch that uses an electromagnet (a current-carrying coil) to mechanically control one or more sets of contacts. In IoT systems, relays act as the **power interface** that allows a low-power, low-voltage microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi GPIO at **3.3 V / 5 V**, sourcing **< 40 mA**) to switch high-power AC loads (**230 V, 50 Hz** in India, drawing **up to 10 A or more**) safely.

### 1.1 The Core Problem This Topic Solves

A microcontroller I/O pin is fundamentally **incompatible** with mains-voltage AC loads for three reasons:

| Limitation of MCU Pin | Requirement of AC Load |
|---|---|
| Logic Level: **3.3 V DC** (ESP32) or **5 V DC** (Arduino Uno) | Mains: **230 V AC @ 50 Hz** |
| Current Sink/Source: **~10 – 40 mA** | Current: **0.5 A – 10 A+** (fans, bulbs, heaters) |
| Direct DC coupling to board | Requires **galvanic isolation** for user safety |

If a student ever connects an MCU pin directly to a wall socket, the board **will be destroyed instantly** and there is a serious risk of **electrocution / fire**. The relay is the universal, low-cost solution to this exact problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Finger" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: Think of the relay as a "robot finger".**
> - The **microcontroller** is your **brain** — it can think and send tiny electrical signals, but it is too weak to flip a heavy wall switch.
> - The **transistor driver + coil** is the **muscle** — it amplifies the weak brain signal into a strong magnetic force.
> - The **relay contacts** are the **robot's finger** — they physically reach over and click the heavy wall switch (the AC circuit) on or off.
> - The **flyback diode** is the **knee pad** — it absorbs the magnetic "kickback" when the muscle suddenly relaxes, preventing damage.
> - The **optocoupler** (when used) is a **pair of walkie-talkies in separate rooms** — the brain and the finger never share the same electrical air; they only talk using light.

This means the microcontroller side and the AC side **never electrically touch** — they are linked only by a magnetic field (in EMR) or a light beam (in SSR / optocoupler). This property is called **galvanic isolation**, and it is the single most important reason relays are mandatory in biomedical and home-automation IoT hardware.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Anatomy of a Relay

A typical **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** signal relay — the one most commonly used in KTU lab kits (e.g., **5 V SRD-05VDC-SL-C**) — has three functional blocks:

1. **Coil (Input / Control Side):** Wound copper wire on an iron core. When energized, it becomes an electromagnet.
2. **Armature (Mechanical Lever):** A ferrous metal strip pulled by the magnetic field.
3. **Contacts (Output / Load Side):** Three terminals — **COM (Common)**, **NO (Normally Open)**, **NC (Normally Closed)**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — De-energized state (MCU output LOW):**
> Coil carries **0 mA**. Spring keeps the armature in its rest position. **COM ↔ NC** is closed; **COM ↔ NO** is open. The AC load is OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Energized state (MCU output HIGH):**
> Current flows through coil. The electromagnet attracts the armature. **COM ↔ NO** closes; **COM ↔ NC** opens. The AC load is ON.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Transition (coil switch-off):**
> The magnetic flux collapses. By **Lenz's law**, the inductor generates a large reverse EMF spike (the "back-EMF" or "flyback voltage"). Without protection, this spike can reach **hundreds of volts** and destroy the driving transistor. A **flyback diode** (1N4007 or 1N5819) is placed in reverse-bias across the coil to clamp this voltage to **≈ -0.7 V**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-grade. Memorize units, boundary conditions, and the standard relay coil assumption used in KTU problems: a 5 V coil drawing 70 mA (R_coil ≈ 71.4 Ω).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coil resistance | $R_{coil} = V_{coil} / I_{coil}$ | Ω | For 5 V / 70 mA relay → **71.4 Ω** |
| 2 | Coil current drawn | $I_{coil} = V_{coil} / R_{coil}$ | A | Direct from Ohm's law on the coil loop |
| 3 | Required collector current | $I_C = I_{coil}$ | A | BJT must sink the entire coil current |
| 4 | Minimum base current | $I_{B(min)} = I_C / \beta$ | A | $\beta$ = DC current gain (h_FE), usually **100 – 400** |
| 5 | **Safe** base current (with margin) | $I_{B(safe)} = k \cdot I_C / \beta$ | A | $k \approx 2$ to $3$ to force **saturation** |
| 6 | Base resistor value | $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE}) / I_{B(safe)}$ | Ω | $V_{OH}$ = MCU high-level output (3.3 V or 5 V); $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V |
| 7 | Flyback (back-EMF) voltage | $V_L = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Magnitude can exceed **100 V** without diode |
| 8 | Power dissipated in BJT (saturated) | $P_{BJT} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$ | W | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V → very small |
| 9 | Power dissipated in coil | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}$ | W | For 5 V / 70 mA → **350 mW** |
| 10 | AC load power (resistive) | $P_{load} = V_{AC} \cdot I_{AC} \cdot \cos\phi$ | W | $\cos\phi = 1$ for heater, $\approx 0.6$ for fan |
| 11 | Snubber RC time constant | $\tau = R_s \cdot C_s$ | s | $R_s C_s$ chosen ≈ 1/10 of AC half-period (**1 ms** for 50 Hz) |
| 12 | DC current gain | $\beta = I_C / I_B$ | dimensionless | Use datasheet min value, not typical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Equations:**
> - **Equations 3 – 6** let a designer pick the correct base resistor — the #1 numerical question KTU asks.
> - **Equation 7** justifies why a flyback diode is **non-negotiable** in the schematic.
> - **Equation 10** lets a biomedical engineer size a relay for an instrument, e.g., a **220 V, 1 A** autoclave heater in a sterilization unit.

### 2.4 Relay Variants You Must Know for KTU Viva

| Type | Full Form | Switching Element | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EMR** | Electromechanical Relay | Moving metal contacts | Cheaper, handles high surge, audible "click" |
| **SSR** | Solid-State Relay | Triac / back-to-back thyristors | Silent, no moving parts, **zero-crossing** switching |
| **Reed Relay** | Reed Switch Relay | Hermetically sealed reed | Fast, used in low-power signal switching |
| **Latching Relay** | Bi-stable Relay | Mechanical with permanent magnet | Stays in last state without continuous power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid-State Relay (SSR) Insight:** SSRs use an **opto-isolator + triac** internally. They have **no contacts to wear out**, switch in **microseconds**, and offer built-in **zero-crossing detection** (so they switch only when the AC sine wave crosses 0 V — this drastically reduces EMI in biomedical instruments like ECG recorders and infant warmers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Designing a Base Resistor for a 5 V Relay

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU problem:** A **5 V, 70 mA** relay is driven by an Arduino Uno digital pin (V_OH = **5 V**) through a BC547 BJT (β_min = **110**, V_BE = **0.7 V**, V_CE(sat) = **0.2 V**). Find the base resistor **R_B** for reliable saturation, assuming a safety factor **k = 2**.

**Step 1 — Identify the load on the collector.**
The BJT must sink the entire relay coil current when switched ON. Therefore:

$$I_C = I_{coil} = 70 \text{ mA}$$

This is the **why** behind Equation 3: in saturation, the collector current is set by the external load (the coil), not by the transistor.

**Step 2 — Compute the minimum theoretical base current.**
Using the worst-case (minimum) β from the datasheet so the design works for *every* transistor of that batch:

$$I_{B(min)} = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta_{min}} = \dfrac{70 \text{ mA}}{110} = 0.6364 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the saturation safety factor.**
To guarantee the BJT is **fully saturated** (V_CE ≈ 0.2 V, not in the active region), we over-drive the base by factor **k = 2**:

$$I_{B(safe)} = k \cdot \dfrac{I_C}{\beta_{min}} = 2 \times 0.6364 \text{ mA} = 1.2727 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KVL on the base–emitter loop.**
The base–emitter junction is forward-biased at V_BE ≈ 0.7 V. The remaining voltage drops across R_B:

$$V_{R_B} = V_{OH} - V_{BE} = 5.0 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V} = 4.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute R_B from Ohm's law.**

$$R_B = \dfrac{V_{R_B}}{I_{B(safe)}} = \dfrac{4.3 \text{ V}}{1.2727 \text{ mA}} = 3.379 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Round down to the nearest standard E12 value.**
The next-lowest E12 value is **3.3 kΩ**. (Choosing a larger value would reduce I_B and could push the BJT out of saturation, causing it to dissipate large power as heat — a classic KTU exam trap.)

$$\boxed{R_B \approx 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega \quad (\text{standard E12 value})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check for verification:** With R_B = 3.3 kΩ, the actual base current becomes 4.3 V / 3.3 kΩ = 1.303 mA. Multiplied by β = 110, the maximum possible collector current is 143 mA — which is **well above** the required 70 mA. Saturation is guaranteed. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Example — Flyback Voltage Magnitude

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU concept question:** A relay coil has **L = 100 mH** and carries **70 mA**. The transistor switches off in **1 μs**. Estimate the peak flyback voltage.

**Step 1 — Write Lenz's law equation.**

$$V_L = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the rate of current change.**

$$\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{70 \text{ mA}}{1 \text{ μs}} = \dfrac{70 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the induced voltage magnitude.**

$$V_L = -0.1 \text{ H} \times 7.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ A/s} = -7000 \text{ V}$$

This is the **theoretical** peak. In practice, the coil's own resistance and parasitic capacitance clamp it to **100 – 300 V** — still more than enough to destroy a 40 V BJT like the BC547. The **flyback diode** clamps this to **≈ 0.7 V**, protecting the transistor.

### 3.3 Power Budget of the Driver Stage

| Component | Equation | Numerical Value (5 V / 70 mA relay) |
|---|---|---|
| Coil power | $P_{coil} = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.07 \text{ A}$ | **350 mW** |
| BJT dissipation (saturated) | $P_{BJT} = 0.2 \text{ V} \times 0.07 \text{ A}$ | **14 mW** |
| BJT dissipation (active, fault) | $P_{BJT} = (5 - 0.7) \text{ V} \times 0.07 \text{ A}$ | **301 mW** (would burn the BC547 — proves why **saturation is mandatory**) |
| Base resistor power | $P_{R_B} = I_B^2 \cdot R_B$ | **5.6 mW** (a ¼ W resistor is overkill — ⅛ W is fine) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's trap:** Students often forget that an MCU pin in HIGH state also sources current through R_B into the BJT. The total current drawn from the MCU pin is **I_B + (any LED current)**. With many relays on one board, the MCU's **per-pin and per-port** limits can be exceeded — a frequent KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The following block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of an MCU-driven AC relay control system — the typical level of abstraction expected in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 answers.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — MCU → Relay → AC Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcu[Microcontroller GPIO] --> isol[Isolation Stage Optocoupler Optional]
    isol --> drv[Driver Stage BJT or MOSFET]
    drv --> coil[Relay Coil 5V DC]
    coil --> flyback[Flyback Diode 1N4007]
    flyback -.-> coil
    coil --> contacts[SPDT Contacts COM NO NC]
    contacts --> acdev[AC Load 230V 50Hz]
    acdev --> acm[AC Mains Supply]
    contacts --> snub[Snubber RC Network]
    snub -.-> contacts
    mcu -. GPIO LOW .-> drv
    drv -. Coil OFF .-> contacts
    contacts -. NO open .-> acdev
```

### 4.2 Detailed Hardware Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlSide[Low Voltage DC Side 5V]
        p1[Arduino GPIO Pin D7]
        p2[Base Resistor R_B 3.3k]
        p3[BC547 BJT Switch]
        p4[Flyback Diode 1N4007]
        p5[Relay Coil 70mA]
    end
    subgraph PowerSide[High Voltage AC Side 230V]
        q1[COM Terminal]
        q2[NO Terminal]
        q3[NC Terminal]
        q4[AC Load Bulb or Fan]
        q5[AC Mains Live and Neutral]
    end
    p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p5
    p4 -. Reverse across coil .-> p5
    p3 --> pgnd[Ground]
    p5 --> pvcc[VCC 5V]
    q1 --- q2
    q1 --- q3
    q2 --> q4 --> q5
    q3 --> q4alt[Alternative Load Path]
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant DRV as Driver BJT
    participant COIL as Relay Coil
    participant CONT as Relay Contacts
    participant LOAD as AC Load
    MCU->>DRV: Set GPIO HIGH 5V
    DRV->>COIL: Sink 70mA from coil
    COL->>COIL: Magnetic field builds
    CONT->>CONT: Armature pulled COM connects to NO
    LOAD->>LOAD: AC mains applied bulb glows
    MCU->>DRV: Set GPIO LOW 0V
    DRV->>COIL: Open circuit
    COIL->>COIL: Flux collapses induces 100V spike
    CONT->>CONT: Flyback diode clamps spike at 0.7V
    CONT->>CONT: Spring returns COM to NC
    LOAD->>LOAD: AC disconnected bulb OFF
```

### 4.4 Relay Internal States Comparison

| State | Coil | COM–NC | COM–NO | AC Load |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **De-energized (default)** | OFF | **Closed** | Open | OFF (if wired through NO) |
| **Energized** | ON | Open | **Closed** | **ON** |
| **Switching OFF** | Coil current falling | Transitioning | Transitioning | Flyback spike generated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical tip for KTU lab:** Always wire the **AC load between COM and NO** (not NC). This way, if the relay fails open or the MCU crashes, the load defaults to **OFF** — the **fail-safe** design philosophy used in biomedical life-support equipment.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 3 Marks]**
> **List any three reasons why a microcontroller GPIO pin cannot directly switch a 230 V AC load. Why is a relay used as an interface?**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Voltage incompatibility:** MCU pins output **3.3 V or 5 V DC**, while AC loads operate at **230 V AC**. Connecting them directly will short the AC mains into the MCU and destroy it.
2. **Current incompatibility:** MCU pins source/sink only **10 – 40 mA**, while AC loads (fans, heaters, bulbs) require **0.5 A to 10 A+**.
3. **Galvanic isolation requirement:** Direct coupling creates a **fatal electric-shock hazard** to the user and the instrument. A relay provides **galvanic isolation** because the input coil and output contacts share no electrical conductor.
4. The relay uses a low-power signal from the MCU to control a high-power AC circuit through magnetic coupling — making it the standard, safe, low-cost interface.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
> **What is the function of the flyback diode connected across a relay coil? What would happen if it were omitted?**

**Model Answer:**
When the relay coil is de-energized, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a large reverse EMF (back-EMF) of up to **100 – 300 V** (from $V_L = -L \, di/dt$). This spike can puncture the driving transistor's collector–emitter junction, permanently damaging it. The **flyback diode** (e.g., 1N4007) is placed in **reverse bias** across the coil so that during normal operation it is OFF. When the spike occurs, the diode becomes forward-biased and **clamps the voltage to ≈ 0.7 V**, providing a safe circulating path for the inductor current. **If omitted, the transistor will be destroyed within a few switching cycles.**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **forget to apply the safety factor k** when computing R_B and end up with a BJT operating in the active region, which overheats and fails. Always state "$k = 2$ to $3$" explicitly.
> - Students **confuse COM and NO terminals** in the wiring diagram, leading to the load being permanently ON. Clearly label **COM, NO, NC** on the schematic.
> - Students **omit the snubber** for inductive AC loads (fans, solenoids). For **resistive** loads (bulbs, heaters) a snubber is optional; for **inductive** loads it is mandatory to prevent contact arcing.
> - **Never compute coil power using the AC side.** Coil current is determined by the **DC side** (5 V / 70 mA), not by the 230 V load.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain how an Arduino Uno controls a **230 V, 60 W incandescent lamp** through a 5 V SPDT relay module. Label the **driver transistor, base resistor, flyback diode, optocoupler (if used), and the AC load path.** (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Design the **base resistor** for a BC547 BJT driving a 5 V, 70 mA relay from a 5 V Arduino pin. Assume β_min = 110, V_BE = 0.7 V, and use a safety factor k = 2. Verify that the BJT enters saturation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block diagram** (reproduce the diagram in Section 4.1, showing the path Arduino → R_B → BC547 base, collector to coil, emitter to GND, flyback diode reverse across coil, and 230 V lamp between COM and NO, returning to AC neutral). Each block labelled in the answer sheet.

**Explanation points (1 mark each):**

1. The Arduino digital pin D7 is configured as OUTPUT and set HIGH (5 V) to energize the relay. (1 M)
2. The base current I_B flows through R_B into the BC547 base, turning it ON (saturation). (1 M)
3. With the BJT saturated, its collector–emitter voltage drops to V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V, so nearly the full 5 V appears across the relay coil, drawing ≈ 70 mA. (1 M)
4. The coil's magnetic field pulls the armature, closing the COM–NO contact. (1 M)
5. AC mains (230 V, 50 Hz) is now connected to the lamp; current flows Live → COM → NO → Lamp → Neutral, and the lamp glows. (1 M)
6. The flyback diode (1N4007) is reverse-biased during normal operation and clamps the back-EMF when the BJT switches off. (1 M)
7. For added safety in biomedical applications, an **optocoupler (e.g., PC817)** is inserted between the Arduino and the BJT, so the MCU and the relay side share **no common ground** — true galvanic isolation. (1 M)

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

Step 1 — Required collector current: $I_C = I_{coil} = 70$ mA. (1 M)
Step 2 — Minimum base current: $I_{B(min)} = 70/110 = 0.636$ mA. (1 M)
Step 3 — Safe base current: $I_{B(safe)} = 2 \times 0.636 = 1.273$ mA. (1 M)
Step 4 — Voltage across R_B: $V_{R_B} = 5.0 - 0.7 = 4.3$ V. (1 M)
Step 5 — R_B calculated: $R_B = 4.3 \text{ V} / 1.273 \text{ mA} = 3.379$ kΩ. (1 M)
Step 6 — Choose standard value: **R_B = 3.3 kΩ (E12 series).** (1 M)
Step 7 — Verification: With 3.3 kΩ, I_B = 1.30 mA, giving maximum possible I_C = 1.30 mA × 110 = 143 mA > 70 mA. Therefore the BJT is **deeply in saturation** (forced β = 70/1.30 = 54, much less than β_min = 110). ✓ (1 M)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain the **internal construction and working** of an electromechanical relay (EMR). Compare EMR with **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** on the basis of **switching speed, lifetime, isolation, EMI, and load type.** (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A relay coil has **L = 200 mH** and carries **100 mA**. The driving transistor switches OFF in **2 μs**. Calculate the **peak flyback voltage**. Explain how a flyback diode protects the circuit, and draw the equivalent circuit during the de-energization transient. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Internal construction (3 Marks):** Draw and label — **coil** (input terminals), **iron core**, **armature**, **spring (return mechanism)**, and the three contact terminals **COM, NO, NC**. Describe the electromagnetic attraction when coil is energized.

**Comparison table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | EMR | SSR |
|---|---|---|
| Switching element | Moving metal contacts | Triac / back-to-back SCR |
| Switching speed | **5 – 15 ms** | **< 1 ms** (often µs) |
| Lifetime | **10⁵ – 10⁶ operations** (contact wear) | **> 10⁹ operations** (no moving parts) |
| Galvanic isolation | Yes (magnetic) | Yes (optical) |
| EMI generated | Higher (contact bounce) | Lower (zero-crossing switch) |
| Audible noise | Audible "click" | Silent |
| Suitable load | AC and DC, high surge | Mostly AC, low surge |
| On-state resistance | ≈ 50 mΩ (very low) | ≈ 1 – 2 Ω (higher losses) |

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

Step 1 — Apply Lenz's law: $V_L = -L \, di/dt$. (1 M)
Step 2 — Compute di/dt: $(100 \times 10^{-3}) / (2 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.0 \times 10^{4}$ A/s. (1 M)
Step 3 — Compute peak: $V_L = -0.2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{4} = -10{,}000$ V = **−10 kV** (theoretical). (1 M)
Step 4 — State that in practice this is clamped to **100 – 400 V** by parasitic capacitance and coil resistance. (1 M)
Step 5 — Explain the diode: 1N4007 is reverse-biased across the coil. During switch-off, the induced voltage forward-biases the diode, which clamps the voltage to **V_D ≈ 0.7 V** and provides a freewheeling path for the decaying inductor current. (2 M)
Step 6 — Draw the equivalent circuit: BJT open, diode conducting, current circulating in the loop Coil → Diode → back to Coil. (1 M)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relay** = electrically controlled switch providing **galvanic isolation** between low-voltage DC control and high-voltage AC load.
- **Three terminals** of SPDT: **COM, NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed)**. Load goes on **COM–NO** for fail-safe behaviour.
- A microcontroller **cannot** directly switch 230 V AC because of **voltage, current, and isolation** constraints.
- **Driver stage is mandatory:** BJT (BC547, 2N2222) or MOSFET (IRFZ44, IRLZ44) sinks the coil current.
- **Base resistor formula (most-asked KTU equation):** $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE}) / (k \cdot I_C / \beta_{min})$.
- **Always** use a **flyback diode (1N4007 / 1N5819)** reverse-biased across the relay coil to protect the driver.
- **Flyback voltage** $V_L = -L \, di/dt$ can reach **hundreds of volts** in a fraction of a microsecond.
- **Snubber RC network** across the AC contacts is required for **inductive loads** (fans, solenoids, transformers) to suppress arcing.
- **Solid-State Relays (SSR)** use a **triac + optocoupler**, have **no moving parts**, support **zero-crossing switching**, and are preferred in **biomedical** and **EMI-sensitive** applications.
- **EMR advantages:** cheaper, handles high inrush current, works for both AC and DC.
- **SSR advantages:** silent, longer life, faster, no contact bounce.
- **Biomedical relevance:** relays switch mains-powered instruments like **infant warmers, sterilizers, infusion pumps, and X-ray view boxes** under microcontroller supervision while keeping the patient isolated from mains.
- **Safety mantra for KTU viva:** "**Isolation, Snubber, Flyback, and Fail-Safe Wiring**" — these are the four words a panel examiner always wants to hear.
- Standard relay assumptions in KTU problems: **5 V coil, 70 mA coil current, β_min = 100 – 200, V_BE = 0.7 V, V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V, k = 2**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlling Servo Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controlling Servo Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Servo Motor** (or **Servomechanism**) is a closed-loop, rotary or linear actuator that uses position feedback to precisely control its angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration. In the context of IoT and embedded systems, the most commonly used variant is the **RC (Radio Control) Hobby Servo Motor**, which is a packaged DC servo motor integrated with a gearbox, a potentiometer (for position feedback), and an embedded control circuit, capable of precise angular positioning typically between **0° and 180°**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A servo motor is an electromechanical device that translates electrical pulse-width modulated (PWM) signals into proportional rotary motion. The position is held against any opposing torque by an internal closed-loop feedback mechanism.

### 1.2 Key Technical Characteristics (Standard Hobby Servo – SG90/MG996R)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | **4.8V – 6.0V DC** |
| Operating Current (Idle) | ~10 mA |
| Operating Current (Stalled) | ~250–650 mA |
| PWM Frequency | **50 Hz (Period = 20 ms)** |
| Pulse Width Range | **1.0 ms (0°) – 2.0 ms (180°)** |
| Neutral Pulse Width | **1.5 ms (90°)** |
| Rotational Range | 0° – 180° |
| Stall Torque (SG90) | ~1.8 kg·cm at 4.8V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCBRT402):** Servo motors are extensively used in **biomedical applications** such as **prosthetic limbs, rehabilitation robots, surgical assistance arms, and insulin pump dispensers** due to their precise, repeatable, and safe angular control.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are steering a car. You turn the steering wheel a little, the wheels turn a little; you turn it a lot, the wheels turn a lot. The steering wheel is **YOU** (the controller), and the wheels are the **servo motor**. But there is a critical difference — even if a strong gust of wind pushes the car sideways, the wheels *resist* and *return* to the angle you set. This is **closed-loop control**.

**Simple Analogy — The Human Arm:**
- Your **brain** = Microcontroller (Arduino/NodeMCU)
- The **electrical signal through nerves** = PWM signal wire
- Your **bicep muscle** = DC motor inside the servo
- Your **eye (visual feedback)** = Potentiometer inside the servo
- Your **joint angle** = Output shaft angle

> [!TIP]
> **Why use a servo in IoT/Biomedical projects?** Because you don't just want something to "spin" (that's a regular DC motor); you want it to **go to a specific, exact angle and stay there**, even against external force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Hobby Servo Motor

A hobby servo motor is a marvel of integrated mechatronics. Inside its compact casing, three sub-systems work in harmony:

1. **DC Motor (Actuator):** Provides raw rotational energy. Cannot be driven directly by a microcontroller as it draws high current.
2. **Gearbox (Torque Multiplier):** A series of plastic or metal gears that reduce the high-speed, low-torque output of the DC motor to a low-speed, high-torque output suitable for mechanical loads.
3. **Control Circuit + Potentiometer (Feedback Loop):** This is the "brain" of the servo. It compares the incoming PWM command with the actual position reported by the potentiometer and drives the motor in the correct direction until the error is zero.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

The microcontroller does **not** send a varying voltage (like 1V, 2V, 3V). Instead, it sends a **fixed-amplitude, fixed-frequency digital pulse train**, where only the **width of the ON pulse (duty cycle)** varies.

**The Golden Rule of Servo Control:**

> The width of each HIGH pulse is directly proportional to the desired output shaft angle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Relationship | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| PWM Period ($T$) | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds (s) | For servos, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ |
| Standard Period | $T = 20\,\text{ms}$ | ms | Fixed for all hobby servos |
| Pulse Width ($t_p$) | $t_p = 1.0 + \dfrac{\theta}{180}$ | ms | Maps angle to pulse width |
| Neutral Pulse | $t_{p} = 1.5\,\text{ms}$ | ms | Corresponds to $\theta = 90°$ |
| Duty Cycle ($D$) | $D = \dfrac{t_p}{T} \times 100\%$ | % | For $0°$: $D = 5\%$; For $180°$: $D = 10\%$ |
| Torque ($\tau$) | $\tau = F \times r$ | N·m or kg·cm | Load-lifting capacity |
| Angular Speed ($\omega$) | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{t}$ | deg/s or rad/s | SG90: ~0.1 s / 60° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The PWM used for LED dimming varies **duty cycle** (0%–100%) at a fixed frequency. The PWM used for servos keeps a **fixed 50 Hz frequency** and varies the **pulse width in milliseconds** (1 ms–2 ms). This is why microcontrollers often have a dedicated `Servo.h` library — it handles this delicate timing automatically.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Field:** Robotic prosthetic hands (e.g., Open Bionics Hero Arm) use micro-servos for finger articulation. Surgical robots like the **Da Vinci System** use high-precision servos for instrument manipulation.
- **IoT/Automation:** Smart door locks, automatic pet feeders, solar panel tracking systems, and pan-tilt CCTV cameras.
- **Rehabilitation:** Exoskeleton suits for paralyzed patients use servo actuators to replicate human joint motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping PWM Pulse Width to Servo Angle

The most fundamental calculation in servo motor control is converting a desired angle (in degrees) into the required PWM pulse width (in microseconds). Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

**Given Boundary Conditions (from servo datasheet):**
- At $\theta = 0°$, pulse width $t_p = 1.0\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\mu\text{s}$
- At $\theta = 180°$, pulse width $t_p = 2.0\,\text{ms} = 2000\,\mu\text{s}$
- The relationship is **linear** (first-order).

**Step 1: Establish the linear equation of the form $t_p = m \cdot \theta + c$**

We have two points: $(0°, 1000\,\mu\text{s})$ and $(180°, 2000\,\mu\text{s})$.

**Step 2: Calculate the slope ($m$)**

$$
m = \frac{\Delta t_p}{\Delta \theta} = \frac{2000 - 1000}{180 - 0} = \frac{1000}{180}
$$

$$
m = \frac{1000}{180} \approx 5.556\,\mu\text{s/deg}
$$

**Step 3: Determine the y-intercept ($c$)**

Substituting the first point $(\theta = 0°,\ t_p = 1000\,\mu\text{s})$:

$$
1000 = \left(\frac{1000}{180}\right)(0) + c
$$

$$
c = 1000\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Write the governing equation**

$$
t_p(\mu\text{s}) = \left(\frac{1000}{180}\right) \cdot \theta + 1000
$$

**Step 5: Simplify (Optional — common KTU form)**

$$
t_p(\mu\text{s}) = \frac{1000 \cdot (\theta + 180)}{180} = \frac{50}{9} \cdot (\theta + 180)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverted form (Angle from Pulse Width):**  
> $\theta = \dfrac{9 \cdot (t_p - 1000)}{50}$ degrees.  
> This is useful when you measure a pulse on an oscilloscope and need to know the actual servo angle.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Evaluation (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A biomedical infusion pump uses a servo to press a syringe plunger. The clinician sets the angle to **135°**. Calculate the required PWM pulse width and the duty cycle. Assume standard 50 Hz operation.

**Step 1: Apply the governing equation**

$$
t_p = \left(\frac{1000}{180}\right) \cdot 135 + 1000
$$

**Step 2: Compute the multiplication**

$$
t_p = (5.5556) \cdot 135 + 1000
$$

$$
t_p = 750.0 + 1000 = 1750\,\mu\text{s} = 1.75\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the duty cycle**

$$
D = \frac{t_p}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{1.75}{20} \times 100\%
$$

$$
D = 8.75\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result**

A pulse width of 1.75 ms (D = 8.75%) at 50 Hz will rotate the servo to exactly 135° from its 0° reference. The servo will internally lock and hold this position.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table

| Desired Angle ($\theta$) | Pulse Width ($t_p$) | Duty Cycle ($D$) | Application Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 1.0 ms | 5.0% | Prosthetic finger fully closed |
| 45° | 1.25 ms | 6.25% | Robotic arm mid-position |
| 90° | 1.5 ms | 7.5% | Neutral / default / home position |
| 135° | 1.75 ms | 8.75% | Syringe plunger partially pressed |
| 180° | 2.0 ms | 10.0% | Prosthetic finger fully open |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Control Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the complete closed-loop control path, from the user's IoT command to the physical shaft rotation and back via the potentiometer feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Cloud / Mobile App] -->|User Command Angle| B[Microcontroller Arduino ESP32]
    B -->|PWM Signal 50Hz 1to2 ms| C[Servo Motor Control Board]
    C -->|H Bridge Driver| D[Internal DC Motor]
    D -->|High Speed Low Torque| E[Gearbox Reducer]
    E -->|Low Speed High Torque| F[Output Shaft Angle 0 to 180 deg]
    F -->|Mechanical Coupling| G[Mechanical Load Prosthetic or Robotic Arm]
    F -->|Shaft Rotation| H[Potentiometer Position Sensor]
    H -->|Analog Voltage Feedback| I[Error Comparator Circuit]
    C -->|PWM Command Reference| I
    I -->|Error Signal Corrected PWM| C
    G -->|External Disturbance Torque| F
    F -->|Self Corrects via Feedback| I

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style H fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style I fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```

### 4.2 Hardware Interfacing Diagram — Arduino to Servo Motor

The hobby servo has a **3-wire female connector**:

| Wire Color | Function | Connects To |
|---|---|---|
| **Orange / White** | PWM Signal | Arduino PWM pin (e.g., D9) |
| **Red** | VCC (4.8V–6V) | External 5V/6V supply (recommended) |
| **Brown / Black** | Ground (GND) | Common GND with Arduino |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MC[Microcontroller Arduino UNO] -->|Digital Pin D9 PWM| S1[Servo Motor Signal Orange]
    PWR[External 5V Power Supply] -->|VCC Red| S2[Servo Motor VCC Red]
    GND[Common Ground Rail] -->|GND Brown| S3[Servo Motor GND Brown]
    MC -->|GND Pin| GND
    PWR -->|GND| GND

    style MC fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style PWR fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style GND fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#424242
    style S1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style S2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style S3 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Wiring Note (KTU Practical Exam Favorite):**  
> Never power a high-torque servo (e.g., MG996R) directly from the Arduino 5V pin. Under load, it can draw **>500 mA**, which will **reset or damage** the Arduino. Always use a **separate regulated 5V/6V supply** with the grounds tied together.

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Servo Response to Pulse Width

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1.0 ms Pulse] --> R1[Servo Rotates to 0 deg]
    P2[1.5 ms Pulse] --> R2[Servo Rotates to 90 deg Neutral]
    P3[2.0 ms Pulse] --> R3[Servo Rotates to 180 deg]

    style P1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style P2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style P3 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
    style R1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style R2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style R3 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a DC motor and a Servo motor. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DC Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Control Type | Open-loop (no feedback) | Closed-loop (with feedback) |
| Output | Continuous rotation | Precise angular position (0°–180°) |
| Control Signal | Voltage level | PWM pulse width (1–2 ms) |
| Feedback | None | Internal potentiometer |
| Application | Fans, wheels, pumps | Robotic joints, prosthetics, valves |
| Holding Torque | None (free spin) | Yes — actively maintains position |

*(Any 3 valid points = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of the potentiometer inside a servo motor? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The potentiometer inside a servo motor acts as a **position sensor** in the closed-loop feedback system. It is mechanically coupled to the output shaft, so as the shaft rotates, the resistance of the potentiometer changes proportionally. This change is converted into an **analog voltage signal** that is fed to the internal control circuit. The control circuit compares this *actual* position voltage with the *desired* position voltage (derived from the incoming PWM signal). The difference (error) drives the motor in the correct direction until the error becomes zero, ensuring the shaft reaches and holds the commanded angle. *(3 marks — include the keywords: feedback, voltage comparison, error correction, closed loop)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024, Model Question]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Complete Internal Choice

**Explain the working principle of a hobby servo motor with a neat block diagram. Describe the PWM control mechanism and derive the relationship between pulse width and servo shaft angle. Also, discuss any two biomedical applications of servo motors.** *(14 Marks)*

#### **(a) Working Principle with Block Diagram — 7 Marks**

A hobby servo motor is a **closed-loop electromechanical actuator** that uses PWM signals for precise angular positioning. Its working can be explained in three stages:

1. **Signal Input Stage:** The microcontroller generates a 50 Hz PWM signal (period 20 ms) and sends it via the signal wire. The pulse width varies between 1.0 ms and 2.0 ms corresponding to 0° and 180° respectively.

2. **Comparison Stage:** Inside the servo, the control circuit decodes the pulse width and compares it with the feedback voltage from the potentiometer. The difference between the desired and actual position is called the **position error**.

3. **Drive Stage:** If an error exists, the H-bridge driver rotates the internal DC motor in the appropriate direction. The motor, through the gearbox, turns the output shaft and the potentiometer simultaneously. When the error reaches zero, the motor stops. The shaft is now at the commanded angle and is actively held there.

*(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 must be drawn — 7 marks: 3 for explanation, 2 for diagram, 2 for labeling & feedback path)*

#### **(b) PWM Control & Derivation + Biomedical Applications — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Given boundary conditions:
- $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow t_p = 1.0\,\text{ms} = 1000\,\mu\text{s}$
- $\theta = 180° \Rightarrow t_p = 2.0\,\text{ms} = 2000\,\mu\text{s}$

Slope:

$$
m = \frac{2000 - 1000}{180 - 0} = \frac{1000}{180} \approx 5.556\,\mu\text{s/deg}
$$

Final equation:

$$
t_p = \left(\frac{1000}{180}\right) \cdot \theta + 1000
$$

**Biomedical Applications (3 marks — any two):**

1. **Robotic Prosthetic Limbs:** Micro-servos actuate finger joints, allowing amputees to perform grasping tasks with precise grip strength control.
2. **Surgical Robots (e.g., Da Vinci System):** High-precision servos translate the surgeon's hand movements into tremor-free, scaled instrument motion inside the patient's body.
3. **Rehabilitation Exoskeletons:** Servos power the joints of wearable robots that assist paralyzed patients in walking or moving their limbs.
4. **Smart Infusion Pumps:** Servos precisely press syringe plungers for controlled drug delivery.
5. **Automated Defibrillator Pad Positioning:** Servos align the electrode pads with optimal contact points on the patient's chest.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how a servo motor is interfaced with an Arduino Uno. Write the algorithm to rotate the servo shaft from 0° to 180° and back, in steps of 10°, with a 500 ms delay. Also, state any two precautions to be taken while interfacing.** *(14 Marks)*

#### **(a) Circuit Interface & Hardware Connections — 7 Marks**

**Components Required:** Arduino Uno, SG90/MG996R Servo Motor, Breadboard, Jumper Wires, External 5V Supply (if using high-torque servo).

**Connections:**

| Servo Wire | Connects To | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Orange (Signal) | Arduino Digital Pin 9 | Receives PWM command |
| Red (VCC) | External 5V (+) | Power supply |
| Brown (GND) | Common GND rail | Common ground with Arduino |

*(Circuit diagram as shown in Section 4.2 must be drawn — 7 marks: 2 for component list, 3 for diagram, 2 for labeling VCC/GND/Signal)*

#### **(b) Algorithm & Precautions — 7 Marks**

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

```
1. START
2. Include <Servo.h> library
3. Declare Servo object: Servo myServo
4. Attach servo signal pin: myServo.attach(9)
5. FOR angle = 0 to 180 STEP 10 DO
       a. myServo.write(angle)
       b. DELAY 500 ms
   END FOR
6. FOR angle = 180 to 0 STEP -10 DO
       a. myServo.write(angle)
       b. DELAY 500 ms
   END FOR
7. STOP / REPEAT from step 5
```

**Equivalent Arduino Sketch (C/C++):**

```cpp
#include <Servo.h>
Servo myServo;

void setup() {
    myServo.attach(9);  // Signal pin D9
}

void loop() {
    for (int angle = 0; angle <= 180; angle += 10) {
        myServo.write(angle);
        delay(500);
    }
    for (int angle = 180; angle >= 0; angle -= 10) {
        myServo.write(angle);
        delay(500);
    }
}
```

**Precautions (3 marks — any two):**

1. **Do not power a high-torque servo directly from the Arduino 5V pin.** Use a separate external 5V/6V power supply to avoid damage to the Arduino voltage regulator.
2. **Always connect the grounds together.** The Arduino GND and the external supply GND must be tied to a common rail; otherwise, the PWM signal will be floating and the servo will jitter.
3. **Avoid forcing the shaft manually.** The internal gears (especially plastic ones in SG90) can strip if rotated beyond the mechanical limits by hand.
4. **Use a decoupling capacitor (100 µF) across VCC and GND** near the servo to absorb voltage spikes caused by the motor's inductive load.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **servo PWM** with **LED dimming PWM**. Remember — servo uses **fixed 50 Hz** with varying **pulse width (1–2 ms)**, NOT varying duty cycle from 0%–100%. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students write *"servos rotate 360°"* — this is **wrong** for standard hobby servos. They rotate only **0° to 180°**. Continuous-rotation servos exist but are a different variant. *(Loss: 1 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention the **feedback (potentiometer)** in the block diagram. Without feedback, the system becomes open-loop and is no longer a servo. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using `delay()` blocks while reading IoT sensor data — the servo library requires precise timing; long delays will cause missed PWM pulses and servo jitter.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Controlling Servo Motor**

- A **servo motor** is a **closed-loop** actuator; a regular DC motor is **open-loop**.
- Internal parts: **DC Motor + Gearbox + Potentiometer + Control Circuit**.
- Control signal: **PWM with fixed 50 Hz frequency** (Period = **20 ms**).
- Pulse width range: **1.0 ms (0°) to 2.0 ms (180°)**; Neutral = **1.5 ms (90°)**.
- Governing equation: $t_p = \left(\dfrac{1000}{180}\right) \cdot \theta + 1000\,\mu\text{s}$.
- Standard operating voltage: **4.8 V – 6.0 V DC**.
- Rotation range: **0° to 180°** (not 360°).
- **Always use an external power supply** for high-torque servos; never power directly from Arduino 5V.
- **Common ground is mandatory** between Arduino and external supply.
- Biomedical applications: **prosthetics, surgical robots, exoskeletons, infusion pumps**.
- Arduino library: `#include <Servo.h>` → `myServo.attach(pin)` → `myServo.write(angle)`.
- Key pitfall: Do **not** confuse **servo PWM (pulse width modulation)** with **LED dimming PWM (duty cycle modulation)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor, Data Handling - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DC Motor, Data Handling - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DC Motor – Technical Definition

A **DC Motor** (Direct Current Motor) is a rotary electromechanical device that converts **direct current electrical energy** into **mechanical rotational energy** based on the principle of the **Lorentz Force Law**. In the context of IoT and Biomedical applications, DC motors are widely used as **actuators** in devices such as infusion pumps, robotic prosthetics, ventilators, and automated laboratory equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> In PCBRT402, the DC Motor is studied primarily as an **IoT actuator element**. The focus is on its operating principle, speed-torque characteristics, and **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) based speed control using microcontrollers** — NOT on industrial-grade motor design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water wheel in a flowing river**. Water (electrons) pushes the paddles (current-carrying conductors placed in a magnetic field), causing the wheel to rotate. The faster the water flows, the faster the wheel spins. In a DC motor, **current** plays the role of water flow, and the **magnetic field** replaces gravity. By controlling the current (using PWM from a microcontroller like Arduino/ESP32), we precisely control the motor's rotational speed.

## 1.2 Data Handling – Technical Definition

**Data Handling** in an IoT system refers to the systematic process of **acquiring, processing, storing, transmitting, and analyzing** sensor data throughout the IoT pipeline — from the physical sensor to the cloud and end-user application.

The five canonical stages of IoT data handling are:

1. **Data Acquisition** — sampling analog/digital signals.
2. **Data Pre-processing** — filtering, noise removal, normalization.
3. **Data Storage** — local (EEPROM/SD card) or remote (cloud databases).
4. **Data Transmission** — wired (UART/SPI/I2C) or wireless (Wi-Fi/BLE/LoRa).
5. **Data Analysis & Visualization** — dashboards, ML models, alerts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Box — Data Acquisition Rate:**
> The **Sampling Rate ($f_s$)** must satisfy the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$
> where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component in the input biomedical signal (e.g., **ECG ≈ 150 Hz**, **EMG ≈ 500 Hz**, **EEG ≈ 100 Hz**). Failing this condition causes **aliasing**, an unrecoverable distortion.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Data Handling like a **hospital's patient monitoring system**. A sensor (heart rate monitor) collects the raw signal (data acquisition), the nurse's station cleans the data (pre-processing), the patient's file is saved (storage), the report is sent to the doctor (transmission), and the doctor interprets the chart (analysis). Every IoT biomedical device follows this exact workflow.

### Standard Physical Constants Used in DC Motors

- **Magnetic Permeability of Free Space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permeability of Vacuum:** Same as $\mu_0$
- **Standard Armature Voltage:** $V_a = 5\text{V}, 12\text{V}, 24\text{V}$ (typical IoT supply)
- **PWM Frequency Range (IoT):** $f_{PWM} = 500 \text{ Hz to } 25 \text{ kHz}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DC Motor — Operating Principle

A DC motor works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force principle**.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1 — Field Setup:** Permanent magnets (or stator windings) create a uniform magnetic field $B$ between north and south poles.
- **Step 2 — Current Injection:** DC voltage $V$ is applied to the armature winding through brushes and a commutator.
- **Step 3 — Force Generation:** Each conductor carrying current $I$ in a magnetic field $B$ experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$
where $L$ is the effective length of the conductor in the field.
- **Step 4 — Torque Production:** Multiple conductors arranged on the armature produce a cumulative torque $T$ on the shaft.
- **Step 5 — Continuous Rotation:** The **commutator** reverses current direction every half-rotation, ensuring **unidirectional torque** (continuous rotation).
- **Step 6 — Back-EMF:** As the armature rotates, it cuts magnetic flux and generates an **opposing EMF** ($E_b$), which limits the current and stabilizes speed.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why commutator?** It mechanically converts DC into alternating current inside the armature coils, keeping torque always in the same rotational direction.
- **Why back-EMF?** Without it, current would rise uncontrollably and burn the winding. Back-EMF provides **self-regulation**.

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of a Separately Excited / Shunt DC Motor

For a typical IoT-grade DC motor, the equivalent circuit has:
- Armature resistance $R_a$ and inductance $L_a$ in series with back-EMF source $E_b$.
- Field winding resistance $R_f$ in parallel (for shunt motor).

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature loop:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

This single equation governs the entire electrical behaviour of the motor.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Force on a current-carrying conductor | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $T = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque equation | N·m |
| 3 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot N$ | Back-EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation (KVL) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi}$ | Speed equation | RPM |
| 6 | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Armature current | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e \phi}$ | No-load speed (at $I_a = 0$) | RPM |
| 8 | $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_{stall}$ | Starting/Stall torque | N·m |
| 9 | $P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Motor efficiency | % |
| 11 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | PWM Duty Cycle (0 to 1) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Average voltage across motor | Volts (V) |
| 13 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Hz |
| 14 | $V_{res} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC Quantization resolution | Volts/step |

Where:
- $K_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A)
- $K_e$ = Back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $\phi$ = Magnetic flux per pole (Weber, Wb)
- $N$ = Speed in RPM
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)
- $D$ = Duty cycle
- $n$ = ADC bit resolution (e.g., 10-bit for Arduino UNO)
- $V_{FS}$ = ADC full-scale voltage (typically 5V)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Note:**
> In biomedical robotics (e.g., surgical robots, prosthetics), **PID control of DC motors** using the back-EMF feedback provides **precise angular positioning** within $\pm 0.1°$ tolerance, critical for minimally invasive procedures.

## 2.4 Data Handling — Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Data Flow Logic

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** Biomedical sensor (e.g., ECG electrode) produces a low-amplitude analog signal.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** Amplification (using INA128 instrumentation amp) and filtering (band-pass 0.5–40 Hz for ECG).
- **Step 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Microcontroller samples the signal at $f_s$ and quantizes it into $2^n$ discrete levels.
- **Step 4 — Digital Processing:** MCU applies algorithms (e.g., moving average filter, FFT).
- **Step 5 — Storage:** Data written to local SD card or sent to cloud via MQTT protocol.
- **Step 6 — Visualization:** Real-time graphs on dashboards (e.g., ThingSpeak, Blynk).

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why sample at Nyquist rate?** To faithfully reconstruct the original signal without aliasing.
- **Why use higher ADC bits?** More bits = finer voltage resolution = better signal fidelity. A 12-bit ADC over 3.3V gives $3.3/4096 = 0.000806$ V per step.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back-EMF Equation ($E_b$)

**Given:** A single armature coil of $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, rotating at speed $N$ RPM in a uniform flux $\phi$ per pole.

**To Derive:** The back-EMF induced in the armature.

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution:**
The total flux cut by the coil in one complete revolution equals:
$$\Phi_{total} = P \cdot \phi \quad \text{(Webers)}$$

**Step 2 — Time for one revolution:**
$$t = \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of flux linkage (Faraday's Law):**
The average rate of change of flux linkages is the total flux cut per second:
$$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — EMF per conductor (parallel paths):**
For a wave-wound armature, number of parallel paths $A = 2$. Therefore, the total number of conductors in series per parallel path is $Z/A = Z/2$.

**Step 5 — Back-EMF per parallel path (which equals $E_b$ across brushes):**
$$E_b = \dfrac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Step 6 — Define EMF constant:**
Let $K_e = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$, then:
$$E_b = K_e \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

This is the **standard back-EMF equation** tested in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation ($T$)

**Step 1 — Force on a single conductor:**
$$F = B \cdot I_c \cdot L$$
where $I_c$ is current per conductor, $L$ is active length.

**Step 2 — Torque on a single conductor (armature radius $r$):**
$$T_{single} = F \cdot r = B \cdot I_c \cdot L \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Total torque from $Z$ conductors arranged on the armature:**
The total mechanical force times the effective radius gives total torque. After substituting $I_c = I_a/A$ and $B \cdot L \cdot r = \dfrac{\phi \cdot P}{2\pi}$ (from flux geometry):
$$T = \dfrac{P \cdot \phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Define torque constant:**
Let $K_t = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi \cdot A}$, then:
$$T = K_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Important relation between $K_e$ and $K_t$:**
In SI units:
$$K_e \text{ (V·s/rad)} = K_t \text{ (N·m/A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often confuse $K_e$ and $K_t$. In SI units, their numerical values are **equal**, but their units differ. Always write units explicitly.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Speed Equation (No-Load vs Loaded)

**Step 1 — Start from KVL equation:**
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E_b = K_e \phi N$:**
$$V = K_e \phi N + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$:**
$$N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e \phi}$$

**Step 4 — No-load condition ($I_a = 0$):**
$$N_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e \phi}$$

**Step 5 — Speed regulation:**
$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \dfrac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100$$

where $N_{fl}$ is the full-load speed.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \;\Omega$, field resistance $R_f = 220 \;\Omega$, and runs at 1000 RPM drawing an armature current of 20 A. Calculate: (a) Back-EMF, (b) No-load speed, (c) Mechanical power developed.

**Step (a) — Back-EMF:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (20 \times 0.5) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}$$

**Step (b) — No-load speed:**
At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_b \approx V = 220$ V.
Using the ratio:
$$\dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{E_{b0}}{E_b} = \dfrac{220}{210}$$
$$N_0 = 1000 \times \dfrac{220}{210} = 1047.6 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step (c) — Mechanical power developed:**
$$P_{mech} = E_b \times I_a = 210 \times 20 = 4200 \text{ W} = 4.2 \text{ kW}$$

## 3.5 Data Handling — Sampling Resolution Worked Example

**Problem:** An Arduino UNO (10-bit ADC, $V_{FS} = 5$V) acquires an ECG signal of peak amplitude 1.2 V. Calculate: (a) Resolution, (b) Digital output at peak.

**Step (a) — ADC Resolution:**
$$V_{res} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} = 4.88 \text{ mV/step}$$

**Step (b) — Digital value at peak:**
$$\text{Digital Value} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{res}} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.00488} \approx 245.9 \approx 246 \text{ (LSB)}$$

In binary (10-bit):
$$246_{10} = 00011110110_2$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DC Motor — Internal Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Voltage Supply V] --> B[Brush 1 Positive]
    A --> C[Brush 2 Negative]
    B --> D[Commutator Segment 1]
    C --> E[Commutator Segment 2]
    D --> F[Armature Coil Winding Z conductors]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Rotating Armature in Magnetic Field B]
    G --> H[Mechanical Shaft Output Torque T and Speed N]
    G --> I[Back EMF Eb induced opposes V]
    I --> J[Kirchhoff Voltage Law V equals Eb plus IaRa]
    J --> K[Self Regulated Current Ia]
    K --> F
    L[Permanent Magnet Stator] --> G
```

## 4.2 IoT Data Handling Pipeline — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
```
