```mermaid
    S1[Biomedical Sensor ECG EMG EEG] --> S2[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    S2 --> S3[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    S3 --> S4[Microcontroller Arduino ESP32]
    S4 --> S5[Local Storage SD Card EEPROM]
    S4 --> S6[Wireless Module WiFi BLE LoRa]
    S6 --> S7[Cloud Server MQTT Broker]
    S7 --> S8[Database InfluxDB Firebase]
    S8 --> S9[Analysis Machine Learning Dashboard]
    S9 --> S10[Visualization Mobile App Web Dashboard]
    S4 --> S11[Actuator Control DC Motor PWM]
    S11 --> S12[Medical Device Movement Infusion Pump Prosthetic]
```

## 4.3 PWM Speed Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[Microcontroller Generates PWM Signal] --> DRV[Motor Driver L298N H-Bridge]
    DRV --> M[DC Motor]
    M --> SHAFT[Output Shaft Rotational Motion]
    M --> FB[Back EMF Feedback Eb]
    FB --> MCU
    POT[User Input Potentiometer or App Command] --> MCU
    MCU --> DUTY[Adjust Duty Cycle D]
    DUTY --> M
```

## 4.4 Data Handling — Sampling and Quantization Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Continuous Time Analog Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler at Frequency fs greater than 2 fmax]
    C --> D[Quantizer with 2 to the power n levels]
    D --> E[Encoder Binary Output B0 to Bn minus 1]
    E --> F[Digital Processor MCU]
    F --> G[Storage and Transmission]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of back-EMF in a DC motor. Why is it essential for motor self-regulation?**

**Model Answer:**

Back-EMF ($E_b$) is the voltage induced in the rotating armature conductors, opposing the applied voltage $V$, as per **Lenz's Law**. From KVL:
$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

- At **startup**, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$, and $I_a = V/R_a$ is at its **maximum (stall current)**.
- As the motor speeds up, $E_b$ rises, reducing $I_a$ and stabilizing speed.
- This **self-regulating feedback** prevents armature burnout and matches motor torque to mechanical load.

Hence, back-EMF is the **natural speed-control mechanism** of a DC motor. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the Nyquist sampling theorem? Why is it critical in biomedical IoT data acquisition?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that to reconstruct a band-limited analog signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least **twice the highest frequency component** $f_{max}$:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Biomedical significance:**
- ECG signal has $f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz → $f_s \geq 300$ Hz (typically sampled at 500 Hz).
- EMG signal has $f_{max} \approx 500$ Hz → $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz.
- Under-sampling causes **aliasing**, where high-frequency noise appears as false low-frequency content, leading to **misdiagnosis** (e.g., false arrhythmia detection).

Thus, Nyquist theorem ensures **faithful representation** of biomedical signals. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the back-EMF equation of a DC motor. Define all terms clearly and state the relationship between the EMF constant and the torque constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has the following specifications:
- Armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \;\Omega$
- Field resistance $R_f = 110 \;\Omega$
- No-load speed $N_0 = 1200$ RPM
- Full-load armature current $I_{a,fl} = 50$ A

Calculate:
- (i) Back-EMF at full load
- (ii) Full-load speed
- (iii) Speed regulation in %
- (iv) Mechanical power developed at full load **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation of Back-EMF Equation:**

Let:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = total armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths
- $N$ = speed (RPM)

**Step 1:** Flux cut per revolution = $P \phi$ Wb.

**Step 2:** Time for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

**Step 3:** Average rate of flux cutting = $\dfrac{P \phi N}{60}$ Wb/s.

**Step 4:** Conductors per parallel path = $Z/A$, each cutting flux.

**Step 5:** Back-EMF across brushes:
$$E_b = \dfrac{P \phi N Z}{60 A}$$

**Step 6:** Define $K_e = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$:
$$\boxed{E_b = K_e \phi N}$$

**Relationship between $K_e$ and $K_t$:**
From torque derivation:
$$T = \dfrac{P \phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = K_t \phi I_a$$
where $K_t = \dfrac{PZ}{2\pi A}$.

Comparing:
$$K_t = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \times K_e = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \times K_e$$

In **SI units (rad/s)**, $K_e \text{ (V·s/rad)} = K_t \text{ (N·m/A)}$ numerically. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Back-EMF at full load:**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (50 \times 0.3) = 220 - 15 = \boxed{205 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Full-load speed:**
Since $E_b \propto N$ (with $\phi$ constant in shunt motor):
$$\dfrac{N_{fl}}{N_0} = \dfrac{E_{b,fl}}{E_{b,0}} = \dfrac{205}{220}$$
$$N_{fl} = 1200 \times \dfrac{205}{220} = \boxed{1118.2 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) Speed regulation:**
$$\text{Reg \%} = \dfrac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1200 - 1118.2}{1118.2} \times 100 = \boxed{7.32\%}$$

**(iv) Mechanical power developed:**
$$P_{mech} = E_b \times I_a = 205 \times 50 = \boxed{10250 \text{ W} = 10.25 \text{ kW}}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **IoT data handling pipeline** for a biomedical application. Discuss each stage in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ESP32 microcontroller (12-bit ADC, $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V) is used to acquire a body temperature signal from an LM35 sensor. The sensor outputs 10 mV/°C. For a body temperature of 38.5 °C:
- (i) Calculate the analog voltage from the sensor
- (ii) Determine the ADC resolution
- (iii) Find the digital output value
- (iv) Justify the choice of sampling rate for body temperature monitoring **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**IoT Data Handling Pipeline (5 Stages):**

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:**
Biomedical sensors (ECG, EMG, temperature, SpO2) produce low-amplitude analog signals. Sampling is performed by the ADC at frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion).

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing:**
- Amplification (e.g., INA128 instrumentation amplifier, gain 100–1000)
- Filtering (band-pass, notch at 50 Hz for powerline noise)
- Normalization and offset removal

**Stage 3 — Data Processing:**
The microcontroller (ESP32, Arduino) applies:
- Digital filters (moving average, FIR, IIR)
- Feature extraction (peak detection, HRV analysis)
- Compression (for low-bandwidth transmission)

**Stage 4 — Storage:**
- **Local:** SD card, EEPROM (non-volatile)
- **Cloud:** Firebase, InfluxDB, AWS IoT Core

**Stage 5 — Transmission & Visualization:**
- **Protocols:** MQTT (lightweight, pub-sub), HTTP, CoAP
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT
- **Visualization:** ThingSpeak, Blynk, custom mobile/web dashboards

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2).** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Analog voltage from LM35 at 38.5 °C:**
$$V_{analog} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times 38.5 = 385 \text{ mV} = \boxed{0.385 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) ADC Resolution (12-bit, $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V):**
$$V_{res} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096} = \boxed{0.806 \text{ mV/step}}$$

**(iii) Digital output value:**
$$D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{res}} = \dfrac{0.385}{0.000806} \approx \boxed{478 \text{ LSB}}$$

Binary (12-bit): $D = 0001 1101 1110_2$

**(iv) Sampling rate justification:**
Body temperature changes **very slowly** (≈ 0.1 °C/min maximum). The highest meaningful frequency is well below 0.1 Hz. A sampling rate of **1–10 Hz** is more than sufficient (vastly exceeds Nyquist minimum). Higher rates waste power and storage — critical in battery-operated biomedical IoT devices. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing $E_b$ in RPM or $T$ in Volts — always state the unit alongside the symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting "per parallel path":** In back-EMF derivation, students often miss the $A$ (parallel paths) factor. For **wave winding $A = 2$**, for **lap winding $A = P$**.
> 3. **PWM ≠ Variable Voltage:** Students incorrectly state "PWM changes the voltage." PWM keeps voltage constant and varies the **duty cycle** $D$, controlling the **average power** delivered.
> 4. **Nyquist = Twice, NOT Equal:** $f_s$ must be **strictly greater than** $2 f_{max}$ (some authors use $\geq$, but practical systems use 5–10× for safety margin).
> 5. **ADC Bit Math:** A 10-bit ADC has $2^{10} = 1024$ levels, NOT 10 levels. Always compute $2^n$ explicitly.
> 6. **Speed Regulation Sign:** Some students compute regulation as $(N_0 - N_{fl})/N_0$. The KTU preferred formula is $(N_0 - N_{fl})/N_{fl} \times 100$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DC Motor** is an IoT **actuator** that converts electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy via the **Lorentz force**.
- The **armature voltage equation** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ is the **master equation** for all DC motor problems.
- **Back-EMF** $E_b = K_e \phi N$ provides **natural self-regulation** of motor speed.
- **Torque** $T = K_t \phi I_a$ is directly proportional to armature current (in linear region).
- **No-load speed** $N_0 = V/(K_e \phi)$ is the **maximum speed**; loaded speed is always lower.
- **PWM control** uses duty cycle $D$ to vary the average voltage: $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$.
- **Motor drivers** (L298N, L293D, DRV8871) are **mandatory** between MCU and motor — MCUs cannot supply motor current directly.
- **IoT Data Handling** has 5 stages: Acquisition → Pre-processing → Processing → Storage → Transmission/Visualization.
- **Nyquist Theorem** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ prevents **aliasing** — critical in ECG, EMG, EEG acquisition.
- **ADC Resolution** $V_{res} = V_{FS}/2^n$ determines the **smallest detectable voltage change**.
- **Biomedical IoT sensors:** ECG ($f_{max} \approx 150$ Hz), EMG ($f_{max} \approx 500$ Hz), EEG ($f_{max} \approx 100$ Hz), body temperature (DC, <1 Hz).
- **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = (E_b I_a)/(V I_a) = E_b/V$ for ideal no-field-loss motors.
- **Speed regulation** quantifies how much speed drops from no-load to full-load — **lower is better** for precision applications.
- Always state **assumptions** clearly: separately excited, constant flux, linear magnetic region.
- The relation $K_e = K_t$ (in SI units) is a **favourite KTU 2-mark shortcut** question.
- For **IoT grade motors**, power ratings are typically **3 W to 100 W**; voltage ratings are **3.3 V, 5 V, 6 V, 12 V, 24 V**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 : BIG DATA — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data** is formally defined as *extremely large, high-velocity, and/or high-variety information assets that demand cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing that enable enhanced insight, decision-making, and process automation.* It is characterized by datasets whose **size, speed, and structural complexity** exceed the capability of conventional relational database management systems (RDBMS) to capture, manage, and process them within tolerable elapsed times.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBRT402)** context, Big Data is the natural consequence of the **Internet of Things (IoT)** explosion: billions of heterogeneous sensors, wearable biomedical devices, EHR systems, and imaging modalities continuously emit data into centralized or edge-cloud pipelines, where it must be stored, transmitted, and mined for clinical insight.

## 1.2 The Canonical 5 V's of Big Data

Big Data is universally characterized by five inter-locking dimensions. Every KTU exam answer must reference at least these five:

| Dimension | Meaning | Biomedical / IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data (TB → PB → ZB) | Genomics data (~200 GB per human genome), continuous ICU monitoring |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation/processing | Real-time ECG stream (256 Hz – 1000 Hz sampling) |
| **Variety** | Heterogeneity of data types | Wearable PPG, MRI DICOM, free-text clinical notes |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / noise / quality | Motion artefacts in ambulatory ECG |
| **Value** | Extractable business / clinical worth | Early arrhythmia prediction from wearables |

> [!NOTE]
> Some extended definitions include a **6th V — Variability** (changes in data meaning over time) and a **7th V — Visualization** (rendering petabyte-scale data in human-readable form).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flood vs. The Bucket"

Imagine you are a **village well-keeper** in medieval India. A normal day, villagers arrive with a small **bucket** and you easily scoop water and serve them — this is **traditional RDBMS** with **megabytes–gigabytes** of structured data.

Suddenly, the monsoon breaches a dam, and a **wall of water** approaches. You cannot serve this with a bucket. You need:

- A **reservoir** (distributed storage — HDFS, cloud object stores)
- **Multiple channels** to divert flow (parallel processing — MapReduce, Spark)
- **Filtration plants** to remove mud and debris (data cleaning & ETL)
- **Forecast analysts** to predict flooding (analytics & ML)

That wall of water is **Big Data from IoT & Biomedical devices** — petabytes of ECG, EEG, imaging, genomic, and environmental signals arriving faster than any single machine can store or interpret.

## 1.4 Structured, Semi-Structured, and Unstructured Data

- **Structured Data**: Fits cleanly into relational tables (rows & columns). Example: patient demographic records in an SQL database, lab reports keyed by patient ID.
- **Semi-Structured Data**: Has some organizational properties but no rigid schema. Example: HL7 / FHIR messages, JSON payloads from IoT wearables, XML DICOM headers.
- **Unstructured Data**: No predefined schema; ~**80%** of healthcare data falls here. Example: MRI/CT pixel arrays, free-text clinical notes, audio stethoscope recordings, histopathology slides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: When asked "Why can't traditional databases handle Big Data?", the expected answer must cite **schema rigidity (Structured only)**, **vertical scaling limits**, and **inability to handle unstructured formats**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Big Data Pipeline

A typical IoT-Biomedical Big Data pipeline follows a layered architecture:

1. **Data Source Layer** — Sensors (ECG leads, SpO₂ probes, accelerometers, smart implants), imaging modalities, genomic sequencers, EHR systems.
2. **Ingestion Layer** — Message brokers (Kafka, MQTT, RabbitMQ) absorb streaming data at high velocity.
3. **Storage Layer** — Distributed file systems (HDFS), NoSQL stores (Cassandra, MongoDB), data lakes (S3, Azure Data Lake).
4. **Processing Layer** — Batch (MapReduce, Spark) and Stream (Flink, Spark Streaming, Kafka Streams) engines.
5. **Analytics / ML Layer** — Classification, clustering, anomaly detection (e.g., arrhythmia classification from raw ECG).
6. **Visualization / Decision Layer** — Dashboards (Grafana, Kibana) and Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS).

## 2.2 The Hadoop Ecosystem (Conceptual, Non-Programmatic)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 asks for **conceptual understanding** of the Hadoop stack, not Hadoop programming. Do not write Java/Python/Scala code.

The Hadoop framework contains two foundational components:

- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — A distributed, fault-tolerant storage layer that splits files into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB**) and replicates them across a cluster (default replication factor = **3**).
- **MapReduce** — A parallel programming model with two stages:
  - **Map Stage**: Each input record is transformed into intermediate key–value pairs.
  - **Reduce Stage**: All values sharing the same key are aggregated.

The supporting ecosystem includes:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **HBase** | NoSQL wide-column store on top of HDFS |
| **Hive** | SQL-like query layer over HDFS |
| **Pig** | Data flow scripting (Pig Latin) |
| **Sqoop** | Bulk transfer between RDBMS ↔ HDFS |
| **Flume** | Log/streaming data ingestion into HDFS |
| **Spark** | In-memory distributed processing (100× faster than MapReduce for iterative ML) |

## 2.3 NoSQL Databases — Why They Exist

RDBMS enforces **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and rigid schema. For Big Data, we need **BASE** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) and schema flexibility.

Four NoSQL families:

- **Key-Value Stores** (Redis, DynamoDB) — Fast lookups, session caching in health portals.
- **Document Stores** (MongoDB, CouchDB) — JSON medical records, FHIR resources.
- **Column-Family Stores** (Cassandra, HBase) — High write throughput for sensor streams.
- **Graph Databases** (Neo4j) — Patient–disease–gene–drug relationship networks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Typical Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage per patient genome | $S = N_{bp} \times 2 \text{ bits}$ | Bytes | Genomics pipeline sizing |
| 2 | Block count in HDFS | $N_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{F}{B_s} \right\rceil$ where $F$ = file size, $B_s$ = block size | Integer | Hadoop cluster planning |
| 3 | Total replicas in HDFS | $R_t = N_b \times R_f$ | Integer blocks | Disk capacity planning |
| 4 | Effective storage | $S_{eff} = F \times R_f$ | Bytes | NAS / SAN sizing |
| 5 | Streaming bitrate | $r = f_s \times n_c \times b$ | bits/s | IoT bandwidth design |
| 6 | Daily data volume | $V_d = r \times 86400$ | Bytes/day | Cloud ingestion costing |
| 7 | MapReduce speedup (Amdahl) | $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | Ratio | Parallelism efficiency |
| 8 | Data growth (exponential) | $V(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{k t}$ | Bytes | 5-year capacity forecast |
| 9 | Compression ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ | Unitless | DICOM archive sizing |
| 10 | Sample size for ML | $n \geq \dfrac{z^2 \sigma^2}{E^2}$ | Samples | Clinical ML training |

## 2.5 Real-World Biomedical & IoT Utilities

- **Predictive Analytics in ICUs**: Continuous Big Data streams from ventilators + infusion pumps + monitors are fed to ML models predicting sepsis 6–12 hours before clinical onset.
- **Population Genomics**: National projects like the *All of Us* program aggregate genomic + lifestyle data of **>1 million** participants.
- **Smart Hospitals**: RFID-tagged assets, BLE wearables, and ambient sensors produce petabyte-scale telemetry yearly.
- **Drug Discovery**: Mining millions of molecular structures and biomedical literature for candidate compounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance**: Big Data is the *fuel*; AI/ML is the *engine*. Without robust Big Data pipelines, the predictive models that power modern telemedicine and personalized medicine are impossible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Storage Capacity of an IoT Wearable Fleet

**Problem**: A hospital deploys **N = 5,000** wearable ECG patches. Each patch samples **f_s = 250 Hz** on **n_c = 2 leads** at **b = 16 bits/sample**. Each patch is worn for **T = 14 days** continuously. Compute:

(a) Daily data volume per patch
(b) Total storage for the entire fleet
(c) Number of HDFS blocks required (block size $B_s = 128$ MB)

### Step (a) — Per-patch Daily Volume

The instantaneous bitrate is:

$$r = f_s \times n_c \times b$$

$$r = 250 \;\text{Hz} \times 2 \times 16 \;\text{bits} = 8{,}000 \;\text{bits/s} = 8 \;\text{kbps}$$

Daily volume per patch:

$$V_d = r \times 86{,}400 \;\text{s/day}$$

$$V_d = 8{,}000 \;\text{bits/s} \times 86{,}400 \;\text{s} = 691{,}200{,}000 \;\text{bits}$$

$$V_d = \dfrac{691{,}200{,}000}{8} = 86{,}400{,}000 \;\text{bytes} \approx 86.4 \;\text{MB/day}$$

### Step (b) — Total Fleet Volume (14 days)

Per-patch 14-day volume:

$$V_{14} = 86.4 \;\text{MB/day} \times 14 \;\text{days} = 1{,}209.6 \;\text{MB} \approx 1.21 \;\text{GB}$$

Total fleet:

$$V_{fleet} = N \times V_{14} = 5{,}000 \times 1.21 \;\text{GB} \approx 6{,}048 \;\text{GB} \approx \mathbf{6.05 \;\text{TB}}$$

### Step (c) — HDFS Block Count

With default replication $R_f = 3$, effective storage on the cluster:

$$S_{eff} = V_{fleet} \times R_f = 6{,}048 \;\text{GB} \times 3 = 18{,}144 \;\text{GB}$$

Convert to MB:

$$S_{eff} = 18{,}144 \times 1024 \approx 18{,}579{,}456 \;\text{MB}$$

Number of blocks:

$$N_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{eff}}{B_s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{18{,}579{,}456}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 145{,}152 \rceil$$

$$\boxed{N_b = 145{,}152 \;\text{HDFS blocks}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows how a *seemingly modest* 5,000-patient pilot balloons into **>145 K blocks** spread across a Hadoop cluster — justifying the move to distributed storage.

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Amdahl's Law for Parallel ECG Analytics

**Problem**: A MapReduce job analyzing ECG archives has **p = 64** parallel reducers. Suppose the **parallelizable fraction is f = 0.92** (rest is single-threaded file IO and result aggregation). Compute the achieved speedup.

### Derivation

Amdahl's Law:

$$S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$$

Substituting:

$$S(64) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \dfrac{0.92}{64}}$$

$$S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.08 + 0.014375}$$

$$S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.094375}$$

$$\boxed{S(64) \approx 10.60 \times}$$

### Interpretation

Even with 64 reducers, we get only **~10.6×** speedup because the **8%** sequential portion bottlenecks the entire job. This is why MapReduce jobs in healthcare pipelines are designed to minimize the sequential fraction (e.g., by co-locating data and computation — *data locality*).

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Genomic Data Storage

**Problem**: A single human genome contains **N_bp = 3.2 × 10⁹** base pairs. Each base pair requires **2 bits** (A, T, G, C). What is the storage requirement per genome? How many genomes fit on a **1 TB** disk?

### Derivation

Per-genome raw storage:

$$S = N_{bp} \times 2 \;\text{bits}$$

$$S = 3.2 \times 10^{9} \times 2 = 6.4 \times 10^{9} \;\text{bits} = 800 \;\text{MB}$$

Including sequencing redundancy (typically ~30× coverage):

$$S_{seq} = 800 \;\text{MB} \times 30 = 24{,}000 \;\text{MB} \approx \mathbf{24 \;\text{GB/genome}}$$

### Number of genomes on 1 TB

Assuming raw (uncompressed, no coverage redundancy):

$$N = \dfrac{1 \;\text{TB}}{800 \;\text{MB}} = \dfrac{1{,}048{,}576 \;\text{MB}}{800 \;\text{MB}} \approx \mathbf{1{,}310 \;\text{genomes}}$$

This is why population genomics demands **petabyte-scale** data lakes, not RDBMS.

## 3.4 Worked Problem 4 — Streaming Bitrate & Network Bandwidth

**Problem**: An ICU bed streams **12** vital signs, each sampled at **100 Hz** with **16-bit** resolution. Calculate the required bandwidth per bed.

### Derivation

$$r = f_s \times n_c \times b = 100 \times 12 \times 16 = 19{,}200 \;\text{bits/s}$$

$$r = \dfrac{19{,}200}{1{,}000} = 19.2 \;\text{kbps}$$

For a **100-bed** ICU:

$$r_{ICU} = 19.2 \times 100 = 1{,}920 \;\text{kbps} \approx 1.92 \;\text{Mbps}$$

This easily fits a standard hospital LAN, but **thousands** of such ICUs streaming to a cloud data lake demands **multi-gigabit** backbone uplinks — reinforcing why Big Data infrastructure (Kafka, compression, edge pre-processing) is essential.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Big Data. Explain any three characteristics of Big Data with biomedical examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be efficiently processed by traditional RDBMS tools. The key characteristics (V's) are:

1. **Volume**: Refers to the massive scale of data. *Biomedical Example*: A single hospital's imaging archive (MRI, CT) can exceed **petabytes** annually.

2. **Velocity**: The speed at which data is generated and must be processed. *Biomedical Example*: Continuous ICU monitoring at **100–1000 Hz** sampling rate per channel.

3. **Variety**: Diverse data formats. *Biomedical Example*: Structured (HL7 lab tables), semi-structured (FHIR JSON), and unstructured (DICOM images, clinical notes) coexist in modern EHRs.

*(Any 3 V's, with biomedical context = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Compare structured and unstructured data. Why is unstructured data a challenge in healthcare analytics?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed (tables) | No predefined schema |
| Storage | RDBMS (MySQL) | Data lakes, NoSQL |
| Query | SQL | NLP, ML pipelines |
| Example | Lab values | MRI pixels, doctor's notes |

Unstructured data comprises ~**80%** of healthcare data. It is challenging because:
- Cannot be queried with SQL directly.
- Requires **NLP** for text mining (clinical notes).
- Requires **computer vision** for imaging.
- High dimensionality (a single CT slice has millions of pixels).

*(Comparison + 2 challenges = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule**: In Part B, the student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each choice contains sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **5 V's of Big Data** in detail. For each V, give one **biomedical or IoT** example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Big Data pipeline** in a smart hospital. List any four technologies used at each layer.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The 5 V's characterize every Big Data workload:

1. **Volume** — Magnitude of data. A modern tertiary-care hospital produces **~50–100 TB/year** from PACS, LIS, and EMR systems. National genomic projects exceed **petabytes**.

2. **Velocity** — Speed of arrival. Wearable ECG patches transmit at **>250 Hz continuously**; streaming analytics must process this within milliseconds for arrhythmia alerts.

3. **Variety** — Format diversity. A single patient record may include HL7 messages, DICOM images, JSON FHIR resources, free-text notes, and waveform XML.

4. **Veracity** — Data quality. Motion artefacts in ambulatory ECG, missing values from disconnected sensors, and transcription errors in clinical notes all degrade veracity.

5. **Value** — Clinical/operational worth. The ultimate purpose: turning raw data into **earlier diagnoses**, **personalized treatment plans**, and **reduced readmissions**.

*(Each V with biomedical example = approx. 1.4 marks × 5 = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Big Data Pipeline for a Smart Hospital**:

1. **Data Source Layer**: Wearables (ECG, SpO₂), Imaging (MRI/CT/PACS), EHR/LIS, Genomic sequencers, Environmental sensors.
2. **Ingestion Layer**: **Apache Kafka** for streaming, **MQTT broker** for IoT, **Flume** for log ingestion.
3. **Storage Layer**: **HDFS** for batch archive, **Cassandra** for time-series vitals, **S3/MinIO** as data lake, **MongoDB** for document records.
4. **Processing Layer**: **Apache Spark** for batch, **Flink** for stream, **MapReduce** for legacy ETL.
5. **Analytics Layer**: Python ML libraries (scikit-learn, TensorFlow), Spark MLlib for distributed training.
6. **Visualization Layer**: **Grafana** for vitals dashboards, **Kibana** for log search, **Tableau/Power BI** for management reports.

*(Block diagram = 3 marks, four tech per layer = 2 marks, smart hospital context = 2 marks)*

---

### **QUESTION B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Hadoop**? Explain **HDFS** and **MapReduce** with a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable ECG device samples **f_s = 500 Hz** on **n_c = 3 leads** at **b = 12 bits/sample**. Calculate: (i) daily data volume per device, (ii) total daily volume for **N = 10,000** devices, (iii) HDFS blocks required (block size 128 MB, replication 3).

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Hadoop** is an open-source Apache framework that enables distributed storage and processing of large datasets across commodity clusters.

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**:
- Splits files into **blocks** (default 128 MB).
- Each block is **replicated** (default factor = 3) across DataNodes.
- Has a master **NameNode** (metadata) and many **DataNodes** (storage).

**MapReduce**:
- **Map Phase**: Input → key-value pairs (e.g., patient ID → heart rate).
- **Shuffle & Sort**: Groups values by key.
- **Reduce Phase**: Aggregates values per key (e.g., average HR per patient).

Block diagram:

```
Input → Split → Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output
```

*(Hadoop overview = 2 marks, HDFS = 2.5 marks, MapReduce = 2.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Per-device daily volume:**

$$r = 500 \times 3 \times 12 = 18{,}000 \;\text{bits/s}$$

$$V_d = 18{,}000 \times 86{,}400 = 1{,}555{,}200{,}000 \;\text{bits} = 194.4 \;\text{MB/day}$$

**(ii) Total daily volume (10,000 devices):**

$$V_{fleet} = 10{,}000 \times 194.4 \;\text{MB} = 1{,}944{,}000 \;\text{MB} \approx 1.944 \;\text{TB/day}$$

**(iii) HDFS block count (with replication = 3):**

$$S_{eff} = 1.944 \;\text{TB} \times 3 = 5.832 \;\text{TB}$$

Convert to MB:

$$S_{eff} = 5.832 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 6{,}115{,}341 \;\text{MB}$$

$$N_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{6{,}115{,}341}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 47{,}776.1 \rceil$$

$$\boxed{N_b = 47{,}777 \;\text{HDFS blocks}}$$

*(Each sub-calculation = ~2.3 marks; final block answer = 1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting replication**: Many students compute block count using raw bytes instead of `raw × replication factor`. Always multiply by **3** for default HDFS.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors**: Bits vs. Bytes vs. MB vs. MiB. **1 MB = 8 × 10⁶ bits**, but **1 MiB = 2²⁰ bytes**. KTU prefers SI units (MB, GB, TB).
> 3. **Confusing Map phases**: Writing *Mapper* and *Reducer* code in non-CS subjects — KTU penalizes this in IoT/Biomedical papers. Stick to **conceptual** block diagrams.
> 4. **Skipping biomedical examples**: Generic "social media" examples lose marks. Always anchor to **ECG, MRI, EHR, genomics** in this course.
> 5. **Missing the 5th V (Value)**: Students often stop at Volume/Velocity/Variety. Listing **Veracity** and **Value** is mandatory for full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Big Data (Module 4)**

- ✅ Big Data = datasets exceeding RDBMS capability — characterized by the **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ✅ **Structured** (RDBMS), **Semi-structured** (JSON, XML, HL7), **Unstructured** (images, notes, audio) — **80%** healthcare data is unstructured.
- ✅ Typical pipeline: **Source → Ingest → Store → Process → Analyze → Visualize**.
- ✅ **Hadoop** = HDFS + MapReduce + ecosystem (Hive, Pig, HBase, Spark, Kafka).
- ✅ **HDFS** default block size = **128 MB**, default replication factor = **3**.
- ✅ **MapReduce** has two stages: **Map** (transform) → **Reduce** (aggregate).
- ✅ **NoSQL** types: Key-Value, Document, Column-Family, Graph — all follow **BASE**, not ACID.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ — quantifies parallel speedup.
- ✅ **Storage formulas**: bitrate $r = f_s \cdot n_c \cdot b$; daily volume $V_d = r \times 86{,}400$ s.
- ✅ **Biomedical relevance**: Genomics (~24 GB/genome with 30× coverage), ICU streaming (~20 kbps/bed), population health (PB-scale archives).
- ✅ Always cite **biomedical / IoT** examples — never generic — for full marks in PCBRT402.
- ✅ Amdahl's bottleneck: even **p = 64** reducers yield only **~10×** speedup if sequential fraction = 8%.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Data ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > In the context of IoT and Biomedical Applications, **Data** refers to any raw, unprocessed fact, value, or signal acquired from a biological system, medical instrument, or connected healthcare device that can be measured, recorded, transmitted, stored, and interpreted to support clinical decisions, diagnostics, and remote monitoring. ### 1.1 What is "Type of Data"? The **"Type of Data"** describes the **structural nature, format, temporal behavior, and semantic content** of information flowing through the IoT–Healthcare ecosystem. Different sensors, imaging systems, wearables, and clinical information systems generate fundamentally different data formats — and each demands its own acquisition rate, storage strategy, compression method, and analytical pipeline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this topic matters in KTU Exams** > Module 4 of PCBRT402 focuses on **biomedical data engineering**. The examiner almost always asks students to **classify biomedical data correctly, identify their acquisition parameters, and justify storage/transmission requirements** — this is a guaranteed 7-to-14 mark question area. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a **hospital as a giant smart kitchen**, and data as the **ingredients** being prepared: - **Structured Data** → Like neatly packed, labelled grocery boxes on a shelf (rows & columns of an Excel sheet — patient IDs, lab values). Easy to find, easy to count. - **Unstructured Data** → Like a smoothie — everything blended together (MRI scans, doctor’s handwritten notes, audio of patient speech). Rich, but harder to search. - **Semi-Structured Data** → Like a box with a label on the outside but messy contents (JSON packets from an IoT pulse-oximeter — tagged but flexible). - **Time-Series Data** → Like a continuous video recording of a boiling pot (ECG trace sampled every millisecond) — order matters. - **Image / Signal Data** → Like a high-resolution photograph or a sound wave (CT slice, EEG waveform) — needs special "decoders" (DICOM viewers, signal-processing toolboxes). > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a question mentions **"real-time, continuous, ordered, high-frequency"** → it is **Time-Series Signal Data**. If it mentions **"pixel, slice, DICOM, scan"** → it is **Imaging Data**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Biomedical Data (Bold Constants & Units) | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | ECG Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **250 – 1000 Hz** (clinical) | | EEG Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **256 – 512 Hz** | | Heart Rate Range | HR | **40 – 200 bpm** | | Body Temperature Range | T | **35 – 42 °C** | | SpO₂ Range | – | **70 – 100 %** | | DICOM Image Size (CT) | – | **512 × 512 × 16 bits** | | Standard EHR Record Size | – | **~100 KB – 5 MB** per patient | | HL7 / FHIR Message | – | **XML / JSON** structured payload | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Master Classification of Biomedical / IoT Data Biomedical data can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: #### Axis A — By Structure 1. **Structured Data** — Fixed schema, tabular form. - *Examples:* Lab results (Hb, glucose), patient demographics, billing records, vitals dashboards. - *Storage:* Relational databases (SQL, MySQL). 2. **Unstructured Data** — No predefined schema. - *Examples:* MRI/CT/X-ray images, clinical narrative notes, audio stethoscope recordings, surgical videos. - *Storage:* Object stores, PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System), NoSQL. 3. **Semi-Structured Data** — Self-describing tags but flexible schema. - *Examples:* HL7 v2/v3 messages, FHIR JSON resources, IoT MQTT payloads, XML-based device logs. - *Storage:* Document databases (MongoDB), message brokers. #### Axis B — By Quantitative Nature 1. **Quantitative (Numerical) Data** - *Discrete:* Number of heartbeats per minute (count). - *Continuous:* Body temperature in °C, blood pressure in mmHg, SpO₂ in %. 2. **Qualitative (Categorical) Data** - *Nominal:* Blood group (A, B, AB, O), gender. - *Ordinal:* Pain scale (0–10), NYHA heart-failure class (I–IV). #### Axis C — By Temporal / Domain Behavior 1. **Time-Series Data** — Indexed by time, sampled at regular intervals. - *Examples:* ECG, EEG, EMG, continuous glucose monitor (CGM), wearable accelerometer. 2. **Image / Volumetric Data** — 2D pixels or 3D voxels. - *Examples:* X-ray, CT (DICOM), MRI, ultrasound, histopathology slides. 3. **Genomic / Omics Data** — High-dimensional, sequence-based. - *Examples:* DNA sequences (FASTA), gene expression (RNA-Seq), proteomics. 4. **Streaming / Real-Time Data** — Continuous unbounded flow from IoT sensors. - *Examples:* ICU ventilator waveforms, smart-watch heart rate streams. 5. **Batch / Static Data** — Acquired and stored before processing. - *Examples:* Annual lab reports, archived EHRs, historical claims. ### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Data Type - **Why Structured?** Enables fast SQL queries, statistical reporting, and dashboarding — critical for hospital management. - **Why Unstructured?** Carries the *visual* and *narrative* richness needed for diagnosis (radiology images, pathology reports). - **Why Semi-Structured?** IoT devices come from many vendors — flexible JSON/HL7 lets them interoperate without rigid schemas. - **Why Time-Series?** Physiology is inherently dynamic; a single snapshot is clinically useless for cardiac arrhythmias. - **Why Streaming?** ICU and remote patient monitoring need sub-second latency — batch processing is too slow. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Module 4 — Memorize Before Exam** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ | **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** (minimum sampling rate) | Hz | | 2 | $R = f_s \times n \times b$ | **Data Rate** = samples/sec × channels × bits/sample | bits/s | | 3 | $S = R \times t$ | **Storage Size** = data rate × recording duration | bits | | 4 | $S_{MB} = \dfrac{S}{8 \times 10^6}$ | Convert bits → Megabytes | MB | | 5 | $f_m = \dfrac{HR_{max}}{60}$ | Max signal frequency from max heart rate | Hz | | 6 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Time between samples | seconds | | 7 | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | Dynamic Range (in dB) | dB | | 8 | $N_q = 2^{b}$ | Quantization levels for $b$-bit ADC | levels | > [!WARNING] > In the markdown table above, notice the use of `\vert`/regular brackets instead of `|` — this prevents LaTeX/markdown table-parser conflicts. **Do not write `\|x\|` inside table rows.** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hospital PACS servers** use DICOM (unstructured imaging data) to store millions of CT scans. - **Apple Watch / Fitbit** generate time-series accelerometer + PPG data streamed via BLE → phone → cloud. - **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** is the modern semi-structured data standard for IoT-Health apps. - **Genomic data lakes** (e.g., NIH dbGaP) require petabyte-scale storage because a single human genome ≈ **3.2 GB** raw. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Below are **fully worked-out derivations** that examiners love to set in the 14-mark Part B questions. Practice writing each step on paper. --- ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate of ECG Signal **Problem Setup:** A clinical ECG has a maximum useful spectral frequency $f_m$ determined by the highest heart rate of interest. **Step 1 — Convert maximum heart rate to frequency.** For $HR_{max} = 240$ bpm, the fundamental cardiac frequency in Hz is: $$f_m = \frac{HR_{max}}{60} = \frac{240}{60} = 4\ \text{Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem.** To reconstruct the waveform losslessly, the sampling frequency must satisfy: $$f_s \ge 2 f_m$$ $$f_s \ge 2 \times 4 = 8\ \text{Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Add a clinical safety margin (oversampling).** In practice, ECG devices oversample at **40×–250×** to capture QRS morphology and high-frequency notches. Choosing an oversampling factor $k = 62.5$: $$f_s = k \times 2 f_m = 62.5 \times 8 = 500\ \text{Hz}$$ This matches the **standard clinical ECG sampling rate of 500 Hz**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Rate of a 12-Lead ECG Monitor **Step 1 — Identify parameters.** - Sampling frequency: $f_s = 500$ Hz - Number of leads (channels): $n = 12$ - ADC resolution: $b = 16$ bits/sample (clinical standard) **Step 2 — Apply the data rate formula.** $$R = f_s \times n \times b$$ $$R = 500 \times 12 \times 16$$ $$R = 96{,}000\ \text{bits/second}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to kilobits per second.** $$R = \frac{96{,}000}{1000} = 96\ \text{kbps}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to KB/s.** $$R_{KB/s} = \frac{96{,}000}{8 \times 1024} \approx 11.72\ \text{KB/s}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — 24-Hour Storage Requirement (Holter Monitor) **Step 1 — Total recording time.** $$t = 24\ \text{hours} = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400\ \text{seconds}$$ **Step 2 — Total data generated.** $$S = R \times t = 96{,}000 \times 86{,}400$$ $$S = 8.2944 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bits}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Gigabytes.** $$S_{GB} = \frac{8.2944 \times 10^{9}}{8 \times 10^{9}} \approx 1.0368\ \text{GB}$$ > [!TIP] > **Result Interpretation:** A single 12-lead, 24-hour Holter recording produces **~1 GB** of raw data. This is why compression (e.g., wavelet-based ECG compression) is a *must-have* in real clinical deployments. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Image Data Size (CT Slice) **Step 1 — Parameters of a typical CT slice.** - Matrix size: $M = 512$ pixels - Rows: $N = 512$ pixels - Bit-depth: $b = 16$ bits (DICOM standard for CT) **Step 2 — Bits per slice.** $$S_{slice} = M \times N \times b = 512 \times 512 \times 16$$ $$S_{slice} = 4{,}194{,}304\ \text{bits}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to bytes.** $$S_{slice} = \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{8} = 524{,}288\ \text{bytes} = 512\ \text{KB}$$ **Step 4 — Full chest CT (300 slices).** $$S_{CT} = 300 \times 512\ \text{KB} = 153{,}600\ \text{KB} \approx 150\ \text{MB}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Data Types | Data Type | Sample Rate / Size | Storage Strategy | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **ECG Time-Series** | 500 Hz, 16-bit | Compressed CSV / WFDB | Arrhythmia detection | | **EEG Time-Series** | 256 Hz, 24 channels | EDF format | Epilepsy monitoring | | **MRI Image (3D)** | 256 × 256 × 180 voxels | DICOM, PACS | Tumor detection | | **Genomic Sequence** | ~3.2 GB / genome | FASTA, BAM, CRAM | Precision medicine | | **EHR Structured** | Transactional | SQL / PostgreSQL | Hospital admin | | **IoT Wearable Stream** | 1–100 Hz, JSON | MQTT → Time-series DB | Remote monitoring | | **Clinical Notes** | Text, ~5 KB/visit | NoSQL document store | NLP extraction | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Biomedical & IoT Data ```mermaid graph TD root["Biomedical and IoT Data"]:::root root --> struct["By Structure"]:::branch root --> quant["By Quantitative Nature"]:::branch root --> tempo["By Temporal Behavior"]:::branch root --> domain["By Clinical Domain"]:::branch struct --> s1["Structured Data
SQL tables lab values demographics"]:::leaf struct --> s2["Unstructured Data
MRI CT clinical notes audio"]:::leaf struct --> s3["Semi Structured Data
HL7 FHIR JSON MQTT"]:::leaf quant --> q1["Quantitative
HR BP Temp SpO2"]:::leaf quant --> q2["Qualitative
Blood group pain scale NYHA class"]:::leaf tempo --> t1["Time Series
ECG EEG EMG CGM"]:::leaf tempo --> t2["Image Volumetric
DICOM CT MRI XRAY"]:::leaf tempo --> t3["Streaming Real Time
ICU vent wearable stream"]:::leaf tempo --> t4["Static Batch
Annual reports archived EHR"]:::leaf domain --> d1["Signal Data
Bioelectric signals"]:::leaf domain --> d2["Imaging Data
Radiology pathology"]:::leaf domain --> d3["Omics Data
Genomics proteomics"]:::leaf domain --> d4["Clinical Text Data
Discharge summaries prescriptions"]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef branch fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Acquisition–Storage–Analytics Pipeline Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Patient Body
Physiological Source"]:::src --> B["Sensor Layer
ECG EEG SpO2 BP Temp"]:::layer B --> C["Acquisition Module
ADC at fs Hz"]:::layer C --> D["Edge Gateway
Smart phone MCU"]:::layer D --> E["Cloud Ingestion
MQTT broker HL7 FHIR"]:::layer E --> F1["Structured Store
SQL Database"]:::store E --> F2["Time Series Store
InfluxDB Timescale"]:::store E --> F3["Object Store
AWS S3 PACS"]:::store E --> F4["Document Store
MongoDB FHIR server"]:::store F1 --> G["Analytics AI ML
Diagnosis Alerts"]:::out F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> H["Clinician Dashboard
Alerts Reports"]:::out classDef src fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef layer fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef store fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef out fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Sampling Theorem Visualization (Nyquist Zones) ```mermaid graph LR A["Continuous
Analog Signal
fm in Hz"]:::ana --> B["Anti Aliasing
Low Pass Filter
cutoff equals fm"]:::filter B --> C["ADC Sampler
fs greater than 2fm"]:::adc C --> D["Digital
Discrete Samples
stream of bits"]:::dig classDef ana fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef filter fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef dig fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The four colored levels in the acquisition pipeline represent the **four distinct data types** (structured, time-series, unstructured imaging, semi-structured) that emerge simultaneously from the same patient — illustrating why modern healthcare systems use **polyglot persistence** (multiple database types side by side). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Differentiate between structured and unstructured data in a biomedical context. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Structured Data:** Has a predefined, fixed schema and is stored in tabular rows and columns. It is easily searchable using SQL. **Example:** Patient demographic table (Patient_ID, Name, Age, Gender, Blood_Group) or lab results (Hb, Glucose, Cholesterol) in a Hospital Information System. - **Unstructured Data:** Has no fixed schema or format. It requires specialized tools (DICOM viewers, NLP) to interpret. **Example:** A **CT/MRI scan in DICOM format** or a doctor's free-text clinical notes. - **Key Difference:** Structured data is *machine-readable* directly; unstructured data needs pre-processing and feature extraction before analytics. > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **What is time-series data? Why is ECG considered time-series data?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Time-series data** is a sequence of data points indexed (ordered) in time, sampled at regular intervals, where each sample depends on previous values. - ECG is time-series because: 1. ECG amplitude is **sampled at fixed time intervals** (typically 500 Hz). 2. The **order of samples** is critical — flipping them destroys the QRS complex. 3. The signal is **continuous and periodic** with the heart's electrical cycle. 4. It requires **temporal analytics** (FFT, wavelet) rather than static statistical tests. --- ### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### **Part (a)** — Classify biomedical data into different types with examples for each. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** Biomedical data is classified along three axes: **1. By Structure (3 marks)** - **Structured Data** → Patient demographics, lab values. Stored in SQL tables. - **Unstructured Data** → MRI/CT images, clinical notes, audio stethoscope recordings. Stored in PACS / object stores. - **Semi-Structured Data** → HL7 v2 messages, FHIR JSON resources, IoT MQTT payloads. Stored in document DBs. **2. By Quantitative Nature (2 marks)** - **Quantitative** → HR (bpm), BP (mmHg), Temperature (°C). - **Qualitative** → Blood group (A/B/AB/O), Pain score (0–10). **3. By Temporal Behavior (2 marks)** - **Time-Series** → ECG, EEG, continuous glucose monitor. - **Image / Volumetric** → CT slices, MRI volumes, X-rays (DICOM). - **Streaming** → ICU ventilator waveforms, wearable streams. - **Static / Batch** → Annual lab summaries, archived claims. #### **Part (b)** — A clinical ECG monitor samples at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution across 8 leads. The recording runs for 30 minutes. Calculate the (i) data rate in kbps, (ii) total data generated in MB. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $f_s = 500$ Hz - $n = 8$ leads - $b = 12$ bits/sample - $t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$ **Step 1 — Data rate:** $$R = f_s \times n \times b = 500 \times 8 \times 12 = 48{,}000\ \text{bits/s}$$ $$R = 48\ \text{kbps}$$ **Step 2 — Total data in bits:** $$S = R \times t = 48{,}000 \times 1800 = 86{,}400{,}000\ \text{bits}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to MB (using $1\ \text{MB} = 8 \times 10^6\ \text{bits}$):** $$S_{MB} = \frac{86{,}400{,}000}{8 \times 10^6} = 10.8\ \text{MB}$$ **Final Answer:** Data rate = **48 kbps**, Total data ≈ **10.8 MB**. --- > **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a)** — Explain the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. A patient’s heart rate can go up to 180 bpm. Determine the minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Theorem Statement (3 marks):** The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a **band-limited analog signal** with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \ge 2 f_m$$ where $2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. If $f_s < 2 f_m$, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency content folds into the lower band, distorting the signal irreversibly. **Step 1 — Convert max heart rate to Hz (2 marks):** $$f_m = \frac{HR_{max}}{60} = \frac{180}{60} = 3\ \text{Hz}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Nyquist criterion (2 marks):** $$f_{s,min} = 2 \times 3 = 6\ \text{Hz}$$ **Final Answer:** Minimum sampling frequency = **6 Hz**. (In practice, ECG is oversampled at 250–500 Hz to preserve waveform morphology.) #### **Part (b)** — Compare structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data with suitable biomedical examples. Which storage technology suits each? (7 marks) **Model Solution:** | Feature | Structured | Semi-Structured | Unstructured | |---|---|---|---| | **Schema** | Fixed, predefined | Flexible, self-describing tags | No schema | | **Format** | Tables (rows/columns) | JSON, XML, HL7, FHIR | DICOM, JPEG, WAV, TXT | | **Biomedical Example** | Lab report, patient demographics | HL7 admission message, IoT pulse-oximeter JSON | MRI scan (DICOM), doctor's dictated notes | | **Storage** | SQL / RDBMS (PostgreSQL) | Document DB (MongoDB), FHIR server | Object store (S3), PACS, NoSQL | | **Query Language** | SQL | XQuery / JSONPath | Custom parsers, NLP, DICOM viewers | | **Analytics** | Statistical SQL queries | JSON parsing + ETL | Deep Learning (CNNs for images, NLP for notes) | **Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern healthcare systems use a **polyglot persistence** approach — multiple storage technologies side-by-side — because no single format can efficiently handle all biomedical data types. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes/seconds** → loses 1–2 marks. Always write $t = 30 \times 60 = 1800$ s explicitly. > 2. **Mixing bits and bytes** → use $1\ \text{MB} = 8 \times 10^6$ bits (decimal) OR $8 \times 2^{20}$ (binary). State which you used. > 3. **Confusing channels and bits/sample** → leads = channels ($n$); ADC resolution = bits ($b$). They are different. > 4. **Calling ECG "discrete" without justifying the sample rate** → examiners expect you to mention **Nyquist + oversampling**. > 5. **Writing `\|x\|` inside a markdown table** → table parser breaks. Use `\vert x \vert`. > 6. **Skipping the "Why"** → always state *why* a data type needs a particular storage (e.g., DICOM for PACS, FHIR for interoperability). --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Biomedical data** is classified along **structure**, **quantitative nature**, and **temporal behavior**. - ✅ **Structured data** = SQL / tabular; **Unstructured** = images/notes; **Semi-structured** = JSON/HL7/FHIR. - ✅ **ECG, EEG, EMG** are classic **time-series** data; **CT, MRI, X-ray** are **image/volumetric (DICOM)** data. - ✅ **Quantitative** = numerical (HR, BP); **Qualitative** = categorical (blood group, pain score). - ✅ **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ — minimum sampling rate. - ✅ **Data rate formula:** $R = f_s \times n \times b$ (bits/s). - ✅ **Storage size:** $S = R \times t$; convert to MB using division by $8 \times 10^6$. - ✅ **DICOM standard** = 16-bit, $512 \times 512$ for CT slices. - ✅ **FHIR / HL7** = modern semi-structured standards for IoT–Health interoperability. - ✅ **Genomic data** ≈ 3.2 GB per human genome — drives big-data needs. - ✅ Always state **units explicitly** in derivations — KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous answers. - ✅ In LaTeX, **never use `|` inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid`. - ✅ A typical **12-lead 24-h Holter** generates ≈ **1 GB** raw data → motivates compression. - ✅ Modern hospitals use **polyglot persistence** (SQL + NoSQL + Object store + Time-series DB) to handle all four data types simultaneously.

topics.title: Characteristics of Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Big Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Big Data** refers to extremely large, complex, and high-velocity datasets that cannot be efficiently processed, managed, or analyzed using traditional data-processing tools and database management systems within an acceptable time frame. In the context of IoT and Biomedical Engineering, Big Data is generated continuously from heterogeneous sources such as wearable sensors, electronic health records (EHR), medical imaging devices, genomic sequencers, and remote patient monitoring systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (PCBRT402, Module 4):**
> *"Big Data is a term that describes the large volume of data – both structured and unstructured – that inundates a business or organization on a day-to-day basis. However, it is not the amount of data that is important; it is what organizations do with the data that matters."*

The **characteristics of Big Data** are universally recognized through the **"V's Framework"**, originally proposed by **Doug Laney (2001)** as the 3 V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety), and later expanded by IBM, Microsoft, and other major technology corporations to include **Veracity, Value, Variability, Visualization,** and **Vagueness** depending on the application domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Mandate for KTU 2024:**
> For PCBRT402 (IoT & Biomedical Applications), the examiner expects students to correlate every characteristic of Big Data with a **biomedical/IoT use-case**. Pure generic definitions without biomedical context WILL lead to mark deduction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **tertiary-care super-specialty hospital** with 5,000 beds:
- Every ICU bed generates **~1,000 data points per second** from ventilators, ECG, SpO₂, and invasive pressure monitors.
- Every patient produces ~**50 MB of imaging data per day** (CT, MRI, X-ray, Ultrasound).
- Genomic sequencing of a single patient produces ~**200 GB** of raw data.
- Wearable devices of discharged patients push data via cloud APIs **24/7**.

This is exactly the situation in which the hospital's traditional SQL-based hospital information system (HIS) **collapses** — it was never designed to handle this scale, speed, and heterogeneity of data. This is what we call **Big Data**, and the specific properties of this data are called its **characteristics**.

## 1.3 The Expanded V-Framework (KTU High-Yield View)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following six primary characteristics of Big Data, each of which is explained in detail with a biomedical correlate in the subsequent sections.

| S.No. | Characteristic | One-line Definition | Biomedical Correlate |
|:-----:|:---------------|:--------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Volume** | Scale/quantity of data | Petabytes of imaging archives |
| 2 | **Velocity** | Speed of generation & processing | Real-time ECG streams |
| 3 | **Variety** | Diversity of data types/genres | EHR + DICOM + Genomics |
| 4 | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness/quality | Noisy sensor data |
| 5 | **Value** | Useful insights extracted | Disease pattern discovery |
| 6 | **Variability** | Inconsistency over time | Variable patient vitals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** A student can recall the 6 V's using the mnemonic **"V²V²V"** → **Vo**lume, **Ve**locity, **Va**riety, **Ver**acity, **Va**lue, **Va**riability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Explanation of the 6 Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Volume — The Scale of Data

Volume refers to the **sheer magnitude of data** generated, stored, and processed. Modern healthcare ecosystems produce data at scales unimaginable two decades ago.

- **Typical Scale:** **Terabytes (TB) → Petabytes (PB) → Exabytes (EB) → Zettabytes (ZB)**.
- **Biomedical Source Examples:**
  - A single Whole Genome Sequence (WGS) ≈ **200 GB**
  - A 3D MRI scan ≈ **150 MB – 1 GB**
  - Continuous ICU monitoring of 500 beds for 1 year ≈ **15 PB**
  - Smart wearable (Fitbit, Apple Watch) ecosystem: globally ≈ **28 PB/day** by 2025.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quantitative Benchmark:**
> $1 \text{ PB} = 10^{15} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}000 \text{ TB}$
> $1 \text{ EB} = 10^{18} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}000 \text{ PB}$

---

### 2.1.2 Velocity — The Speed of Data

Velocity measures the **rate at which data is generated, captured, and must be processed** to enable timely decision-making.

- **Three Speeds of Velocity:**
  1. **Batch Velocity** — Periodic uploads (e.g., nightly EHR sync)
  2. **Near Real-Time** — Few-second latency (e.g., ambulance telemetry)
  3. **Real-Time / Streaming** — Millisecond latency (e.g., ICU alarms, robotic surgery)
- **Biomedical Example:** A defibrillator's ECG analyzer requires processing in **< 10 ms** to detect Ventricular Fibrillation and deliver a shock.

---

### 2.1.3 Variety — The Diversity of Data Types

Variety captures the **heterogeneity of data formats and sources**. Data can be broadly classified into three categories:

1. **Structured Data** — Has a predefined schema. Example: Lab reports, billing records, HL7 messages.
2. **Semi-Structured Data** — Partially organized. Example: JSON/XML from IoT devices, DICOM headers.
3. **Unstructured Data** — No predefined model. Example: Free-text clinical notes, MRI images, doctor dictation audio, histopathology slide images.

> [!NOTE]
> **Healthcare Insight:** Approximately **80% of healthcare data is unstructured** (IBM Watson Health, 2020). This is the primary reason traditional relational databases fail in modern hospitals.

---

### 2.1.4 Veracity — The Trustworthiness of Data

Veracity addresses the **quality, accuracy, and reliability** of data. In IoT biomedical systems, veracity is critical because incorrect data leads to wrong clinical decisions.

- **Sources of Data Dirtiness in Healthcare:**
  - Sensor noise (motion artifacts in wearables)
  - Calibration drift in glucometers
  - Misentered data by overworked clinical staff
  - Missing values (patient removed the wearable)
- **Statistical Measure:** Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), Confidence Intervals, Data Provenance scores.

---

### 2.1.5 Value — The Worth Extracted

Value is the **most important characteristic** — it represents the actionable insights and business/clinical worth extracted from raw data. Massive volumes of data are useless unless they can be transformed into decisions.

- **Value Realization Pipeline:**
  `Raw Data → Cleaned Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom → Actionable Value`
- **Biomedical Examples of Value:**
  - Predicting sepsis 6 hours before clinical onset (using EHR + vitals streams)
  - Drug discovery through genomic pattern mining
  - Population health analytics for epidemic prediction

---

### 2.1.6 Variability — The Inconsistency Over Time

Variability refers to the **changes in data meaning, rate, or structure over time**, often confused with Variety but distinct.

- **Biomedical Example:**
  - Heart rate variability (HRV) — a real physiological phenomenon, but also a data characteristic.
  - Seasonal flu data follows a cyclical pattern.
  - Hospital admission rates vary by weekday/weekend (data inflow is non-uniform).
  - The same ICD-10 code may have different interpretations across hospital departments.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Boundary | Application |
|:-:|:--------|:-----------------|:----------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Data Volume** | $V = N \times s$ | Bytes (B) | Total storage = Records × Size per record |
| 2 | **Exponential Data Growth** | $D(t) = D_0 \cdot e^{rt}$ | Bytes at time $t$ | $D_0$ = Initial data, $r$ = growth rate, $t$ = years |
| 3 | **Throughput (Velocity)** | $\theta = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta t}$ | Bytes per second (B/s) | Required processing bandwidth |
| 4 | **Veracity (Accuracy)** | $A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 5 | **Veracity (Precision)** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Value (Information Gain)** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Bits | Entropy reduction from features |
| 7 | **Data Reduction Ratio** | $\rho = 1 - \frac{D_{\text{compressed}}}{D_{\text{original}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $\in [0, 1]$ |
| 8 | **Storage Capacity Required** | $S_{req} = V \times (1 + r_{replication}) \times (1 + r_{overhead})$ | Bytes | With redundancy & metadata |
| 9 | **Data Variety Index** | $V_{idx} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits (Shannon Entropy) | Measures heterogeneity |
| 10 | **Velocity Latency** | $L = t_{\text{process}} - t_{\text{arrive}}$ | Seconds (s) | End-to-end delay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **units** in the final answer line. Examiner allot marks specifically for unit mention in physics/engineering problems.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Big Data Characteristics |
|:------|:----------------------------------------|
| **Precision Medicine** | Volume + Value → Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) |
| **Epidemic Surveillance** | Velocity + Variety → Real-time multi-source pandemic dashboards |
| **Smart Hospitals** | Volume + Veracity → Fault-tolerant clinical decision support |
| **Robotic Surgery** | Velocity + Variability → Millisecond feedback loops |
| **Pharma R&D** | Value + Variety → Cross-domain drug repurposing databases |
| **Insurance & Billing** | Variety + Veracity → Fraud detection in claims |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations establish the quantitative foundations required by the KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on the characteristics of Big Data. Each step is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Exponential Data Growth Model (Volume Characteristic)

The most common model for the **Volume** characteristic of Big Data is the **exponential growth model**, frequently used in healthcare data projection.

### Step 1: Assumption
We assume data accumulates at a rate proportional to its current size, similar to compound interest. This is mathematically expressed as:

$$\frac{dD(t)}{dt} = r \cdot D(t)$$

where $D(t)$ is the data volume at time $t$, and $r$ is the **continuous growth rate** (per year).

### Step 2: Separation of Variables
We separate variables to integrate both sides:

$$\frac{dD(t)}{D(t)} = r \cdot dt$$

### Step 3: Integration
Integrating both sides from initial data $D_0$ at $t = 0$ to $D(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{D_0}^{D(t)} \frac{dD}{D} = \int_{0}^{t} r \, dt$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integrals
The left side gives the natural logarithm, and the right side gives a linear term:

$$\ln D(t) - \ln D_0 = r \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{D(t)}{D_0}\right) = r \cdot t$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate Both Sides
Exponentiating eliminates the logarithm:

$$\frac{D(t)}{D_0} = e^{r \cdot t}$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form
Rearranging for $D(t)$:

$$\boxed{D(t) = D_0 \cdot e^{r \cdot t}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is the fundamental equation showing why healthcare Big Data grows so explosively. A typical hospital data warehouse with $D_0 = 5 \text{ TB}$ and $r = 0.40$ (40% annual growth) becomes:

$$D(5) = 5 \cdot e^{0.40 \times 5} = 5 \cdot e^{2.0} = 5 \cdot 7.389 \approx 36.94 \text{ TB in 5 years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Reference:** The **doubling time** of Big Data can be obtained by setting $D(t) = 2D_0$, giving $t_{\text{double}} = \frac{\ln 2}{r} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Veracity via Classification Accuracy

For the **Veracity** characteristic, the most rigorous metric is the **Accuracy** of a binary classification (e.g., disease vs. healthy), derived from the confusion matrix.

### Step 1: Define Confusion Matrix Components
Let:
- $TP$ = True Positives (correctly identified sick patients)
- $TN$ = True Negatives (correctly identified healthy patients)
- $FP$ = False Positives (false alarms — healthy classified as sick)
- $FN$ = False Negatives (missed diagnoses — sick classified as healthy)

### Step 2: Total Number of Predictions
The total number of samples evaluated is:

$$N_{total} = TP + TN + FP + FN$$

### Step 3: Definition of Accuracy
Accuracy is the ratio of **correct** predictions to the total number of predictions:

$$A = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

### Step 4: Boundary Conditions
- **Maximum Accuracy:** $A = 1$ when $FP = FN = 0$ (perfect classifier).
- **Minimum Accuracy:** $A = 0$ when $TP = TN = 0$ (everything wrong).
- **Random Baseline (binary):** $A = 0.5$ (no information gain).

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}, \quad 0 \le A \le 1}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** In clinical AI, accuracy alone is misleading for **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., a rare disease where 99.9% of patients are healthy). Therefore, the **F1-Score** is also used:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}, \quad \text{where} \quad P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Throughput for Velocity

The **Velocity** characteristic is quantitatively measured by **throughput** ($\theta$), the rate of data processing.

### Step 1: Define Throughput
Throughput is the volume of data successfully processed per unit time:

$$\theta = \frac{D_{processed}}{t_{elapsed}}$$

### Step 2: Streaming Variant
For real-time IoT biomedical streams (e.g., ECG), throughput must exceed the **arrival rate** to prevent buffer overflow:

$$\theta_{required} \ge \lambda_{arrival}$$

### Step 3: Buffer Occupancy (Little's Law Applied)
The average number of items in the processing buffer is:

$$Q_{avg} = \lambda_{arrival} \cdot W_{avg}$$

where $W_{avg}$ is the average waiting time in the buffer.

### Step 4: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta t} \text{ (Bytes/second)}, \quad Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Link:** Velocity is the most operationally important V because in **emergency medicine** (stroke, cardiac arrest), a delay of even **5 seconds** in alert generation can be fatal. This is the "Why" behind Velocity being graded more heavily in 2024 scheme questions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inter-V Correlations

The 6 V's are **not independent**; they form a tightly coupled system. The following analytical matrix (using a 1–5 relative scale) helps students remember correlations:

| V's Paired | Correlation Strength | Biomedical Implication |
|:-----------|:--------------------:|:-----------------------|
| Volume ↔ Variety | **5/5** (Strong) | More sources ⇒ more formats ⇒ more volume |
| Volume ↔ Velocity | **4/5** (Strong) | Modern systems generate AND transmit faster |
| Velocity ↔ Veracity | **4/5** (Strong) | High-speed streams often have noisy/incomplete data |
| Variety ↔ Veracity | **5/5** (Strong) | Unstructured data is harder to validate |
| Volume ↔ Value | **3/5** (Moderate) | More data ≠ more value; value depends on processing |
| Value ↔ Variability | **3/5** (Moderate) | Inconsistent data reduces extracted value |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** A common 14-mark question in 2024 Scheme asks students to **draw this correlation matrix** and justify at least two pairs with biomedical examples. Memorize this table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

Arrows represent data flow and influence.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Big Data Processing Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight:** This 5-layer architecture (Ingestion → Storage → Processing → Analytics → Action) is the **de-facto industry standard** for any Big Data system. KTU 2024 may ask students to map each "V" to one or more of these layers. For example:
> - **Volume** → Storage layer
> - **Velocity** → Ingestion + Processing layers
> - **Veracity** → Analytics layer (data cleaning)
> - **Value** → Action layer

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mapping Each V to a Biomedical Quantity

| V Characteristic | Quantitative Metric (KTU-preferred) | Typical Biomedical Range |
|:-----------------|:-------------------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Volume** | $\text{TB/day}$ | $0.1 - 50$ TB/day for a hospital |
| **Velocity** | $\text{ms latency}$ | $1 - 100$ ms for ICU systems |
| **Variety** | Number of data formats | $5 - 20$ formats in a smart hospital |
| **Veracity** | Accuracy $A$ | $0.85 - 0.99$ for clinical AI |
| **Value** | Cost saved / QALYs gained | Variable |
| **Variability** | Std. deviation of arrival rate | $0.1 \lambda - 0.5\lambda$ |
