# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six characteristics of Big Data. Give one biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The six primary characteristics of Big Data are:

1. **Volume** — The sheer scale of data. *Biomedical Example:* A single Whole Genome Sequence produces ~200 GB of raw data; a tertiary hospital generates petabytes of imaging and EHR data per year.

2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is generated and must be processed. *Biomedical Example:* ICU ventilators and ECG machines stream data in **real-time** with sub-second latency requirements for alarm generation.

3. **Variety** — The diversity of data types and formats. *Biomedical Example:* Hospital data includes structured HL7 messages, semi-structured DICOM images, and unstructured free-text clinical notes.

4. **Veracity** — The trustworthiness and quality of data. *Biomedical Example:* Wearable heart-rate sensors suffer from motion artifacts, requiring signal-cleaning algorithms before clinical use.

5. **Value** — The useful insights that can be extracted. *Biomedical Example:* Mining millions of EHR records has enabled early prediction of sepsis 6–12 hours before clinical onset.

6. **Variability** — Inconsistency in data rate or meaning over time. *Biomedical Example:* Hospital patient inflow, and consequently data inflow, varies significantly between weekdays, weekends, and seasonal flu periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Pattern:** 0.5 marks per characteristic + 0.5 marks per example = **3 marks total**. Always write examples — definitions alone get partial credit only.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data with biomedical examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Structured Data | Semi-Structured Data | Unstructured Data |
|:-------|:----------------|:---------------------|:-------------------|
| **Schema** | Predefined, rigid | Partial / flexible | None |
| **Storage Format** | RDBMS tables (SQL) | JSON, XML, DICOM headers | Images, audio, free text |
| **Biomedical Example** | Lab results in a MySQL table | HL7 FHIR JSON messages from wearable | MRI images, pathology slide scans, doctor's dictation |
| **Proportion in Hospitals** | ~10% | ~10% | ~80% |
| **Query Method** | SQL queries | XPath / JSONPath | NLP / Computer Vision |

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Exam Shortcut:** *Structured = SQL; Semi = Tags (XML/JSON); Unstructured = Human Media.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions offer **internal choice**. You must answer **either OR** pattern. Below are TWO complete alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, PCBRT402]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six characteristics of Big Data** with relevant examples from the IoT and biomedical domain. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hospital information system stores patient records in a centralized data lake. The initial data volume is **$D_0 = 8 \text{ TB}$**, and the data grows at a continuous rate of **$r = 0.35$ per year**.
   - (i) Derive the exponential growth model $D(t) = D_0 e^{rt}$. *(3 marks)*
   - (ii) Calculate the data volume after **6 years**. *(2 marks)*
   - (iii) Estimate the **doubling time** of the hospital's data. *(2 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Each characteristic carries approximately **1 mark**, with biomedical example carrying **0.2 marks** within that.

1. **Volume (1.0 mark):** Volume refers to the magnitude of data. In IoT-biomedical systems, this includes imaging archives (PACS systems often exceed petabytes), genomic sequences (~200 GB per patient), and 24×7 ICU telemetry streams. Modern hospitals routinely exceed **1 PB** of cumulative data.

2. **Velocity (1.2 marks):** Velocity is the rate at which data is generated and processed. Three tiers exist — batch, near real-time, and real-time streaming. *Biomedical example:* A defibrillator must analyze ECG waveforms within **10 ms** to detect lethal arrhythmias; robotic surgery requires sub-millisecond feedback.

3. **Variety (1.2 marks):** Variety captures the heterogeneity of data — structured (lab reports, billing), semi-structured (DICOM headers, HL7 messages, JSON from wearables), and unstructured (MRI images, clinical notes, audio dictation). About **80% of healthcare data is unstructured** (IBM Watson Health).

4. **Veracity (1.0 mark):** Veracity is the trustworthiness and quality of data. Biomedical sources introduce noise through sensor motion artifacts, calibration drift, and human entry errors. A noisy SpO₂ reading can falsely trigger an alarm, causing alarm fatigue in nurses.

5. **Value (1.3 marks):** Value is the actionable insight extracted. Without analytics, Big Data is a cost center. *Examples:* Early sepsis prediction (saving ~\$8 billion/year in US ICUs), drug repurposing (e.g., identifying baricitinib for COVID-19), and population-level epidemic forecasting.

6. **Variability (1.0 mark):** Variability refers to inconsistencies over time — seasonal, daily, or contextual. *Example:* Same ICD-10 code for "chest pain" may mean angina in cardiology and gastroesophageal reflux in gastroenterology, leading to data interpretation challenges.

7. **(Bonus 0.3 marks)** for a concluding remark on how all six V's are interlinked and the absence of any one breaks the Big Data pipeline.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the rate equation:

$$\frac{dD(t)}{dt} = r \cdot D(t)$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dD}{D} = r \, dt$$

Integrating from $D_0$ to $D(t)$ and $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{D_0}^{D(t)} \frac{dD}{D} = \int_0^t r \, dt$$

$$\ln D(t) - \ln D_0 = r \cdot t$$

Exponentiating:

$$D(t) = D_0 \cdot e^{r \cdot t} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(ii) Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given $D_0 = 8$ TB, $r = 0.35$ /year, $t = 6$ years:

$$D(6) = 8 \cdot e^{0.35 \times 6} = 8 \cdot e^{2.1}$$

Computing the exponential:

$$e^{2.1} \approx 8.166$$

Therefore:

$$D(6) = 8 \times 8.166 \approx 65.33 \text{ TB}$$

**The hospital will have approximately 65.33 TB of data after 6 years.**

**(iii) Doubling Time (2 marks):**

Doubling time is when $D(t) = 2 D_0$:

$$2 D_0 = D_0 \cdot e^{r t_d} \implies 2 = e^{r t_d}$$

$$t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{r} = \frac{0.6931}{0.35} \approx 1.98 \text{ years}$$

**The hospital's data doubles approximately every 1.98 years (~2 years).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCBRT402]**

**(a)** Discuss how the characteristics of Big Data — **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability** — apply specifically to a **smart hospital** ecosystem. Include the role of the **Hadoop ecosystem** in addressing these characteristics. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wearable ECG monitor produces data with the following confusion matrix after a 6-month clinical trial:
   - $TP = 850$, $TN = 9200$, $FP = 120$, $FN = 80$.
   - (i) Calculate the **Accuracy**. *(2 marks)*
   - (ii) Calculate the **Precision** and **Recall**. *(2 marks)*
   - (iii) Compute the **F1-Score** and interpret its clinical significance. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A **smart hospital** is an interconnected ecosystem of IoT devices, EHRs, imaging systems, and analytics platforms. The Big Data characteristics manifest as follows:

1. **Volume in Smart Hospitals:** A 1000-bed smart hospital with full IoT integration generates **5–10 TB of data per day**, accumulating to petabytes annually. The Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) addresses this by distributing data across commodity hardware clusters.

2. **Velocity in Smart Hospitals:** Continuous monitoring requires real-time processing. **Apache Kafka** and **Apache Storm/Flink** provide streaming analytics, while **MapReduce** handles batch processing. ICU alarms require millisecond-level response.

3. **Variety in Smart Hospitals:** Data arrives as HL7 messages, DICOM images, JSON streams, and free text. **NoSQL databases** (HBase, MongoDB, Cassandra) within the Hadoop ecosystem natively handle this variety, unlike rigid SQL systems.

4. **Veracity in Smart Hospitals:** Sensor noise, missing values, and inconsistent coding are mitigated by data cleaning tools like **Apache Pig** and **Hive** along with **machine learning-based imputation** in the analytics stage.

5. **Value in Smart Hospitals:** The end goal — predictive alerts, clinical decision support, and operational efficiency. **Apache Mahout** and **Spark MLlib** provide machine learning libraries for value extraction.

6. **Variability in Smart Hospitals:** Patient inflow varies diurnally and seasonally. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** dynamically allocates cluster resources to handle load variability.

**Hadoop Ecosystem Summary (1 mark bonus):**
- **HDFS** — Storage (Volume)
- **MapReduce / Spark** — Processing (Velocity)
- **HBase / Cassandra** — NoSQL Variety
- **Hive / Pig** — Data Quality (Veracity)
- **Mahout / Spark MLlib** — Analytics (Value)
- **YARN** — Resource Management (Variability)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $TP = 850$, $TN = 9200$, $FP = 120$, $FN = 80$.

**Total samples:** $N = 850 + 9200 + 120 + 80 = 10{,}250$

**(i) Accuracy (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{850 + 9200}{10{,}250} = \frac{10{,}050}{10{,}250} \approx 0.9805$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 98.05\%}$$

**(ii) Precision and Recall (2 marks):**

$$\text{Precision (P)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{850}{850 + 120} = \frac{850}{970} \approx 0.8763$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 87.63\%}$$

$$\text{Recall (R)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{850}{850 + 80} = \frac{850}{930} \approx 0.9139$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 91.39\%}$$

**(iii) F1-Score and Interpretation (3 marks):**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8763 \times 0.9139}{0.8763 + 0.9139}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8010}{1.7902} = 2 \cdot 0.4474 \approx 0.8949$$

$$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.895 \text{ or } 89.5\%}$$

**Clinical Interpretation:**
The F1-score of 0.895 indicates a **strong balance** between precision and recall for the wearable ECG monitor. Clinically:
- The **recall of 91.39%** means the device correctly identifies ~91% of true arrhythmia events — important since **missing a true event (FN)** can be life-threatening.
- The **precision of 87.63%** means when the device alerts, it is correct ~88% of the time, which is acceptable to minimize **alarm fatigue** in patients and clinicians.
- The F1-score confirms the device is **clinically reliable** for continuous ambulatory monitoring, though it may be supplemented with physician review for borderline cases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **DO NOT confuse** Variety with Variability — they are distinct. Variety = different formats; Variability = inconsistency over time.
> 2. **Always write the unit** in derivation answers (e.g., "TB", "ms", "Bytes/s") — losing 0.5–1 mark is common otherwise.
> 3. In Part B 14-mark questions, examiners check for **biomedical context** in every example. Generic Big Data examples (e.g., social media) will fetch only partial credit.
> 4. **Confusion matrix formulas:** Mixing up $FN$ with $FP$ in the Precision formula is the most common error. Memorize: *Precision is about positives, Recall is about actuals.*
> 5. **No hand-waving in derivations** — every algebraic step must be shown. Even if the final answer is correct, intermediate steps carry 50% of the marks.
> 6. **Do not use Velocity and Veracity interchangeably** — Velocity is *speed*, Veracity is *truth*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following 12 points constitute the minimum required for a KTU 2024 full-score attempt on this topic:**

- ☐ **Big Data** is data whose Volume, Velocity, and Variety exceed the capacity of traditional database systems.
- ☐ The **6 V's** are: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability** (memorize the order).
- ☐ **Volume** is measured in TB/PB/EB; the growth model is **$D(t) = D_0 e^{rt}$**.
- ☐ **Velocity** has three tiers: batch, near real-time, real-time/streaming.
- ☐ **Variety** includes structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data; **~80% of healthcare data is unstructured**.
- ☐ **Veracity** is measured quantitatively via **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**.
- ☐ **Value** is the most important V — without it, data is just a storage cost.
- ☐ **Variability** ≠ Variety; variability is about inconsistency, variety is about diversity.
- ☐ The **Hadoop ecosystem** (HDFS, MapReduce, HBase, Hive, Pig, YARN, Mahout) addresses all 6 V's.
- ☐ **Biomedical examples are mandatory** — generic examples get only partial marks.
- ☐ **Doubling time** formula: $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{r} \approx \frac{0.693}{r}$.
- ☐ Always state the **unit** in the final answer of any numerical derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (exam hall):**
> *"VVVVVV"* → *Very Valuable Variables Verify* the health data's *Veracity* and *Variability*.
> Or: *Volume, Velocity, Variety — the original 3 V's — were expanded with Veracity, Value, Variability for healthcare.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Handling Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Handling Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Handling Technologies** in IoT refer to the comprehensive set of techniques, architectures, and computational tools used to **acquire, transmit, store, process, analyze, secure, and visualize** the enormous volumes of heterogeneous data generated by interconnected IoT devices and biomedical sensors throughout the data lifecycle.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, IoT data handling is not a single operation but a **multi-stage pipeline** that transforms raw sensor signals (e.g., ECG waveforms, body temperature, motion data) into actionable knowledge for healthcare professionals, smart systems, and automated decision-making engines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Diagnostic Lab

> [!NOTE]
> Think of IoT data handling like a **modern hospital diagnostic laboratory chain**:
>
> - **Blood samples (raw IoT sensor data)** arrive continuously from patients (IoT devices).
> - **Reception desk (Data Acquisition layer)** registers and labels every sample with a unique ID and timestamp.
> - **Pre-analytical room (Data Preprocessing)** centrifuges, dilutes, and cleans the samples — removing noise and inconsistencies.
> - **Storage vault (Data Storage layer)** preserves prepared samples in refrigerators, freezers, or cloud archives.
> - **Pathologist (Analytics engine)** examines the sample, runs ML models, and produces a diagnosis.
> - **Doctor's report (Data Visualization)** translates complex pathology results into a simple chart the patient can read.
> - **Hospital network (Networking layer)** ensures the sample moves between stations without contamination or loss.
>
> Just as a lab cannot function with only one station, IoT data handling requires **all stages working in harmony**.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of IoT Data

| Property | Description | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Terabytes to Petabytes per day | Continuous ECG from 10,000 ICU patients |
| **Velocity** | Real-time or near real-time generation | Stream of SpO₂ readings at 100 Hz |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | MRI images, JSON vitals, video feeds |
| **Veracity** | Uncertainty and noise levels | Motion artifacts in wearable ECG |
| **Value** | Hidden insights requiring extraction | Predicting cardiac arrest 6 hrs early |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**5 V's of Big Data**" are an essential KTU high-yield point — examiners frequently ask students to list and explain them with biomedical examples.

### 1.4 Core Subsystems of Data Handling

1. **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** — sampling sensors, ADC conversion, timestamping.
2. **Data Transmission** — wired/wireless protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, BLE).
3. **Data Preprocessing** — cleaning, filtering, normalization, feature extraction.
4. **Data Storage** — local (edge), distributed (fog), and centralized (cloud) repositories.
5. **Data Processing** — batch, stream, and hybrid paradigms.
6. **Data Analytics** — descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive.
7. **Data Visualization** — dashboards, charts, alerts, AR/VR overlays.
8. **Data Security & Governance** — encryption, anonymization, regulatory compliance (HIPAA, GDPR).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Data Lifecycle — Operational Breakdown

The IoT data lifecycle can be broken into **seven sequential logical steps**:

1. **Generation** — Sensors (e.g., PPG, ECG, temperature, accelerometer) convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
2. **Acquisition** — Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs) sample the analog signal at a defined frequency.
3. **Preprocessing** — Noise removal (Kalman, Moving Average, Butterworth filters), outlier detection, missing-value imputation.
4. **Transmission** — Encoded packets travel through MQTT brokers, CoAP servers, or HTTP gateways.
5. **Storage** — Data is persisted in Time-Series Databases (TSDB), NoSQL stores, Data Lakes, or Cloud warehouses.
6. **Processing & Analytics** — Batch jobs (Hadoop, Spark) or stream processors (Apache Flink, Kafka Streams) extract insights.
7. **Visualization & Action** — Dashboards (Grafana, Tableau), alerts, automated actuator responses.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield formula, threshold, and rule required to solve KTU 2024 numerical and conceptual questions on Data Handling.

| **#** | **Formula / Rule** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ in Hz, $f_{max}$ is highest signal frequency |
| 2 | **Quantization Step Size** | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $V_{FS}$ full-scale voltage, $n$ = ADC bit depth |
| 3 | **Quantization SNR (Theoretical)** | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | $n$ = number of bits (dB) |
| 4 | **Data Rate of Single Channel** | $R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c$ | $c$ = number of channels; bits/sec |
| 5 | **Aggregate IoT Data Rate (N sensors)** | $R_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_{s,i} \cdot n_i \cdot c_i$ | bits/sec (network bottleneck) |
| 6 | **Storage Capacity Required** | $S = R \cdot T_{ret}$ | $T_{ret}$ = retention period |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Higher = better compression |
| 8 | **Space Savings Percentage** | $SS\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \cdot 100$ | Output in % |
| 9 | **Aggregation (Mean)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Reduces N readings to 1 |
| 10 | **Moving Average Filter (Window k)** | $y[t] = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} x[t-j]$ | Smooths temporal noise |
| 11 | **End-to-End Latency** | $L = T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{trans} + T_{prop}$ | Critical for real-time alerts |
| 12 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $TH = \dfrac{W}{L}$ | $W$ = work, $L$ = latency |
| 13 | **MAPE (Prediction Accuracy)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \right\vert \cdot 100$ | Use \vert for absolute value |
| 14 | **HDFS Block Size Constraint** | $B \geq 64$ MB (default 128 MB) | Determines file split count |
| 15 | **Hadoop Replication Factor** | $RF \in \{2, 3\}$ | Default 3 in production |
| 16 | **Cloud Storage Cost** | $C = S_{GB} \cdot P_{GB} \cdot T_{months}$ | $P_{GB}$ = price per GB/month |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Pitfall:** Examiners often test whether students know **why fs must be strictly greater than** $2f_{max}$ (aliasing) and not equal. Memorize the difference between **Nyquist Rate** ($2f_{max}$) and **Nyquist Frequency** ($f_{max}$).

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Pipeline Stage

- **Why preprocess?** — Raw IoT data is dirty (drift, outliers, missing values). Feeding it directly to ML models causes "garbage-in, garbage-out" failures in diagnostic predictions.
- **Why edge storage?** — Latency-critical applications (e.g., defibrillator triggering) cannot tolerate the 50–200 ms cloud round-trip; edge caches cut latency to **< 5 ms**.
- **Why time-series databases?** — Generic RDBMS are inefficient for the append-heavy, timestamp-indexed nature of IoT data. TSDBs (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) achieve **10–100× compression**.
- **Why stream processing for vitals?** — A patient in septic shock can deteriorate in minutes; batch processing (Hadoop) is too slow. **Apache Flink** processes events in milliseconds.
- **Why data lakes for biomedical imaging?** — X-rays, MRIs are unstructured and large (10–500 MB each). A data lake stores raw formats (DICOM) without forced schemas.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Smart ICU Monitoring** — Real-time analytics on streamed vitals predicts patient deterioration 4–6 hours before clinical events (e.g., MoMeKardia, EarlySense).
- **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)** — Wearables (Apple Watch, Fitbit) offload terabytes of PPG/ECG data to the cloud for atrial fibrillation detection.
- **Epidemic Surveillance** — Aggregated IoT data from millions of thermometers and wearables helped model COVID-19 spread (e.g., Kinsa Health's US thermometer network).
- **Smart Hospitals** — RFID-tagged equipment streams location data to asset-tracking platforms, reducing search time by **>70%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1 — Sampling Rate and Storage Calculation

> **[KTU-Style Numerical Problem]**
> A wearable ECG patch samples the cardiac signal at $f_s = 360$ Hz with a 12-bit ADC across 3 leads (channels). If the device operates for 24 hours, calculate:
> (a) The Nyquist Rate and verify if aliasing occurs for a typical QRS complex (assume $f_{max} = 150$ Hz).
> (b) The total data generated per day in **Megabytes (MB)**.
> (c) The required cloud storage cost in INR, given a rate of **₹1.20 per GB-month** and a 1-year retention policy.

**Given:**
- $f_s = 360$ Hz
- $n = 12$ bits
- $c = 3$ channels
- $T = 24 \text{ hours} = 86400 \text{ seconds}$
- $f_{max} = 150$ Hz
- Price $P = ₹1.20$ / GB-month, retention = 12 months

**Solution:**

**(a) Nyquist Rate verification:**

$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

Since $f_s = 360 \text{ Hz} \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$, the **Nyquist criterion is satisfied**, and no aliasing will occur.

**(b) Total data generated per day:**

$$R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c = 360 \cdot 12 \cdot 3 = 12960 \text{ bits/sec}$$

Total bits in 24 hours:

$$S_{bits} = R \cdot T = 12960 \cdot 86400 = 1{,}119{,}744{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

Convert to Megabytes (1 MB = 8 × 10⁶ bits):

$$S_{MB} = \frac{1{,}119{,}744{,}000}{8 \cdot 10^6} = 139.968 \text{ MB} \approx 140 \text{ MB per patient-day}$$

**(c) Cloud storage cost for 1 year:**

Convert to GB: $140 \text{ MB} = 0.140 \text{ GB}$.

Storage for 12 months: $0.140 \text{ GB} \times 12 = 1.68 \text{ GB-month}$.

Cost: $1.68 \cdot 1.20 = ₹2.016$ per patient-year.

> For a hospital with 10,000 patients, the annual cost is only **₹20,160** — demonstrating how cloud storage economics make large-scale RPM feasible.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2 — Quantization SNR and Bit Depth Trade-off

> **[KTU Numerical Problem]**
> An IoT pulse oximeter uses an 8-bit ADC with a full-scale voltage of 3.3 V. Compute:
> (a) The quantization step size $\Delta$.
> (b) The theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio.
> (c) If we upgrade to a 12-bit ADC, by how many dB does the SNR improve?

**Solution:**

**(a) Quantization step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^8} = \frac{3.3}{256} = 0.01289 \text{ V} = 12.89 \text{ mV}$$

**(b) Theoretical SNR for n = 8:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**(c) SNR for n = 12 and improvement:**

$$SNR_{12} = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}$$

$$\Delta SNR = 74.00 - 49.92 = 24.08 \text{ dB}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Each additional bit of resolution provides **~6 dB** of SNR improvement — a 4-bit upgrade here delivers 24 dB ≈ **15.85× better noise rejection**, critical for detecting subtle SpO₂ variations during sleep apnea.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Problem 3 — Compression Ratio Analysis

> **[KTU Numerical Problem]**
> A 24-hour continuous ECG recording produces 1.4 GB of raw data. After applying a wavelet-based compression algorithm, the file size is reduced to 280 MB.
> (a) Calculate the Compression Ratio (CR).
> (b) Calculate the Space Savings percentage.
> (c) If this is deployed across 5,000 patients, how many TB of storage are saved annually?

**Solution:**

**(a) Compression Ratio:**

$$CR = \frac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}} = \frac{1400 \text{ MB}}{280 \text{ MB}} = 5:1$$

**(b) Space Savings:**

$$SS\% = \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \cdot 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{5}\right) \cdot 100 = 80\%$$

**(c) Annual storage saved across 5,000 patients:**

Per patient saved = $1400 - 280 = 1120$ MB = $1.12$ GB.

Total saved = $1.12 \cdot 5000 = 5600$ GB = **5.6 TB per year**.

> **Clinical Note:** Wavelet compression preserves diagnostic QRS morphology — a key KTU conceptual point linking compression to **diagnostic fidelity**.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Problem 4 — Edge Latency vs Cloud Latency

> **[KTU Analytical Problem]**
> A wearable fall-detection system has two deployment options:
> - **Edge:** $T_{proc} = 2$ ms, $T_{queue} = 1$ ms, $T_{trans} = 0$ ms, $T_{prop} = 0$ ms.
> - **Cloud:** $T_{proc} = 5$ ms, $T_{queue} = 8$ ms, $T_{trans} = 3$ ms, $T_{prop} = 50$ ms.
>
> Compare end-to-end latencies and state which is suitable for emergency alerts.

**Solution:**

Edge latency:
$$L_{edge} = 2 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

Cloud latency:
$$L_{cloud} = 5 + 8 + 3 + 50 = 66 \text{ ms}$$

> **Verdict:** Edge processing (3 ms) is **22× faster** than cloud (66 ms). For fall detection, where every 10 ms of delay can mean additional injury, edge is the only acceptable choice. The 66 ms cloud latency could allow a patient to hit the ground before the alert reaches a caregiver.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End IoT Data Handling Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the complete data handling flow, from sensor acquisition to clinical visualization in a biomedical IoT system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Sensor Layer\nECG, SpO2, BP, Temp] --> B[Data Acquisition Module\nADC, Timestamping]
    B --> C{Preprocessing\nRequired?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Preprocessing Engine\nFiltering, Cleaning, Normalization]
    C -- No --> E[Edge Storage\nSQLite, Redis]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Gateway / Edge Node\nRaspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson]
    F --> G[Network Layer\nMQTT, CoAP, HTTPS]
    G --> H[Cloud Ingestion\nKafka, AWS Kinesis]
    H --> I[Data Lake\nS3, HDFS, Azure Blob]
    I --> J[Processing Engine\nSpark, Flink, Hadoop]
    J --> K[Analytics Module\nML, AI, Statistical Models]
    K --> L[Visualization Dashboard\nGrafana, Tableau, Web App]
    L --> M[Clinical Decision\nAlerts to Doctors and Caregivers]
    I --> N[Long-term Archive\nGlacier, Cold Storage]
    K --> O[Regulatory Compliance\nHIPAA, GDPR, HL7]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow is unidirectional with two side branches — one for **long-term archival** (cold storage for legal/audit purposes) and one for **compliance/security** (HIPAA/GDPR audit trails). This is a high-yield schematic commonly drawn in KTU 14-mark answers.

### 4.2 Storage Hierarchy — Edge / Fog / Cloud Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EdgeLayer [EDGE LAYER - On Device]
        E1[Flash Memory]
        E2[SD Card]
        E3[Local SSD]
    end
    subgraph FogLayer [FOG LAYER - Gateway or LAN]
        F1[Local Server]
        F2[Redis Cache]
        F3[InfluxDB Local]
    end
    subgraph CloudLayer [CLOUD LAYER - Data Center]
        C1[Data Lake S3]
        C2[Time Series DB]
        C3[Data Warehouse]
        C4[ML Training Cluster]
    end
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> C1
    C1 --> C4
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F3
    F2 --> C2
    F3 --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Point:**
> - **Edge** = lowest latency, smallest capacity, used for critical decisions.
> - **Fog** = intermediate, used for local aggregation and regional analytics.
> - **Cloud** = largest capacity, used for heavy analytics, ML training, archival.

### 4.3 Stream vs Batch Processing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New IoT Data Arrives] --> Q1{Need Immediate\nAction?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Stream[Stream Processing\nFlink, Kafka Streams]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Data Volume\n>1 TB per Day?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Batch[Batch Processing\nHadoop, Spark]
    Q2 -- No --> Lambda[Lambda Architecture\nStream + Batch Combined]
    Stream --> Out1[Real-time Alerts and Dashboards]
    Batch --> Out2[Daily Reports and Retraining]
    Lambda --> Out3[Hybrid Output Layer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define the 5 V's of Big Data with one biomedical example for each.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The 5 V's characterize the nature of big data in IoT systems:
> 1. **Volume** — Massive scale of data. *Example:* Continuous EEG streams from a neuro-ICU produce ~2 TB/day per patient.
> 2. **Velocity** — Speed of data generation. *Example:* ECG monitors generate samples at 500 Hz requiring millisecond processing.
> 3. **Variety** — Multiple data formats. *Example:* DICOM images, HL7 messages, and CSV vitals coexisting in a hospital.
> 4. **Veracity** — Data trustworthiness. *Example:* Motion artifacts corrupting wearable pulse readings.
> 5. **Value** — Extracting useful insights. *Example:* Predicting sepsis 6 hours before clinical onset from vital streams.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing in the context of IoT data handling.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Parameter | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
> |---|---|---|
> | Location | On/near the IoT device | Remote data center |
> | Latency | < 10 ms | 50 – 200 ms |
> | Storage | Limited (GBs) | Virtually unlimited (PBs) |
> | Processing Power | Low (microcontrollers, SBCs) | High (GPU/TPU clusters) |
> | Use Case | Fall detection, defibrillator trigger | ML model training, epidemiology |
> | Connectivity Dependency | Can work offline | Requires internet |
> | Privacy | High (data stays local) | Lower (data leaves premise) |

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 ESE Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an IoT data handling system** for a smart hospital. List and briefly describe the **five major layers** involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable IoT device samples a biomedical signal at $f_s = 500$ Hz using a 16-bit ADC across 2 channels. Calculate:
- (i) The data rate in **kbps**.
- (ii) The total data generated in **8 hours** in **MB**.
- (iii) The compression ratio required to fit the entire 8-hour recording into a **250 MB** memory card. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a):**
> *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.)* The five major layers are:
> 1. **Perception/Sensor Layer** — Wearable sensors (ECG, SpO₂, temperature, accelerometer) convert physiological signals to electrical signals.
> 2. **Network/Transmission Layer** — MQTT, CoAP, BLE, Wi-Fi transmit data to gateways/brokers.
> 3. **Processing/Edge Layer** — Local preprocessing, filtering, and time-critical decisions (e.g., arrhythmia detection).
> 4. **Cloud/Storage Layer** — Data Lake (S3, HDFS), TSDB (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB), and warehouses.
> 5. **Application/Visualization Layer** — Dashboards (Grafana), ML-based alerts, EMR integration, and mobile apps for doctors.
>
> Cross-cutting concerns: **Security** (TLS, AES), **Compliance** (HIPAA, GDPR), **Device Management** (OTA updates).

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **(i) Data rate in kbps:**
>
> $$R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c = 500 \cdot 16 \cdot 2 = 16000 \text{ bits/sec} = 16 \text{ kbps}$$
>
> **(ii) Total data in 8 hours:**
>
> $$T = 8 \cdot 3600 = 28800 \text{ seconds}$$
>
> $$S_{bits} = 16000 \cdot 28800 = 460{,}800{,}000 \text{ bits}$$
>
> $$S_{MB} = \frac{460{,}800{,}000}{8 \cdot 10^6} = 57.6 \text{ MB}$$
>
> **(iii) Required Compression Ratio:**
>
> $$CR = \frac{S_{original}}{S_{target}} = \frac{57.6}{250} \approx 0.23$$
>
> Since $CR < 1$, no compression is needed — the **57.6 MB** fits comfortably into 250 MB. In fact, the **effective CR is 0.23:1 (expansion needed)** if we use 250 MB, or we can store up to 4.34× the duration (≈34.7 hours) on the same card. *(Examiner-friendly interpretation note.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **three paradigms of data processing** — Batch, Stream, and Hybrid (Lambda/Kappa) — with biomedical IoT examples. Compare their latency, throughput, and complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital's IoT platform receives vitals from 200 ICU patients, each generating 5 KB/s of structured data. Calculate:
- (i) The **aggregate data rate** in **Mbps**.
- (ii) The **daily storage** in **GB**.
- (iii) The **annual cost** if cloud storage is priced at **₹0.80 per GB-month**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **1. Batch Processing:**
> - Data is collected over a period and processed in bulk.
> - Tools: Hadoop MapReduce, Apache Spark (batch mode).
> - Latency: minutes to hours.
> - Biomedical example: Daily aggregation of patient movement logs to generate hospital efficiency reports.
>
> **2. Stream Processing:**
> - Data is processed event-by-event as it arrives.
> - Tools: Apache Flink, Kafka Streams, AWS Kinesis.
> - Latency: milliseconds to seconds.
> - Biomedical example: Real-time ECG arrhythmia detection alerting a nurse within 200 ms of an abnormal beat.
>
> **3. Hybrid (Lambda / Kappa) Architecture:**
> - Combines batch (accuracy) and stream (speed) layers.
> - **Lambda:** Maintains both a batch layer and a speed layer.
> - **Kappa:** Uses a single stream-processing engine that reprocesses from log storage when needed.
> - Biomedical example: Continuous glucose monitoring that triggers immediate hypo/hyper alerts (stream) while nightly recalibrates insulin prediction models (batch).
>
> **Comparison:**
>
> | Aspect | Batch | Stream | Hybrid |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Latency | High (min–hr) | Low (ms–s) | Tunable |
> | Throughput | Very High | Moderate–High | High |
> | Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
> | Cost | Low | Higher | Highest |

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **(i) Aggregate data rate:**
>
> $$R_{agg} = N \cdot R_{per\,patient} = 200 \cdot 5 \text{ KB/s} = 1000 \text{ KB/s} = 1 \text{ MB/s}$$
>
> Convert to Mbps (1 MB/s = 8 Mbps):
>
> $$R_{agg} = 1 \cdot 8 = 8 \text{ Mbps}$$
>
> **(ii) Daily storage in GB:**
>
> $$S_{daily} = 1 \text{ MB/s} \cdot 86400 \text{ s} = 86400 \text{ MB} = 86.4 \text{ GB/day}$$
>
> **(iii) Annual cost:**
>
> Monthly storage = $86.4 \text{ GB/day} \cdot 30 = 2592 \text{ GB-month}$.
> Annual = $2592 \cdot 12 = 31{,}104 \text{ GB-month}$.
> Cost = $31{,}104 \cdot 0.80 = ₹24{,}883.20$ per year.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency.** $f_N = 2f_{max}$ is the rate; $f_{max}$ itself is the frequency. Examiners dock 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions.** Always convert bits ↔ Bytes (÷8) and MB ↔ GB (÷1024 or ÷1000 depending on context; KTU typically uses SI = 10⁶).
> 3. **Missing the bit-depth in data rate calculations.** Students often write $R = f_s \cdot n$ and forget to multiply by number of channels — costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **No diagram in architecture answers.** A 7-mark architecture question without a labeled diagram fetches at most **4 marks**. Always draw a clean block diagram.
> 5. **Vague "5 V's" explanations.** Listing them without biomedical examples is incomplete — examiners expect domain-specific illustrations for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up batch and stream processing use cases.** Applying Hadoop to a real-time alert scenario is a conceptual red flag.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Handling Technologies (Module 4)**

- ☐ **5 V's of Big Data** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — always cite biomedical examples.
- ☐ **Nyquist Theorem** — $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$; Nyquist **Rate** = $2f_{max}$, Nyquist **Frequency** = $f_{max}$.
- ☐ **Data Rate Formula** — $R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c$ — multiply by ALL channels and the bit depth.
- ☐ **Storage** — $S = R \cdot T_{ret}$ — remember to convert to MB/GB using $10^6$ or $2^{20}$ consistently.
- ☐ **SNR Rule** — Each extra bit ≈ 6.02 dB improvement.
- ☐ **Compression Ratio** — $CR = S_{orig}/S_{comp}$; Space Savings = $(1 - 1/CR) \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Edge < Fog < Cloud** — increasing latency, increasing capacity, increasing computational power.
- ☐ **Processing Paradigms** — Batch (Hadoop/Spark) for offline, Stream (Flink/Kafka) for real-time, Lambda/Kappa for hybrid.
- ☐ **Time-Series Databases (TSDB)** — InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, OpenTSDB — preferred for IoT vitals.
- ☐ **Data Lake vs Data Warehouse** — Lake stores raw (DICOM, JSON); Warehouse stores structured, query-optimized.
- ☐ **MQTT vs HTTP vs CoAP** — MQTT is pub-sub, lightweight, ideal for low-bandwidth vitals; HTTP is request-response, heavier; CoAP is UDP-based, suited for constrained devices.
- ☐ **HIPAA & GDPR** — Mandatory for biomedical data; encryption-at-rest and in-transit is non-negotiable.
- ☐ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in 7+ mark architecture answers — it is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
- ☐ **End-to-End Latency** — Sum of processing + queuing + transmission + propagation delays.
- ☐ **Hadoop block size default = 128 MB**, replication factor default = 3 (high-yield trivia).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flow of Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **IoT and Biomedical Applications**, the **Flow of Data** refers to the structured, sequential movement of information — from the point of acquisition (sensors, wearable medical devices, actuators) through intermediate processing layers (gateways, fog/edge nodes) to storage, analytics, and end-user applications. It encompasses the entire data lifecycle: **acquisition → aggregation → transmission → processing → storage → visualization/actuation**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the Flow of Data is governed by:

- **Data Generation Layer** (sensors/actuators in medical IoT)
- **Data Communication Layer** (protocols such as MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, BLE)
- **Data Processing Layer** (edge/fog/cloud computing)
- **Data Management Layer** (databases, time-series stores, cloud buckets)
- **Application Layer** (clinical dashboards, alerts, ML-based diagnostics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Flow of Data is NOT merely "sending bits over a network." It is a *holistic architectural pipeline* that includes data formatting, encryption (HIPAA/GDPR compliance), quality-of-service (QoS), and decision-making at each node.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Circulatory System"

Think of data flow in IoT as the **human circulatory system**:

- The **heart** is the *data generator* (sensors measuring ECG, SpO₂, blood pressure).
- The **arteries** are the *transmission network* (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, 5G).
- The **capillaries** are the *gateways and edge nodes* that filter and pre-process the data.
- The **organs** (brain, liver) are the *cloud and analytics platforms* where meaningful interpretation happens.
- The **motor neurons** are the *actuator feedback* that closes the loop (e.g., an insulin pump responding to glucose data).

Just as blood carries oxygen, nutrients, and waste products, IoT data flow carries **raw sensor values, control commands, alerts, and processed insights** — each with different priorities and QoS requirements.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Ready Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **exact definitions** expected in KTU theory exams. Memorize the underlined keywords.

- **Data Acquisition:** The process of sampling real-world physiological signals (e.g., ECG at **250–500 Hz**, SpO₂ at **1 Hz**) and converting them to digital form.
- **Data Aggregation:** Combining multiple sensor streams at a gateway to reduce bandwidth (e.g., averaging 1000 readings into 1 summary per second).
- **Data Transmission:** The physical and logical movement of bits across communication media.
- **Data Ingestion:** The acceptance of incoming data streams by a processing system (e.g., AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub).
- **Data Latency (τ):** The time delay between data generation at the sensor and its arrival at the destination. Critical for **real-time biomedical monitoring** where τ < **1 second** is often mandatory.
- **Data Throughput (R):** The rate of successful data delivery, measured in **bits per second (bps)**, **kbps**, or **Mbps**.

## 1.4 The 4 V's of Biomedical IoT Data

| Characteristic | Meaning | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Scale of data | A 12-lead ECG at 500 Hz for 24 hours ≈ **259 MB/day/patient** |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation/flow | Continuous EEG at 1 kHz = **thousands of samples/sec** |
| **Variety** | Different formats/protocols | ECG (waveform), SpO₂ (scalar), Accelerometer (vector) |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness/accuracy | Noise, motion artifacts, sensor drift |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Flow Architecture (5-Layer Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes a **5-layer architectural model** for IoT data flow:

### Layer 1 — Perception/Sensing Layer
- **Function:** Acquire raw biomedical signals.
- **Components:** Wearable biosensors (ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG, temperature, glucose).
- **Output:** Analog signals → ADC → Digital samples.
- **KTU Key Point:** Sampling must obey the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency and $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Example:** For ECG with frequency content up to **150 Hz**, minimum sampling rate is **300 Hz**. KTU exams often use **360 Hz** or **500 Hz** as the standard.

### Layer 2 — Network/Communication Layer
- **Function:** Route data from device to gateway to cloud.
- **Protocols:** MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT.
- **KTU Key Point:** Protocol selection is based on **power budget, data rate, and latency requirement**.

### Layer 3 — Middleware/Processing Layer
- **Function:** Filter, compress, encrypt, and route data.
- **Operations:** Edge analytics, noise filtering (Kalman, Moving Average, Wavelet).
- **Example:** A wearable ECG patch detects arrhythmia locally and only sends alerts to the cloud — reducing bandwidth by **~95%**.

### Layer 4 — Application/Service Layer
- **Function:** Deliver processed data to clinicians, patients, or ML systems.
- **Components:** Dashboards, mobile apps, electronic health records (EHR), alert systems.

### Layer 5 — Business/Management Layer
- **Function:** Manage the entire data flow ecosystem — billing, SLA, security, compliance (HIPAA, GDPR, India's DISHA).

## 2.2 Data Flow Models (KTU High-Yield)

There are **four primary data flow models** in IoT:

| Model | Direction | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Device-to-Device (D2D)** | Peer-to-peer | Body Area Networks (BAN) between implants |
| **Device-to-Cloud (D2C)** | Direct to cloud | Fitness trackers → manufacturer cloud |
| **Device-to-Gateway (D2G)** | Device → local gateway | Hospital bedside monitors → ward server |
| **Cloud-to-Device (C2D)** | Reverse direction | Physician sends insulin dosage to pump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Device-to-Gateway-to-Cloud** (D2G2C) is the **most clinically relevant** model in biomedical IoT because it allows local pre-processing (latency reduction) before cloud transmission.

## 2.3 Communication Protocols — KTU Formula Sheet

| Protocol | Transport | Pattern | Header Size | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/HTTPS** | TCP | Request-Response | Large | Cloud APIs, EHR |
| **MQTT** | TCP | Publish-Subscribe | 2 bytes | Low-power IoT, telemetry |
| **CoAP** | UDP | Request-Response | 4 bytes | Constrained devices |
| **AMQP** | TCP | Publish-Subscribe | 8 bytes | Enterprise, hospital systems |
| **DDS** | TCP/UDP | Publish-Subscribe | Variable | Real-time, robotics |
| **BLE** | Radio | GATT | Small | Wearables, short range |
| **LoRaWAN** | Radio | Star | Tiny | Long-range, low-power |

> [!NOTE]
> **MQTT uses 3 QoS levels:**
> - **QoS 0** — At most once (fire-and-forget)
> - **QoS 1** — At least once (acknowledged)
> - **QoS 2** — Exactly once (4-step handshake, used for critical alerts)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Data Flow Metrics)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = f_s \times n \times b$ | Data size per unit time | bits/sec |
| 2 | $S = D \times T$ | Total storage required | bits |
| 3 | $C = \frac{S_{orig} - S_{comp}}{S_{orig}} \times 100$ | Compression ratio | % |
| 4 | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{prop} + \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{proc}$ | Total latency | seconds |
| 5 | $\tau_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay | seconds |
| 6 | $\tau_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | seconds |
| 7 | $B_{req} = N \times D \times (1 + O)$ | Required bandwidth with overhead | bps |
| 8 | $P_{avg} = \frac{E_{bit}}{T}$ | Average power consumption | Watts |

Where:
- $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz)
- $n$ = number of channels/sensors
- $b$ = bits per sample
- $L$ = packet size (bits)
- $R$ = link rate (bps)
- $d$ = distance, $v$ = signal propagation speed
- $N$ = number of devices
- $O$ = protocol overhead fraction

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **hospital-grade biomedical IoT**, data flow engineering determines:

1. **Patient Safety:** A latency spike from 200 ms to 2 s in an ICU ventilator alarm could mean missed apnea events.
2. **Network Capacity:** A 1000-bed hospital with continuous SpO₂ monitoring at 1 Hz × 16 bits/patient = **16 kbps** sustained upstream bandwidth — easy for Wi-Fi, but problematic over cellular for home-care.
3. **Battery Life:** An ECG patch transmitting raw 500 Hz data consumes ~10× more power than one transmitting only QRS-detected beats (event-driven).
4. **Data Governance:** HIPAA requires that PHI (Protected Health Information) be **encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+)** and **at rest (AES-256)** — a non-negotiable aspect of the data flow pipeline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "explain the flow of data," always mention **all 5 layers** and **at least one protocol** with its QoS/routing characteristics. Generic answers that only say "sensor sends data to cloud" will lose 60% of marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — ECG Data Storage Calculation

**Problem (Modeled on KTU University Exam pattern):**
A 3-lead ECG monitor samples at 360 Hz with 12-bit resolution. If the device records continuously for 24 hours, calculate:
(a) Data generated per second.
(b) Total data generated in 24 hours.
(c) Total data in MB.
(d) If transmission occurs over BLE at 100 kbps, how long does it take to upload the entire 24-hour recording?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Sampling frequency, $f_s = 360$ Hz
- Resolution, $b = 12$ bits/sample
- Number of channels, $n = 3$
- Duration, $T = 24$ hours
- BLE bandwidth, $R = 100$ kbps $= 100{,}000$ bps

### Part (a) — Data per Second

Apply the formula $D = f_s \times n \times b$:

$$D = 360 \times 3 \times 12$$

$$D = 12{,}960 \text{ bits/second}$$

**Logical interpretation:** Every second, 12,960 bits (≈ 1.62 KB) of raw ECG data are produced.

### Part (b) — Total Data in 24 Hours

Total seconds in 24 hours:

$$T_{sec} = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \text{ seconds}$$

Total data using $S = D \times T$:

$$S = 12{,}960 \times 86{,}400$$

$$S = 1{,}119{,}744{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Logical interpretation:** Approximately 1.12 billion bits are produced in one day.

### Part (c) — Convert to Megabytes (MB)

Convert bits to bytes (divide by 8):

$$S_{bytes} = \frac{1{,}119{,}744{,}{000}}{8} = 139{,}968{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

Convert to MB (divide by $10^6$ for engineering MB):

$$S_{MB} = \frac{139{,}968{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \approx 139.97 \text{ MB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Using $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ for MiB gives $\approx 133.5$ MiB. KTU prefers the engineering MB ($10^6$).

### Part (d) — BLE Upload Time

Using $\tau_{trans} = \frac{L}{R}$:

$$\tau_{upload} = \frac{1{,}119{,}744{,}000}{100{,}000}$$

$$\tau_{upload} = 11{,}197.44 \text{ seconds}$$

Convert to hours:

$$\tau_{upload} = \frac{11{,}197.44}{3600} \approx 3.11 \text{ hours}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A full day's ECG takes over 3 hours to upload over BLE — this is why **edge compression** (e.g., wavelet-based ECG compression achieving **~10:1 ratio**) is clinically critical.

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Total Latency Breakdown

**Problem:**
A wearable pulse oximeter sends 32-byte packets over a 4G LTE link. Given:
- Link rate: $R = 1$ Mbps
- Distance to base station: $d = 5$ km
- Signal speed: $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Queuing delay: $\tau_{queue} = 50$ ms
- Processing delay at gateway: $\tau_{proc} = 20$ ms

Calculate total latency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert packet size to bits:**

$$L = 32 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 256 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate transmission delay:**

$$\tau_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{256}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.000256 \text{ s} = 256 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate propagation delay:**

$$\tau_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{5{,}000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 25 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 4 — Sum all components:**

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{prop} + \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{proc}$$

$$\tau_{total} = 25 \text{ \mu s} + 256 \text{ \mu s} + 50{,}000 \text{ \mu s} + 20{,}000 \text{ \mu s}$$

$$\tau_{total} = 70{,}281 \text{ \mu s} \approx 70.28 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The wearable delivers SpO₂ data to the cloud in ~70 ms — well within the **< 1 second** clinical threshold for continuous monitoring.

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Compression Gain Analysis

**Problem:**
A remote patient monitoring system transmits raw ECG at 139.97 MB/day. After wavelet compression, it reduces to 14 MB/day. Calculate:
(a) Compression ratio.
(b) Bandwidth saved if the original link was 200 kbps.
(c) New link utilization if compressed.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Compression Ratio:**

$$C = \frac{S_{orig} - S_{comp}}{S_{orig}} \times 100$$

$$C = \frac{139.97 - 14}{139.97} \times 100 = \frac{125.97}{139.97} \times 100$$

$$C \approx 90.0\%$$

**Part (b) — Bandwidth Saved:**

Original data rate:

$$D_{orig} = \frac{139.97 \times 8 \times 10^6}{86{,}400} \approx 12{,}960 \text{ bps} = 12.96 \text{ kbps}$$

Compressed data rate:

$$D_{comp} = \frac{14 \times 8 \times 10^6}{86{,}400} \approx 1{,}296 \text{ bps} = 1.30 \text{ kbps}$$

Bandwidth saved:

$$B_{saved} = 12.96 - 1.30 = 11.66 \text{ kbps}$$

**Part (c) — New Link Utilization:**

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{D_{comp}}{R_{link}} \times 100 = \frac{1.30}{200} \times 100 = 0.65\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After compression, the system uses less than 1% of the link capacity — enabling **massive scaling** (hundreds of patients per gateway).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection Decision

| Requirement | Best Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous 1 kHz neural recording | **MQTT over 5G / Wired** | High throughput, reliable TCP |
| Weekly BP upload from home patient | **CoAP over NB-IoT** | Low power, small payload |
| Real-time ICU ventilator alarm | **MQTT QoS 2** | Guaranteed delivery |
| Implant-to-implant in-body comms | **DDS or proprietary RF** | Ultra-low latency |
| Wearable fitness band to phone | **BLE GATT** | Short range, low energy |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question carries 3 marks. Answers should be **2–4 sentences with a labeled diagram where possible**.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the "Flow of Data" in IoT and list the five layers of the IoT data flow architecture.**

**Model Answer:**
The Flow of Data in IoT refers to the structured movement of information from physical sensors through processing layers to applications and users. The five layers are: (1) **Perception/Sensing Layer** (sensors/actuators), (2) **Network Layer** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, BLE), (3) **Middleware/Processing Layer** (edge/fog analytics), (4) **Application Layer** (dashboards, EHR), and (5) **Business/Management Layer** (security, compliance, billing). In biomedical IoT, this pipeline ensures real-time, secure, and accurate delivery of patient data. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare MQTT and CoAP protocols. State two advantages of MQTT for biomedical telemetry.**

**Model Answer:**
MQTT is a **TCP-based, publish-subscribe** protocol with a 2-byte header, supporting 3 QoS levels. CoAP is a **UDP-based, request-response** protocol with a 4-byte header, designed for constrained devices. Two advantages of MQTT for biomedical telemetry: (1) **QoS 1 and 2 guarantee message delivery**, critical for alerts like cardiac arrhythmias, and (2) **low overhead** suits low-power wearables and intermittent networks. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** One full question = 14 marks, with internal choice (Question A or Question B). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer architecture of IoT data flow** as applied to a **remote cardiac patient monitoring system**. Identify the protocol most suitable for the network layer and justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ICU patient is monitored using a 5-lead ECG at 500 Hz, 16-bit resolution, continuously for 48 hours. Calculate: **(i)** the total data generated in bits, **(ii)** the equivalent size in GB, and **(iii)** the time required to transmit this data over a 1 Mbps Wi-Fi link. Also compute the bandwidth utilization if the data is first compressed with a 12:1 ratio. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 5-Layer Architecture for Remote Cardiac Monitoring**

The block diagram (refer Section 4.1) shows:
- **Layer 1 (Sensing):** 5-lead ECG electrodes on the patient's chest, sampling at 500 Hz, 16-bit ADC.
- **Layer 2 (Network):** **MQTT over Wi-Fi/4G** — chosen because of its low overhead (2-byte header), QoS support, and reliable TCP backbone.
- **Layer 3 (Middleware):** Raspberry Pi/edge gateway performs R-peak detection, noise filtering, and event-driven alerting.
- **Layer 4 (Application):** Cardiologist's dashboard, mobile alerts, EHR auto-update.
- **Layer 5 (Business):** Hospital IT ensures HIPAA compliance, encrypted storage, and SLA management.

**Justification for MQTT:** Cardiac data demands **reliable delivery (QoS ≥ 1)**, **low bandwidth** (wearable power budget), and **bi-directional communication** (cloud-to-device firmware updates). MQTT is optimal. *(7 marks: 4 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 1 for justification)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $f_s = 500$ Hz, $b = 16$ bits, $n = 5$ leads, $T = 48$ hours, $R = 1$ Mbps, compression ratio = 12:1.

**Step 1 — Data per second:**

$$D = 500 \times 5 \times 16 = 40{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} = 40 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Total seconds in 48 hours:**

$$T_{sec} = 48 \times 3600 = 172{,}800 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Total data in bits:**

$$S = 40{,}000 \times 172{,}800 = 6{,}912{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to GB:**

$$S_{GB} = \frac{6{,}912{,}000{,}000}{8 \times 10^9} = 0.864 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 5 — Transmission time over 1 Mbps:**

$$\tau_{upload} = \frac{6{,}912{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 6{,}912 \text{ seconds} = 1.92 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 — Compressed data:**

$$S_{comp} = \frac{6{,}912{,}000{,}000}{12} = 576{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits} = 0.072 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 7 — New bandwidth utilization:**

$$\text{Util} = \frac{40{,}000 / 12}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}333}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100 = 0.33\%$$

**Final Answer:** Original 0.864 GB → compressed 0.072 GB → utilization drops from **4% to 0.33%** of the 1 Mbps link. *(7 marks: 2 + 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1)*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol** in detail with its **publish-subscribe architecture**, message format, and the three QoS levels. Why is MQTT preferred over HTTP for biomedical IoT? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless body area network (WBAN) has 8 sensor nodes, each transmitting 256-byte packets every 100 ms over a shared 250 kbps link. Calculate: **(i)** the per-node data rate, **(ii)** the aggregate network load, and **(iii)** the average queuing delay per packet if the link utilization is 80%. Comment on whether this network can support real-time ECG monitoring. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — MQTT Protocol**

MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) is a lightweight **publish-subscribe** protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**, designed by IBM (1999). It runs over TCP port 1883 (or 8883 for TLS).

**Architecture:**
- **Broker** (central server, e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) — receives all messages and forwards them.
- **Publisher** (sensor/device) — sends data to a "topic" (e.g., `hospital/ward3/patient42/ecg`).
- **Subscriber** (application, dashboard) — receives data from topics of interest.
- Decoupling of publisher and subscriber enables **asynchronous, scalable** communication.

**Message Format:**
- Fixed header: 2 bytes (control packet type + flags).
- Variable header: optional fields like Topic Name, Packet Identifier.
- Payload: up to 256 MB.

**QoS Levels:**
1. **QoS 0** — At most once. No acknowledgement. Fire-and-forget.
2. **QoS 1** — At least once. Uses PUBACK. Possible duplicates.
3. **QoS 2** — Exactly once. 4-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP). Guaranteed single delivery.

**Why MQTT over HTTP for Biomedical IoT:**
1. **Header overhead:** MQTT = 2 bytes vs HTTP ≈ 200+ bytes → ~100× more efficient.
2. **Power efficiency:** Persistent TCP connection avoids handshake per transmission.
3. **Push-based:** MQTT pushes data; HTTP requires polling → lower latency.
4. **QoS guarantees:** Critical for life-saving alerts.
5. **Asynchronous:** Decouples publisher and subscriber — ideal for intermittently connected wearable devices.

*(7 marks: 2 architecture + 2 QoS + 1 message format + 2 comparison)*

**Part (b) — WBAN Numerical Analysis**

**Given:**
- $N = 8$ nodes, packet size $L = 256$ bytes $= 2048$ bits, interval $T_{int} = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s, link rate $R = 250$ kbps, utilization $\rho = 0.8$.

**Step 1 — Per-node data rate:**

$$D_{node} = \frac{2048}{0.1} = 20{,}480 \text{ bps} = 20.48 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate network load:**

$$D_{agg} = 8 \times 20.48 = 163.84 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with link capacity:**

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{163.84}{250} \times 100 = 65.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: The question states 80% utilization. We use $\rho = 0.8$ for queuing delay.

**Step 4 — Average queuing delay using M/M/1 model:**

For an M/M/1 queue:

$$\tau_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \times \tau_{service}$$

Service time (time to transmit one packet on the shared link):

$$\tau_{service} = \frac{2048}{250{,}000} = 8.192 \text{ ms}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_{queue} = \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} \times 8.192 = \frac{0.8}{0.2} \times 8.192$$

$$\tau_{queue} = 4 \times 8.192 = 32.77 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Total per-packet delay (approx):**

$$\tau_{total} \approx \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{prop}$$

Assuming $\tau_{prop} \approx 1$ ms:

$$\tau_{total} \approx 8.19 + 32.77 + 1 \approx 41.96 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** With ~42 ms end-to-end delay, this WBAN **can comfortably support real-time ECG monitoring** (clinical threshold ≈ 250 ms for waveform display). However, operating at 80% utilization is risky — a burst from any node can cause jitter. Recommended utilization for medical WBAN ≤ **60%**.

*(7 marks: 2 per-node rate + 1.5 aggregate + 2 queuing + 1.5 conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing bits and bytes** — Students frequently forget to multiply by 8 when converting packet sizes. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Skipping units** — Always write "bits/sec", "MB", "ms" in every step. Unit-less answers lose 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 3. **Using the wrong compression formula** — Compression *ratio* (10:1) ≠ Compression *percentage* (90%). State both clearly.
> 4. **Ignoring overhead** — MQTT/HTTP headers add 5–20% overhead. KTU bonus marks if you mention this.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention HIPAA/GDPR** — Any biomedical data flow answer without security mention is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Wrong MQTT QoS description** — Do NOT say "QoS 2 = reliable." Specify *"exactly once, 4-step handshake"*.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **Use this checklist the night before your KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Flow of Data** = Acquisition → Aggregation → Transmission → Processing → Storage → Visualization.
- ✅ **5-Layer IoT Architecture:** Perception, Network, Middleware, Application, Business.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ECG (150 Hz max) → 360 Hz standard.
- ✅ **Data rate formula:** $D = f_s \times n \times b$. Always in **bits/sec**.
- ✅ **Storage formula:** $S = D \times T$. Convert bits → bytes (÷8) → MB (÷$10^6$).
- ✅ **Latency components:** $\tau = \tau_{prop} + \tau_{trans} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{proc}$.
- ✅ **Transmission delay:** $\tau_{trans} = L/R$. **Propagation delay:** $\tau_{prop} = d/v$.
- ✅ **MQTT** = TCP, Pub/Sub, 2-byte header, 3 QoS levels, port 1883/8883.
- ✅ **CoAP** = UDP, Request-Response, 4-byte header, for constrained devices.
- ✅ **MQTT QoS:** 0 = at most once, 1 = at least once, 2 = exactly once.
- ✅ **4 V's of Big Data:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity.
- ✅ **Biomedical thresholds:** SpO₂ latency < 1 s; ECG < 250 ms; Ventilator alarm < 100 ms.
- ✅ **Compression ratio formula:** $C = \frac{S_{orig} - S_{comp}}{S_{orig}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Security mandates:** TLS 1.2+ in transit, AES-256 at rest, HIPAA/GDPR compliance.
- ✅ **M/M/1 queuing delay:** $\tau_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} \times \tau_{service}$.
- ✅ **D2G2C model** is the clinically preferred IoT data flow pattern.
- ✅ **WBAN utilization** should stay ≤ 60% for safety-critical biomedical apps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DATA ACQUISITION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the systematic process of **sampling real-world physical phenomena**, converting the resulting **analog signals** into a **digital format** that is measurable, storable, processable, and transmissible by a computing system. In the specific context of **Biomedical Engineering and IoT**, Data Acquisition refers to the end-to-end pipeline that captures **physiological signals** (ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO₂, body temperature, blood pressure, respiration rate, etc.) from the human body using appropriate **biomedical sensors/transducers**, conditions these signals to remove noise and amplify weak bio-potentials, digitises them through an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**, and routes them to a **microcontroller/processor/edge node** for local analysis, cloud upload, or remote clinical monitoring.

The full KTU 2024 definition of a **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is therefore:

> A Data Acquisition System is an integrated assembly of **sensors**, **signal conditioning circuitry**, **analogue-to-digital converters**, **multiplexers**, **sample-and-hold (S/H) circuits**, and a **host processor/controller** working in synchrony to acquire, digitise, and deliver one or more physical parameters in a machine-readable digital form.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Digital Stethoscope of a Modern Hospital"**

Imagine you are a doctor in the **1920s** listening to a patient's heart with a **mechanical stethoscope**. The sound waves from the heart are converted to mechanical vibrations that your ears interpret. The information **stays in your head** and **vanishes** the moment the patient leaves.

Now imagine a **modern CCU (Cardiac Care Unit)**. The patient's heart is connected to:

1. **Electrodes (sensors)** placed on the skin — like your stethoscope.
2. An **amplifier** — making the tiny millivolt signals loud enough.
3. A **filter** — removing the electrical noise of the room.
4. A **digital display** — showing a clean waveform.
5. A **central monitor / cloud server** — recording every heartbeat **24/7**.

This entire chain — **sensor → conditioning → ADC → processor → display/network** — is a **Data Acquisition System**.

In the **IoT era**, this is the same chain, but the "central monitor" is replaced by a **wireless gateway**, and the heart's data travels to a **cloud server on another continent**, where an **AI algorithm** can detect a heart attack *before it even happens*.

**Data Acquisition is therefore the "senses" of any IoT or biomedical system.** Without it, the digital world is blind to the physical world.

---

### 1.3 Core Highlights of the Topic (Syllabus Anchors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Sub-Topics to Master:**
> 1. **Block diagram and functional components** of a Data Acquisition System.
> 2. **Sensors and Transducers** for biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO₂, BP, temperature).
> 3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification (INA, IA), Filtering (LPF, HPF, BPF, Notch at 50/60 Hz), Isolation, Linearisation.
> 4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion** — Resolution, Quantisation, Sampling Theorem, Types of ADC.
> 5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) and Multiplexing** in multi-channel DAQ.
> 6. **Data Logging, Storage and Transmission** — local memory, BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa, IoT protocols (MQTT, CoAP).
> 7. **Wearable & Wireless Biomedical DAQ** — design considerations (power, SNR, safety, biocompatibility).
> 8. **Standards & Safety** — IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, AAMI, patient isolation, leakage current limits.

**Standard metrics you must commit to memory (board exam favourites):**

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 60601-1 Patient Leakage Current Limit** | **≤ 100 µA (Type B), ≤ 10 µA (Type CF, direct cardiac)** | Safety threshold for biomedical equipment |
| **ECG Bandwidth** | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** | Frequency range of cardiac bio-signal |
| **EEG Bandwidth** | **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** | Brain wave spectrum |
| **EMG Bandwidth** | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | Muscle action potential range |
| **Standard ECG amplitude** | **~1 mV** | Calibrates the front-end gain |
| **Standard sampling rate for clinical ECG** | **500 Hz** (≥ 2 × 150 Hz per Nyquist) | Safe oversampling |
| **Standard NIBP cuff pressure** | **20 – 300 mmHg** | Indirect blood pressure range |
| **Normal SpO₂** | **95 – 100 %** | Oxygen saturation in arterial blood |
| **Resolution of a 12-bit ADC with 5 V reference** | **5 / 2¹² ≈ 1.22 mV per LSB** | Quantisation step |
| **Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist)** | **f_s ≥ 2 · f_max** | Sampling theorem |

---

### 1.4 Why Data Acquisition is the Heart of Biomedical IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — The Five Engineering Realms of DAQ**
> 1. **The Clinical Realm** — Patient monitoring, ICU, ECG telemetry, foetal monitors.
> 2. **The Diagnostic Realm** — CT, MRI, ultrasound, where raw sensor data becomes pixel images.
> 3. **The Therapeutic Realm** — Closed-loop drug delivery, neuro-stimulators, pacemakers.
> 4. **The Research Realm** — Brain-computer interfaces (BCI), prosthetics, sports biomechanics.
> 5. **The Consumer/IoT Realm** — Smartwatches, fitness bands, continuous glucose monitors (CGM), remote patient monitoring (RPM).
>
> *Every one of these applications depends on a correctly designed Data Acquisition front-end.*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Data Acquisition System

A canonical biomedical DAQ chain has **seven functional blocks**, in this exact order. The KTU board repeatedly asks for this block diagram, so memorise it.

**Block 1 — Sensor / Transducer (the "sense organ")**
A *transducer* converts one form of energy into another. A *sensor* is a transducer whose output is a measurable electrical quantity. In biomedical DAQ, common sensors are:

| Physiological Parameter | Sensor / Transducer | Output Signal |
|---|---|---|
| ECG (electrocardiogram) | Ag/AgCl surface electrodes | Differential voltage ~ 1 mV |
| EEG (electroencephalogram) | Scalp electrodes | ~ 10 – 100 µV |
| EMG (electromyogram) | Needle or surface electrodes | ~ 50 µV – 5 mV |
| Body temperature | Thermistor / RTD / LM35 / DS18B20 | Voltage or digital |
| SpO₂ (pulse oximetry) | Red + IR photodiode (PPG) | Photocurrent |
| Blood pressure (invasive) | Strain-gauge transducer on cannula | Bridge voltage |
| Blood pressure (non-invasive) | Oscillometric cuff + pressure sensor | Cuff oscillation waveform |
| Respiration rate | Piezo belt / impedance pneumograph | Voltage / impedance |
| Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) | Ag/AgCl electrodes | Resistance change |
| Blood glucose | Enzymatic electrochemical strip | Micro-current |

**Block 2 — Signal Conditioning (the "translator")**
Raw bio-signals are weak, noisy, and often ride on a high DC offset. The signal conditioning stage:

- **Amplifies** the signal (Instrumentation Amplifier — IA / INA).
- **Filters** noise (active low-pass, high-pass, notch 50 Hz).
- **Isolates** the patient from mains (opto-isolator / transformer isolation) for **IEC 60601-1** safety.
- **Linearises** non-linear sensor curves (e.g., thermistor β-equation).

**Block 3 — Multiplexer (MUX) — the "traffic controller"**
When multiple channels are sampled (e.g., 12-lead ECG), an **analogue MUX** (e.g., CD4051, ADG726) routes one channel at a time to the ADC. This allows a **single expensive ADC** to serve many channels.

**Block 4 — Sample-and-Hold (S/H) — the "snapshot camera"**
A **S/H circuit** (e.g., LF398) holds the rapidly-changing analogue input **constant** during the ADC's conversion time. Without it, the input would change mid-conversion, producing errors.

**Block 5 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) — the "digital translator"**
Converts the conditioned analogue voltage to a binary number. **Resolution** in bits *n* gives $2^n$ discrete levels. Types encountered: **Flash, Successive Approximation (SAR), Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ), Dual-Slope, Pipeline.**

**Block 6 — Digital Controller / Microprocessor — the "brain"**
A microcontroller (e.g., STM32, ESP32, MSP430, Arduino, Raspberry Pi Pico) reads the ADC, performs **DSP** (digital filtering, FFT, feature extraction), stores to memory, and transmits over BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa.

**Block 7 — Display, Storage, Network — the "output"**
Local **LCD/OLED**, **SD card** storage, and uplink to **cloud (AWS IoT, Azure, ThingSpeak, Firebase)** via **MQTT/CoAP/HTTP** protocols.

---

## 2.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

The foundational rule of all DAQ. The KTU board will give you a numerical problem on this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited analogue signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be **completely reconstructed** from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s$ is **at least twice** the highest frequency present in the signal.
>
> $$f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
>
> The minimum theoretical rate $2 f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**; in practice engineers sample at **3 to 5 times** $f_{max}$ to allow for non-ideal anti-aliasing filters. This is called **oversampling**.

**Anti-Aliasing Filter** — a sharp analogue LPF placed **before** the ADC whose cutoff is at $f_s/2$. Without it, frequencies above $f_s/2$ "fold back" into the pass-band (aliasing) and corrupt the signal irrecoverably.

---

## 2.3 Resolution and Quantisation

An ADC with *n* bits and reference voltage $V_{REF}$ has a **quantisation step** (size of one Least Significant Bit):

$$\Delta V \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$$

The **quantisation error** is bounded by:

$$-\frac{\Delta V}{2} \;\leq\; e_q \;\leq\; +\frac{\Delta V}{2}$$

The **Signal-to-Quantisation-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** of an ideal *n*-bit ADC is:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\approx\; 6.02 \cdot n \;+\; 1.76$$

> This famous 6.02 dB-per-bit rule is a board-exam staple. **Memorise it.**

---

## 2.4 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — the King of Biomedical Front-Ends

ECG/EEG/EMG signals are tiny (µV–mV) and sit on a large common-mode voltage (50 Hz mains interference, electrode half-cell potential ~ ±300 mV). A plain op-amp differential amplifier fails because of mismatched input resistors.

The **three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** solves this:

- **Infinite input impedance** (no current drawn from skin electrodes).
- **Single resistor $R_G$** sets the differential gain:

$$A_v \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} \;\;=\;\; 1 \;+\; \frac{2R_1}{R_G}$$

- **Excellent CMRR** (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio):

$$\text{CMRR}_{dB} \;\;=\;\; 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

A good INA (e.g., **AD620, INA128, INA333**) gives **CMRR ≥ 100 dB at DC**, sufficient to reject 50 Hz mains pickup.

---

## 2.5 Filtering Equations

**First-order RC low-pass filter (anti-aliasing):**

$$f_c \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**First-order RC high-pass filter (DC blocking):**

$$f_c \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Twin-T Notch filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz line rejection):**

$$f_{notch} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas and concepts are used in practice:**
> 1. **Design of 12-lead ECG machines** — IA gain = 1000, sampling at 500 Hz, 16-bit ADC, 5 V reference → LSB ≈ 76 µV, comfortably below the 1 mV ECG amplitude.
> 2. **Wearable PPG smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** — use Σ-Δ ADCs with 20-bit effective resolution; use 100 Hz green-light PPG.
> 3. **BCI (Brain–Computer Interface) — Neuralink, OpenBCI** — sample at 1 kHz–30 kHz per channel with 24-bit Σ-Δ ADCs.
> 4. **ICU multiparameter monitors** — use 8-channel MUX, single 16-bit SAR ADC at 1 kHz aggregate.
> 5. **Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGM — Dexcom, Abbott FreeStyle Libre)** — enzymatic electrochemical sensor sampled every 1–5 min, transmitted via BLE to a phone.
> 6. **IoT air-quality / environmental monitoring** — MEMS gas sensors (MQ-135, BME680) sampled at 1 Hz, transmitted via MQTT to AWS IoT Core.
