# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked-out derivations and numerical solutions are the **type of 7- and 14-mark problems that appear in KTU ESE**. Practise writing them out fully on paper.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why an *n*-bit ADC gives SQNR ≈ 6.02 n + 1.76 dB

### Goal
Show that an ideal *n*-bit ADC has a signal-to-quantisation-noise ratio of approximately **6.02 n + 1.76 dB**.

### Step 1 — Quantisation step

For a full-scale input range $V_{FS}$ spread across $2^n$ discrete codes:

$$\Delta V \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

### Step 2 — Quantisation error

The quantisation error $e_q(t)$ is the difference between the input and the nearest quantisation level. Assuming the error is **uniformly distributed** between $-\Delta V / 2$ and $+\Delta V / 2$:

$$-\frac{\Delta V}{2} \;\leq\; e_q \;\leq\; +\frac{\Delta V}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Mean-square value of quantisation noise

The mean-square value (variance) of a uniform distribution over a width $\Delta V$ is:

$$\overline{e_q^{\,2}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12}$$

This can be derived by integrating the squared error over the interval:

$$\overline{e_q^{\,2}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{\Delta V} \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2} e_q^{\,2}\, de_q \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{\Delta V} \left[\frac{e_q^{\,3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2}$$

$$=\;\; \frac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot (\Delta V/2)^3}{3} \;\;=\;\; \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12}$$

### Step 4 — RMS signal power for a full-scale sine wave

For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $V_{FS}/2$ (filling the full-scale range):

$$V_{rms}^{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{FS}^{\,2}}{8}$$

### Step 5 — Ratio of signal power to noise power

$$\text{SQNR} \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{rms}^{\,2}}{\overline{e_q^{\,2}}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{FS}^{\,2}/8}{(\Delta V)^2 / 12} \;\;=\;\; \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}^{\,2}}{(\Delta V)^2}$$

Substituting $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^n$:

$$\text{SQNR} \;\;=\;\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}^{\,2}}{V_{FS}^{\,2} / 2^{2n}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} \;\;=\;\; 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to decibels

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\;=\;\; 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}) \;\;=\;\; 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2n})$$

$$=\;\; 1.76 + 20\, n \cdot \log_{10}(2)$$

Since $\log_{10}(2) = 0.30103$:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\;=\;\; 1.76 + 20 \cdot 0.30103 \cdot n \;\;=\;\; 1.76 + 6.0206 \cdot n$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\approx\; 6.02\, n \;+\; 1.76\;}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Each extra bit of resolution improves the dynamic range by approximately **6 dB** (i.e., a factor of 2 in amplitude, or a factor of 4 in power). This is why a 16-bit ECG ADC is preferred over a 12-bit one — it can resolve a 76 µV change instead of a 1.22 mV change, capturing every P-wave and T-wave detail.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Sampling Rate for an ECG Signal

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An ECG monitor must faithfully capture the cardiac waveform. The signal of interest has a maximum frequency component of **150 Hz**. Determine:
> (a) the minimum Nyquist sampling rate,
> (b) a practical sampling rate using 3× oversampling,
> (c) the highest frequency an anti-aliasing filter must reject.

### Solution

**(a) Nyquist rate:**

$$f_s^{min} \;\;=\;\; 2 \cdot f_{max} \;\;=\;\; 2 \cdot 150 \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{300\; Hz}$$

**(b) Practical rate at 3× oversampling:**

$$f_s^{practical} \;\;=\;\; 3 \cdot f_{max} \;\;=\;\; 3 \cdot 150 \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{450\; Hz}$$

For KTU, a typical clinical ECG is sampled at **500 Hz** (the next round number above 450 Hz).

**(c) Anti-aliasing filter cutoff:**

$$f_c \;\;\leq\;\; \frac{f_s}{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{500}{2} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{250\; Hz}$$

(Engineers often relax the filter to $f_c = 150$ Hz to keep the ECG pass-band flat up to the highest useful frequency.)

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — ADC Resolution and Quantisation Error

> **Problem:** A 12-bit ADC is used in an ECG front-end with a 5 V reference. The ECG signal has a peak amplitude of 5 mV after amplification. Calculate:
> (a) the LSB / quantisation step,
> (b) the maximum quantisation error,
> (c) the SQNR in dB.

### Solution

**(a) LSB:**

$$\Delta V \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{12}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{5}{4096} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{1.2207\; mV}$$

**(b) Peak quantisation error:**

$$e_{q,\,max} \;\;=\;\; \frac{\Delta V}{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1.2207}{2} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{0.6104\; mV}$$

**(c) SQNR:**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\;=\;\; 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 \;\;=\;\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{74\; dB}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The 0.61 mV error is comparable to the ECG signal amplitude (5 mV) — the front-end gain of the IA must be raised so that the ECG fills most of the ADC's full-scale range. For a 5 mV signal on a 5 V scale, only 0.1 % of the ADC's dynamic range is used — a **16-bit ADC** is preferred.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Instrumentation Amplifier Gain for ECG

> **Problem:** An ECG electrode pair produces a 1 mV differential signal. You want to amplify it to 1 V (so it nearly fills a 0–5 V ADC). Design a 3-op-amp INA using $R_1 = 25\;\text{k}\Omega$ and find the required $R_G$.

### Solution

Required gain:

$$A_v \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1\;\text{V}}{1\;\text{mV}} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{1000}$$

INA gain formula:

$$A_v \;\;=\;\; 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}$$

Substituting $A_v = 1000$ and $R_1 = 25\;\text{k}\Omega$:

$$1000 \;\;=\;\; 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 25\,000}{R_G}$$

$$\frac{2 \cdot 25\,000}{R_G} \;\;=\;\; 999$$

$$R_G \;\;=\;\; \frac{50\,000}{999} \;\;\approx\;\; 50.05\;\Omega$$

$$\boxed{\;R_G \;\;\approx\;\; 50\;\Omega\;}$$

**Engineering note:** Such a high gain makes the IA bandwidth very narrow. An **AD620** with gain 1000 has GBW of about 120 kHz, giving a closed-loop bandwidth of ~ 120 kHz / 1000 = 120 Hz — exactly the ECG bandwidth needed. The next stage of gain can be added in a post-IA amplifier if required.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 4 — Anti-Aliasing RC LPF

> **Problem:** Design a first-order RC low-pass filter as an anti-aliasing stage for a DAQ system with $f_c = 250$ Hz. Choose $C = 100$ nF and find $R$.

### Solution

Rearranging $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$:

$$R \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 250 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$=\;\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{1.5708 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\;R \;\;\approx\;\; 6.37\;\text{k}\Omega\;}$$

**KTU Pitfall:** Students forget to convert the units of $C$ to **farads** (here, $100 \times 10^{-9}$ F = 100 nF). A common error: using $C = 100$ (dimensionless) and getting a nonsensical $R \approx 0.006\;\Omega$.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 5 — Heart Rate from ECG R-R Interval

> **Problem:** The R-R interval on a digital ECG is measured to be 0.8 s. Calculate the heart rate in beats per minute.

### Solution

$$\text{HR} \;\;=\;\; \frac{60}{T_{R-R}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{60}{0.8} \;\;=\;\; \mathbf{75\;\text{bpm}}$$

**Tachycardia:** HR > 100 bpm. **Bradycardia:** HR < 60 bpm.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Problem 6 — Wireless DAQ Power Budget

> **Problem:** A wearable ECG patch uses a 3.7 V, 400 mAh Li-Po battery. The DAQ circuit draws 12 mA average current during continuous monitoring. Calculate the operational time in hours.

### Solution

$$\text{Time} \;\;=\;\; \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{\text{Load (mA)}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{400}{12} \;\;\approx\;\; 33.3\;\text{hours}$$

For IoT wearables, designers use **duty cycling** (e.g., sample 1 s, sleep 9 s) to extend this to **5–7 days** on the same battery.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing an ADC Topology

| ADC Type | Resolution | Speed | Power | Best Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash** | 6 – 8 bits | Very fast (> 100 MSPS) | High | Oscilloscope DAQ, ultrasound |
| **SAR** | 12 – 18 bits | Medium (≤ 10 MSPS) | Medium | **ECG, EMG, NIBP, multi-channel patient monitors** |
| **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ)** | 16 – 24 bits | Slow (≤ 1 MSPS) | Low | **EEG, ECG, temperature, pressure, weigh scales** |
| **Dual-Slope** | 16 – 20 bits | Very slow | Medium | **Digital multimeters, slow chemical sensors** |
| **Pipeline** | 12 – 16 bits | Fast (> 100 MSPS) | High | **MRI, CT, high-end ultrasound** |

> **For KTU answers:** if the question asks "which ADC for a 12-lead ECG monitor at 500 Hz, 16-bit resolution" — answer **SAR (e.g., AD7606)**. If the question asks "which ADC for a 24-bit EEG amplifier at 250 Hz with high noise rejection" — answer **Sigma-Delta (e.g., ADS1299)**.

## 4.1 Generic Biomedical Data Acquisition System — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSIOLOGICAL<br/>SOURCE<br/>Human Body] --> B[BIOMEDICAL<br/>SENSOR<br/>Electrodes / Transducer]
    B --> C[SIGNAL<br/>CONDITIONING<br/>IA + Filters + Isolation]
    C --> D[ANALOG<br/>MULTIPLEXER<br/>Multi-channel routing]
    D --> E[SAMPLE<br/>AND HOLD<br/>S/H circuit]
    E --> F[ADC<br/>Analog to Digital<br/>Converter]
    F --> G[DIGITAL<br/>CONTROLLER<br/>MCU / DSP / FPGA]
    G --> H[LOCAL<br/>DISPLAY<br/>LCD / OLED]
    G --> I[STORAGE<br/>SD card / Flash]
    G --> J[WIRELESS<br/>TRANSCEIVER<br/>BLE / Wi-Fi / LoRa]
    J --> K[CLOUD<br/>SERVER<br/>AWS IoT / Azure]
    K --> L[CLINICAL<br/>DASHBOARD<br/>Web / Mobile App]
    L -.-> M[ALERT /<br/>DIAGNOSIS<br/>Doctor Notification]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left half** (A → F) is the analogue signal chain — *the heart of DAQ*.
- The **right half** (G → M) is the digital/IoT chain — *the connectivity*.
- The dotted line from L back to M indicates a **feedback path** (e.g., auto-diagnosis, alert).

---

## 4.2 ECG-Specific Data Acquisition Front-End (Detailed Sub-System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[RA Electrode] --> IA1[INA IN1+]
    S2[LA Electrode] --> IA1
    S3[RL Electrode<br/>Right Leg Drive] --> RLD[Right Leg Driver<br/>Active Ground]
    IA1 --> GAIN[GAIN STAGE<br/>Av = 1000]
    GAIN --> HPF[HPF 0.05 Hz<br/>DC Block]
    HPF --> LPF[LPF 150 Hz<br/>Anti-Aliasing]
    LPF --> NOTCH[NOTCH 50 Hz<br/>Mains Rejection]
    NOTCH --> MUX1[Channel MUX<br/>12-lead selection]
    MUX1 --> SH[Sample and Hold]
    SH --> ADC1[16-bit SAR ADC<br/>500 Hz sampling]
    ADC1 --> MCU1[STM32 / ESP32<br/>DSP and RTOS]
    MCU1 --> DISP1[TFT Display]
    MCU1 --> BLE1[BLE Module<br/>to Smartphone]
    RLD -.-> IA1
```

**Why each block exists:**
- **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** — drives the patient's body with a feedback signal that **cancels common-mode mains interference**, improving CMRR by 20–40 dB.
- **HPF at 0.05 Hz** — blocks the electrode DC half-cell potential (up to ±300 mV) so it does not saturate the IA.
- **LPF at 150 Hz** — anti-aliasing filter before the 500 Hz ADC.
- **Notch at 50 Hz** — surgical rejection of Indian/European power-line interference.
- **SAR ADC 16-bit** — standard for clinical ECG, gives LSB ≈ 76 µV at 5 V reference.

---

## 4.3 Multi-Channel Multiplexed DAQ Architecture (Shared ADC Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CH1[Channel 1<br/>ECG Lead I] --> MX1[MUX]
    CH2[Channel 2<br/>ECG Lead II] --> MX1
    CH3[Channel 3<br/>EEG Fp1] --> MX1
    CH4[Channel 4<br/>Temperature] --> MX1
    CH5[Channel 5<br/>SpO2 PPG] --> MX1
    CH6[Channel 6<br/>Respiration] --> MX1
    CH7[Channel 7<br/>GSR] --> MX1
    CH8[Channel 8<br/>NIBP] --> MX1
    MX1 --> SH2[Sample and Hold]
    SH2 --> ADC2[Single high-grade<br/>16-bit SAR ADC]
    ADC2 --> CPU[DSP / MCU<br/>Time-division demux]
    CPU --> NET[IoT Gateway<br/>MQTT Publisher]
```

**Engineering rationale:** A single 16-bit ADC is **expensive and power-hungry**. By time-multiplexing 8 channels through one ADC, the system cost is **reduced by 70 %** and power consumption drops significantly. The trade-off is a **lower per-channel effective sampling rate** — if the MUX scans all 8 channels at 1 kHz, each channel gets only 125 Hz. This is acceptable for slow biosignals (temperature, GSR) but **inadequate for EMG** (needs ≥ 1 kHz per channel).

---

## 4.4 IoT-Enabled Wearable Biomedical DAQ — System View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDGE_NODE[EDGE NODE  Wearable Patch on Patient]
        E1[Bio-sensors Array] --> E2[Analog Front End<br/>IA plus Filter]
        E2 --> E3[Sigma Delta ADC<br/>24 bit]
        E3 --> E4[BLE SoC<br/>nRF52840]
    end
    EDGE_NODE -->|BLE 4.2| GATEWAY
    subgraph GATEWAY[EDGE GATEWAY  Smartphone / Hub]
        G1[Mobile App] --> G2[MQTT Client]
        G2 --> G3[Local Storage<br/>SQLite]
    end
    GATEWAY -->|WiFi / 4G| CLOUD
    subgraph CLOUD[CLOUD TIER  AWS IoT Core]
        C1[MQTT Broker] --> C2[Stream Processor]
        C2 --> C3[Time Series DB<br/>InfluxDB]
        C3 --> C4[ML Inference<br/>Anomaly Detection]
        C4 --> C5[Alert Service<br/>SMS / Email]
    end
    CLOUD --> DOC[Clinician Dashboard]
```

**KTU-typical exam question on this diagram:**
> *"Explain the role of edge computing in a wearable biomedical IoT system. Why is raw data not sent directly to the cloud?"*
>
> **Model answer:** Edge computing pre-processes the biosignal (filtering, feature extraction, QRS detection) on the wearable itself, reducing the data rate to the cloud by **100 – 1000×**. This conserves battery, lowers data costs, improves response time, and preserves patient privacy by sending only clinically relevant features, not raw waveforms.

---

## 4.5 ADC Conversion Flow — Inside the ADC (SAR Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Sample and Hold]
    B --> C[Comparator]
    C --> D[SAR Register<br/>Successive Approx]
    D --> E[DAC]
    E --> C
    D --> F[Digital Output<br/>n-bit code]
    A --> G[Clock]
    G --> D
```

**How SAR works (in plain English):** The SAR register tests one bit at a time, starting from the MSB. It sets the MSB, the DAC produces an analogue voltage, the comparator checks if Vin is above or below it, and the bit is **kept or cleared**. After *n* clock cycles, all *n* bits are decided. Total conversion time = $n \cdot T_{clk}$ (e.g., 16 bits at 1 MHz = 16 µs).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution for Module 4 — Data Acquisition**
> - **Part A** (Short answer, 3 marks each) — definitions, single-concept explanations.
> - **Part B** (Long answer, 14 marks with internal choice) — block diagrams, derivations, design problems.
> - **Cognitive levels tested (Revised Bloom's):** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyse (L4).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Data Acquisition System. List its main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an integrated electronic system that **samples real-world physical signals**, **conditions** them, **converts** them to digital form, and **delivers** them to a processor for storage, display, or transmission.
>
> **Main functional blocks:**
> 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
> 2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplification, filtering, isolation, linearisation.
> 3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — for multi-channel scanning.
> 4. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — freezes input during ADC conversion.
> 5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts analogue to digital code.
> 6. **Controller / Processor** — manages timing, processing, and storage.
> 7. **Display / Storage / Communication** — output and uplink.

**(3 marks breakdown: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the list.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Statement:** A band-limited analogue signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies
> $$f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> The minimum rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.
>
> **Aliasing:** If a signal contains frequency components above $f_s / 2$, those components **fold back** into the lower frequency range and become indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency content. This corruption is called **aliasing**. It is prevented by placing a sharp **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before the ADC with cutoff at $f_s / 2$.

**(3 marks breakdown: 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for aliasing explanation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 regulations**, Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and offer an **internal choice** (either **Question A** or **Question B**). Both options are presented below for full practice.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a biomedical Data Acquisition System. Describe the role of each block in the context of acquiring an ECG signal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **biomedical Data Acquisition System (biomedical DAS)** is a specialised electronic system designed to capture, condition, digitise, and process bio-electric and bio-physical signals from the human body. The block diagram is as follows:

```
[Patient / Body]  →  [Sensor / Electrodes]
       →  [Signal Conditioning]  →  [MUX]  →  [S/H]  →  [ADC]
       →  [Microcontroller]  →  [Display]  →  [Storage / Wireless]
```

**Block-by-block description in ECG context:**

1. **Sensor / Electrodes (1 mark):** Ag/AgCl surface electrodes pick up the differential cardiac potential between two points on the body (e.g., Right Arm and Left Arm). The signal amplitude is **~ 1 mV** and frequency content is **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**.

2. **Signal Conditioning (2 marks):** This is the most critical stage.
   - **Instrumentation Amplifier (e.g., AD620):** Amplifies the 1 mV signal with a gain of ~ 1000 to produce a 1 V signal. Provides high input impedance and CMRR > 100 dB.
   - **High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz):** Removes the DC electrode half-cell potential (~ 300 mV) to prevent IA saturation.
   - **Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz):** Acts as anti-aliasing filter.
   - **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Rejects Indian/European mains interference.
   - **Isolation Barrier (optical or transformer):** Patient safety per IEC 60601-1.

3. **Multiplexer (1 mark):** For a **12-lead ECG**, the system scans 8 signal channels + 4 lead-computed channels through one MUX (e.g., CD4051).

4. **Sample-and-Hold (0.5 mark):** Holds the analogue voltage steady for the ADC's conversion time.

5. **ADC (1 mark):** **16-bit SAR ADC** (e.g., AD7606) samples at **500 Hz** per channel, giving LSB = 5 V / 65536 ≈ **76 µV** — small enough to resolve all ECG features.

6. **Microcontroller (0.5 mark):** STM32 / ESP32 runs **DSP filters**, detects **R-peaks**, computes **heart rate**, applies **Pan-Tompkins QRS algorithm**, and controls display/IoT.

7. **Display / Storage / Wireless (1 mark):** TFT/LCD shows the live waveform, SD card stores 24-hour Holter data, and BLE/Wi-Fi transmits to the cloud for remote monitoring.

**(Total 7 marks distributed across the seven blocks.)**

---

**Q. (b)** An ECG signal has a maximum frequency of 150 Hz. Calculate the minimum sampling rate, a practical sampling rate using 3× oversampling, and the cutoff frequency of the anti-aliasing filter. If a 12-bit ADC is used with a 5 V reference, calculate the LSB, peak quantisation error, and the SQNR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Nyquist rate (1 mark):**

$$f_s^{min} \;\;=\;\; 2 \cdot f_{max} \;\;=\;\; 2 \cdot 150 \;\;=\;\; 300\;\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Practical rate (1 mark):**

$$f_s^{practical} \;\;=\;\; 3 \cdot 150 \;\;=\;\; 450\;\text{Hz} \;\;\approx\;\; 500\;\text{Hz}\;(\text{clinical standard})$$

**Step 3 — Anti-aliasing cutoff (1 mark):**

$$f_c \;\;\leq\;\; \frac{f_s}{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{500}{2} \;\;=\;\; 250\;\text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — LSB of 12-bit ADC (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta V \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{12}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{5}{4096} \;\;=\;\; 1.2207\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 5 — Peak quantisation error (1 mark):**

$$e_{q,\,max} \;\;=\;\; \frac{\Delta V}{2} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1.2207}{2} \;\;=\;\; 0.6104\;\text{mV}$$

**Step 6 — SQNR (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\;=\;\; 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 \;\;=\;\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;\;=\;\; 74\;\text{dB}$$

**Engineering comment:** The LSB of 1.22 mV is comparable to the ECG amplitude. To use the ADC's full dynamic range, the front-end IA must amplify the 1 mV ECG to nearly 5 V, requiring a gain of ~ 5000 (typically a 2-stage amplifier: INA gain 1000 + post-amp gain 5).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier. Why is it preferred for biomedical signal acquisition over a simple difference amplifier? Derive the expression for its differential gain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of the 3-op-amp INA (3 marks):**

The 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier consists of **two input buffer stages** (op-amps A1, A2) feeding a **unity-gain difference amplifier** (A3).

- **Input stage:** The two input op-amps form a high-impedance differential amplifier. Resistors $R_1$ connect from each input to a shared gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between the two inverting inputs. The signal current through $R_G$ is the same as the current through the two $R_1$ resistors, and a single resistor $R_G$ therefore sets the differential gain.
- **Output stage:** Op-amp A3 is a standard difference amplifier with four matched resistors (typically $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5$) that subtracts the two input signals and converts the differential signal to a single-ended output referenced to ground.

**Why INA is preferred for biomedical signals (2 marks):**

1. **Very high input impedance** — both inputs see op-amp non-inverting terminals, so input impedance is > 1 GΩ. Essential for **high-impedance EEG electrodes** where any loading would distort the µV-level signal.
2. **Single resistor $R_G$ sets the gain** — gain can be tuned without disturbing the CMRR.
3. **Excellent CMRR** (> 100 dB) — critical to reject 50 Hz mains pickup on the body.
4. **Low noise** — critical for ECG/EEG where the signal is in the µV range.
5. **No need for precisely matched input resistors** — a simple difference amplifier requires $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_3 = R_4$ precisely, which is hard to achieve in practice.

**Derivation of differential gain (2 marks):**

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the two input voltages.

Current through $R_G$:

$$I \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

This same current flows through the two $R_1$ resistors:

$$V_{out1} - V_{out2} \;\;=\;\; (V_1 - V_2) + 2 I R_1 \;\;=\;\; (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

The difference amplifier (A3) then gives:

$$V_{out} \;\;=\;\; (V_{out2} - V_{out1}) \cdot \frac{R_5}{R_2}$$

After KCL at the two nodes of A3 (with $R_2 = R_3$ and $R_4 = R_5$):

$$V_{out} \;\;=\;\; (V_2 - V_1) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;A_v \;\;=\;\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} \;\;=\;\; 1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\;}$$

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT-enabled wearable biomedical Data Acquisition System. Discuss the role of edge computing and list two popular IoT protocols used for biomedical data transmission. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Bio-sensors on body]
       ↓
[Analog Front End: IA, Filters, Isolation]
       ↓
[24-bit Sigma-Delta ADC]
       ↓
[BLE SoC / Microcontroller]  ←  [Edge DSP: QRS detection, FFT]
       ↓
[BLE 4.2 / 5.0 Link]
       ↓
[Smartphone App / Hub Gateway]
       ↓ (WiFi / 4G / 5G)
[Cloud Server: AWS IoT / Azure]
       ↓
[Database + ML Analytics]
       ↓
[Clinician Dashboard + Alerts]
```

**Layer-wise explanation (2 marks):**

1. **Edge Layer (Wearable):** Sensors → AFE → ADC → MCU. Performs **local signal processing** such as R-peak detection, heart-rate computation, motion artefact rejection.
2. **Gateway Layer:** Smartphone or hub aggregates data from multiple wearables, formats it (JSON over MQTT), and uplinks to the cloud.
3. **Cloud Layer:** Receives data via MQTT broker, runs **time-series storage (InfluxDB)**, **ML-based anomaly detection**, and issues **alerts (SMS / push notifications)** when parameters cross thresholds (e.g., HR > 120 bpm sustained for 5 min).

**Role of edge computing (1 mark):**

- Reduces data rate to the cloud by **100 – 1000×** (raw ECG at 500 Hz × 16 bits × 8 channels = 64 kbps; edge QRS features are only ~ 100 bps).
- Saves **battery** and **cellular data cost**.
- Provides **real-time local response** (e.g., audio alarm for arrhythmia).
- **Enhances privacy** — sensitive raw waveforms stay on the device.

**Two popular IoT protocols (1 mark):**
1. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport):** Lightweight publish/subscribe protocol over TCP. Port 1883. Used in 80 % of biomedical IoT deployments (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT).
2. **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol):** REST-like protocol over UDP. Used in low-power lossy networks (6LoWPAN) typical of body-area networks (BANs).

**Optional bonus protocols:** HTTP/HTTPS, AMQP, LwM2M, Zigbee, BLE GATT.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings / Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Data Acquisition questions — and how to avoid it:**

| # | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | How to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forgetting to draw the block diagram** in 14-mark answers. | 2 – 3 marks | Always draw a **labelled** block diagram — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own. |
| 2 | **Confusing *sampling rate* with *Nyquist rate*.** Sampling rate = actual rate used; Nyquist rate = minimum theoretical 2f_max. | 1 mark | Use the exact phrase "Nyquist rate is the *minimum*; practical rate is **higher**." |
| 3 | **Wrong units in RC filter** — using $C$ in nF without conversion. | 1 – 2 marks | Always show the unit conversion: $C = 100\;\text{nF} = 100 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{F}$. |
| 4 | **In INA derivation, getting the sign of $V_{out}$ wrong** (positive vs negative). | 1 mark | Re-derive using KCL carefully; the convention is $A_v = (V_2 - V_1) \cdot (1 + 2R_1/R_G)$. |
| 5 | **Not mentioning anti-aliasing filter** in ADC discussions. | 1 mark | The phrase "anti-aliasing LPF before the ADC with cutoff $f_s/2$" is **mandatory**. |
| 6 | **Confusing SQNR with SNR** — SQNR is the theoretical quantisation-noise-limited ratio; SNR is the measured ratio including thermal noise. | 0.5 – 1 mark | Use the exact terms; the formula 6.02 n + 1.76 dB applies **only to ideal SQNR**. |
| 7 | **Citing wrong sampling rates for biosignals** — e.g., saying ECG is sampled at 1 kHz when standard is 500 Hz. | 0.5 mark | Memorise standard rates: ECG = 500 Hz, EEG = 250 Hz, EMG = 1 kHz, NIBP = 100 Hz. |
| 8 | **Omitting patient safety (IEC 60601-1) and isolation** in biomedical DAQ answers. | 1 – 2 marks | Always end biomedical answers with: *"Patient isolation and IEC 60601-1 compliance are essential for safety."* |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and pin it to your study wall — these are the most-asked KTU points on Data Acquisition.**

### A. Core Definitions
- [ ] **Data Acquisition System (DAS):** the integrated system that **samples, conditions, digitises, processes, and outputs** real-world signals.
- [ ] **Sensor vs Transducer:** A sensor's output is a measurable electrical quantity; a transducer is the broader term (any energy converter).
- [ ] **Active vs Passive sensors:** Active sensors need external power (e.g., strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge); passive sensors generate their own voltage (e.g., piezoelectric, thermocouple).
- [ ] **Biomedical DAS:** specialised DAS for **ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG, SpO₂, BP, temperature, respiration, GSR, glucose**.

### B. The Seven Block Chain (Always Memorise)
- [ ] **Sensor → Signal Conditioning → MUX → Sample & Hold → ADC → Controller → Display/Storage/Network**

### C. Sampling and Quantisation
- [ ] **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — board exam favourite.
- [ ] **Practical sampling:** $f_s = (3 \text{ to } 5) \cdot f_{max}$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter cutoff:** $f_c = f_s / 2$.
- [ ] **LSB / Quantisation step:** $\Delta V = V_{REF} / 2^n$.
- [ ] **Peak quantisation error:** $\Delta V / 2$.
- [ ] **SQNR formula:** $6.02\, n + 1.76\;\text{dB}$.

### D. Signal Conditioning Essentials
- [ ] **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** $A_v = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$. Best for biomedical.
- [ ] **CMRR:** $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ dB. Aim for > 100 dB.
- [ ] **Filters:** HPF (0.05 Hz ECG), LPF (150 Hz ECG), Notch (50 Hz).
- [ ] **Isolation:** opto-isolator / transformer — IEC 60601-1 safety.
- [ ] **Right-Leg Drive (RLD):** feedback to patient's body to cancel 50 Hz common-mode.

### E. ADC Choices
- [ ] **Flash** = fast, low resolution → ultrasound.
- [ ] **SAR** = balanced, 12–18 bit, medium speed → ECG, NIBP.
- [ ] **Sigma-Delta** = 16–24 bit, slow, low noise → EEG, weight scale.
- [ ] **Dual-Slope** = integrating, slow, DMMs.
- [ ] **Pipeline** = fast, video/medical imaging.

### F. IoT Integration
- [ ] **Edge computing** reduces data, saves power, improves privacy.
- [ ] **MQTT** — most common biomedical IoT protocol.
- [ ] **CoAP** — for low-power BANs.
- [ ] **BLE** — for wearable-to-phone link.
- [ ] **Wi-Fi / 4G / 5G** — for phone-to-cloud uplink.

### G. Standard Sampling Rates
- [ ] **ECG:** 500 Hz
- [ ] **EEG:** 250 Hz (clinical), 1 kHz–30 kHz (research)
- [ ] **EMG:** 1 kHz
- [ ] **PPG / SpO₂:** 100 – 500 Hz
- [ ] **NIBP (cuff):** 100 Hz
- [ ] **Temperature:** 1 Hz
- [ ] **Audio (heart sounds, lung sounds):** 44.1 kHz

### H. Safety and Standards
- [ ] **IEC 60601-1** — general electrical safety for medical electrical equipment.
- [ ] **Patient leakage current limit:** ≤ 100 µA (Type B), ≤ 10 µA (Type CF, direct cardiac).
- [ ] **Defibrillator-proof** — equipment must survive 5 kV defibrillator pulses.
- [ ] **ISO 13485** — quality management for medical devices.
- [ ] **AAMI EC-13** — American standard for ECG cables/leadwires.

### I. Numerical Problem Shortcuts
- [ ] **2× Nyquist** = theoretical minimum; **3–5× Nyquist** = practical.
- [ ] **6 dB per bit** = SQNR rule.
- [ ] **Bit rate of PCM:** $f_s \cdot n \cdot k$ (with $k$ = number of channels).
- [ ] **HPF cutoff = 1 / (2πRC)**.

### J. The "Must-Draw" Diagrams
- [ ] **Block diagram of generic DAQ** (sensor → ADC → MCU → display/IoT).
- [ ] **3-op-amp INA circuit** with all resistors labelled ($R_1, R_G, R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$).
- [ ] **12-lead ECG DAQ** (electrodes → IA → filters → MUX → ADC).
- [ ] **IoT DAQ layered architecture** (edge → gateway → cloud).

---

> **End of Module 4 — Data Acquisition. Proceed to the next module after a 10-minute break and a quick re-read of Section 5.4 (the recap). Best of luck for your KTU exams!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Data Storage # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Storage in IoT** refers to the systematic acquisition, organization, persistence, retrieval, and management of heterogeneous data streams generated by distributed IoT devices, sensors, and biomedical instruments across a multi-tier storage architecture (Edge → Fog → Cloud) to ensure data **availability**, **durability**, **consistency**, and **low-latency access** for real-time analytics, decision-making, and long-term archival. In the **biomedical context**, IoT data storage must additionally satisfy regulatory and interoperability standards such as **HIPAA** (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act), **HL7 FHIR** (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources), and **DICOM** (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine). The three primary storage tiers recognized in the KTU 2024 scheme are: | Tier | Location | Latency | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Edge Storage** | On-device (sensor / gateway) | **< 1 ms** | Real-time buffering, transient caching | | **Fog Storage** | LAN-level (gateway / fog node) | **1–10 ms** | Local aggregation, time-windowed analytics | | **Cloud Storage** | Remote data center | **50–500 ms** | Long-term archival, big-data analytics, ML training | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital Library" 🏥📚 Imagine a smart hospital as a **giant library**: - **Edge storage** is the *nurse's pocket notebook* — quick, immediate, but limited in capacity. A patient's wearable ECG device stores the last **30 seconds** of heart rhythm on its local flash memory. - **Fog storage** is the *ward-level filing cabinet* — a gateway at each hospital ward aggregates data from dozens of devices every few minutes, enabling shift-level analysis. - **Cloud storage** is the *central national medical archive* — every patient's longitudinal history (10+ years) is stored in a Tier-3 data center, accessible to any authorized physician worldwide. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world mapping:** A continuous glucose monitor (CGM) like Dexcom G7 stores **~8 hours** of data on-device (Edge), syncs to a phone every **5 minutes** (Fog), and uploads to the cloud for endocrinology review (Cloud). This three-tier flow is the canonical IoT biomedical storage pipeline. ## 1.3 Key Storage Metrics (Standard KTU High-Yield Definitions) > [!IMPORTANT] > The five standard metrics used by KTU examiners to evaluate any storage system: > 1. **Capacity** — Maximum volume of data (in TB / PB) the system can hold. > 2. **Throughput** — Rate of successful data delivery (MB/s or IOPS). > 3. **Latency** — Time delay between a write request and its acknowledgment. > 4. **Durability** — Probability that data remains intact over **10 years** (e.g., AWS S3 = **99.999999999%** = "eleven nines"). > 5. **Consistency** — Degree to which all replicas of a data item return the same value at the same time (governed by the **CAP Theorem**). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Multi-Tier IoT Storage Architecture (End-to-End Flow) The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a structured understanding of the **three-tier hierarchy**. Below is the operational logic broken into discrete steps: ### **Step 1 — Data Ingestion at the Edge** - Sensors (e.g., MAX30102 for SpO₂, AD8232 for ECG) sample at rates from **1 Hz to 1000 Hz**. - Raw samples are written into a **ring buffer** in non-volatile memory (EEPROM / Flash). - Local compression (e.g., **delta encoding** or **LZW**) reduces payload size by **40–70%** before transmission. ### **Step 2 — Fog / Gateway Aggregation** - Edge gateways (Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano) collect data from multiple sensors. - The gateway performs **temporal alignment**, **outlier rejection**, and **feature extraction** (e.g., R-R interval from ECG). - Data is temporarily persisted in a **time-series database** (e.g., InfluxDB) for local dashboards. ### **Step 3 — Cloud Persistence & Analytics** - Long-term data is transmitted via **MQTT / CoAP** over TLS to a cloud broker. - Data lands in a **Data Lake** (raw, schema-less) and a **Data Warehouse** (structured, OLAP-optimized). - Analytics engines (Apache Spark, Flink) and ML pipelines (TensorFlow, PyTorch) consume the stored data. > [!NOTE] > **Why three tiers?** A 2024 study by IEEE IoT Journal showed that pushing just **20% of analytics** to the edge reduces cloud bandwidth costs by **~65%** and decision latency by **~8x**. ## 2.2 Database Taxonomy for IoT The KTU 2024 module specifically highlights the following four database paradigms: | Database Type | Schema | Best For | IoT Examples | Example Products | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Relational (RDBMS)** | Fixed, tabular | Structured transactional data | Patient registration, billing | PostgreSQL, MySQL | | **NoSQL — Key-Value** | Schema-less | High-throughput caching, sessions | Device state, session tokens | Redis, Amazon DynamoDB | | **NoSQL — Document** | JSON / BSON | Semi-structured sensor records | CGM logs, FHIR bundles | MongoDB, CouchDB | | **Time-Series (TSDB)** | Time-indexed | Sequential sensor streams | ECG, temperature, vibration | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, OpenTSDB | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** A *time-series database* is **NOT** a regular relational database. It uses **time as the primary index** and employs compression algorithms (e.g., Gorilla compression, Facebook's paper 2015) achieving up to **90% storage reduction** for repeated float values. ## 2.3 CAP Theorem & Storage Trade-offs The **CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide only **two** out of the following **three guarantees**: $$\boxed{C + A + P \leq 2}$$ Where: - **C** = **Consistency** — Every read receives the most recent write. - **A** = **Availability** — Every request receives a (non-error) response. - **P** = **Partition tolerance** — The system continues to operate despite network failures. > [!NOTE] > Since **network partitions are inevitable** in distributed IoT systems, the real choice is **CP vs. AP**, not CA. **MongoDB defaults to CP**, while **Cassandra defaults to AP**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table) | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Data Volume per Day** | $D_{day} = N \times f_s \times B \times 86400$ | Bytes/day | $N$ = sensors, $f_s$ = sampling freq (Hz), $B$ = bits/sample | | 2 | **Data Rate (per sensor)** | $R = f_s \times B$ | bits/sec (bps) | Direct Shannon-rate analog | | 3 | **Storage Capacity with Replication** | $C_{total} = C_{logical} \times R_f$ | TB | $R_f$ = replication factor (commonly 3) | | 4 | **Data Compression Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Dimensionless | Higher = better compression | | 5 | **Write Latency (queuing)** | $L_{total} = L_{service} + \dfrac{L_{service}^2 \times \lambda}{1 - \rho}$ | seconds | M/M/1 queue; $\rho = \lambda \mu$ | | 6 | **Durability (multi-region)** | $P_{loss} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Probability | $p_i$ = independent region loss prob | | 7 | **Energy per Bit Storage** | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{P_{write}}{R_{write}}$ | Joules/bit | Critical for battery-powered edge | | 8 | **Shannon-Hartley (bandwidth check)** | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/sec | Validates if link can sustain $R$ | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe `\|` Substitution Mandate:** In all KTU answer scripts, absolute value and determinant bars are typed as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\lvert x \rvert$) — never as `|x|` — to avoid LaTeX rendering errors in the digital valuation portal. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Insulin Pumps (Medtronic 780G):** Store **90 days** of CGM + insulin data on-device, sync to cloud for endocrinology AI. - **Hospital PACS (Picture Archiving & Communication System):** Stores DICOM imaging data — a single CT scan can be **~150 MB**, requiring **petascale** archival storage. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT) Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration sensors at **10 kHz** generate **~3.4 GB/day per machine**, demanding time-series storage with downsampling. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Daily Data Volume from an IoT Biomedical Sensor Network **Problem Statement:** A remote patient monitoring (RPM) system uses **$N = 50$** wearable sensors. Each sensor samples at **$f_s = 250$ Hz** with a **$B = 16$-bit** ADC resolution. Compute the total daily data volume $D_{day}$ before and after applying **delta-encoding compression with CR = 4**. ### **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** We use the canonical IoT throughput identity: $$D_{day} = N \cdot f_s \cdot B \cdot T_{day}$$ where $T_{day}$ is the seconds in a day. ### **Step 2 — Substitute the known values** $$D_{day} = 50 \times 250 \times 16 \times 86400$$ ### **Step 3 — Perform the multiplication in stages** First, compute the single-sensor per-second bit rate: $$R_{single} = f_s \times B = 250 \times 16 = 4000 \text{ bits/sec} = 4 \text{ kbps}$$ Next, scale to 50 sensors: $$R_{total} = 50 \times 4000 = 200{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} = 200 \text{ kbps}$$ Finally, scale to one day: $$D_{day} = 200{,}000 \times 86400 = 1.728 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits/day}$$ ### **Step 4 — Convert to practical storage units** $$D_{day} = \dfrac{1.728 \times 10^{10}}{8} = 2.16 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 2.16 \text{ GB/day}$$ ### **Step 5 — Apply compression** $$D_{compressed} = \dfrac{D_{day}}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{2.16}{4} = 0.54 \text{ GB/day}$$ ### **Step 6 — Scale to one year and apply replication factor $R_f = 3$** $$D_{year,raw} = 2.16 \times 365 = 788.4 \text{ GB/year}$$ $$C_{total} = 788.4 \times 3 = 2365.2 \text{ GB} \approx 2.37 \text{ TB/year (with 3x replication)}$$ $$\boxed{D_{day} = 2.16 \text{ GB/day}, \quad D_{compressed} = 0.54 \text{ GB/day}, \quad C_{yearly} \approx 2.37 \text{ TB}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage System Availability from Redundancy (MTTF / MTTR Model) **Problem Statement:** A cloud region has an individual node **Mean Time To Failure MTTF = 10,000 hours** and **Mean Time To Repair MTTR = 10 hours**. A system uses **$n = 3$** such nodes in parallel. Compute the **single-node availability** and the **triple-redundant system availability**. ### **Step 1 — Single-node availability formula** $$A_{single} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ ### **Step 2 — Substitute the values** $$A_{single} = \dfrac{10000}{10000 + 10} = \dfrac{10000}{10010} \approx 0.999001$$ ### **Step 3 — Single-node unavailability** $$U_{single} = 1 - A_{single} = 1 - 0.999001 = 9.99 \times 10^{-4}$$ ### **Step 4 — Triple-redundant system unavailability (all three must fail)** For $n$ parallel nodes, the system is down **only when ALL nodes fail simultaneously**. Assuming independent failures: $$U_{system} = (U_{single})^n$$ $$U_{system} = (9.99 \times 10^{-4})^3 \approx 9.97 \times 10^{-10}$$ ### **Step 5 — Triple-redundant system availability** $$A_{system} = 1 - U_{system} = 1 - 9.97 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.999999999$$ $$\boxed{A_{single} \approx 99.90\%, \quad A_{triple} \approx 99.9999999\% \text{ (nine nines)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why cloud providers (AWS, Azure) guarantee **"eleven nines"** durability — by combining **multi-region replication** ($n = 3$–$5$) with **Erasure Coding**, the failure probability becomes astronomically small. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link Feasibility Check (Shannon-Hartley Theorem) **Problem Statement:** Verify whether a **LoRaWAN channel with bandwidth $B = 125$ kHz** and **SNR = 10 dB** can sustain the 4 kbps data rate of a single ECG sensor (from Derivation 1). ### **Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale** $$\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{dB}/10} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$ ### **Step 2 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley theorem** $$C_{max} = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ $$C_{max} = 125{,}000 \times \log_2(1 + 10)$$ ### **Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm** $$\log_2(11) = \dfrac{\ln 11}{\ln 2} \approx \dfrac{2.3979}{0.6931} \approx 3.459$$ ### **Step 4 — Compute maximum capacity** $$C_{max} = 125{,}000 \times 3.459 \approx 432{,}438 \text{ bps} \approx 432.4 \text{ kbps}$$ ### **Step 5 — Compare with required data rate** $$R_{required} = 4 \text{ kbps} \ll C_{max} = 432.4 \text{ kbps}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Link is feasible with } \sim 108\times \text{ headroom margin.}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A single LoRaWAN channel can simultaneously support **~108 ECG sensors** at 250 Hz / 16-bit. This is the **multi-tenant scalability** justification used in IoT biomedical literature. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Storage Backend | Selection Criterion | Edge (SQLite) | Fog (InfluxDB) | Cloud (S3 + Athena) | Cloud (Cassandra) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Latency** | **< 1 ms** | 1–10 ms | 50–500 ms | 10–50 ms | | **Data Volume** | MB–GB | GB–TB | **TB–PB** | **TB–PB** | | **Query Type** | Simple key lookup | Time-range SQL | OLAP / Ad-hoc | CQL / Wide-column | | **CAP Profile** | CA (single node) | AP | Configurable | **AP (tunable)** | | **Best For** | Wearable buffer | Ward-level dashboard | ML training lake | Multi-region telemetry | | **Cost per GB/month** | $0 (device) | $0.10 | **$0.023** (S3 Standard) | $0.10 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Tier IoT-Biomedical Data Storage Architecture The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the end-to-end data flow from sensor acquisition to cloud persistence, with explicit storage technology mappings at each tier. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph E1["EDGE TIER - On Device"] S1["Sensor A: ECG AD8232"] S2["Sensor B: SpO2 MAX30102"] S3["Sensor C: Temp DS18B20"] BUF["Ring Buffer 8MB Flash"] CMP["Delta Encoder CR=4"] S1 --> BUF S2 --> BUF S3 --> BUF BUF --> CMP end subgraph F1["FOG TIER - Gateway Raspberry Pi"] GW["MQTT Broker Mosquitto"] TS["Time Series DB InfluxDB"] DSH["Local Grafana Dashboard"] ALERT["Threshold Alert Engine"] CMP -->|TLS over WiFi| GW GW --> TS TS --> DSH TS --> ALERT end subgraph C1["CLOUD TIER - AWS Azure GCP"] INGEST["Kafka Ingestion Topic"] LAKE["Data Lake S3 Raw Zone"] WARE["Data Warehouse Redshift"] ML["ML Pipeline SageMaker"] FHIR["FHIR R4 API Server"] GW -->|MQTT over TLS| INGEST INGEST --> LAKE LAKE --> WARE WARE --> ML WARE --> FHIR end subgraph U1["CONSUMERS"] DOC["Physician Dashboard"] PAT["Patient Mobile App"] RES["Researcher Jupyter"] FHIR --> DOC FHIR --> PAT ML --> RES end ``` ## 4.2 Storage Decision Flowchart — Database Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is data strongly typed
and relational?"} A1["Use RDBMS
PostgreSQL MySQL"] Q2{"Is data primarily
time indexed?"} A2["Use TSDB
InfluxDB TimescaleDB"] Q3{"Is data
document or JSON?"} A3["Use Document Store
MongoDB CouchDB"] Q4{"Need extreme
write throughput?"} A4["Use Key Value
DynamoDB Redis Cassandra"] Q5{"Archival cost
is the priority?"} A5["Use Object Storage
S3 Glacier Azure Blob"] Q1 -->|Yes| A1 Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| A2 Q2 -->|No| Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| A3 Q3 -->|No| Q4 Q4 -->|Yes| A4 Q4 -->|No| Q5 ``` ## 4.3 Data Lifecycle Management (DLM) State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> HOT: Ingest from sensor HOT: HOT Storage - SSD - Frequent Access - Days 0 to 7 WARM: WARM Storage - HDD - Infrequent Access - Days 8 to 90 COLD: COLD Storage - Tape or Object - Rare Access - Days 91 to 365 ARCHIVE: ARCHIVE - Glacier - Compliance Only - Year 2 onward HOT --> WARM: Age 7 days WARM --> COLD: Age 90 days COLD --> ARCHIVE: Age 1 year ARCHIVE --> [*]: Retention policy expires HOT --> HOT: Read or update WARM --> WARM: Periodic query ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram-to-Text Mapping:** Edge devices (ECG, SpO₂, Temperature) push data through a ring buffer and delta encoder before relaying to a fog gateway running **InfluxDB** and **Grafana**. The gateway then forwards via **MQTT over TLS** to a cloud **Kafka** topic, which fans out to a **Data Lake (S3)**, a **Data Warehouse (Redshift)**, an **ML pipeline (SageMaker)**, and a **FHIR R4 API** for downstream clinical consumers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] **Define IoT data storage and explain the three-tier storage architecture with one biomedical example for each tier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** IoT data storage refers to the systematic persistence, organization, and retrieval of data generated by IoT devices across multiple storage tiers — Edge, Fog, and Cloud — to meet application-specific latency, durability, and scalability requirements. **Three-tier architecture (2 marks):** - **Edge Tier:** Storage on the device itself. *Example:* A wearable ECG patch stores the last 30 seconds of cardiac rhythm in 8 MB onboard flash. - **Fog Tier:** Storage at the LAN gateway. *Example:* A ward-level Raspberry Pi running InfluxDB aggregates SpO₂ data from 20 patients every 5 minutes. - **Cloud Tier:** Storage in remote data centers. *Example:* AWS S3 archives 10 years of longitudinal diabetic patient data for endocrinology research. --- ### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] **Compare Time-Series Databases (TSDB) and Relational Databases (RDBMS) in the context of IoT applications. State any two advantages of TSDB.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | RDBMS | TSDB | |---|---|---| | **Primary index** | Primary key | **Time stamp** | | **Write throughput** | ~1k rows/s | **> 1M points/s** | | **Compression** | None built-in | **Gorilla / Delta-of-delta (~90%)** | | **Schema flexibility** | Rigid | **Tag-based, schema-on-read** | **Two advantages of TSDB (2 marks):** 1. **Native time-based compression** — Reduces storage cost by up to **90%** for sequential float values. 2. **Built-in time-windowed queries** — Functions like `moving_average()`, `derivative()`, and `group_by_time()` eliminate the need for application-level aggregation. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by failing to state **units** in derivations (always write MB, GB, ms, bps explicitly). > - Avoid writing "Cloud is best" without justification — KTU examiners award marks for **CAP-theorem trade-off reasoning**. > - In compression questions, **never** state the compression ratio without showing the **before-and-after** numerical comparison. > - Always include **HIPAA / HL7-FHIR** compliance discussion for biomedical storage answers — this is a **high-frequency 2-mark value-addition** expected by the 2024 scheme examiners. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 (Model)] **OR** ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 (Model)] #### **(A) Part (a) — 7 marks** *(For Question A)* **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** A smart ICU monitors **$N = 20$** patients. Each patient is connected to **4 sensors** (ECG, SpO₂, BP, Temperature) sampling at **$f_s = 500$ Hz** with **$B = 12$ bits/sample**. Compute: - (i) The daily data volume per patient. (3 marks) - (ii) The annual cloud storage required with **replication factor $R_f = 3$** and **compression ratio CR = 5**. (4 marks) **Complete Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Per-sensor data rate:** $$R_{sensor} = f_s \times B = 500 \times 12 = 6000 \text{ bps} = 6 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 2 — Per-patient data rate (4 sensors):** $$R_{patient} = 4 \times 6000 = 24{,}000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 3 — Daily volume per patient in bytes:** $$D_{patient} = \dfrac{24{,}000 \times 86400}{8} = 2.592 \times 10^8 \text{ bytes} \approx 259.2 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Step 4 — Total daily volume (20 patients):** $$D_{day,total} = 20 \times 259.2 = 5184 \text{ MB/day} \approx 5.18 \text{ GB/day}$$ **Step 5 — Annual raw volume (no compression, no replication):** $$D_{year,raw} = 5.18 \times 365 = 1890.7 \text{ GB/year} \approx 1.89 \text{ TB/year}$$ **Step 6 — Apply compression (CR = 5):** $$D_{year,compressed} = \dfrac{1.89}{5} = 0.378 \text{ TB/year}$$ **Step 7 — Apply replication factor (R_f = 3):** $$C_{cloud} = 0.378 \times 3 = 1.134 \text{ TB/year}$$ $$\boxed{D_{patient} \approx 259.2 \text{ MB/day}, \quad C_{cloud} \approx 1.13 \text{ TB/year}}$$ #### **(A) Part (b) — 7 marks** *(For Question A)* **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** Explain the **CAP Theorem** with respect to IoT data storage. Compare **MongoDB (CP)** and **Cassandra (AP)** and justify which is more suitable for a **multi-continent telemedicine platform**. **Complete Model Solution:** **CAP Theorem (3 marks):** The CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee **only two of the three** properties: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)**. Since network partitions are inevitable in distributed IoT systems, the practical choice reduces to **CP vs AP**. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Property | MongoDB (CP) | Cassandra (AP) | |---|---|---| | **Consistency** | Strong (default) | **Tunable** (eventual by default) | | **Availability** | Reduced during partitions | **Always-on** | | **Write Latency** | Higher (sync replication) | **Lower** (async, log-structured) | | **Multi-Region** | Master-slave (single primary) | **Masterless** (peer-to-peer) | | **Best Use** | Banking, billing, prescriptions | Telemetry, sensor streams, telemedicine | **Justification for Telemedicine (2 marks):** A multi-continent telemedicine platform requires **always-on availability** (a physician in Tokyo must access patient data even if the Frankfurt region is partitioned) and **masterless multi-region writes**. **Cassandra's AP nature** with tunable consistency (e.g., `LOCAL_QUORUM`) makes it more suitable than MongoDB, which would experience primary-failover downtime. #### **(B) Part (a) — 7 marks** *(For Question B — Alternative)* **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** Describe the **HIPAA-compliant storage requirements** for biomedical IoT data. List any **four technical safeguards** mandated by HIPAA for cloud-hosted medical records. **Complete Model Solution:** **HIPAA Overview (2 marks):** The **Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Security Rule** mandates administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect **Electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI)**. For IoT biomedical data, ePHI includes ECG streams, SpO₂ logs, and any other PHI transmitted or stored electronically. **Four Technical Safeguards (4 marks):** 1. **Access Control (45 CFR § 164.312(a))** — Implement **role-based access control (RBAC)** with unique user IDs, emergency access procedures, automatic logoff, and **AES-256 encryption** for data at rest. 2. **Audit Controls (§ 164.312(b))** — Hardware, software, and procedural mechanisms that record and examine activity in information systems containing ePHI (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with immutable logs). 3. **Integrity Controls (§ 164.312(c))** — Mechanisms to verify that ePHI has **not been altered or destroyed** in an unauthorized manner (e.g., SHA-256 hashing, digital signatures, WORM storage). 4. **Transmission Security (§ 164.312(e))** — End-to-end encryption (**TLS 1.3**) and integrity protection for ePHI transmitted over electronic networks. **Real-world mapping (1 mark):** A hospital using **AWS HealthLake** must enable **KMS-managed encryption**, **CloudTrail audit logging**, **IAM-based RBAC**, and **TLS 1.3** endpoints to satisfy HIPAA technical safeguards. #### **(B) Part (b) — 7 marks** *(For Question B — Alternative)* **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** A regional hospital network generates **2 TB of DICOM imaging data per day** across 5 hospitals. Design a **multi-tier storage architecture** with specific technology choices and justify each tier with cost, latency, and compliance considerations. **Complete Model Solution:** **Architecture Design (4 marks):** | Tier | Technology | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Edge (Modality)** | Local DICOM cache on CT/MRI console | Provides **sub-second access** for in-progress scans | | **Fog (Hospital PACS)** | On-premise PACS server (e.g., Orthanc) with **5 TB SSD** | **< 10 ms** retrieval for active studies; HIPAA-compliant on-prem control | | **Warm Cloud** | AWS S3 Standard-IA | **$0.0125/GB/month**, 99.9% availability, immediate retrieval for recent archives (30–90 days) | | **Cold Cloud (Long-term)** | AWS S3 Glacier Deep Archive | **$0.00099/GB/month**, 12-hour retrieval, ideal for **7-year HIPAA retention** | **Cost Calculation (2 marks):** $$D_{year,raw} = 2 \text{ TB} \times 365 = 730 \text{ TB/year}$$ After **5 years** with replication factor 2 (for disaster recovery): $$C_{5yr} = 730 \times 5 \times 2 = 7300 \text{ TB} = 7.3 \text{ PB}$$ **Monthly cold-storage cost:** $$\text{Cost} = 7300 \times 1024 \times \$0.00099 \approx \$7,400/\text{month}$$ **Compliance (1 mark):** All tiers must implement **AES-256 at rest**, **TLS 1.3 in transit**, and a **Business Associate Agreement (BAA)** with the cloud provider — both AWS and Azure offer HIPAA-eligible BAA-covered services. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam** - ✅ **Three storage tiers** = Edge (flash, < 1 ms) → Fog (gateway TSDB, 1–10 ms) → Cloud (data lake/warehouse, 50–500 ms). - ✅ **TSDB vs RDBMS:** TSDB is **time-indexed**, supports **Gorilla compression (~90% savings)**, and offers built-in time-windowed SQL functions. - ✅ **CAP Theorem:** $C + A + P \leq 2$ — IoT systems almost always choose **AP** (Cassandra) or **CP** (MongoDB); CA is unrealistic in distributed settings. - ✅ **Data volume formula:** $D_{day} = N \times f_s \times B \times 86400$ (bits/day); divide by 8 for bytes; apply CR and $R_f$ for cloud sizing. - ✅ **Availability from redundancy:** $A_{system} = 1 - (U_{single})^n$ — triple replication yields "nine nines" availability. - ✅ **Shannon-Hartley check:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ must exceed the per-sensor bit rate to confirm link feasibility. - ✅ **HIPAA four technical safeguards:** Access Control, Audit Controls, Integrity Controls, Transmission Security — must be cited in **every biomedical storage answer**. - ✅ **DICOM** (~150 MB/scan) and **HL7 FHIR** are the two **non-negotiable** standards for biomedical IoT storage interoperability. - ✅ **Replication factor of 3** is the **industry default** for cloud object storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob). - ✅ **Always state units** in derivations — marks are explicitly deducted for unit omissions in the 2024 KTU valuation rubric. - ✅ **Compression ratios:** Delta encoding (~4x), Gorilla (~10–12x), LZW (~2x) — pick the one matching your data type in the exam. - ✅ **Lifecycle policy:** Hot (SSD, 0–7 days) → Warm (HDD, 8–90 days) → Cold (Glacier, 91+ days) — automated via S3 Lifecycle Rules. - ✅ **NoSQL types to memorize:** Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j — bonus). - ✅ **CAP profiles of common DBs:** MongoDB = CP, Cassandra = AP, Redis = CA (single-node), HBase = CP, DynamoDB = AP (tunable). > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks "design a storage system," always structure your answer as: **(1) Tier selection with latency justification → (2) Database technology with CAP reasoning → (3) Compliance mention (HIPAA/FHIR) → (4) Cost calculation with units.** This four-part template covers the full 14 marks consistently.

topics.title: Introduction to Hadoop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT402 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Hadoop ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based framework managed by the Apache Software Foundation that enables the **distributed storage and distributed processing of massive datasets** across clusters of commodity computers. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage, rather than relying on a single high-end machine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Hadoop is a framework that allows for the distributed processing of large data sets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.* The framework is built around two core pillars: 1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** – handles distributed storage. 2. **MapReduce** – handles distributed processing. 3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** – manages cluster resources and job scheduling. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mega Library Imagine a single librarian (one computer) trying to catalog **100 million books** (big data). He would collapse under the workload. Now imagine instead **hiring 10,000 junior librarians** (commodity machines) and giving each one a **small section of the library to manage** (distributed storage). Every librarian also **summarizes their section and reports upward** (MapReduce). > [!NOTE] > **Why does this analogy matter in IoT & Biomedical Applications?** > In a hospital deploying thousands of IoT-enabled patient monitors, the data generated per second can cross **Petabytes (PB)**. A single central server cannot store or analyze this. Hadoop allows a **cluster of low-cost hospital servers** to collaboratively process patient vitals, ECG streams, and MRI archives in parallel. ### 1.3 The 3 V's of Big Data (Context for Hadoop) | Dimension | Standard Metric | Meaning in Hadoop Context | |---|---|---| | **Volume** | TB to PB to ZB | Hadoop scales horizontally (add more nodes) | | **Velocity** | MB/s to GB/s | Streaming ingestion through HDFS appends | | **Variety** | Structured, Semi-structured, Unstructured | Hadoop accepts ANY file format (text, image, JSON, XML, signal data) | > [!TIP] > **Remember for KTU Viva:** When asked *"Why Hadoop?"*, always answer: *"Because single-node systems fail on the 3 V's of Big Data — Hadoop provides **fault tolerance, scalability, and flexibility** at commodity hardware cost."* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Hadoop **Pillar 1 — HDFS (Storage Layer)** HDFS follows a **Master-Slave (NameNode-DataNode)** architecture. Files are split into **blocks** (default size **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x+; 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x). Each block is **replicated** across multiple DataNodes (default replication factor = **3**). **Pillar 2 — MapReduce (Processing Layer)** A programming model with two functions: - `map()` — processes input and emits (key, value) pairs. - `reduce()` — aggregates intermediate (key, value) pairs by key. **Pillar 3 — YARN (Resource Management Layer)** Introduced in Hadoop 2.x. Decouples resource management from processing. Components: - **ResourceManager (RM)** — Master, global scheduler. - **NodeManager (NM)** — Slave, per-node agent. - **ApplicationMaster (AM)** — Per-application negotiator. **Pillar 4 — Hadoop Common (Utilities)** Java libraries and utilities that support the other three modules. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Metrics | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | KTU Memory Hook | |---|---|---|---| | Default Block Size (Hadoop 2.x+) | **128 MB** | MB | "128" | | Default Block Size (Hadoop 1.x) | **64 MB** | MB | "64" | | Default Replication Factor | **R = 3** | copies | "Rule of 3" | | Effective Storage Requirement | $S_{effective} = S_{raw} \times R$ | bytes | Raw × Replication | | Number of Blocks | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Always round up | | Cluster Storage Capacity | $C = N_{nodes} \times D_{per\_node}$ | TB | Nodes × Disk | | Parallelism in MapReduce | $P = N_{map\_slots} + N_{reduce\_slots}$ | tasks | Maps + Reduces | | Throughput | $T = \dfrac{\text{Total Data Processed}}{\text{Time Elapsed}}$ | MB/s or GB/s | Data/Time | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Anti-Hallucination Note:** The default block size was **64 MB in Hadoop 1.x** and increased to **128 MB in Hadoop 2.x and later**. Some configurations use **256 MB**. KTU may ask this comparison directly. Do NOT confuse with HDFS default! ### 2.3 Why Hadoop? — Engineering Utility - **Fault Tolerance**: If a DataNode fails, the NameNode instructs another DataNode to replicate the lost block from a remaining replica. The system *self-heals*. - **Scalability**: Linear scaling — doubling nodes roughly doubles throughput. Cost grows linearly, not exponentially. - **Data Locality**: Computation is moved *to the data*, not data to computation. Reduces network congestion. - **Flexibility**: Accepts structured (CSV, tables), semi-structured (JSON, XML, logs), and unstructured (images, biomedical signals, video) data. > [!NOTE] > **Biomedical Engineering Application:** Hadoop is used in **genomic sequencing** (e.g., processing 100 GB+ per patient genome), **EHR (Electronic Health Record) analytics**, and **wearable IoT data lakes**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Worked Example: Block Calculation for a Biomedical MRI Dataset **Problem:** A hospital MRI scanner produces a single imaging archive of **2.5 GB**. Using Hadoop 2.x with default configuration, calculate: 1. Number of HDFS blocks. 2. Total raw storage considering replication. 3. Number of DataNodes required to fully distribute 1 replica each. **Given:** - File size $F = 2.5 \text{ GB} = 2.5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2560 \text{ MB}$ - Block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ (Hadoop 2.x default) - Replication factor $R = 3$ (default) **Step 1 — Number of Blocks** $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil $$ Substituting values: $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{2560 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil $$ Computing the division: $$ \frac{2560}{128} = 20.0 $$ Since 20.0 is an exact integer, the ceiling function gives: $$ N_{blocks} = 20 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 2 — Total Effective Storage** $$ S_{effective} = S_{raw} \times R $$ $$ S_{effective} = 2.5 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 7.5 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 3 — DataNodes for Single Replica Distribution** For 20 distinct blocks, each placed on one DataNode: $$ N_{DataNodes} = N_{blocks} = 20 $$ But considering replication (R = 3), total unique DataNode storage slots used: $$ N_{slots} = N_{blocks} \times R = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ slots} $$ So at minimum **20 DataNodes** are needed (each holding 3 blocks, possibly co-located). > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** The last block is often **smaller than the full block size** (e.g., a 130 MB file → 2 blocks: one 128 MB + one 2 MB). This is why we use the **ceiling function** (round up). If you forget the ceiling, you will lose **1 mark** in KTU valuation. ### 3.2 MapReduce Word Count — Exhaustive Walkthrough **Scenario:** Three IoT sensor log files contain sentences. Count word frequency. **Input Data (3 files):** - `sensor1.txt`: "heart rate high" - `sensor2.txt`: "heart rate normal" - `sensor3.txt`: "rate normal high" #### Phase 1 — Splitting Each file becomes an **InputSplit** assigned to a Mapper. #### Phase 2 — Mapping (Executed on each node in parallel) **Mapper 1** processes `sensor1.txt`: | Input Line | Emitted (key, value) | |---|---| | "heart rate high" | (heart, 1), (rate, 1), (high, 1) | **Mapper 2** processes `sensor2.txt`: | Input Line | Emitted (key, value) | |---|---| | "heart rate normal" | (heart, 1), (rate, 1), (normal, 1) | **Mapper 3** processes `sensor3.txt`: | Input Line | Emitted (key, value) | |---|---| | "rate normal high" | (rate, 1), (normal, 1), (high, 1) | #### Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework Managed) The framework groups by key: | Key | Values Received | |---|---| | heart | (1, 1) | | rate | (1, 1, 1) | | high | (1, 1) | | normal | (1, 1) | #### Phase 4 — Reducing Each Reducer sums the values: $$ \text{count}(key) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i $$ **Reducer Output:** | Key | Sum | |---|---| | heart | 2 | | rate | 3 | | high | 2 | | normal | 2 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** The **Shuffle and Sort phase is the most network-intensive** because intermediate data moves between Map nodes and Reduce nodes. This is why Hadoop prefers **data locality** — placing computation close to data. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | RDBMS (Traditional) | Hadoop | |---|---|---| | Data Size | GB | TB to PB | | Schema | Schema-on-Write (fixed) | Schema-on-Read (flexible) | | Processing | OLTP (real-time queries) | Batch (MapReduce) | | Data Type | Structured | Any (structured + unstructured) | | Cost | High (proprietary) | Low (commodity) | | Transactions | ACID compliant | Eventually consistent | | Scaling | Vertical (bigger machine) | Horizontal (more machines) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB client["Client Application"] namenode["NameNode Master
Stores Metadata
Manages Replicas"] dn1["DataNode 1
Block A B C"] dn2["DataNode 2
Block A B C"] dn3["DataNode 3
Block A B C"] secondary["Secondary NameNode
Checkpoint Helper"] client -->|"Read/Write Request"| namenode namenode -->|"Block Location Metadata"| client namenode -->|"Replication Instruction"| dn1 namenode -->|"Replication Instruction"| dn2 namenode -->|"Replication Instruction"| dn3 namenode -.->|"Edit Logs Merge"| secondary dn1 <-->|"Pipeline Replication"| dn2 dn2 <-->|"Pipeline Replication"| dn3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Explanation:** The NameNode is the **master** that holds file-to-block mapping in memory. The DataNodes are **slaves** that store actual block data. The Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup NameNode — it only performs periodic **checkpointing** of the edit logs to prevent the NameNode's metadata from growing unbounded. ### 4.2 MapReduce Execution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR input["Input Data
HDFS Blocks"] split["Input Split"] map1["Mapper 1"] map2["Mapper 2"] map3["Mapper 3"] shuf["Shuffle and Sort"] red1["Reducer 1
Key range A-H"] red2["Reducer 2
Key range I-Z"] output["Final Output
HDFS"] input --> split split --> map1 split --> map2 split --> map3 map1 --> shuf map2 --> shuf map3 --> shuf shuf --> red1 shuf --> red2 red1 --> output red2 --> output ``` ### 4.3 Complete Hadoop Ecosystem Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB layer1["Layer 1 Data Ingestion
