KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** *Define Hadoop. List any TWO features of Hadoop.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Hadoop is an open-source Apache framework that enables the **distributed storage and processing of large datasets** across clusters of commodity computers using the HDFS and MapReduce programming model. **Features (1 mark — any 2 of the following):** 1. **Scalability** — Horizontal scaling by adding more nodes. 2. **Fault Tolerance** — Automatic data replication and recovery. 3. **Data Locality** — Computation is moved to data. 4. **Cost-effective** — Uses commodity hardware instead of high-end servers. 5. **Flexibility** — Handles structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Many students write "Hadoop is a database." This is **WRONG** and costs full marks. Hadoop is a **framework for distributed storage and processing**, not a database. HBase is the database component, not Hadoop itself. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** *Explain the 3 V's of Big Data.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 3 V's of Big Data, as defined by Gartner, are: 1. **Volume** (1 mark): The sheer amount of data generated, ranging from **Terabytes (TB) to Petabytes (PB) to Zettabytes (ZB)**. Example: A single MRI scan producing ~250 MB; thousands of patients generate PB-scale archives. 2. **Velocity** (1 mark): The speed at which data is produced and must be processed. Example: IoT wearable sensors streaming **vitals at 1 kHz** continuously. 3. **Variety** (1 mark): The diverse formats of data — structured (tables), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, signals, videos). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) --- **Question A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** *(a) Explain the HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. List any two features of HDFS. (7 marks)* *(b) Explain the working of MapReduce with a suitable example. (7 marks)* --- #### (a) HDFS Architecture and Features (7 marks) **Architecture Explanation (5 marks):** HDFS follows a **master-slave architecture** with the following components: 1. **NameNode (Master)** (1 mark): - Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block-to-DataNode mapping) in RAM. - Manages the file system namespace. - Does NOT store actual data blocks. - Single point of failure (mitigated by Secondary NameNode in Hadoop 1.x, and High Availability setup in Hadoop 2.x+). 2. **DataNode (Slave)** (1 mark): - Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk. - Sends **heartbeat signals** (default every 3 seconds) to NameNode to confirm liveness. - Performs block creation, deletion, and replication upon NameNode instruction. 3. **Secondary NameNode** (1 mark): - Performs periodic **checkpointing** by merging the fsimage and edit logs. - Prevents NameNode's metadata from growing unbounded. - **It is NOT a hot standby** for the NameNode. 4. **Block Concept** (1 mark): - Default block size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+). - Files larger than 128 MB are split across multiple blocks. - Each block is **replicated 3 times** by default across different DataNodes (and preferably different racks). 5. **Write Pipeline** (1 mark): - Client → NameNode (metadata request) → Client → DataNode 1 → DataNode 2 → DataNode 3 (replica chain). **Features (2 marks — any 2):** - **Fault Tolerance**: Data is replicated; lost blocks are re-replicated automatically. - **Scalability**: Easily scales to thousands of nodes and petabytes of data. - **High Throughput**: Optimized for batch processing of large files rather than low-latency access. - **Data Locality**: Computation is scheduled on nodes that hold the relevant data blocks, reducing network I/O. *(Include the HDFS diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)* --- #### (b) MapReduce Working with Example (7 marks) **Explanation of Phases (4 marks):** MapReduce is a **distributed programming paradigm** based on two functions: 1. **Map Phase (2 marks)**: - Input: Key-value pairs from HDFS. - Processing: User-defined `map()` function processes each (k1, v1) pair and emits a list of intermediate (k2, v2) pairs. - Output: Written to local disk (not HDFS) for fault tolerance. 2. **Shuffle and Sort Phase (1 mark)**: - Framework-managed. - All intermediate values for the same key are grouped together across all mappers. - Most network-intensive phase. 3. **Reduce Phase (1 mark)**: - Input: Grouped (key, list of values). - Processing: User-defined `reduce()` function aggregates the values. - Output: Written back to HDFS. **Word Count Example (3 marks):** *Input: Two files:* - `file1.txt`: "Deer Bear River" - `file2.txt`: "Bear Car Bear" | Phase | Output | |---|---| | **Map file1** | (Deer, 1), (Bear, 1), (River, 1) | | **Map file2** | (Bear, 1), (Car, 1), (Bear, 1) | | **Shuffle & Sort** | Bear: (1, 1, 1); Car: (1); Deer: (1); River: (1) | | **Reduce** | Bear: 3; Car: 1; Deer: 1; River: 1 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Do **NOT** skip the **Shuffle and Sort phase** — it is a frequently asked 2-mark question. Students often jump from Map directly to Reduce and lose marks. > - In the word count example, students often miss the **second "Bear"** in `file2.txt` and write Bear: 2 instead of 3. This is a common arithmetic error. > - Drawing a **labeled flow diagram** adds **1-2 marks** in the examiner's discretion. --- **Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question — Alternative Choice]** *(a) Compare Hadoop and RDBMS. Explain any three components of the Hadoop ecosystem. (7 marks)* *(b) A hospital generates 850 GB of MRI imaging data daily. Using Hadoop 2.x with default block size and replication factor, calculate: (i) Number of blocks per day. (ii) Effective storage consumption per day. (iii) Number of DataNodes required if each holds 1 TB of usable storage. (7 marks)* --- #### (a) Hadoop vs RDBMS and Hadoop Ecosystem (7 marks) **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | RDBMS | Hadoop | |---|---|---| | Data Volume | GB | TB to PB | | Data Type | Structured only | All types (structured/unstructured) | | Schema | Static (Schema-on-Write) | Dynamic (Schema-on-Read) | | Processing | OLTP, real-time | Batch, high-throughput | | Transactions | ACID compliant | BASE (Eventually consistent) | | Cost | High (licenses) | Low (open-source) | | Scaling | Vertical | Horizontal | | Speed | Fast for small data | Fast for huge data | **Hadoop Ecosystem Components (3 marks — any 3):** 1. **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System): Distributed, fault-tolerant storage layer that splits files into blocks and replicates them across cluster nodes. 2. **MapReduce**: Distributed processing programming model with map and reduce functions for parallel computation. 3. **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator): Resource management and job scheduling layer introduced in Hadoop 2.x, decoupling resource management from MapReduce. 4. **HBase**: A NoSQL, columnar, real-time database built on HDFS for random read/write access. 5. **Hive**: A data warehouse system that provides SQL-like query (HQL) capabilities over HDFS data. 6. **Pig**: A high-level scripting platform (Pig Latin) for transforming large datasets. 7. **Sqoop**: Tool for bulk transfer of data between Hadoop and relational databases. 8. **Flume**: Service for collecting, aggregating, and moving large amounts of streaming log data into HDFS. --- #### (b) Storage Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - Daily data: $D = 850 \text{ GB} = 850 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 870{,}400 \text{ MB}$ - Block size: $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ - Replication factor: $R = 3$ - Per-DataNode usable storage: $S_{node} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$ **Part (i) — Number of Blocks per Day (2 marks):** $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{870{,}400 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil $$ Performing the division: $$ \frac{870{,}400}{128} = 6800.0 $$ Since this is an exact integer: $$ N_{blocks} = 6800 \text{ blocks/day} $$ **Part (ii) — Effective Storage Consumption per Day (2 marks):** $$ S_{effective} = D \times R = 850 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 2550 \text{ GB/day} $$ Converting to TB for clarity: $$ S_{effective} = \frac{2550}{1024} \approx 2.49 \text{ TB/day} $$ **Part (iii) — Number of DataNodes Required (3 marks):** Assuming each DataNode must store the full effective load (worst-case sizing): $$ N_{DataNodes} = \left\lceil \frac{S_{effective}}{S_{node}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2550 \text{ GB}}{1024 \text{ GB}} \right\rceil $$ Performing the division: $$ \frac{2550}{1024} \approx 2.49 $$ Applying the ceiling function: $$ N_{DataNodes} = 3 \text{ DataNodes} $$ **Verification (2 marks):** - 3 DataNodes × 1024 GB = 3072 GB available. - Required: 2550 GB. - 3072 GB > 2550 GB ✓ (sufficient). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse **GB vs GiB**. Hadoop uses binary (1 GB = 1024 MB), but some literature uses decimal (1 GB = 1000 MB). **Always show your unit conversion step explicitly** to avoid losing 1 mark. Also, applying the **ceiling function** is essential — using floor or simple division is a frequent 1-mark deduction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - [x] **Hadoop = Framework** (not a database) for **distributed storage + processing** of big data. - [x] **Two core components**: **HDFS** (storage) and **MapReduce** (processing); **YARN** added in 2.x. - [x] **HDFS Block Size**: **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+), **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x). - [x] **Default Replication Factor**: **R = 3** copies per block. - [x] **Number of Blocks Formula**: $\lceil F / B \rceil$ — always **round up**. - [x] **Effective Storage**: $S_{effective} = S_{raw} \times R$. - [x] **NameNode** stores **metadata only**; **DataNodes** store **actual data blocks**. - [x] **Secondary NameNode** is a **checkpoint helper**, NOT a NameNode backup. - [x] **MapReduce has 4 phases**: Split → Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce. - [x] **Shuffle & Sort** is the most **network-intensive** phase. - [x] **3 V's of Big Data**: Volume, Velocity, Variety. - [x] **Data Locality**: Move computation to data, not data to computation. - [x] **Schema-on-Read** (Hadoop) vs **Schema-on-Write** (RDBMS). - [x] **Biomedical/IoT Use Cases**: Genomic sequencing, EHR analytics, wearable vitals streams, MRI archives. - [x] **Hadoop Ecosystem Layer Memory**: Ingestion → Storage → Processing → Resource Mgmt → Query → Coordination → UI. - [x] **Default Heartbeat Interval** of DataNode to NameNode: **3 seconds**. - [x] **Hadoop is written in Java** and licensed under **Apache License 2.0**. --- **End of Module 4 — Introduction to Hadoop Notes** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) | CO-Mapped | Board-Exam Ready*
